## Agent 2/B result: O1 folded main/dual nonalignment

I attacked the Agent 2 target: prove or break

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\bigl(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2\bigr),
\]

assuming Agent 1 supplies same-branch cell mass. This is exactly the folded cancellation subproblem requested in the coordinator prompt. 

### Verdict

**Tilted regime:** closes conditionally by branch dominance.

**Symmetric regime:** not currently provable from same-branch mass or the AFE phase relation alone.

So the correct status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Folded main/dual nonalignment must remain an independent O1 input.}
}
\]

The AFE phase relation does **not** by itself prevent

\[
B^+ \approx -e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-.
\]

In fact, at a \(Z_\alpha\)-zero, global plus/dual cancellation is structurally expected. What O1 needs is much stronger: it needs to rule out **cellwise** folded cancellation across all paired cells.

---

## 1. Exact folded phase condition

Let

\[
a=B^+,\qquad b=B^-,\qquad \theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha),
\qquad \delta=Q^{-A}.
\]

The target is

\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2\ge \delta(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2).
\]

Squaring gives the exact obstruction:

\[
\|a\|^2+\|b\|^2
+
2\Re\!\left(e^{-i\theta}\langle a,b\rangle\right)
\ge
\delta^2(\|a\|+\|b\|)^2.
\]

Thus failure is equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
\Re\!\left(e^{-i\theta}\langle B^+,B^-\rangle\right)
\le
-\frac{\|B^+\|^2+\|B^-\|^2}{2}
+
\frac{\delta^2}{2}
\left(\|B^+\|+\|B^-\|\right)^2.
}
\]

This is the correct phase-specific condition. The stronger condition

\[
|\langle B^+,B^-\rangle|
\le
(1-\delta)\|B^+\|\|B^-\|
\]

is only sufficient, not equivalent. It forbids all near-collinearity, whereas the actual problem only forbids collinearity in the **specific bad direction**

\[
B^+ \approx -e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-.
\]

---

## 2. Tilted regime: conditional branch-dominance lemma

In the regime

\[
|\alpha|Q\gtrsim \log Q,
\]

the AFE weights contain asymmetric factors of the form

\[
e^{-\alpha\log n},
\qquad
e^{+\alpha\log(m_0/(2\pi n))}.
\]

Since the two folded branches occupy paired frequency windows

\[
x^+\in[c_-,c_+],
\qquad
x^-\in[1-c_+,1-c_-],
\]

with \(c_+<1/2\), the relative branch weight gains a polynomial factor once

\[
|\alpha|Q\ge C\log Q.
\]

A safe lemma is:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Tilted folded nonalignment from branch dominance]
Let \(a=B^+(m_0,\alpha)\), \(b=B^-(m_0,\alpha)\), and
\(\theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)\).  Suppose
\[
\min\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}
\le
\rho\max\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}
\]
with
\[
\rho\le \frac{1-\delta}{1+\delta},
\qquad
\delta=Q^{-A}.
\]
Then
\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\ge
\delta(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2).
\]
In particular, if \(\rho\le Q^{-C}\) for \(C>A\), folded nonalignment holds
with polynomial margin.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
By the reverse triangle inequality,
\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\ge
\bigl|\|a\|_2-\|b\|_2\bigr|.
\]
Assume \(\|b\|_2\le \rho\|a\|_2\).  Then
\[
\frac{\|a\|_2-\|b\|_2}{\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2}
\ge
\frac{1-\rho}{1+\rho}
\ge \delta.
\]
The other case is identical.
\end{proof}
```

This proves the tilted case **only after** one has a legitimate AFE-to-cell norm dominance transfer. Same-branch mass alone does not automatically give branch dominance, because one branch may suffer stronger internal cell cancellation than the other.

So the tilted route should be recorded as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Tilted O1 closes if AFE tilt implies branch-norm dominance after cell projection.}
}
\]

---

## 3. Symmetric regime: countermodel / obstruction

In the symmetric regime

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll \log Q,
\]

and especially

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1,
\]

the two branches have comparable weight scale. Then same-branch mass does not prevent folded cancellation.

Take any nonzero vector \(v\in\mathbb C^{M_{\rm cell}}\) and set

\[
B^+=v,
\qquad
B^-=-e^{-i\Theta_Z}v.
\]

Then

\[
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2=2\|v\|_2^2,
\]

so same-branch mass is large, but

\[
B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-
=
v-v
=
0.
\]

Thus Agent 1’s same-branch O1 input is logically insufficient.

More importantly, this countermodel is aligned with the exact obstruction: it saturates

\[
\Re\!\left(e^{-i\Theta_Z}\langle B^+,B^-\rangle\right)
=
-\|B^+\|_2\|B^-\|_2.
\]

The only missing step between this vector countermodel and the actual zeta object is an arithmetic theorem excluding simultaneous realization by the prime-log orbit

\[
m_0\log n \pmod{2\pi}
\]

inside every paired cell at a prescribed zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\). That is exactly the unproved nonrecurrence content.

So the symmetric branch reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Exclude phase-specific cellwise anti-alignment of the actual prime-log orbit.}
}
\]

This is not a consequence of the AFE structure alone.

---

## 4. Why \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\) does not save this

At a zero,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

only imposes a **global scalar cancellation** among all cells:

\[
\sum_r
\left(
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
+
B_r^{\rm bg}
+
B_r^E
\right)
\approx 0.
\]

O1 needs the much stronger statement

\[
\sum_r
\left|
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
+
B_r^{\rm bg}
+
B_r^E
\right|^2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The zero condition is compatible with, and may even encourage, total main/dual cancellation. It does not prohibit cancellation cell by cell. Therefore it cannot be used by itself to prove folded nonalignment.

---

## 5. Background/error transfer

Once a main/dual bound exists, background/error transfer is straightforward.

Let

\[
E_{\rm cell}=B^{\rm bg}+B^E.
\]

If

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\ge
\delta(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2)
\]

and

\[
\|E_{\rm cell}\|_2
\le
\frac12\delta(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2),
\]

then

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-+E_{\rm cell}\|_2
\ge
\frac12\delta(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2).
\]

So background/error is not the hard part if it is perturbative. If it is not perturbative, then O1 must be stated directly for the full canonical vector, as the current draft already does.

---

## 6. Proposed named input to keep

The clean formulation is:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1b: folded main/dual cell nonalignment]
\label{hyp:O1b-folded-main-dual-nonalignment}
At every admissible ordinary-scale zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the paired
main and dual cell vectors satisfy
\[
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)+e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)\|_2+\|B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2
\right),
\]
unless the tilted branch-dominance alternative
\[
\min\{\|B^+\|_2,\|B^-\|_2\}
\le
Q^{-A'}
\max\{\|B^+\|_2,\|B^-\|_2\}
\]
already holds with \(A'\) sufficiently large.
Equivalently, in the symmetric regime the exact bad direction
\[
B^+(m_0,\alpha)\approx -e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\]
is excluded at polynomial scale for the actual prime-log orbit.
\end{hypothesis}
```

And a useful remark:

```latex
\begin{remark}[Why same-branch mass does not imply folded nonalignment]
Same-branch cell mass controls
\[
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2,
\]
but it does not control the phase-specific cross term
\[
\Re\!\left(e^{-i\Theta_Z}\langle B^+,B^-\rangle\right).
\]
Indeed, the vector configuration
\[
B^+=v,\qquad B^-=-e^{-i\Theta_Z}v
\]
has maximal same-branch mass but zero folded cell vector.  Therefore the
folded main/dual nonalignment is a separate actual-orbit nonrecurrence input,
except in the tilted branch-dominance regime.
\end{remark}
```

## Bottom line

Agent 2 cannot close O1 from Agent 1’s same-branch mass. The tilted case is tractable under branch-norm dominance, but the symmetric case remains a real arithmetic obstruction.

Final status:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1 = O1a same-branch mass + O1b folded phase-specific nonalignment + canonical bg/error transfer.}
}
\]

O1b is not discharged by the current draft.

## Agent 2/B — continued attack on O1 folded main/dual nonalignment

I pushed the folded part one level deeper. The coordinator’s target is specifically the full canonical cell vector

\[
B_r=B_r^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B_r^-+B_r^{\rm bg}+B_r^E
\]

and the core task is to prove or break

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\bigl(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2\bigr),
\]

with background/error handled afterward. 

### New result: exact bad-lock normal form

Let

\[
a=B^+(m_0,\alpha),\qquad b=B^-(m_0,\alpha),\qquad
\theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha),
\]

and suppose folded nonalignment fails at scale \(\varepsilon\):

\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\le
\varepsilon(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2).
\]

Then two things are forced.

First, the branch norms must be almost equal:

\[
\frac{1-\varepsilon}{1+\varepsilon}
\le
\frac{\|a\|_2}{\|b\|_2}
\le
\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}.
\]

Second, the normalized branch vectors must be almost exactly anti-parallel in the **single AFE-prescribed phase direction**:

\[
\left\|
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2}
+
e^{i\theta}
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\|_2
\ll \varepsilon.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\Re\left(
e^{-i\theta}
\left\langle
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2},
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\rangle
\right)
\le
-1+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]

So the true obstruction is not generic correlation. It is a **phase-locked anti-isometry**

\[
B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^+(m_0,\alpha)
\]

between the plus and folded-dual cell vectors.

This is stronger and cleaner than the previous vector countermodel.

---

## Tilted regime: stronger conditional closure

The tilted regime should be split into two statements.

### Lemma: abstract branch dominance closes folded O1

If

\[
\min\{\|B^+\|_2,\|B^-\|_2\}
\le
Q^{-C}
\max\{\|B^+\|_2,\|B^-\|_2\}
\]

with \(C>A\), then

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\bigl(\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2\bigr)
\]

for \(Q\) sufficiently large.

Proof is just reverse triangle:

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\ge
\bigl|\|B^+\|_2-\|B^-\|_2\bigr|.
\]

So tilted O1 reduces to proving actual branch-norm dominance.

### AFE tilt gives dominance only with a no-internal-collapse input

For \(|\alpha|Q\gtrsim \log Q\), the plus and folded-dual weights are separated by approximately

\[
\exp\bigl(c|\alpha|Q\bigr),
\]

because the wide packet satisfies

\[
c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad c_+<\frac12,
\]

while the folded dual lives near the complementary range

\[
(1-c_+)Q\le \log n^\vee\le (1-c_-)Q.
\]

Thus tilt should give a polynomially dominant branch once

\[
|\alpha|Q\ge C\log Q.
\]

But there is a caveat: dominance of **raw AFE coefficients** does not automatically imply dominance of the **cell vector norms** if the dominant branch has severe internal same-cell cancellation. Therefore the tilted branch closes only under the following sharpened input:

\[
\boxed{
\text{tilted raw branch dominance}
+
\text{same-branch no-collapse for the dominant branch}
\Longrightarrow
\text{folded O1}.
}
\]

This is a real improvement: in the tilted regime, Agent 2 does not need a separate folded phase theorem if Agent 1 proves same-branch mass in a branch-sensitive way.

---

## Symmetric regime: remaining hard obstruction

In the symmetric regime

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll \log Q,
\]

especially

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1,
\]

the two branches have comparable AFE size. Then folded failure is exactly the anti-lock event

\[
B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^+(m_0,\alpha).
\]

The zero condition

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\]

does not exclude this. In fact, it only imposes a scalar global cancellation, whereas O1 requires an \(\ell^2\) lower bound over cells. The scalar zero is compatible with every cell cancelling separately.

So the symmetric regime reduces to the following prime-log anti-lock exclusion:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\not\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^+(m_0,\alpha)
\text{ at polynomial scale.}
}
\]

I do not see a way to derive this from the AFE functional equation alone. It is an actual arithmetic nonrecurrence statement about the paired prime-log orbit.

---

## Sharper named input: O1b should be stated this way

I would refine the paper’s O1 package into:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1b: phase-locked folded anti-alignment exclusion]
\label{hyp:O1b-phase-locked-folded-anti-alignment}
Let \(m_0\) be an admissible ordinary-scale zero of \(Z_\alpha\), and let
\[
a=B^+(m_0,\alpha),\qquad b=B^-(m_0,\alpha),
\qquad \theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha).
\]
Then either the tilted branch-dominance alternative holds,
\[
\min\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}
\le Q^{-A'}\max\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\},
\]
with \(A'\) large enough for reverse-triangle absorption, or else the
symmetric anti-lock is excluded:
\[
\left\|
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2}
+
e^{i\theta}
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\|_2
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Re\left(
e^{-i\theta}
\left\langle
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2},
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\rangle
\right)
\ge
-1+Q^{-A}.
\]
\end{hypothesis}
```

This is better than the earlier formulation because it isolates the exact bad geometry: **not correlation, but normalized phase-locked anti-parallelism**.

---

## Useful lemma to insert before O1b

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Folded failure forces phase-locked anti-alignment]
\label{lem:folded-failure-forces-phase-locked-anti-alignment}
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb C^N\), \(\theta\in\mathbb R\), and \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).
If
\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\le
\varepsilon(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2),
\]
then
\[
\frac{1-\varepsilon}{1+\varepsilon}
\le
\frac{\|a\|_2}{\|b\|_2}
\le
\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}
\]
and
\[
\left\|
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2}
+
e^{i\theta}
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\|_2
\ll \varepsilon.
\]
In particular,
\[
\Re\left(
e^{-i\theta}
\left\langle
\frac{a}{\|a\|_2},
\frac{b}{\|b\|_2}
\right\rangle
\right)
\le
-1+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The reverse triangle inequality gives
\[
|\|a\|_2-\|b\|_2|
\le
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\le
\varepsilon(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2),
\]
which implies the norm-ratio bound.  Write \(A=\|a\|_2\), \(B=\|b\|_2\),
\(u=a/A\), and \(v=b/B\).  Since \(A/B=1+O(\varepsilon)\), we have
\[
\|u+e^{i\theta}v\|_2
\le
A^{-1}\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
+
|A^{-1}B-1|
\ll \varepsilon.
\]
Squaring gives the stated inner-product form.
\end{proof}
```

## Agent 2/B — O1b folded anti-lock attack

I continued the assigned O1b track: prove the tilted case if possible, then test whether the symmetric anti-lock can be broken using \(m\)- or \(\alpha\)-variation. The coordinator’s Agent 2 instruction is the folded main/dual phase-specific bad-zero problem. 

# Result

\[
\boxed{
\text{Tilted O1b closes under branch-sensitive O1a.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symmetric O1b remains an independent prime-log anti-lock input.}
}
\]

The extra attack via \(m\)- and \(\alpha\)-variation gives useful **transversality**, but it does **not** exclude a pointwise anti-lock at a specified zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\).

---

# 1. Tilted regime theorem

Let

\[
a=B^+(m_0,\alpha),\qquad b=B^-(m_0,\alpha),
\qquad \theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha).
\]

The desired O1b inequality is

\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\bigl(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2\bigr).
\]

Assume the wide packet satisfies

\[
c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad 0<c_-<c_+<\frac12,
\]

and define

\[
\eta:=1-2c_+>0.
\]

In the AFE-paired support, the folded dual occupies the complementary range

\[
(1-c_+)Q\le \log n^\vee\le (1-c_-)Q.
\]

Thus the \(\alpha\)-tilt separates the two branches by at least

\[
\exp(\eta|\alpha|Q),
\]

up to polynomial normalization factors, provided the cell projection does not internally collapse the favored branch.

A clean theorem is:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Tilted folded nonalignment from branch-sensitive O1a]
\label{lem:tilted-folded-nonalignment-from-branch-sensitive-O1a}
Let \(a=B^+(m_0,\alpha)\), \(b=B^-(m_0,\alpha)\), and
\(\theta=\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)\).  Suppose that in the tilted regime
\[
|\alpha|Q\ge C_\alpha\log Q
\]
the AFE weights and branch-sensitive same-branch cell noncollapse imply
\[
\frac{\min\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}}
     {\max\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}}
\le
Q^{C_0}e^{-\eta|\alpha|Q},
\qquad
\eta=1-2c_+>0.
\]
If \(C_\alpha>(A+C_0+2)/\eta\), then
\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2
\right)
\]
for all sufficiently large \(Q\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Set
\[
\rho:=
\frac{\min\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}}
     {\max\{\|a\|_2,\|b\|_2\}}.
\]
By hypothesis,
\[
\rho\le Q^{C_0}e^{-\eta|\alpha|Q}
\le
Q^{C_0-\eta C_\alpha}
\le Q^{-A-2}.
\]
The reverse triangle inequality gives
\[
\|a+e^{i\theta}b\|_2
\ge
\bigl|\|a\|_2-\|b\|_2\bigr|
=
\frac{1-\rho}{1+\rho}
\left(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2\right).
\]
Since \(\rho\le Q^{-A-2}\), the prefactor is \(>Q^{-A}\) for large \(Q\).
\end{proof}
```

So the tilted part is no longer a conceptual blocker, but it depends on Agent 1 proving O1a in a **branch-sensitive** form. A total estimate

\[
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2\ge Q^{-A}
\]

is not enough. One needs enough information to say the branch favored by the tilt has not collapsed under cell projection.

---

# 2. Symmetric bad-lock normal form

In the symmetric regime

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll \log Q,
\]

especially

\[
|\alpha|Q\ll1,
\]

the AFE weights are comparable. Folded failure means

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_2
\le
\varepsilon
\left(
\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2
\right).
\]

As already derived, this forces

\[
\frac{\|B^+\|_2}{\|B^-\|_2}=1+O(\varepsilon)
\]

and

\[
\left\|
\frac{B^+}{\|B^+\|_2}
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}
\frac{B^-}{\|B^-\|_2}
\right\|_2
\ll \varepsilon.
\]

Thus the bad event is the phase-locked anti-isometry

\[
\boxed{
B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B^+(m_0,\alpha).
}
\]

This is not generic decorrelation. It is a single precise bad direction.

---

# 3. \(m\)-variation does not exclude pointwise anti-lock

Write the folded cell vector as

\[
F_r(m,\alpha)
:=
B_r^+(m,\alpha)
+
e^{i\Theta_Z(m,\alpha)}B_r^-(m,\alpha).
\]

Bad lock says

\[
F_r(m_0,\alpha)\approx0
\qquad \text{for all }r.
\]

Differentiate in \(m\). Abstractly,

\[
\partial_mF_r
=
\partial_mB_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}\partial_mB_r^-
+
i\Theta_Z'(m)e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-.
\]

In the folded AFE coordinates, the phase \(\Theta_Z'(m)\) is precisely what aligns the dual branch with the complementary frequency window. Therefore \(\partial_mF_r\) is a **first folded moment mismatch**, not an automatic contradiction.

More concretely, if the effective plus and folded-dual frequencies inside the \(r\)-th cell are \(\xi_{r,n}^+\) and \(\xi_{r,n}^-\), then

\[
F_r(m_0,\alpha)\approx0
\]

only says the zeroth moments anti-align:

\[
\sum_n A_{r,n}^+
\approx
-
\sum_n A_{r,n}^-.
\]

The derivative tests the first moments:

\[
\partial_mF_r(m_0,\alpha)
\sim
i\left(
\sum_n \xi_{r,n}^+A_{r,n}^+
+
\sum_n \xi_{r,n}^-A_{r,n}^-
\right).
\]

This may be large, but largeness of \(\partial_mF_r\) does not contradict \(F_r(m_0)=0\). It only says the anti-lock is transverse in \(m\).

Therefore \(m\)-variation yields:

\[
\boxed{
\text{anti-lock is unstable unless higher folded moments also anti-lock.}
}
\]

But instability is not exclusion at a specified zero.

---

# 4. \(\alpha\)-variation gives stronger transversality, but still not exclusion

The \(\alpha\)-derivative is more revealing.

On paired supports,

\[
\partial_\alpha B_r^+
\approx
-(x_rQ)B_r^+,
\]

while the folded dual carries approximately

\[
\partial_\alpha B_r^-
\approx
-((1-x_r)Q)B_r^-,
\]

up to lower-order normalization and smooth cutoff terms.

If

\[
B_r^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-\approx0,
\]

then

\[
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-\approx -B_r^+.
\]

Thus

\[
\partial_\alpha F_r
\approx
\left((1-2x_r)Q-i\partial_\alpha\Theta_Z\right)B_r^+
+
\text{cell-width errors}.
\]

Since

\[
x_r\le c_+<\frac12,
\]

the real coefficient satisfies

\[
1-2x_r\ge \eta>0.
\]

So unless an additional weighted moment cancellation occurs,

\[
\|\partial_\alpha F\|_2
\gtrsim
Q\|B^+\|_2.
\]

This proves a useful local statement:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Symmetric anti-lock is \(\alpha\)-transverse]
\label{lem:symmetric-antilock-alpha-transverse}
Assume \(F=B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\) satisfies
\[
\|F(m_0,\alpha_0)\|_2
\le
\varepsilon
\left(
\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2
\right),
\]
and assume the paired cell frequencies obey
\[
x_r\le c_+<1/2.
\]
If the first \(\alpha\)-weighted folded moment does not itself anti-lock, then
\[
\|\partial_\alpha F(m_0,\alpha_0)\|_2
\ge
cQ
\left(
\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2
\right)
-
O(Q\varepsilon)
\left(
\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2
\right).
\]
Consequently the folded anti-lock tube has \(\alpha\)-width at most
\(O(\varepsilon/Q)\) near \((m_0,\alpha_0)\), modulo higher-moment anti-lock.
\end{lemma}
```

But again, this is not enough. The bad event can occur at an isolated value of \(\alpha\). The RH architecture needs exclusion at the actual \((m_0,\alpha)\), not merely small measure in \(\alpha\).

So \(\alpha\)-variation gives:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b bad lock is thin in the \(\alpha\)-family, but not impossible.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Global zero equation does not help

At a zero,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

The folded decomposition gives schematically

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)
=
\sum_r
\left(
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
\right)
+
B^{\rm bg}
+
B^E.
\]

If each cell satisfies

\[
B_r^+
+
e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
\approx0,
\]

then the global zero equation is automatically satisfied up to background/error. It is not contradictory. In fact, cellwise folded cancellation is a stronger form of the scalar cancellation required by \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\text{ is compatible with O1b failure.}
}
\]

The zero condition alone cannot prove O1b.

---

# 6. Neighboring cells do not create a deterministic contradiction

The same global phase \(e^{i\Theta_Z}\) applies to all cells. Therefore O1b failure imposes

\[
B_r^-
\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z}B_r^+
\qquad \text{for many }r.
\]

This is a strong multi-cell anti-lock condition. But in a phase-relaxed model it is still realizable by choosing the dual cell phases to match the plus cell phases with the prescribed global anti-phase.

So neighboring cells make the condition arithmetically rigid, but not algebraically impossible.

The missing theorem is precisely that the **actual prime-log orbit** cannot realize this multi-cell phase lock at a prescribed \(Z_\alpha\)-zero.

---

# 7. Stronger reduction: O1b requires a zero-restricted anti-lock theorem

The correct symmetric-regime missing input is:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[O1b-sym: zero-restricted folded anti-lock exclusion]
\label{hyp:O1b-sym-zero-restricted-folded-antilock}
Let \(m_0\) be an admissible ordinary-scale zero of \(Z_\alpha\), and assume
\[
|\alpha|Q\ll\log Q.
\]
Set
\[
F_r(m_0,\alpha)
=
B_r^+(m_0,\alpha)
+
e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}B_r^-(m_0,\alpha).
\]
Then
\[
\left(
\sum_r |F_r(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\right)^{1/2}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B^+(m_0,\alpha)\|_2+\|B^-(m_0,\alpha)\|_2
\right).
\]
Equivalently, the actual prime-log paired cell orbit avoids the phase-locked
anti-diagonal tube
\[
B^-
\approx
-e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^+
\]
at every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\).
\end{hypothesis}
```

A still sharper version, incorporating the variation attack, is:

```latex
\begin{hypothesis}[Higher-moment folded anti-lock exclusion]
\label{hyp:higher-moment-folded-antilock-exclusion}
At every admissible zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the actual paired prime-log orbit
does not satisfy simultaneous folded anti-lock for the zeroth and first
effective moments:
\[
\sum_r |F_r(m_0,\alpha)|^2
+
Q^{-2}\sum_r |\partial_\alpha F_r(m_0,\alpha)|^2
+
Q^{-2}\sum_r |\partial_m F_r(m_0,\alpha)|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B^+\|_2+\|B^-\|_2
\right)^2.
\]
\end{hypothesis}
```

This higher-moment version is probably more realistic as an arithmetic target, because exact zeroth-order folded cancellation can occur in toy models, but simultaneous anti-lock of zeroth, \(m\)-moment, and \(\alpha\)-moment data is much more rigid.

However, the current ordinary Gate–1 route still needs the zeroth-order O1b unless the paper explicitly modifies Gate–1 to accept higher folded moments.

---

# Final Agent 2 status

\[
\boxed{
\text{Tilted O1b: conditionally Green.}
}
\]

It closes from AFE branch dominance plus branch-sensitive O1a.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symmetric O1b: Red / independent input.}
}
\]

The symmetric bad lock is not broken by:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\]

nor by one-point \(m\)-variation, nor by one-point \(\alpha\)-variation.

Those variations prove only that anti-lock is thin/transverse in parameter space. They do not exclude an isolated anti-lock at a specified zero.

The exact missing theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted folded anti-lock exclusion for the actual prime-log paired cell orbit.}
}
\]

So O1b should remain a named independent arithmetic input unless the architecture is revised to use higher folded moments instead of zeroth-order folded cell mass.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:38:12)

_Thought for 1m 11s_

## Continued Agent 2 attack: reduce O1b to rank-lock plus transversality

I pushed the symmetric folded anti-lock further. The useful new object is not the folded vector itself,

\[
F=B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-,
\]

but the **projective rank-lock** between \(B^+\) and \(B^-\).

Let

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-.
\]

If O1b fails, then

\[
a\approx -e^{i\Theta_Z}b
\]

after normalization. This implies a stronger phase-free consequence:

\[
a_r b_s-a_s b_r\approx 0
\qquad\text{for all }r,s.
\]

So O1b failure forces the two cell vectors \(B^+\) and \(B^-\) to become nearly rank one:

\[
B^+\wedge B^-\approx 0.
\]

This removes the nuisance global phase \(\Theta_Z\). The bad event decomposes into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{projective lock }B^+\wedge B^-\approx0
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\text{specific scalar phase }B^+\approx -e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-.
}
\]

The projective lock is the more rigid part.

---

## 1. Exact consequence of folded failure

Suppose

\[
\|a+e^{i\Theta}b\|_2
\le
\varepsilon(\|a\|_2+\|b\|_2).
\]

Then, as before,

\[
\|a\|_2=(1+O(\varepsilon))\|b\|_2
\]

and

\[
a=-e^{i\Theta}b+O(\varepsilon\|a\|_2).
\]

Therefore for every pair of cells \(r,s\),

\[
a_r b_s-a_s b_r
=
O\!\left(\varepsilon\|a\|_2\|b\|_2\right).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\|a\wedge b\|
\ll
\varepsilon\|a\|_2\|b\|_2.
\]

So a sufficient route to O1b is the projective non-lock bound

\[
\boxed{
\|B^+\wedge B^-\|
\ge
Q^{-A}\|B^+\|_2\|B^-\|_2.
}
\]

This is stronger than O1b, but it has an advantage: it is independent of the global folded phase.

---

## 2. \(\alpha\)-variation of the rank-lock minors

Define the \(2\times2\) cell minor

\[
D_{rs}:=a_r b_s-a_s b_r.
\]

In the symmetric regime, the leading \(\alpha\)-derivatives are

\[
\partial_\alpha a_r
\approx
-x_r Q\,a_r,
\]

\[
\partial_\alpha b_r
\approx
-(1-x_r)Q\,b_r,
\]

where \(x_r\in[c_-,c_+]\) is the normalized log-frequency of the plus cell, and the dual cell sits at \(1-x_r\).

Then

\[
\partial_\alpha D_{rs}
=
(\partial_\alpha a_r)b_s+a_r(\partial_\alpha b_s)
-(\partial_\alpha a_s)b_r-a_s(\partial_\alpha b_r).
\]

Using the leading multipliers,

\[
\partial_\alpha D_{rs}
\approx
-Q(x_r+1-x_s)a_rb_s
+
Q(x_s+1-x_r)a_sb_r.
\]

At projective lock,

\[
a_rb_s\approx a_sb_r.
\]

Therefore

\[
\partial_\alpha D_{rs}
\approx
-2Q(x_r-x_s)a_rb_s.
\]

So if two cells \(r,s\) carry genuine mass and have separated normalized frequencies,

\[
|x_r-x_s|\ge \Delta,
\]

then projective lock is \(\alpha\)-transverse:

\[
|\partial_\alpha D_{rs}|
\gtrsim
Q\Delta |a_r b_s|.
\]

This is a real structural gain.

---

## 3. Along the zero trajectory \(m=m(\alpha)\)

At an ordinary zero, assuming \(Z_t(m_0,\alpha_0)\neq0\), the zero continues locally as

\[
Z(m(\alpha),\alpha)=0.
\]

Then

\[
m'(\alpha)
=
-\frac{Z_\alpha}{Z_t}.
\]

Now differentiate the rank-lock minor along this zero curve:

\[
\frac{d}{d\alpha}D_{rs}(m(\alpha),\alpha)
=
\partial_\alpha D_{rs}
+
m'(\alpha)\partial_mD_{rs}.
\]

The \(m\)-derivative has the analogous first-moment form. Schematically,

\[
\partial_mD_{rs}
\approx
-i\,2Q(x_r-x_s)a_rb_s
\]

up to folded phase convention and lower-order terms.

Thus along the zero trajectory,

\[
\frac{d}{d\alpha}D_{rs}
\approx
-2Q(x_r-x_s)(1+i m')a_rb_s.
\]

Since \(m'\in\mathbb R\), the factor \(1+i m'\) cannot vanish. Hence

\[
\left|
\frac{d}{d\alpha}D_{rs}
\right|
\gtrsim
Q|x_r-x_s|\,|a_rb_s|
\]

whenever the leading approximation is valid.

This is the strongest useful fact so far:

\[
\boxed{
\text{projective folded lock is transverse even along the zero manifold.}
}
\]

So the zero condition does not automatically align with the anti-lock condition.

---

## 4. But this still does not exclude an isolated bad zero

The above transversality proves that bad lock cannot persist over a macroscopic \(\alpha\)-interval unless higher moment locks also occur.

But it does **not** rule out

\[
D_{rs}(m_0,\alpha_0)=0
\]

at a single isolated zero.

A transverse crossing is still a crossing.

Therefore the attack upgrades O1b as follows:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b failure is not structurally stable in }\alpha,
\text{ but it remains pointwise possible.}
}
\]

To turn this into a proof, one needs an additional zero-restricted theorem saying that the zero trajectory cannot hit the projective anti-lock hypersurface.

That missing theorem is sharper than before.

---

## 5. Refined missing theorem

The prior missing input was:

\[
B^+\approx -e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^-.
\]

The sharper version is:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
B^+(m_0,\alpha)\wedge B^-(m_0,\alpha)
\not\approx0
}
\]

unless the mass is concentrated in one effective cell.

More explicitly, if there exist two cells \(r,s\) with

\[
|a_r|,|a_s|,|b_r|,|b_s|
\ge Q^{-A}\|a\|_2
\]

and

\[
|x_r-x_s|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then O1b would follow from a pointwise lower bound

\[
|a_rb_s-a_sb_r|
\ge
Q^{-A}\|a\|_2\|b\|_2.
\]

If no such pair exists, then same-branch mass is concentrated in one narrow cell cluster. In that case O1b degenerates to a scalar anti-phase problem:

\[
a_{r_0}
\approx
-e^{i\Theta_Z}b_{r_0}.
\]

So the full symmetric O1b splits into two subcases:

\[
\boxed{
\text{distributed mass: exclude projective rank-lock;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{single-cell mass: exclude scalar folded anti-phase.}
}
\]

This is a better decomposition than the previous global vector statement.

---

## 6. What this gives and what it does not

### It gives

A concrete overdetermined structure:

\[
Z=0,
\qquad
B^+\wedge B^-\approx0,
\qquad
B^+\approx -e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^-.
\]

The wedge condition is phase-free and has strong \(\alpha\)- and \(m\)-transversality when mass is distributed across separated cells.

### It does not give

A proof that no zero trajectory intersects the anti-lock hypersurface.

The transversality estimate says the intersection is simple, not impossible.

So the current status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b is reduced to a zero-restricted projective anti-lock exclusion.}
}
\]

That is a more precise missing arithmetic theorem than before.

---

## Updated Agent 2 conclusion

The tilted branch remains conditionally green under branch-sensitive O1a.

The symmetric branch is now sharpened to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{At }Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,\text{ the actual paired prime-log cell vectors must avoid}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
B^+\wedge B^-\approx0
\quad\text{and}\quad
B^+\approx -e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^-.
}
\]

The next mathematical target is no longer generic folded nonalignment. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-trajectory avoidance of the projective rank-lock hypersurface.}
}
\]

I do not see a route to prove that from local AFE algebra alone. It requires an actual prime-log bad-zero theorem.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:40:11)

I’ll now attack the remaining single-cell loophole and the distributed projective-lock case separately. The goal is to see whether one of them can be forced into a stronger contradiction than mere transversality.

_Thought for 1m 4s_

## Continued Agent 2 attack: symmetric anti-lock can be killed at first jet level

The previous round left symmetric O1b as an independent **value-level** anti-lock input:

\[
F_0:=B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-
\]

could be small at a zero. I now attacked the next natural question:

\[
\text{If }F_0\approx0,\text{ can the first local jet also be small?}
\]

The answer appears to be **no**, under the ordinary wide-packet separation \(c_+<1/2\). This is the strongest Agent 2 progress so far.

---

# 1. The key observation

Folded cancellation is value-level cancellation between two branches with **different local derivative symbols**.

For one cell \(r\), write

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-,
\qquad e=e^{i\Theta_Z}.
\]

The value-level folded cell is

\[
F_{0,r}=a_r+eb_r.
\]

Now apply a normalized first derivative in \(m\), after including the derivative of the folding phase. Schematically,

\[
F_{1,r}
=
Q^{-1}\partial_m(a_r+eb_r)
=
\sigma_r^+ a_r
+
e\sigma_r^- b_r
+
\text{lower-order/cell-width errors}.
\]

The important point is:

\[
\sigma_r^+\neq\sigma_r^-.
\]

In the ordinary wide packet,

\[
c_-Q\le\log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad c_+<\frac12,
\]

the branch-symbol gap is bounded below:

\[
|\sigma_r^+-\sigma_r^-|
\ge \kappa>0,
\]

with \(\kappa\) depending on \(1/2-c_+\).

Thus even if

\[
a_r+eb_r\approx0,
\]

the first jet becomes

\[
F_{1,r}
\approx
(\sigma_r^+-\sigma_r^-)a_r,
\]

which is large unless \(a_r\) itself is small.

This is not arithmetic. It is local branch-symbol linear algebra.

---

# 2. Deterministic two-symbol observability lemma

For each cell, consider the linear map

\[
(a_r,eb_r)
\longmapsto
\left(
a_r+eb_r,\,
\sigma_r^+a_r+e\sigma_r^-b_r
\right).
\]

Its matrix is

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 1\\
\sigma_r^+ & \sigma_r^-
\end{pmatrix},
\]

with determinant

\[
\sigma_r^- - \sigma_r^+.
\]

Therefore, if

\[
|\sigma_r^+-\sigma_r^-|\ge\kappa,
\qquad
|\sigma_r^\pm|\le C,
\]

then the smallest singular value is bounded below by a constant

\[
c=c(\kappa,C)>0.
\]

Hence

\[
|a_r+eb_r|^2
+
|\sigma_r^+a_r+e\sigma_r^-b_r|^2
\ge
c\left(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2\right).
\]

Summing over cells gives

\[
\boxed{
\|F_0\|_2^2+\|F_1\|_2^2
\ge
c
\left(
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2
\right)
-
\text{cell-width/error terms}.
}
\]

This is the main new result.

It means:

\[
\boxed{
\text{folded value anti-lock forces first-jet mass.}
}
\]

So symmetric O1b is only a blocker if the proof insists on a **value-only** folded cell lower bound.

---

# 3. Consequence for the bad-lock normal form

Previously, O1b failure meant

\[
B^-\approx -e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^+.
\]

Under this condition,

\[
F_0\approx0.
\]

But now

\[
F_1
=
Q^{-1}\partial_mF_0
\]

satisfies, at leading order,

\[
F_1
\approx
(\sigma^+-\sigma^-)B^+.
\]

Since

\[
|\sigma^+-\sigma^-|\ge\kappa,
\]

we get

\[
\|F_1\|_2
\gtrsim
\|B^+\|_2
\sim
\|B^-\|_2.
\]

Thus exact anti-lock does not destroy the folded packet. It merely moves its mass from the value row to the first derivative row.

This changes the diagnosis:

\[
\boxed{
\text{value-level O1b is independent, but jet-level folded observability is deterministic.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Single-cell loophole is also killed at first jet level

The previous decomposition left a possible single-cell scalar anti-phase case:

\[
a_{r_0}\approx -e b_{r_0},
\]

with nearly all mass concentrated in one cell.

This is exactly where value-level O1b is weakest. But the two-symbol argument handles it immediately.

If

\[
F_{0,r_0}=a_{r_0}+eb_{r_0}\approx0,
\]

then

\[
F_{1,r_0}
=
\sigma_{r_0}^+a_{r_0}
+
e\sigma_{r_0}^-b_{r_0}
\approx
(\sigma_{r_0}^+-\sigma_{r_0}^-)a_{r_0}.
\]

Since the packet is bounded away from the central wall \(x=1/2\),

\[
|\sigma_{r_0}^+-\sigma_{r_0}^-|\ge\kappa,
\]

so

\[
|F_{1,r_0}|
\gtrsim |a_{r_0}|.
\]

Therefore the single-cell scalar anti-phase is not a true obstruction if the ordinary Gate–1 row can use the first \(m\)-jet.

---

# 5. Distributed projective lock is also killed

If mass is distributed across several cells and

\[
B^+\wedge B^-\approx0,
\]

then value-level projective lock holds. But the same two-symbol map applies cellwise. It does not require two separated cells. Each cell already has plus/dual branch-symbol separation.

So the distributed projective-lock obstruction is also demoted:

\[
B^+\wedge B^-\approx0
\]

can make \(F_0\) small, but it cannot make both

\[
F_0
\quad\text{and}\quad
F_1
\]

small unless the same-branch mass itself is small.

Thus O1b’s symmetric obstruction disappears at first jet level.

---

# 6. What must be checked in the draft architecture

This result closes a **modified** O1b, not the value-only O1b as originally stated.

The question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does ordinary Gate 1 need value-level folded cell mass, or merely finite-jet folded cell mass?}
}
\]

If Gate 1 can accept

\[
\sum_r |F_{0,r}|^2
+
\sum_r |Q^{-1}\partial_mF_{0,r}|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2
\right),
\]

then O1b is no longer an independent arithmetic input. It becomes a deterministic two-symbol observability lemma plus O1a.

If Gate 1 is hard-coded to use only

\[
\sum_r |F_{0,r}|^2,
\]

then value-level O1b remains independent.

So the mathematical status is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b-value: still open.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b-jet: closed by branch-symbol separation.}
}
\]

---

# 7. Required hypotheses for the two-symbol closure

The deterministic closure needs only the following:

1. **Branch symbol separation**

   \[
   |\sigma_r^+-\sigma_r^-|\ge\kappa>0
   \]

   uniformly over ordinary cells. This is exactly where \(c_+<1/2\) is used.

2. **Bounded symbols**

   \[
   |\sigma_r^\pm|\le C.
   \]

3. **Small cell-width errors**

   If the symbols vary within a cell, their variation must be

   \[
   o(\kappa)
   \]

   or at least absorbable into the singular-value margin.

4. **Canonical background/error perturbativity**

   The \(B^{\rm bg}+B^E\) transfer must be smaller than the recovered first-jet mass, or else included in the same two-jet observability statement.

5. **Gate–1 compatibility**

   The derivative row produced by \(Q^{-1}\partial_m\) must feed into the same \(Z_\alpha\)-jet row used for ordinary Gate 1.

The only nontrivial architectural check is item 5.

---

# 8. Updated Agent 2 conclusion

Agent 2 now has a concrete mathematical improvement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Symmetric folded anti-lock is not a finite-jet obstruction.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_0\|_2^2+\|Q^{-1}\partial_mF_0\|_2^2
\gtrsim
\|B^+\|_2^2+\|B^-\|_2^2
}
\]

under ordinary branch-symbol separation.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O1a}
+
\text{two-symbol folded jet observability}
\Longrightarrow
\text{folded O1 at finite-jet level.}
}
\]

This is stronger than the previous “O1b is independent” conclusion, but only if the proof architecture allows O1 to supply a **finite-jet row** rather than a pure value row.

The next attack should be to inspect the ordinary Gate–1 bridge and determine whether its use of O1 is value-only or finite-jet-compatible. If finite-jet-compatible, O1b can likely be removed as an independent arithmetic hypothesis.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 18:42:08)

I’ll now test whether the two-symbol finite-jet observability can actually replace value-only O1b in the ordinary Gate–1 bridge. The main risk is loss when cellwise two-jets are compressed into the global \(C_\ell(\mathcal D)Z_\alpha\) row.

_Thought for 38s_

## Continued Agent 2 attack: finite-jet O1b hits a rank-count obstruction

The last round found a promising fact:

\[
F_{0,r}=a_r+e b_r
\]

may vanish by folded anti-lock, but the first folded jet

\[
F_{1,r}=\sigma_r^+a_r+e\sigma_r^-b_r
\]

is large because

\[
\sigma_r^+\neq \sigma_r^-.
\]

Cellwise, this is decisive. But the ordinary Gate–1 bridge does not necessarily observe cellwise two-jets. It observes a finite global derivative row of \(Z_\alpha\). The next question is therefore:

\[
\text{Does the global finite jet have enough rank to see the two-branch cellwise observability?}
\]

The answer is mixed.

---

# 1. Cellwise folded anti-lock is killed

For each cell \(r\), let

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-,
\qquad e=e^{i\Theta_Z}.
\]

Let the branch symbols be

\[
\sigma_r^+,\qquad \sigma_r^-,
\]

with ordinary separation

\[
|\sigma_r^+-\sigma_r^-|\ge \kappa>0.
\]

Then the two-output map

\[
(a_r,eb_r)
\mapsto
\left(
a_r+eb_r,\,
\sigma_r^+a_r+\sigma_r^-eb_r
\right)
\]

has determinant

\[
\sigma_r^- - \sigma_r^+.
\]

So

\[
|a_r+eb_r|^2
+
|\sigma_r^+a_r+\sigma_r^-eb_r|^2
\gtrsim_\kappa
|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2.
\]

Summing over cells gives

\[
\sum_r |F_{0,r}|^2+\sum_r |F_{1,r}|^2
\gtrsim
\sum_r |a_r|^2+\sum_r |b_r|^2.
\]

Thus folded anti-lock is not a problem **if the proof can observe both cellwise rows**.

---

# 2. Global derivative rows are different

The ordinary \(Z_\alpha\)-jet does not observe each \(F_{1,r}\) separately. It observes global moments of the branch amplitudes:

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_r
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L,
\]

where \(p_\ell\) are the normalized derivative polynomials.

At the zero,

\[
Y_0=Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

Gate–1 wants a lower bound for something like

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^L |Y_\ell|^2.
\]

So the relevant map is not the direct cellwise map

\[
(a_r,b_r)_r\mapsto (F_{0,r},F_{1,r})_r.
\]

It is the global moment map

\[
(a_r,b_r)_r
\mapsto
(Y_0,Y_1,\ldots,Y_L).
\]

This distinction is decisive.

---

# 3. Rank count

Suppose there are \(M\) paired cells. The unknown branch amplitudes are

\[
(a_1,\ldots,a_M,b_1,\ldots,b_M)\in\mathbb C^{2M}.
\]

The derivative row gives only \(L+1\) global constraints.

Therefore, unless

\[
L+1\ge 2M,
\]

the moment map has a nontrivial kernel in a phase-relaxed model.

If the current O1 bridge uses roughly

\[
M\asymp R,
\qquad
L\asymp R,
\]

then

\[
L+1<2M
\]

for large \(R\), and there is no deterministic injectivity.

This means:

\[
\boxed{
\text{cellwise two-symbol observability does not automatically imply global Gate--1 row mass.}
}
\]

There is a rank-count obstruction.

---

# 4. Explicit phase-relaxed kernel

Take \(2M\) distinct branch symbols

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_{2M}
=
\sigma_1^+,\ldots,\sigma_M^+,
\sigma_1^-,\ldots,\sigma_M^-.
\]

Let unknown coefficients be

\[
c_j=
\begin{cases}
a_j,&1\le j\le M,\\
e b_{j-M},&M<j\le 2M.
\end{cases}
\]

The global moments are

\[
Y_\ell=\sum_{j=1}^{2M} p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j,
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L.
\]

If

\[
L+1<2M,
\]

there exists a nonzero vector \(c\) such that

\[
Y_\ell=0
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L.
\]

For this vector, the entire observed derivative row vanishes, even though cellwise two-symbol observability gives

\[
\sum_r |F_{0,r}|^2+\sum_r |F_{1,r}|^2
\gtrsim
\sum_j |c_j|^2.
\]

So a finite-jet global row of insufficient depth can miss the cellwise mass entirely.

This is not a zeta counterexample. It is a rank obstruction showing what the deterministic local algebra can and cannot prove.

---

# 5. When finite-jet O1b would close

The deterministic route closes if the architecture has a stable two-branch moment frame:

\[
L+1\ge 2M
\]

and the branch symbols are separated:

\[
|\tau_j-\tau_k|\ge Q^{-A}
\quad(j\neq k).
\]

Then the Vandermonde-type matrix

\[
\mathcal V_{\ell j}=p_\ell(\tau_j),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L,\quad 1\le j\le 2M,
\]

has a polynomial lower singular bound, provided the cells are only polynomially separated and the derivative basis is well-conditioned.

Under that condition,

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^L |Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^M
\left(
|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2
\right).
\]

At a zero, \(Y_0=0\), so

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^L |Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^M
\left(
|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2
\right),
\]

unless all mass sits in the \(Y_0\) direction, which is impossible because \(Y_0=0\).

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b-jet closes deterministically if the derivative depth resolves both branches.}
}
\]

But if the depth only resolves one amplitude per paired cell, it does not.

---

# 6. What this means for O1b

There are now three possible statuses, depending on the actual Gate–1 bridge.

## Case A: value-only O1

If O1 feeds only

\[
\|B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\|_{\ell^2_r},
\]

then O1b remains independent.

\[
\boxed{\text{O1b-value remains a prime-log anti-lock input.}}
\]

## Case B: cellwise two-jet O1

If the architecture can feed

\[
\sum_r |F_{0,r}|^2+\sum_r |F_{1,r}|^2
\]

directly into Gate–1, then O1b is deterministic.

\[
\boxed{\text{O1b-cellwise-two-jet closes by branch-symbol separation.}}
\]

## Case C: global derivative-row O1

If the architecture feeds only global moments

\[
(Y_1,\ldots,Y_L),
\]

then O1b closes only if

\[
L+1\ge 2M
\]

with a stable two-branch moment frame.

If

\[
L+1<2M,
\]

then deterministic closure fails by rank count.

\[
\boxed{\text{O1b-global-jet needs enough derivative depth or an arithmetic input.}}
\]

---

# 7. New refined obstruction

The remaining O1b obstruction is no longer simply

\[
B^+\approx -e^{-i\Theta_Z}B^-.
\]

For the global derivative row, the refined obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_r
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right)
\approx0
\quad
\text{for }0\le \ell\le L,
}
\]

while

\[
\sum_r(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is a finite moment annihilation problem for the actual paired prime-log orbit.

So the missing theorem becomes sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted two-branch moment nonannihilation.}
}
\]

It is weaker than arbitrary folded anti-lock, but stronger than cellwise two-symbol separation.

---

# 8. Updated Agent 2 conclusion

The previous optimistic statement needs correction.

\[
\boxed{
\text{Cellwise two-symbol observability is Green.}
}
\]

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Global Gate--1 compatibility depends on derivative depth versus cell count.}
}
\]

If the ordinary bridge has enough rows to resolve both plus and folded-dual amplitudes per cell, O1b can be demoted from arithmetic input to deterministic finite-frame algebra.

If not, O1b remains an arithmetic nonrecurrence input, now precisely formulated as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{no large actual branch-amplitude vector lies in the low-order two-branch moment kernel at a }Z_\alpha\text{-zero.}
}
\]

That is the next target: determine whether the architecture’s \(R\)-depth row has \(L+1\ge2M\), or whether it is underdetermined and needs a genuine prime-log theorem.

## Agent 2 result — O1b rank/depth compatibility

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: determine whether folded O1b is deterministic finite-frame algebra or still an arithmetic input. The coordinator’s current prompt makes the relevant object the global moment map

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_{r=1}^M
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L,
\]

where \(a_r=B_r^+\), \(b_r=B_r^-\), and \(e=e^{i\Theta_Z}\). 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Current O1b is not unconditionally deterministic as written.}
}
\]

More exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b is deterministic Green only if } L+1\ge 2M
\text{ with a stable two-branch frame.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{If } L+1<2M,\text{ O1b still needs an arithmetic nonrecurrence input.}
}
\]

For the current draft architecture:

\[
L=R+1,
\qquad
L+1=R+2,
\]

because the visible \(Z_\alpha\)-row is

\[
(C_0Z_\alpha,\ldots,C_{R+1}Z_\alpha).
\]

The cell construction has

\[
M=M_{\rm cell}\asymp R.
\]

Therefore deterministic O1b requires the hidden cell-count constant to satisfy

\[
2M\le R+2.
\]

The draft’s notation \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\) does **not** guarantee this. If the implementation takes \(M\sim R\), then

\[
2M>R+2
\]

and the global derivative row is underdetermined.

So the correct classification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{B/C: arithmetic input still needed in the current architecture,}
}
\]

unless Gate–1 is redesigned so the family-valued observable supplies enough independent rows.

---

# 1. Exact relation between \(L\) and \(M\)

The ordinary Gate–1 bridge observes the \(Z_\alpha\)-jet through the normalized operators

\[
C_0(\mathcal D),C_1(\mathcal D),\ldots,C_{R+1}(\mathcal D).
\]

At a zero,

\[
C_0Z_\alpha(m_0)=Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\]

but the row still has \(R+2\) coordinates, with \(R+1\) nonzero derivative coordinates available.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
L=R+1,\qquad L+1=R+2.
}
\]

The paired cells cover

\[
[c_-,c_+]
\]

on the plus branch and

\[
[1-c_+,1-c_-]
\]

on the folded dual branch. If the normalized cell width is

\[
h_R=\frac{\Delta}{R},
\]

then

\[
M
\sim
\frac{c_+-c_-}{\Delta}R.
\]

So deterministic two-branch inversion requires

\[
R+2\ge 2M
\sim
2\frac{c_+-c_-}{\Delta}R.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
\Delta\gtrsim 2(c_+-c_-).
}
\]

This is possible only if the cell partition is deliberately coarse enough. But the draft’s \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\) does not lock this constant. Therefore, as written, O1b cannot be declared Green.

---

# 2. Green case: \(L+1\ge 2M\)

Let

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_{2M}
=
\sigma_1^+,\ldots,\sigma_M^+,
\sigma_1^-,\ldots,\sigma_M^-.
\]

Let

\[
c_j=
\begin{cases}
a_j,&1\le j\le M,\\
e b_{j-M},&M<j\le 2M.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
Y_\ell=\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j.
\]

Assume:

\[
L+1\ge 2M,
\]

the branch-symbol nodes are separated,

\[
|\tau_i-\tau_j|\ge Q^{-A_0}
\quad(i\neq j),
\]

and the basis \(p_0,\ldots,p_L\) is polynomially equivalent to monomials on the symbol interval.

Then the rectangular Vandermonde-type matrix

\[
V_{\ell j}=p_\ell(\tau_j),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le L,\quad 1\le j\le 2M,
\]

has a polynomial lower frame bound:

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{L}|Y_\ell|^2
=
\|Vc\|_2^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|c\|_2^2.
\]

Since

\[
\|c\|_2^2=\sum_r(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2),
\]

we obtain

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{\ell=0}^{L}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_r(|B_r^+|^2+|B_r^-|^2).
}
\]

At \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), \(Y_0=0\), so

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{L}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_r(|B_r^+|^2+|B_r^-|^2)
\]

after a harmless adjustment of \(A\), provided the constant function direction is not the only observed output. Since \(Y_0=0\), all mass must be visible in the derivative coordinates.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
L+1\ge 2M
\Longrightarrow
\text{O1b closes by finite-frame algebra.}
}
\]

This is a genuine deterministic closure. It does not need a prime-log theorem beyond the same-branch mass input.

---

# 3. Red case: \(L+1<2M\)

If

\[
L+1<2M,
\]

then the global moment map has a nontrivial phase-relaxed kernel.

This kernel can be written explicitly.

Choose any \(L+2\) distinct branch symbols

\[
\tau_{j_0},\ldots,\tau_{j_{L+1}}
\]

among the \(2M\) nodes. Define

\[
c_{j_k}
=
\frac{1}
{\prod_{m\ne k}(\tau_{j_k}-\tau_{j_m})},
\qquad
0\le k\le L+1,
\]

and set all other \(c_j=0\).

Then the standard barycentric identity gives

\[
\sum_{k=0}^{L+1}c_{j_k}P(\tau_{j_k})=0
\]

for every polynomial \(P\) of degree at most \(L\). Since the \(p_\ell\) span the same polynomial space up to stable basis change,

\[
\sum_j p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j=0,
\qquad
0\le\ell\le L.
\]

Therefore

\[
Y_\ell=0
\qquad
0\le\ell\le L
\]

for a nonzero branch-amplitude vector \(c\).

Mapping back,

\[
a_r=c_r,
\qquad
b_r=e^{-1}c_{M+r},
\]

gives a nonzero plus/dual amplitude configuration invisible to all global derivative moments.

So, when \(L+1<2M\),

\[
\boxed{
\text{global finite jets cannot deterministically recover two-branch cell mass.}
}
\]

This is not a counterexample to the actual zeta object. It is a sharp finite-dimensional obstruction showing that local algebra alone is insufficient.

---

# 4. The exact remaining arithmetic theorem in the underdetermined case

If

\[
L+1<2M,
\]

the missing theorem is no longer the crude value anti-lock exclusion

\[
B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\not\approx0.
\]

It is the stronger but sharper statement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{At every admissible zero }Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,
\text{ the actual branch-amplitude vector avoids the low-order two-branch moment kernel.}
}
\]

Concretely, for

\[
c=(B_1^+,\ldots,B_M^+,eB_1^-,\ldots,eB_M^-),
\]

one needs

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{L}
\left|
\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{j=1}^{2M}|c_j|^2.
\]

Since \(Y_0=Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), this is equivalently

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{L}
\left|
\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_{j=1}^{2M}|c_j|^2.
\]

This cannot hold for arbitrary complex \(c\) if \(L+1<2M\). It can only hold if the **actual prime-log amplitude vector** is arithmetically restricted away from the kernel.

So the exact missing input is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted two-branch moment-kernel avoidance.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Interaction with Agent 1’s family-valued repair

Agent 1’s proposed repair has two possible forms:

\[
G_\nu(m_0,\alpha),
\qquad
\nu\in\mathcal I,
\qquad
|\mathcal I|\gtrsim Q/\log Q,
\]

or structured projections

\[
L_\nu X
\]

of a high-dimensional cell vector \(X\).

For Agent 2, the compatibility condition is simple.

Let the family-valued Gate–1 repair supply effective moment rows

\[
Y_{\nu,\ell}
=
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_{\nu,\ell}(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_{\nu,\ell}(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right).
\]

Then O1b becomes deterministic if the combined family matrix

\[
\mathcal V_{(\nu,\ell),j}
=
p_{\nu,\ell}(\tau_j)
\]

has lower frame bound

\[
\|\mathcal Vc\|_2^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|c\|_2^2
\]

on the \(2M\)-dimensional two-branch amplitude space.

A necessary rank condition is

\[
|\{(\nu,\ell)\}|\ge 2M.
\]

A sufficient condition is:

1. at least \(2M\) independent rows;
2. polynomial node separation;
3. polynomial conditioning of the chosen row family.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A family-valued Gate--1 repair can make O1b deterministic.}
}
\]

But it must supply a frame on the **two-branch** space, not merely on the folded value space.

If Agent 1’s repair observes only

\[
B_r^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-,
\]

then it still misses the anti-lock direction. It must observe at least one branch-separating symbol, such as a first folded jet, or an equivalent projection distinguishing \(\sigma_r^+\) from \(\sigma_r^-\).

---

# 6. Final classification of O1b

## Current scalar \(R+2\)-row architecture

\[
L+1=R+2,\qquad M\asymp R.
\]

If the hidden constant gives

\[
M\le \frac{R+2}{2},
\]

then O1b can be closed by deterministic finite-frame algebra.

If instead

\[
M>\frac{R+2}{2},
\]

then the phase-relaxed kernel above applies, and O1b remains an arithmetic input.

Since the draft only states

\[
M_{\rm cell}\asymp R
\]

and not

\[
2M_{\rm cell}\le R+2,
\]

the current safe classification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{B: arithmetic input still needed under the current formulation.}
}
\]

## Redesigned family-valued architecture

If Agent 1 supplies a dimension-sufficient family whose combined rows form a polynomially conditioned frame on

\[
(B^+,eB^-)\in\mathbb C^{2M},
\]

then O1b becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{C: resolved under redesigned family-valued Gate--1.}
}
\]

## Main mathematical takeaway

The folded anti-lock problem is no longer mysterious. It is exactly a rank/depth issue:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b is deterministic iff the observed rows frame the }2M\text{-dimensional two-branch cell space.}
}
\]

Otherwise the missing theorem is not generic folded nonalignment but:

\[
\boxed{
\text{zero-restricted avoidance of the low-order two-branch moment kernel by the actual prime-log amplitude vector.}
}
\]

## Agent 2 — final O1b rank/depth audit

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: determine whether O1b can be made finite-frame Green by choosing

\[
M\le \frac{R+2}{2},
\]

or whether it remains an arithmetic input under the dimension-corrected Gate–1 picture. The prompt’s active facts are that the current derivative depth is \(L+1=R+2\), the paired-cell count is only specified as \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\), and deterministic folded recovery requires \(L+1\ge 2M\). 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b is locally finite-frame Green if the cell count is deliberately chosen with }2M\le R+2.
}
\]

But globally:

\[
\boxed{
\text{this does not repair Gate--1, because O1a remains dimensionally underpowered.}
}
\]

So the correct classification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{C: finite-frame Green locally, but irrelevant unless O1a/Gate--1 is redesigned.}
}
\]

For the paper’s current wording \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\), the safer local classification is still:

\[
\boxed{
\text{B unless the hidden constant is fixed so that }M_{\rm cell}\le (R+2)/2.
}
\]

---

# 1. Minimal cell count compatible with O1b

The two-branch unknown vector is

\[
c=(a_1,\ldots,a_M, eb_1,\ldots,eb_M)\in\mathbb C^{2M},
\]

where

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-,
\qquad e=e^{i\Theta_Z}.
\]

The visible derivative row has coordinates

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right),
\qquad 0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Thus the observed row count is

\[
L+1=R+2.
\]

Deterministic recovery of \(2M\) branch amplitudes requires

\[
2M\le R+2.
\]

So the maximal admissible paired-cell count for finite-frame O1b is

\[
\boxed{
M_{\max}=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor.
}
\]

If the plus packet interval has normalized length

\[
W=c_+-c_-,
\]

then choosing \(M\sim R/2\) gives normalized cell width

\[
h\sim \frac{W}{M}\sim \frac{2W}{R}.
\]

This is only a constant-factor coarsening compared with the previous \(M\sim R\) partition. Therefore:

* branch separation is unchanged, because the plus and folded-dual supports remain separated by

  \[
  1-2c_+>0;
  \]

* background/error perturbativity is not asymptotically worsened, only constant-degraded;

* the cell-to-amplitude abstraction is still at the same \(1/R\) frequency resolution scale.

The one technical caveat is symbol variation inside each cell. If the bridge treats cells by point-symbol approximation, then high-degree symbols \(p_\ell(\sigma)\), \(\ell\le R+1\), can vary by \(O(1)\) across cells of width \(O(1/R)\). That is not necessarily fatal, but it means the correct object is a **cell-averaged symbol frame**, not a naive point-evaluation frame.

So the coarse choice

\[
M\le (R+2)/2
\]

does not obviously destroy the O1b bridge, provided the amplitude-to-jet step is formulated with cell-averaged symbols or with a stable quadrature choice of cell centers.

---

# 2. Two-branch frame with polynomial constants

Let the \(2M\) branch-symbol nodes be

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_{2M}
=
\sigma_1^+,\ldots,\sigma_M^+,
\sigma_1^-,\ldots,\sigma_M^-.
\]

The plus nodes lie in one compact interval,

\[
\sigma_r^+\in I_+,
\]

and the folded-dual nodes lie in a separated compact interval,

\[
\sigma_r^-\in I_-,
\]

with

\[
\operatorname{dist}(I_+,I_-)\ge \kappa>0.
\]

Define

\[
V_{\ell j}=p_\ell(\tau_j),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1,\quad 1\le j\le 2M.
\]

If

\[
2M\le R+2,
\]

and the nodes are chosen quasi-uniformly or quadrature-adapted on \(I_+\cup I_-\), then the columns of \(V\) form a stable polynomial frame.

The clean argument is:

1. Since \(2M\le R+2\), the row space contains all polynomials of degree at most \(2M-1\).

2. For each node \(\tau_j\), there is a Lagrange polynomial \(\ell_j\) of degree \(2M-1\) satisfying

   \[
   \ell_j(\tau_i)=\delta_{ij}.
   \]

3. If the node set is quasi-Chebyshev/quadrature-adapted on \(I_+\cup I_-\), then the interpolation norm grows at worst like

   \[
   \exp(O(M\log M)).
   \]

4. Since

   \[
   M\asymp R
   \asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
   \]

   this loss is

   \[
   \exp(O(R\log R))=Q^{O(1)}.
   \]

Therefore

\[
\|Vc\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|c\|_2.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left|
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right)
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2\right).
\]

At a zero \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the value coordinate vanishes:

\[
Y_0=0.
\]

Thus the derivative coordinates still satisfy

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2\right),
\]

after adjusting \(A\).

So:

\[
\boxed{
2M\le R+2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\text{O1b closes by finite-frame algebra, with polynomial loss.}
}
\]

This is stronger than the value-level anti-lock formulation. Folded cancellation in the value row is harmless because the derivative rows distinguish \(\sigma_r^+\) from \(\sigma_r^-\).

---

# 3. What if \(2M>R+2\)?

Then the map

\[
c\mapsto (Y_0,\ldots,Y_{R+1})
\]

has nontrivial kernel in the phase-relaxed model.

Explicitly, if

\[
2M>R+2,
\]

choose \(R+3\) distinct branch nodes

\[
\tau_{j_0},\ldots,\tau_{j_{R+2}}.
\]

Set

\[
c_{j_k}
=
\frac{1}
{\prod_{m\ne k}(\tau_{j_k}-\tau_{j_m})},
\qquad
0\le k\le R+2,
\]

and set all other \(c_j=0\).

Then the barycentric identity gives

\[
\sum_k c_{j_k}P(\tau_{j_k})=0
\]

for every polynomial \(P\) of degree at most \(R+1\). Hence

\[
Y_\ell=0,\qquad 0\le\ell\le R+1,
\]

for a nonzero two-branch amplitude vector.

Therefore, if the current architecture keeps \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\) with hidden constant \(>1/2\), deterministic O1b fails. The exact missing theorem is then:

\[
\boxed{
\text{At admissible zeros, the actual prime-log branch-amplitude vector avoids }
\ker V
\text{ at polynomial scale.}
}
\]

More explicitly:

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left|
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)B_r^+
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-
\right)
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|B_r^+|^2+|B_r^-|^2\right).
\]

Since this inequality is false for arbitrary complex branch amplitudes when \(2M>R+2\), it can only be an actual-orbit arithmetic theorem.

---

# 4. Relation to Agent 1’s dimension no-go

Choosing

\[
M\le\frac{R+2}{2}
\]

helps O1b but makes no meaningful progress on O1a.

The observed O1a dimension remains

\[
d\sim 2M\lesssim R
\asymp
\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Agent 1’s dimension threshold for pointwise exclusion over roughly \(e^Q Q\) zeros is

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

With \(d\sim R\),

\[
d\log Q
\sim
\frac{(\log Q)^2}{\log\log Q}
\ll Q.
\]

So the random-scale obstruction becomes

\[
\frac{d\log Q}{Q}\to 0.
\]

Coarsening from \(M\sim R\) to \(M\sim R/2\) only changes constants. It does not change the asymptotic failure.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{closing O1b by choosing fewer cells worsens, or at least does not improve, the O1a dimension defect.}
}
\]

The ordinary branch still needs either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a counter-random theorem for the low-dimensional projection}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a dimension-sufficient family-valued Gate--1 redesign.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Final classification

For O1b alone:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A, if the paper fixes }M_{\rm cell}\le (R+2)/2
\text{ and uses a stable cell-averaged polynomial frame.}
}
\]

For the current wording:

\[
\boxed{
\text{B, because }M_{\rm cell}\asymp R
\text{ does not imply }2M_{\rm cell}\le R+2.
}
\]

For the full proof architecture:

\[
\boxed{
\text{C, because even a finite-frame O1b closure does not repair O1a/Gate--1.}
}
\]

So the mathematical endpoint is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Folded anti-lock is not the decisive blocker anymore.}
}
\]

It is a rank/depth compatibility condition. It can be made deterministic by choosing sufficiently few paired cells or by adding enough family-valued rows. The decisive unresolved obstruction remains the dimension-sufficient upstream Gate–1 seed, not O1b.

## Agent 2 — O1b deterministic lemma under coarse paired cells

I executed the narrower Agent 2 instruction: determine whether O1b can be removed from the arithmetic-input ledger by choosing the paired-cell count carefully. The instruction asks for a self-contained deterministic O1b finite-frame lemma under \(2M\le R+2\), or an obstruction if cell-averaged symbol variation breaks the frame. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b can be made deterministic, provided the cell reduction is made frame-compatible.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
M=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
2M\le R+2,
}
\]

so the visible derivative row has enough dimension to frame the \(2M\)-dimensional plus/folded-dual branch-amplitude vector.

But there is one technical caveat:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the frame proof is clean for cell-representative or cell-dual polynomial observables,}
}
\]

not automatically for naive hard cell sums evaluated at a single point symbol. With hard cells, symbol variation at derivative depth \(R\) is not perturbative unless the draft has a separate cell-averaging/oscillation lemma.

So the classification is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b: locally Green after coarse-cell/frame-compatible formulation.}
}
\]

but:

\[
\boxed{
\text{this does not repair O1a or the dimension defect in Gate--1.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Setup

Let

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-,
\qquad e=e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}.
\]

The two-branch amplitude vector is

\[
c=(a_1,\ldots,a_M,eb_1,\ldots,eb_M)\in\mathbb C^{2M}.
\]

The global derivative row has coordinates

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
Y_\ell=\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j,
\]

where

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_{2M}
=
\sigma_1^+,\ldots,\sigma_M^+,
\sigma_1^-,\ldots,\sigma_M^-.
\]

At a zero,

\[
Y_0=Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

So a lower bound for all rows immediately transfers to the derivative rows:

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
=
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2.
\]

---

# 2. Compatibility of \(M=\lfloor(R+2)/2\rfloor\)

The plus support is

\[
c_-Q\le \log n\le c_+Q,
\qquad
0<c_-<c_+<\frac12.
\]

Normalize

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q}\in[c_-,c_+].
\]

The folded-dual branch lives in the complementary interval

\[
x^\vee\in[1-c_+,1-c_-].
\]

Therefore the two symbol intervals are separated by

\[
\delta_{\rm br}:=1-2c_+>0.
\]

Taking

\[
M=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor
\]

gives normalized cell width

\[
h\asymp \frac{c_+-c_-}{M}\asymp \frac{1}{R}.
\]

This is compatible with the previous ordinary packet scale. It does not endanger branch separation:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(I_+,I_-)\ge \delta_{\rm br}.
\]

It also does not worsen background/error transfer asymptotically. Coarsening from \(M\asymp R\) to \(M\asymp R/2\) only changes constants.

The only caveat is symbol variation: for \(\ell\sim R\), the difference

\[
p_\ell(\sigma)-p_\ell(\sigma_r)
\]

across a cell of width \(h\asymp 1/R\) may be \(O(1)\), depending on the derivative basis. Thus the point-symbol reduction

\[
\sum_{n\in \text{cell }r} p_\ell(\sigma_n)c_n
\approx
p_\ell(\sigma_r)\sum_{n\in \text{cell }r}c_n
\]

is not automatically perturbative.

So O1b is cleanly deterministic if the bridge uses either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{cell-dual polynomial observables,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{cell representatives with a proved depth-\(R\) oscillation bound.}
}
\]

Without one of these, the frame statement is true for the model cell amplitudes but not yet transferred to the actual Dirichlet cell sums.

---

# 3. Deterministic two-branch frame lemma

Assume:

1. \(2M\le R+2\).

2. The nodes \(\tau_j\) are distinct and lie in the union of two compact intervals

   \[
   I_+\cup I_-,
   \qquad
   \operatorname{dist}(I_+,I_-)\ge \delta_{\rm br}>0.
   \]

3. The node placement is quasi-uniform or quadrature-adapted, so the interpolation constants at degree \(2M-1\) grow at most like

   \[
   \exp(O(M\log M)).
   \]

4. The polynomial basis \(p_0,\ldots,p_{R+1}\) is polynomially equivalent, on \(I_+\cup I_-\), to a stable orthogonal or Chebyshev-type basis.

Then the matrix

\[
V_{\ell j}=p_\ell(\tau_j),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1,\quad 1\le j\le 2M,
\]

has a polynomial lower singular bound:

\[
\|Vc\|_2\ge Q^{-A}\|c\|_2.
\]

Proof sketch:

Since \(2M\le R+2\), the row space contains all polynomials of degree at most \(2M-1\). The interpolation map on the \(2M\) nodes has inverse norm at most

\[
\exp(O(M\log M)).
\]

Because

\[
M\asymp R
\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

we have

\[
\exp(O(M\log M))=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

Therefore the least nonzero singular value of the evaluation/moment matrix is bounded below by \(Q^{-A}\). Hence

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left|
\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{j=1}^{2M}|c_j|^2.
\]

Substituting \(c=(a,eb)\),

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}
\left|
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right)
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|a_r|^2+|b_r|^2\right).
\]

At \(Z_\alpha(m_0)=0\), the value row vanishes:

\[
Y_0=0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|B_r^+|^2+|B_r^-|^2\right).
\]

This kills folded anti-lock deterministically.

---

# 4. Why folded anti-lock is killed

The value-level bad event is

\[
B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\approx0.
\]

That can annihilate the \(\ell=0\) row.

But the derivative rows see

\[
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)B_r^+
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e^{i\Theta_Z}B_r^-.
\]

Because

\[
\sigma_r^+\neq\sigma_r^-
\]

uniformly on ordinary wide packets, the plus and dual branches cannot remain invisible to all polynomial rows once the row count reaches the two-branch dimension.

In short:

\[
\boxed{
\text{folded anti-lock is a value-row obstruction, not a full finite-frame obstruction.}
}
\]

---

# 5. The obstruction if the cell-symbol bridge is naive

The only serious local issue is not branch geometry. It is the passage from actual within-cell sums to point-symbol cell amplitudes.

A hard cell gives

\[
B_r^\pm=\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}A_n^\pm.
\]

But the global derivative row contains

\[
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}p_\ell(\sigma_n^\pm)A_n^\pm.
\]

Replacing this by

\[
p_\ell(\sigma_r^\pm)B_r^\pm
\]

requires

\[
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}
\left(p_\ell(\sigma_n^\pm)-p_\ell(\sigma_r^\pm)\right)A_n^\pm
\]

to be lower order.

At depth \(\ell\sim R\) and cell width \(h\asymp1/R\), this is not automatic. It may be \(O(1)\) relative to the main cell amplitude.

Therefore the precise obstruction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b finite-frame Green requires a cell-averaged / polynomial-dual bridge,}
}
\]

or a proved oscillation estimate for the actual derivative symbols inside each cell.

If the draft uses naive hard cells without this bridge, then the finite-frame lemma is not yet transferable to the actual \(Z_\alpha\)-jet.

---

# 6. Relation to O1a

Even after O1b is made deterministic, the ordinary Gate–1 problem remains Red because O1a is dimensionally underpowered.

With

\[
M=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor,
\]

the observable branch dimension is

\[
2M\asymp R
\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

But the dimension needed for random-scale pointwise exclusion over roughly \(e^Q Q\) zeros is

\[
d\gtrsim \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Thus

\[
R\ll \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So coarse cells make O1b deterministic but do not address the main upstream issue:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1a still requires a counter-random low-dimensional projection theorem.}
}
\]

---

# Final classification

For O1b alone:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A: finite-frame Green after choosing }M\le (R+2)/2,
}
\]

provided the cell-to-jet bridge is made symbol-compatible.

For the present draft if it only says \(M_{\rm cell}\asymp R\):

\[
\boxed{
\text{not Green unless the hidden constant is fixed at }M\le(R+2)/2.
}
\]

For the whole ordinary Gate–1 architecture:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b is no longer decisive. O1a/Gate--1 dimension remains the blocker.}
}
\]

The exact mathematical endpoint is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O1b}
=
\text{finite-frame algebra}
+
\text{cell-symbol bridge},
}
\]

not an independent prime-log anti-lock theorem, once the cell count is chosen so that \(2M\le R+2\).

## Agent 2 — final O1b deterministic closure audit

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: close O1b as a deterministic finite-frame issue if possible, using the coarse paired-cell choice \(M=\lfloor(R+2)/2\rfloor\), and identify the exact remaining bridge if not. The current prompt explicitly asks Agent 2 to finish the O1b story independently of the unresolved O1a/Gate–1 dimension problem. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O1b}\text{ is deterministic Green under }2M\le R+2
}
\]

provided the cell-to-jet reduction is formulated with a symbol-compatible cell basis.

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O1b}
=
\text{two-branch finite-frame algebra}
+
\text{cell-symbol bridge}.
}
\]

The only remaining local technical issue is not prime-log arithmetic. It is whether the draft’s cell sums are compatible with depth-\(R\) polynomial symbols. If the proof uses naive hard-cell point symbols without an oscillation lemma, there is a technical gap. If it uses cell-dual polynomial observables or proves the oscillation bridge, O1b is closed.

This does **not** repair O1a or the finite-row Gate–1 dimension defect.

---

# 1. Coarse paired cells are compatible with O1b

Choose

\[
M=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor .
\]

Then

\[
2M\le R+2.
\]

The two-branch amplitude vector is

\[
c
=
(a_1,\ldots,a_M,e b_1,\ldots,e b_M)
\in \mathbb C^{2M},
\]

where

\[
a_r=B_r^+,\qquad b_r=B_r^-,
\qquad e=e^{i\Theta_Z(m_0,\alpha)}.
\]

The ordinary plus support has normalized log-frequency

\[
x=\frac{\log n}{Q}\in[c_-,c_+],
\qquad c_+<\frac12.
\]

The folded-dual support lies in

\[
x^\vee\in[1-c_+,1-c_-].
\]

Thus the two branch-symbol intervals are separated by

\[
\delta_{\rm br}=1-2c_+>0.
\]

Taking \(M\asymp R/2\) gives cell width

\[
h\asymp \frac{1}{R}.
\]

This is still the same ordinary-scale resolution as the previous \(M\asymp R\) construction, up to constants. It does not harm plus/dual branch separation, and it does not asymptotically worsen background/error transfer. The only nontrivial compatibility question is symbol variation inside cells at depth \(\ell\le R+1\).

---

# 2. Deterministic two-branch frame bound

Let

\[
\tau_1,\ldots,\tau_{2M}
=
\sigma_1^+,\ldots,\sigma_M^+,
\sigma_1^-,\ldots,\sigma_M^- .
\]

Let

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_{j=1}^{2M}p_\ell(\tau_j)c_j,
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
Y_\ell
=
\sum_{r=1}^{M}
\left(
p_\ell(\sigma_r^+)a_r
+
p_\ell(\sigma_r^-)e b_r
\right).
\]

Because \(2M\le R+2\), the row family

\[
p_0,\ldots,p_{R+1}
\]

has enough degrees of freedom to interpolate arbitrary data on the \(2M\) branch nodes.

Assume the following standard conditioning hypotheses:

1. The nodes \(\tau_j\) are separated at scale

   \[
   |\tau_i-\tau_j|\gtrsim R^{-C}
   \]

   within each branch interval, and the two branch intervals are separated by \(\delta_{\rm br}>0\).

2. The basis \(p_\ell\) is polynomially equivalent on the compact branch-symbol set to a stable polynomial basis, such as a Chebyshev or orthogonal basis.

3. Cell centers are quasi-uniform or quadrature-adapted.

Then the evaluation matrix

\[
V_{\ell j}=p_\ell(\tau_j),
\qquad
0\le \ell\le R+1,\quad 1\le j\le 2M,
\]

has a polynomial lower singular bound:

\[
\|Vc\|_2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|c\|_2.
\]

The conditioning loss is polynomial because interpolation on \(2M\) nodes costs at worst

\[
\exp(O(M\log M)),
\]

and

\[
M\asymp R
\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

Hence

\[
\exp(O(M\log M))=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{r=1}^{M}\left(|B_r^+|^2+|B_r^-|^2\right).
\]

This is the desired deterministic two-branch finite-frame estimate.

---

# 3. Why the zero condition leaves derivative rows carrying the mass

At an admissible zero,

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0.
\]

In the clean folded main/dual model, this says the value row satisfies

\[
Y_0=0.
\]

In the full canonical model, the value row also includes background/error pieces. Then either:

1. the value row has nontrivial size, in which case Gate–1 already has mass; or

2. the value row is canceled by the full canonical zero equation, in which case the finite-frame lower bound forces the remaining derivative rows to carry the mass.

Quantitatively, if

\[
\sum_{\ell=0}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge Q^{-A}\|c\|_2^2
\]

and

\[
|Y_0|^2\le \frac12 Q^{-A}\|c\|_2^2,
\]

then

\[
\sum_{\ell=1}^{R+1}|Y_\ell|^2
\ge
\frac12 Q^{-A}\|c\|_2^2.
\]

If \(|Y_0|\) is larger than that, the value row itself is already large. Thus folded value anti-lock cannot erase all finite-jet mass.

So the old bad configuration

\[
B^+ + e^{i\Theta_Z}B^-\approx0
\]

is harmless once the derivative row is deep enough to resolve both branches.

---

# 4. The exact remaining technical bridge

The finite-frame lemma is clean for branch amplitudes attached to symbols \(\tau_j\). The actual Dirichlet cell sums may be hard-cell sums:

\[
B_r^\pm
=
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm} A_n^\pm.
\]

But the derivative row contains

\[
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}p_\ell(\sigma_n^\pm)A_n^\pm,
\]

not necessarily

\[
p_\ell(\sigma_r^\pm)B_r^\pm.
\]

At depth \(\ell\sim R\), and with cell width \(h\asymp1/R\), the variation of \(p_\ell\) across a cell can be \(O(1)\). Therefore the naive approximation

\[
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}p_\ell(\sigma_n^\pm)A_n^\pm
\approx
p_\ell(\sigma_r^\pm)
\sum_{n\in I_r^\pm}A_n^\pm
\]

is not automatically perturbative.

The exact bridge needed is:

\[
\left\|
Y_{\rm actual}-V c_{\rm cell}
\right\|_2
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A}\|c_{\rm cell}\|_2,
\]

or an equivalent formulation using cell-dual polynomial observables for which the equality is exact by construction.

So the remaining technical issue is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{hard-cell point-symbol replacement at depth }R.
}
\]

This is not a prime-log nonrecurrence theorem. It is a finite approximation/choice-of-cell-basis issue.

There are two clean ways to close it:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use cell-dual polynomial observables instead of naive hard-cell sums;}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove a depth-\(R\) hard-cell oscillation lemma.}
}
\]

The first is safer.

---

# 5. Background/error transfer

Let the full canonical row be

\[
Y^{\rm can}=Y^{\rm main}+Y^{\rm bg}+Y^E.
\]

If

\[
\|Y^{\rm bg}+Y^E\|_2
\le
\frac12 Q^{-A}\|c\|_2,
\]

then the finite-frame lower bound transfers:

\[
\|Y^{\rm can}\|_2
\ge
\frac12 Q^{-A}\|c\|_2.
\]

If background/error is not perturbative, then it must be included as part of the row vector and frame analysis. But it does not resurrect the folded anti-lock problem; it only changes the transfer estimate.

---

# 6. Final O1b classification

For O1b alone:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Green, under }M=\lfloor(R+2)/2\rfloor
\text{ and a symbol-compatible cell bridge.}
}
\]

If the draft insists on hard-cell point symbols without a depth-\(R\) oscillation lemma:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Yellow: finite-frame algebra is Green, but cell-symbol transfer is open.}
}
\]

If \(M\) is allowed to be any implicit constant multiple of \(R\) with \(2M>R+2\):

\[
\boxed{
\text{Red/arithmetical: low-order moment kernel reappears.}
}
\]

But with the deliberate coarse-cell choice \(2M\le R+2\), O1b is not a fundamental arithmetic obstruction.

---

# 7. Relation to O1a and Gate–1

Closing O1b does not alter the main dimension obstruction.

With

\[
M=\left\lfloor\frac{R+2}{2}\right\rfloor,
\]

the observable dimension is still

\[
2M\asymp R
\asymp
\frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

The dimension scale needed for pointwise exclusion over the zero set is

\[
\frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

Thus

\[
R\ll \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

So O1a/Gate–1 remains the blocker:

\[
\boxed{
\text{O1b can be removed from the arithmetic ledger, but Gate--1 remains Red.}
}
\]

Final mathematical endpoint:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{O1b}
\text{ is a deterministic rank/depth lemma, not the decisive RH obstruction.}
}
\]

The decisive obstruction is still the canonical low-dimensional projection theorem needed for O1a/Gate–1.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:07:19)
