## Agent 2 — Path B1 audit: high-dimensional canonical-source route

I tested the conservative Path B assigned to Agent 2: keep the source canonical by observing high-dimensional data of \(q''_{\can}\), rather than introducing arbitrary new observables, and ask whether this can feed the existing scalar downstream engine without proving the old low-dimensional projection theorem. The prompt’s fork is Path A scalar projection versus Path B high-dimensional/source-family redesign. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{High-dimensional canonical observability does not preserve the existing downstream engine by itself.}
}
\]

It either:

\[
\boxed{
\text{collapses back to the old low-dimensional projection theorem,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{requires a genuine downstream redesign for high-order/source-family energy.}
}
\]

The reason is simple: downstream does not consume “some large canonical high-dimensional energy.” It consumes the low-order scalar canonical row

\[
J_R(q''_{\can})(m_0),
\qquad
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q}.
\]

A high-dimensional canonical observable such as

\[
J_K(q''_{\can})(m_0),
\qquad
K\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\]

can be large while the low-order projection \(J_R(q''_{\can})\) is small. Any theorem excluding that is precisely the counter-random projection theorem in another form.

---

# 1. High-to-low compression fails deterministically

Let \(X\) be the refined canonical cell vector.

Let

\[
T_R X \sim J_R(q''_{\can})(m_0)
\]

be the current downstream-compatible low-order row, with

\[
\dim \operatorname{range} T_R\asymp R.
\]

Let

\[
T_K X \sim J_K(q''_{\can})(m_0)
\]

be a high-dimensional canonical row, with

\[
K\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q}.
\]

The desired compression would be

\[
\|T_KX\|\ge Q^{-A}\|X\|
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|T_RX\|\ge Q^{-A'}\|X\|.
\]

This is false as finite-dimensional algebra.

The kernel

\[
\ker T_R
\]

has dimension essentially

\[
\frac{Q}{\log Q}-R,
\]

so one can have

\[
T_RX=0
\]

while

\[
T_KX\neq0.
\]

In moment language, choose a nonzero amplitude vector whose moments vanish for

\[
0\le k\le R
\]

but not for some

\[
R<k\le K.
\]

This is just the usual low-degree moment-kernel construction. It is not exotic.

Thus any actual-zeta theorem saying

\[
T_KX \text{ large}
\Longrightarrow
T_RX \text{ large}
\]

would be another form of

\[
\boxed{
X(m_0,\alpha)\notin \ker T_R
\text{ at admissible zeros.}
}
\]

That is exactly the old counter-random low-dimensional projection theorem.

So high-to-low compression does not give a new route unless one proves the same missing theorem.

---

# 2. High-order canonical jets do not automatically feed Gate 2

Could Gate 2 be reformulated to accept

\[
J_K(q''_{\can})
\]

directly?

Possibly in a new architecture, but not inside the current one.

The current Gate–2 chain is calibrated to finite depth

\[
R\asymp \frac{\log Q}{\log\log Q},
\]

because finite-dimensional conditioning losses of type

\[
\exp(O(R\log R))
\]

remain polynomial in \(Q\):

\[
\exp(O(R\log R))=Q^{O(1)}.
\]

If one raises the depth to

\[
K\asymp \frac{Q}{\log Q},
\]

the same conditioning losses become

\[
\exp(O(K\log K))
=
\exp(O(Q)),
\]

which is far beyond the polynomial bookkeeping used by Gate 2, wall escape, and D4.

So a high-depth \(J_K\) Gate–2 is not a small modification. It needs a new proof architecture with genuinely stable high-dimensional bases, probably orthogonal or frame-based rather than monomial/Taylor based.

The current Gate–2 proof cannot simply replace \(R\) by \(K\).

---

# 3. Frequency-local Gate 2 does not preserve the scalar identities

Suppose instead one uses frequency-filtered canonical components

\[
q''_{\can}
=
\sum_{\nu} u_\nu,
\]

and upstream proves

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu(m_0)|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is dimensionally plausible. But downstream needs the scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can},
\]

not merely component energy.

The obstruction is cancellation:

\[
\sum_\nu |u_\nu|^2\text{ large}
\quad\not\Rightarrow\quad
\left|\sum_\nu u_\nu\right|\text{ large}.
\]

One might try to run Gate 2 on a single large \(u_\nu\). But the downstream scalar identities are tied to

\[
u=q''_{\can}
\]

as the curvature of the actual canonical phase. In particular, the key objects are of the form

\[
\Gamma_G=u-6q+2q^3,
\]

and higher canonical combinations such as

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

For a frequency component \(u_\nu\), there is no corresponding canonical phase \(q_\nu\) with the same Gram/gauge meaning. Defining an artificial primitive

\[
q_\nu''=u_\nu
\]

does not make

\[
\Gamma_{G,\nu}=u_\nu-6q_\nu+2q_\nu^3
\]

a canonical geometric object in the existing proof. It is no longer the curvature/gauge invariant used by wall escape and D4.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{frequency-local source energy is not downstream-compatible unless it recombines into scalar }u.
}
\]

But recombining into scalar \(u\) reintroduces cancellation and collapses back to the projection theorem.

---

# 4. D4 is intrinsically scalar/canonical

D4 is the sharpest compatibility check.

The carrier-quotient D4 row energy uses scalar canonical combinations of

\[
q,\qquad u=q''_{\can},\qquad \Gamma_G,\qquad \Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

The leading carrier term has schematic form

\[
H\cdot L_{\rm clean}
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G\\
\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix},
\]

with

\[
H\sim q_0u
\]

or, in the associated graded cubic channel, a term proportional to

\[
q_0u^3.
\]

This is nonlinear in the scalar \(u\). If

\[
u=\sum_\nu u_\nu,
\]

then

\[
u^3
=
\sum_\nu u_\nu^3
+
3\sum_{\nu\ne\mu}u_\nu^2u_\mu
+
6\sum_{\nu<\mu<\lambda}u_\nu u_\mu u_\lambda.
\]

Thus componentwise D4 does not sum cleanly into scalar D4. The cross terms are not residual noise; they are part of the actual scalar geometry.

So a high-dimensional decomposition cannot be fed into D4 componentwise unless one proves a new source-family D4 identity. That would be a different downstream theorem.

Current D4 is Green only for the scalar canonical source

\[
u=q''_{\can}.
\]

It is not a converter for arbitrary projected canonical components.

---

# 5. The only viable conservative Path B variant

There is one possible conservative variant:

\[
\text{use high-dimensional canonical data only to prove a lower bound for }T_RX.
\]

That is, the high-dimensional frame could serve as an intermediate proof device for

\[
\|T_RX\|\ge Q^{-A}\|X\|.
\]

But the output is still the low-order scalar row.

This is not a new architecture. It is Path A with a high-dimensional auxiliary proof.

The theorem would have to say:

\[
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0,\quad
\|T_KX(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A}\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\|T_RX(m_0,\alpha)\|\ge Q^{-A'}\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|.
\]

The hard implication is the second one. It is again a zero-conditioned low-dimensional projection theorem.

So the conservative Path B reduces to Path A unless the downstream is rebuilt.

---

# 6. Final classification of Path B1

## High-to-low compression

\[
\boxed{
\text{Fails deterministically; any actual-zeta rescue is the old projection theorem.}
}
\]

## Multi-scale Gate 2

\[
\boxed{
\text{Not compatible with current polynomial conditioning at }K\sim Q/\log Q.
}
\]

A new high-dimensional stable Gate–2 theorem would be required.

## Frequency-local Gate 2

\[
\boxed{
\text{Fails for current scalar identities unless component energy certifies scalar }u.
}
\]

Certifying scalar \(u\) again returns to the low-dimensional projection problem.

## D4 compatibility

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 is scalar/canonical and nonlinear. It does not accept decomposed source-family energy.}
}
\]

---

# Conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{High-dimensional canonical-source Path B does not preserve the existing downstream engine.}
}
\]

It gives a real alternative only if the project rebuilds downstream around high-dimensional/source-family energy.

If the project wants to keep the repaired scalar downstream chain, the necessary upstream theorem remains:

\[
\boxed{
Z_\alpha(m_0)=0
\Longrightarrow
\|T_{\can}X(m_0,\alpha)\|
\ge
Q^{-A}\|X(m_0,\alpha)\|.
}
\]

So Path B1 does not avoid the fork. It clarifies it:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Either prove the scalar counter-random projection theorem, or rebuild downstream.}
}
\]

## Agent 2 — good-subset extraction and threshold bookkeeping

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: formalize good-subset extraction from aggregate Gate–1, including normalized versus unnormalized energies, required polynomial bounds, spacing/density inheritance, and the final theorem downstream agents can use. The active route is Path C: aggregate canonical Gate–1 plus good-subset downstream extraction. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset extraction is mathematically clean, but only after normalizing the energy correctly.}
}
\]

The correct extraction variable is not always \(\rho(m_0)\). It is usually either

\[
\rho(m_0)
\]

if the aggregate theorem is already normalized, or

\[
\rho(m_0)\|X(m_0)\|^2
\]

if the aggregate theorem is stated in unnormalized form.

The central lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_m \rho_m E_m\ge \eta\sum_m\rho_m,\quad
0\le E_m\le B
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\rho(\{E_m\ge \theta\eta\})
\ge
\frac{(1-\theta)\eta}{B-\theta\eta}\rho(\mathcal C).
}
\]

With

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},\qquad B=Q^C,
\]

this gives only a polynomial loss:

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A-C-o(1)}\rho(\mathcal C).
\]

So good-subset extraction itself is not the blocker.

---

# 1. Basic extraction lemma

Let \(\mathcal C\) be a finite routed set of centers, with weights

\[
\rho_m\ge0.
\]

Let

\[
E_m\ge0
\]

be a normalized Gate–1 energy. Assume

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m E_m
\ge
\eta
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m,
\]

and assume a uniform polynomial upper bound

\[
E_m\le B.
\]

For \(0<\theta<1\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:E_m\ge \theta\eta\}.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{\mathcal C}\rho_m E_m
\le
\theta\eta\sum_{\mathcal C\setminus\mathcal G_\theta}\rho_m
+
B\sum_{\mathcal G_\theta}\rho_m.
\]

Combining with the lower bound gives

\[
\eta W
\le
\theta\eta(W-W_G)+BW_G,
\]

where

\[
W=\sum_{\mathcal C}\rho_m,\qquad
W_G=\sum_{\mathcal G_\theta}\rho_m.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
W_G
\ge
\frac{(1-\theta)\eta}{B-\theta\eta}W.
}
\]

If \(B\ge2\theta\eta\), then

\[
W_G
\ge
\frac{1-\theta}{2}\frac{\eta}{B}W.
\]

For

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},\qquad B=Q^C,
\]

this becomes

\[
\boxed{
W_G\ge Q^{-A-C-O(1)}W.
}
\]

Taking \(\theta=1/2\),

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m:E_m\ge \frac12 Q^{-A}}\rho_m
\ge
Q^{-A-C-O(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m.
}
\]

So the good subset has polynomial total weight.

---

# 2. Why the upper bound is essential

Without

\[
E_m\le B,
\]

the conclusion is false. The entire aggregate lower bound could come from one outlier with enormous energy.

Thus every aggregate Gate–1 theorem must be paired with a polynomial upper bound of the form

\[
E_m\le Q^C.
\]

For canonical finite rows this should usually follow from derivative-size bounds, coefficient normalization, and packet truncation. But it must be stated explicitly.

---

# 3. Normalized versus unnormalized ordinary Gate–1

The aggregate ordinary Gate–1 theorem is currently of the form

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

This is **unnormalized**. Define

\[
E_m
=
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2}{\|X_m\|^2}
\]

for \(X_m\neq0\), and define the effective extraction weight

\[
\mu_m
=
\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

Then the aggregate theorem becomes

\[
\sum_m \mu_m E_m
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m \mu_m.
\]

So extraction gives

\[
\sum_{m:E_m\ge \frac12Q^{-A}}\mu_m
\ge
Q^{-A-C-O(1)}
\sum_m\mu_m.
\]

This is the clean form.

If downstream can run with weights \(\mu_m\), this is enough.

If downstream needs a lower bound in the original \(\rho_m\)-weight, then we need comparability:

\[
Q^{-C_X}\le \|X_m\|^2\le Q^{C_X}
\]

on the routed set, or at least after discarding a negligible tail. Under this comparability,

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\rho_m
\ge
Q^{-A-C-2C_X-O(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m.
\]

Without such comparability, the good subset may have large \(\mu\)-weight but tiny \(\rho\)-weight because \(\|X_m\|^2\) could be concentrated on a few centers.

---

# 4. Correct normalized formulation

The best aggregate Gate–1 input is therefore either:

## Option A: normalized aggregate form

\[
\sum_m\rho_m
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2}{\|X_m\|^2}
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho_m,
\]

with

\[
0\le
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2}{\|X_m\|^2}
\le Q^C.
\]

Then extraction gives a \(\rho\)-large good subset directly.

## Option B: unnormalized aggregate form

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

Then extraction gives a \(\mu\)-large subset for

\[
\mu_m=\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

This is acceptable only if downstream can use \(\mu\)-weights, or if \(\|X_m\|^2\) is polynomially comparable across the routed set.

So the preferred statement is Option A.

---

# 5. Spacing and density of the good subset

Good-subset extraction alone gives total weight. It does **not** create spacing or prevent clustering.

However, if the original routed set \(\mathcal C\) satisfies a local packing condition, then every subset inherits the same upper packing bound.

For example, suppose for all intervals \(I\),

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C\cap I}\rho_m
\le
Q^C(1+|I|/\Delta).
\]

Then for \(\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C\),

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G\cap I}\rho_m
\le
Q^C(1+|I|/\Delta).
\]

Thus the good subset cannot be worse than the original set in terms of local upper density.

If downstream row mobility needs separated centers rather than merely bounded local density, perform greedy thinning. Under the packing bound, one can extract a separated sub-subset

\[
\mathcal G'\subseteq\mathcal G
\]

with separation \(\Delta\) and polynomial weight loss:

\[
\rho(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-C'}\rho(\mathcal G).
\]

So separation costs at most another polynomial factor, provided the original routed set has polynomial local packing.

---

# 6. Branch coverage

If ordinary, transition, and singular branches are handled separately, then extraction should be branchwise:

\[
\mathcal C
=
\mathcal C_{\rm ord}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm tr}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm sing}.
\]

For each branch \(b\), prove

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_m E_b(m)
\ge
Q^{-A_b}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_b}\rho_m.
\]

Then extract

\[
\mathcal G_b
=
\{m\in\mathcal C_b:E_b(m)\ge Q^{-A_b'}\}.
\]

The final good routed set is

\[
\mathcal G
=
\mathcal G_{\rm ord}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm tr}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm sing}.
\]

If one branch has negligible total weight, it can be ignored. If all three are needed for coverage, then each branch must have its own aggregate lower bound and polynomial-weight extraction.

---

# 7. Intersecting with other good conditions

Suppose downstream also needs O2/O3, Gram-goodness, wall-goodness, or carrier-goodness.

Let \(\mathcal G_1\) be the Gate–1 good subset. If a bad set \(\mathcal B\) satisfies

\[
\rho(\mathcal B\cap\mathcal G_1)
\le
\frac12\rho(\mathcal G_1),
\]

then

\[
\mathcal G_2=\mathcal G_1\setminus\mathcal B
\]

still has

\[
\rho(\mathcal G_2)\ge \frac12\rho(\mathcal G_1).
\]

Thus downstream compatibility requires bad-set estimates of the form

\[
\rho(\text{bad-wall})+\rho(\text{Gram-bad})+\rho(\text{O2/O3-bad})
\le
(1-Q^{-C})\rho(\mathcal G_1)
\]

or, more usefully,

\[
\le \frac12\rho(\mathcal G_1).
\]

If O2/O3 are also aggregate rather than pointwise, apply the same extraction lemma to their energies and then intersect the resulting good subsets. But independent polynomial-weight lower bounds do not automatically guarantee a large intersection. To guarantee intersection, either:

1. extract from a **combined energy**

   \[
   E_{\rm total}
   =
   E_1+E_{23}+E_{\rm wall}+E_{\rm Gram},
   \]

   with appropriate badness penalties; or

2. prove each bad set has small weight relative to the previous extracted set, not merely relative to the original \(\mathcal C\).

This is important.

---

# 8. Final extraction theorem for downstream use

Here is the theorem downstream agents can use.

Let \(\mathcal C\) be a routed set with weights \(\rho_m\ge0\). Let \(E_1(m)\ge0\) be the normalized canonical Gate–1 energy. Assume:

1. Aggregate lower bound:

   \[
   \sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m E_1(m)
   \ge
   Q^{-A}
   \sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m.
   \]

2. Polynomial upper bound:

   \[
   E_1(m)\le Q^C
   \qquad(m\in\mathcal C).
   \]

3. Local packing:

   \[
   \sum_{m\in\mathcal C\cap I}\rho_m
   \le
   Q^{C_{\rm pack}}(1+|I|/\Delta)
   \]

   for all relevant intervals \(I\).

Then for any fixed \(0<\theta<1\), the set

\[
\mathcal G
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:E_1(m)\ge \theta Q^{-A}\}
\]

satisfies

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\rho_m
\ge
Q^{-A-C-O(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m.
\]

Moreover \(\mathcal G\) inherits the same local packing bound, and it contains a \(\Delta\)-separated subset \(\mathcal G'\) with

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G'}\rho_m
\ge
Q^{-A-C-C_{\rm pack}-O(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}\rho_m.
\]

On every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

for some polynomially adjusted \(A'\).

If the aggregate theorem is unnormalized, replace \(\rho_m\) by

\[
\mu_m=\rho_m\|X_m\|^2,
\]

or assume

\[
Q^{-C_X}\le\|X_m\|^2\le Q^{C_X}
\]

to return to \(\rho_m\)-weights.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{Good-subset extraction is viable with only polynomial losses.}
}
\]

The necessary inputs are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{normalized aggregate Gate--1 lower bound,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{polynomial pointwise upper bound for the normalized energy,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{local packing/separation control for the routed centers,}
}
\]

and, if using unnormalized energy,

\[
\boxed{
\text{polynomial comparability of }\|X(m)\|^2
\text{ or downstream acceptance of }\rho(m)\|X(m)\|^2\text{ weights.}
}
\]

So Path C does not fail at extraction. The real remaining tasks are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove aggregate canonical Gate--1,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{control O2/O3 and wall/Gram bad sets on the extracted subset,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{show row mobility has bounded overlap on a polynomial-weight subset.}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:27:37)

I’ll treat this as the extraction layer for Stack C2. The key point is that separate aggregate bounds are not enough for intersection; Stack C2 needs product, min, or sequential bad-set control.

## Agent 2 — Joint good-subset extraction for Stack C2

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: make the good-subset extraction layer rigorous for Stack C2, where we need a polynomial-weight subset on which both Gate–1 and O2/O3 hold pointwise. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{Separate aggregate bounds are not enough.}
}
\]

To guarantee a useful intersection, Stack C2 needs one of:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a product aggregate bound } \int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{a sequential bad-set/correlation theorem.}
}
\]

The cleanest route is the product aggregate theorem:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C} E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Together with polynomial upper bounds, this extracts a polynomial-weight subset

\[
\mathcal G
=
\{m:E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},\ E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{23}}\}.
\]

---

# 1. One-energy extraction lemma

Let \((\mathcal C,\mu)\) be a finite weighted set, and let

\[
0\le E(m)\le B.
\]

Assume

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}E(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
\eta\,\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

For \(0<\theta<1\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta=\{m:E(m)\ge \theta\eta\}.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{(1-\theta)\eta}{B-\theta\eta}\,\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

In particular, if

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},
\qquad
B=Q^C,
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
Q^{-A-C-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

So one-energy extraction loses only a polynomial factor.

---

# 2. Separate aggregate bounds do not imply intersection

Suppose we know only

\[
\int E_1\,d\mu\ge \eta_1\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\int E_{23}\,d\mu\ge \eta_{23}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This does **not** force a nontrivial intersection.

Countermodel: split

\[
\mathcal C=\mathcal C_1\sqcup \mathcal C_2,
\qquad
\mu(\mathcal C_1)=\mu(\mathcal C_2)=\frac12\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and set

\[
E_1=B_1,\ E_{23}=0
\quad\text{on }\mathcal C_1,
\]

\[
E_1=0,\ E_{23}=B_{23}
\quad\text{on }\mathcal C_2.
\]

Then both separate averages are large, but

\[
\{E_1>0\}\cap\{E_{23}>0\}=\varnothing.
\]

Therefore Stack C2 cannot rely on separate aggregate Gate–1 and aggregate O2/O3 bounds unless we also prove a correlation or sequential bad-set estimate.

---

# 3. Product aggregate extraction lemma

Assume

\[
0\le E_1\le B_1,
\qquad
0\le E_{23}\le B_{23},
\]

and

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
\eta\,\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Choose thresholds \(a,b>0\), and define

\[
\mathcal G(a,b)
=
\{m:E_1(m)\ge a,\ E_{23}(m)\ge b\}.
\]

On the bad complement,

\[
E_1E_{23}
\le
\max(aB_{23},bB_1).
\]

Set

\[
\tau:=\max(aB_{23},bB_1).
\]

If \(\tau<\eta\), then

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G(a,b))
\ge
\frac{\eta-\tau}{B_1B_{23}-\tau}\,\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

A useful polynomial choice is

\[
a=\frac{\eta}{4B_{23}},
\qquad
b=\frac{\eta}{4B_1}.
\]

Then \(\tau=\eta/4\), so

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G(a,b))
\ge
\frac{3\eta}{4B_1B_{23}}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},
\qquad
B_1=Q^{C_1},
\qquad
B_{23}=Q^{C_{23}},
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-A-C_1-C_{23}-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

and on \(\mathcal G\),

\[
\boxed{
E_1\ge Q^{-A-C_{23}-O(1)},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A-C_1-O(1)}.
}
\]

This is the clean Stack C2 extraction theorem.

---

# 4. Sequential alternative

Instead of a product aggregate theorem, one may use a sequential estimate.

Assume

\[
\int E_1\,d\mu\ge \eta_1\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Extract

\[
\mathcal G_1=\{E_1\ge \theta\eta_1\}.
\]

To also get \(E_{23}\), it is not enough to know \(\int E_{23}\,d\mu\) is large on \(\mathcal C\). We need a relative bad-set estimate such as

\[
\int_{\{E_{23}<\beta\}}E_1\,d\mu
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}E_1\,d\mu.
\]

Then the set

\[
\mathcal G
=
\{E_1\ge \theta\eta_1,\ E_{23}\ge \beta\}
\]

has polynomial weight.

This is equivalent in spirit to product control, because

\[
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu
\]

is a natural way to prevent \(E_{23}\)-bad centers from carrying all Gate–1 energy.

Thus the two viable joint mechanisms are:

\[
\boxed{
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\text{-bad centers carry only a small fraction of the }E_1\text{-weighted mass.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Penalized combined energies

A penalized combined energy can also work if it is designed to be large only when both inputs are large.

For example, define truncated energies

\[
\widetilde E_1=\min(E_1/a,1),
\qquad
\widetilde E_{23}=\min(E_{23}/b,1),
\]

and set

\[
F=\widetilde E_1\widetilde E_{23}.
\]

If

\[
\int F\,d\mu\ge \kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

then

\[
\{E_1\ge a/2,\ E_{23}\ge b/2\}
\]

has \(\mu\)-measure at least a polynomial fraction of \(\kappa\mu(\mathcal C)\). This is essentially a bounded/truncated product version of the product theorem.

The product energy \(E_1E_{23}\) is simpler unless outliers are a serious issue. If outliers are hard to bound, use the truncated product.

---

# 6. Normalized versus unnormalized Gate–1

The cleanest Stack C2 formulation uses a common measure \(d\mu\) and normalized energies:

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2}{\|X(m)\|^2},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)=\sigma_{\min}(A_m)^2.
\]

Then use

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)
\]

and prove

\[
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C).
\]

If aggregate Gate–1 is only available in unnormalized form,

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\|X(m)\|^2,
\]

then define

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)\|X(m)\|^2
\]

and

\[
E_1(m)=
\frac{\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2}{\|X(m)\|^2}.
\]

The product aggregate should then be stated as

\[
\int E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This extracts a \(\mu\)-large good subset.

To convert that into a \(\rho\)-large subset, one needs comparability:

\[
Q^{-C_X}\le \|X(m)\|^2\le Q^{C_X}
\]

on \(\mathcal C\), or at least after discarding a negligible tail.

Without comparability, the good subset may be large in \(\rho\|X\|^2\)-weight but small in \(\rho\)-weight. That is acceptable only if downstream row-energy summation is also performed in the same \(d\mu=\rho\|X\|^2\) weight.

---

# 7. Required polynomial upper bounds

For product extraction we need

\[
E_1(m)\le Q^{C_1},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\le Q^{C_{23}}.
\]

For \(E_1\), this is a finite-row operator norm bound:

\[
\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2
\le
Q^{C_1}\|X(m)\|^2.
\]

For \(E_{23}\), since

\[
E_{23}=\sigma_{\min}(A_m)^2,
\]

a crude upper bound follows from

\[
\sigma_{\min}(A_m)^2\le \|A_m\|_{\mathrm{op}}^2\le Q^{C_{23}}.
\]

These bounds are normally much easier than lower bounds, but they must be explicit because they determine the extraction loss.

---

# 8. Packing and separated sub-subsets

Extraction gives weight, not spacing.

Assume the routed set has a polynomial local packing condition: every interval of relevant length \(\Delta\) contains at most \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}\) centers.

Then any subset inherits this upper packing.

If downstream row mobility requires separated centers, color the proximity graph whose edges connect centers within distance \(\Delta\). Its degree is at most \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}\). Hence it can be colored with \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}+1\) colors. One color class is \(\Delta\)-separated and has at least

\[
Q^{-C_{\rm pack}}
\]

of the total extracted weight.

Thus from the product aggregate theorem we obtain a separated good subset

\[
\mathcal G'
\]

with

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-A-C_1-C_{23}-C_{\rm pack}-O(1)}
\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is still polynomial.

---

# 9. Final GoodSubsetIntersection theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\mu)\) be a routed weighted set. Suppose

\[
0\le E_1(m)\le Q^{C_1},
\qquad
0\le E_{23}(m)\le Q^{C_{23}},
\]

and assume the product aggregate lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C}
E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then there exists a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A-C_{23}-O(1)}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A-C_1-O(1)}
}
\]

for every \(m\in\mathcal G\), and

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G)
\ge
Q^{-A-C_1-C_{23}-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If \(\mathcal C\) satisfies polynomial local packing, then \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated sub-subset \(\mathcal G'\) with

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-A-C_1-C_{23}-C_{\rm pack}-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is the extraction layer needed by good-subset downstream.

---

# 10. Final Stack C2 recommendation from Agent 2

The correct Stack C2 input is not merely:

\[
\mathrm{AggG1}_{\can}
\quad+\quad
\mathrm{AggFrame}_{23}.
\]

Separate aggregates can produce disjoint good subsets.

The correct extraction-compatible input is either:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}:
\quad
\sum_m E_1(m)E_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

or an equivalent sequential bad-set theorem:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\text{-bad centers carry at most half of the }E_1\text{-weighted mass.}
}
\]

Given this, good-subset extraction is clean, polynomial-loss, and compatible with downstream provided the same weight \(d\mu\) is used consistently.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: robust joint extraction for Stack C2

The previous extraction result showed:

\[
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

is enough to extract a polynomial-weight subset where both

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A_1},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A_{23}}.
\]

But the raw product has two weaknesses:

1. it needs pointwise polynomial upper bounds for \(E_1,E_{23}\);
2. it may be dominated by outliers.

A cleaner Stack C2 formulation is to use **capped/truncated joint energies**.

---

# 1. Capped energies are the right extraction object

Fix target thresholds

\[
\eta_1=Q^{-A_1},\qquad \eta_{23}=Q^{-A_{23}}.
\]

Define

\[
F_1(m):=\min\left(\frac{E_1(m)}{\eta_1},1\right),
\qquad
F_{23}(m):=\min\left(\frac{E_{23}(m)}{\eta_{23}},1\right).
\]

Then

\[
0\le F_1,F_{23}\le1.
\]

Instead of raw product energy, assume the capped product lower bound

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C} F_1(m)F_{23}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
\kappa\,\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where

\[
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m:F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{23}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

On the complement of \(\mathcal G_\theta\), at least one of \(F_1,F_{23}\) is \(<\theta\), hence

\[
F_1F_{23}\le \theta.
\]

Therefore

\[
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
\theta\,\mu(\mathcal C\setminus\mathcal G_\theta)
+
1\cdot\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

Combining with the lower bound gives

\[
\kappa\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
\theta\mu(\mathcal C)
+
(1-\theta)\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Taking, for example,

\[
\theta=\kappa/2
\]

gives

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_{\kappa/2})
\ge
\frac{\kappa}{2}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

On \(\mathcal G_{\kappa/2}\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge \frac{\kappa}{2}\eta_1,
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge \frac{\kappa}{2}\eta_{23}.
\]

If

\[
\kappa,\eta_1,\eta_{23}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then both pointwise lower bounds remain polynomial.

So the clean extraction statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min\left(\frac{E_1}{\eta_1},1\right)
\min\left(\frac{E_{23}}{\eta_{23}},1\right)
\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

implies a polynomial-weight subset where

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This avoids outlier dependence entirely.

---

# 2. Raw product lower bound implies capped product only with upper control

If we only know

\[
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

that does not automatically imply capped product mass unless we also control outliers.

For example, the raw product could be large because a tiny set has

\[
E_1E_{23}\gg Q^C.
\]

Thus either:

1. prove polynomial upper bounds

   \[
   E_1\le Q^{C_1},
   \qquad
   E_{23}\le Q^{C_{23}},
   \]

   and use the raw-product extraction from the previous round; or

2. formulate the aggregate theorem directly with capped energies.

The capped formulation is more robust and better aligned with good-subset extraction.

---

# 3. Separate capped averages still do not imply intersection

Even if

\[
\int F_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\int F_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

we still do not get a useful intersection.

The same disjoint-support counterexample applies:

\[
F_1=1,\ F_{23}=0
\quad\text{on }\mathcal C_1,
\]

\[
F_1=0,\ F_{23}=1
\quad\text{on }\mathcal C_2.
\]

So Stack C2 needs either:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or a sequential correlation theorem.

---

# 4. Sequential version as a relative bad-set theorem

The capped product theorem is equivalent to saying:

\[
F_{23}\text{ is not small on the }F_1\text{-weighted mass.}
\]

Indeed,

\[
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
=
\int F_{23}\,d\nu_1,
\]

where

\[
d\nu_1:=F_1\,d\mu.
\]

Thus one can replace product aggregation by the sequential theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{23}\,d\nu_1
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu_1(\mathcal C),
}
\]

together with

\[
\nu_1(\mathcal C)=\int F_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This says:

1. Gate–1 has enough capped aggregate mass;
2. O2/O3 is good on average relative to the Gate–1 mass.

This is often more natural than a symmetric product statement.

Equivalently, the \(E_{23}\)-bad set must satisfy

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\{F_{23}<\theta\}}F_1\,d\mu
\le
(1-\delta)
\int F_1\,d\mu
}
\]

with \(\delta\ge Q^{-A}\).

That is the exact correlation theorem needed to prevent disjoint good sets.

---

# 5. Include wall/Gram/gauge conditions in the same extraction formalism

Downstream also needs additional good conditions:

\[
\text{Gram-good},\qquad
\text{wall-good},\qquad
\text{gauge-good},\qquad
\text{carrier-good}.
\]

Let

\[
\chi_{\mathrm{dw}}(m)
\]

be the indicator that all downstream local hypotheses hold at \(m\). Or, more generally, define a capped downstream-good factor

\[
F_{\mathrm{dw}}(m)\in[0,1].
\]

Then the strongest extraction-compatible theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\mathrm{dw}}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This directly extracts a set where all three hold.

If that is too strong, use sequential relative estimates:

\[
\int F_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\int F_{23}\,d(F_1\mu)
\ge Q^{-A}\int F_1\,d\mu,
\]

\[
\int F_{\mathrm{dw}}\,d(F_1F_{23}\mu)
\ge Q^{-A}\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu.
\]

Then the final good set has weight at least

\[
Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the most modular Stack C2 extraction scheme.

---

# 6. Correct measure choice

The measure \(d\mu\) must be chosen once and used consistently through all aggregate theorems.

For normalized energies, the clean choice is

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m).
\]

For unnormalized Gate–1,

\[
\sum \rho(m)\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum \rho(m)\|X(m)\|^2,
\]

the natural measure is

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)\|X(m)\|^2.
\]

Then

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{\|T_{\can}X(m)\|^2}{\|X(m)\|^2}.
\]

The same \(d\mu\) must be used for \(E_{23}\) and downstream-good factors. Otherwise extracted subsets may not intersect in the relevant weight.

So the Stack C2 measure should be either:

\[
\boxed{
d\mu=\rho\,dm
\quad\text{with normalized aggregate Gate--1,}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
d\mu=\rho\|X\|^2\,dm
\quad\text{with unnormalized aggregate Gate--1.}
}
\]

I prefer the normalized version because it makes downstream row-energy bookkeeping cleaner, but the unnormalized version may be easier to prove analytically.

---

# 7. Multi-condition extraction theorem

Let

\[
F_1,F_{23},F_{\rm dw}\in[0,1].
\]

Assume

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm dw}\,d\mu
\ge
\kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m:F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{23}(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{\rm dw}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

On the complement, at least one factor is below \(\theta\), and all factors are \(\le1\), so

\[
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm dw}\le\theta.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Taking \(\theta=\kappa/2\),

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)\ge \frac{\kappa}{2}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This gives a polynomial-weight set where all conditions hold with polynomial thresholds.

This theorem is the robust version of GoodSubsetIntersection.

---

# 8. Packing and separated extraction still work

If the original routed set satisfies local packing

\[
\mu(\mathcal C\cap I)
\le
Q^{C_{\rm pack}}(1+|I|/\Delta),
\]

then every extracted subset inherits the upper packing bound.

If downstream needs \(\Delta\)-separation, color the proximity graph. With degree \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}\), one color class has weight at least

\[
Q^{-C_{\rm pack}}\mu(\mathcal G).
\]

Thus separation costs only another polynomial factor.

---

# 9. Final Stack C2 extraction recommendation

The best formulation is not raw

\[
\int E_1E_{23}\,d\mu.
\]

It is the capped joint theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min\!\left(\frac{E_1}{\eta_1},1\right)
\min\!\left(\frac{E_{23}}{\eta_{23}},1\right)
F_{\rm dw}
\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Here:

\[
\eta_1=Q^{-A_1},
\qquad
\eta_{23}=Q^{-A_{23}}.
\]

If this holds, then there exists a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) where

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
F_{\rm dw}\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

This single theorem automatically avoids the disjoint-good-subset problem, avoids outlier domination, and gives the exact subset needed for downstream.

---

# 10. New endpoint for Agent 2

The extraction layer is now essentially closed.

The remaining mathematical burden is not extraction but proving one of the joint aggregate inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

or the sequential equivalent:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\text{ remains good on average relative to Gate--1 weighted mass.}
}
\]

With wall/Gram/gauge included, the final needed form is:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm dw}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Once that is available, good-subset extraction has only polynomial losses and no further conceptual obstruction.

## Agent 2 — robust GoodSubsetExtraction for corrected Stack C2

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: finalize the extraction layer for

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalGate2/D4}
+
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}
+
\mathrm{GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
\]

The task is to extract a polynomial-weight subset where all three local conditions hold:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A},\qquad
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

The prompt correctly requires capped energies rather than separate averages or raw products. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetExtraction is Green if the joint capped aggregate theorem is available.}
}
\]

The right extraction input is:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then one extracts a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) where

\[
E_1,\ E_{23},\ E_{\rm wall}
\]

are all polynomially large.

Separate aggregate bounds do **not** suffice. Raw product bounds suffice only with polynomial upper bounds.

---

# 1. Capped energies

Fix target thresholds

\[
\eta_1=Q^{-A_1},\qquad
\eta_{23}=Q^{-A_{23}},\qquad
\eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Define

\[
F_1(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_1(m)}{\eta_1},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{23}(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_{23}(m)}{\eta_{23}},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{\rm wall}(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_{\rm wall}(m)}{\eta_{\rm wall}},1\right).
\]

Then

\[
0\le F_1,F_{23},F_{\rm wall}\le 1.
\]

The robust joint aggregate input is:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
\kappa\,\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

---

# 2. Multi-factor extraction lemma

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:
F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ 
F_{23}(m)\ge\theta,\ 
F_{\rm wall}(m)\ge\theta
\}.
\]

On the complement \(\mathcal C\setminus\mathcal G_\theta\), at least one capped factor is \(<\theta\), and all factors are \(\le1\). Hence

\[
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\le \theta.
\]

Therefore

\[
\kappa\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\le
\theta\mu(\mathcal C)
+
(1-\theta)\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Taking

\[
\theta=\frac{\kappa}{2}
\]

gives

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)\ge \frac{\kappa}{2}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

On \(\mathcal G_\theta\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge \theta\eta_1,
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge \theta\eta_{23},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge \theta\eta_{\rm wall}.
\]

Since \(\theta,\eta_1,\eta_{23},\eta_{\rm wall}\) are all polynomially small, the extracted pointwise lower bounds remain polynomial.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{joint capped aggregate input}
\Longrightarrow
\text{polynomial-weight subset with pointwise Gate--1, O2/O3, and wall-goodness.}
}
\]

---

# 3. Why separate aggregates are insufficient

Separate bounds

\[
\int F_1\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\int F_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

do not imply a nontrivial intersection.

A disjoint-support counterexample suffices:

\[
F_1=1,\quad F_{23}=F_{\rm wall}=0
\]

on one region,

\[
F_{23}=1,\quad F_1=F_{\rm wall}=0
\]

on a second region, and

\[
F_{\rm wall}=1,\quad F_1=F_{23}=0
\]

on a third. Each separate aggregate can be large, while no point has all three good.

Therefore Stack C2 should not be formulated as three unrelated aggregate estimates unless supplemented by a correlation or sequential bad-set theorem.

---

# 4. Raw product version

If instead one proves

\[
\int E_1E_{23}E_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

then extraction requires polynomial upper bounds:

\[
E_1(m)\le Q^{C_1},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\le Q^{C_{23}},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)\le Q^{C_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Set thresholds

\[
a_1=\frac{Q^{-A}}{4Q^{C_{23}+C_{\rm wall}}},
\]

\[
a_{23}=\frac{Q^{-A}}{4Q^{C_1+C_{\rm wall}}},
\]

\[
a_{\rm wall}=\frac{Q^{-A}}{4Q^{C_1+C_{23}}}.
\]

Then outside

\[
\{E_1\ge a_1,\ E_{23}\ge a_{23},\ E_{\rm wall}\ge a_{\rm wall}\},
\]

the product is bounded by a fixed fraction of the average scale. One obtains a subset of measure at least

\[
Q^{-A-C_1-C_{23}-C_{\rm wall}-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is acceptable, but capped products are cleaner because they prevent outlier domination without needing sharp upper bounds.

---

# 5. Correct measure choice

There are two viable choices.

## Normalized Gate–1

If

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2}{\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2}
\]

and AggG1 is stated as

\[
\int E_1\,d\rho\ge Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C),
\]

then take

\[
d\mu=d\rho.
\]

This is best for downstream if row-energy summation is naturally in \(\rho\)-weight.

## Unnormalized Gate–1

If the available aggregate theorem is

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2,
\]

then define

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2.
\]

Then

\[
E_1(m)=\frac{\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2}{\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2}
\]

and extraction is performed in \(d\mu\)-weight.

To convert back to \(\rho\)-weight, one needs polynomial comparability:

\[
Q^{-C_X}\le \|X_{\can}(m)\|^2\le Q^{C_X}
\]

on the routed set, or after discarding a negligible tail.

Without this, the good subset may be large in \(\rho\|X_{\can}\|^2\)-weight but tiny in \(\rho\)-weight. That is acceptable only if all downstream summations also use \(d\mu\).

The cleanest formulation is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{use normalized }E_1\text{ and }d\mu=d\rho,
}
\]

unless the analytic proof of AggG1 is available only in unnormalized form.

---

# 6. Packing and separated extraction

Assume the routed set \(\mathcal C\) has polynomial local packing:

\[
\mu(\mathcal C\cap I)
\le
Q^{C_{\rm pack}}
\left(1+\frac{|I|}{\Delta}\right)
\]

for all relevant intervals \(I\).

Every subset, including \(\mathcal G_\theta\), inherits this upper packing.

If downstream requires \(\Delta\)-separation, form the graph on \(\mathcal G_\theta\) joining centers within distance \(\Delta\). The degree is at most \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}\). Coloring gives a \(\Delta\)-separated sub-subset \(\mathcal G'\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-C_{\rm pack}}\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-A-C_{\rm pack}-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Separation costs only polynomial weight.

---

# 7. Branchwise extraction

For branch-complete Stack C2, write

\[
\mathcal C=
\mathcal C_{\rm ord}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm tr}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm sing}.
\]

Each branch has its own Gate–1 energy:

\[
E_{1,\rm ord},\qquad
E_{1,\rm tr},\qquad
E_{1,\rm sing}.
\]

But the O2/O3 energy and wall energy should be stated in the local coordinates appropriate to that branch, then transferred to the same canonical downstream quantities.

For each branch \(b\), define

\[
F_{1,b}=\min(E_{1,b}/\eta_{1,b},1),
\]

and use

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_b}
F_{1,b}F_{23,b}F_{{\rm wall},b}\,d\mu_b
\ge
Q^{-A_b}\mu_b(\mathcal C_b).
\]

Then extract

\[
\mathcal G_b\subseteq \mathcal C_b
\]

with polynomial branch-weight.

The final good set is

\[
\mathcal G=
\mathcal G_{\rm ord}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm tr}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm sing}.
\]

If one branch has negligible total weight, it can be ignored. If coverage requires all branches, each branch needs its own joint aggregate theorem.

---

# 8. Final GoodSubsetExtraction theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\mu)\) be a routed weighted set. Let

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\rm wall}\ge0
\]

be normalized local energies. Choose thresholds

\[
\eta_1,\eta_{23},\eta_{\rm wall}\ge Q^{-A_0}.
\]

Define capped factors

\[
F_i=\min(E_i/\eta_i,1),
\qquad i\in\{1,23,\rm wall\}.
\]

Assume

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then there exists a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C
\]

such that

\[
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A-O(1)}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

and for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

If \(\mathcal C\) has polynomial local packing, then \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated subset \(\mathcal G'\) with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-A''}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

All losses are polynomial.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetExtraction is not a blocker for Stack C2.}
}
\]

The exact input needed is the joint capped aggregate theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Given this, extraction produces a polynomial-weight subset where Gate–1, O2/O3, and wall-goodness hold pointwise, with only polynomial losses.

The remaining hard problems are therefore upstream/downstream of extraction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove JointAgg}_{1,23,\mathrm{wall}},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove local Gate2/D4 on the extracted subset,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove RowMobility}_{\rm lower},
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

## Agent 2 — robust GoodSubsetExtraction with full wall eligibility

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: finalize the extraction theorem for corrected Stack C2, including full wall/downstream eligibility, branchwise form, measure choice, local packing, and polynomial losses. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetExtraction is Green if the input is a joint capped aggregate theorem.}
}
\]

The correct extraction input is not separate aggregate Gate–1, O2/O3, and wall estimates. It is the joint capped estimate

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1(m)F_{23}(m)F_{\rm wall}(m)\,d\mu(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

From this, one extracts a polynomial-weight subset \(\mathcal G\) on which all three pointwise conditions hold:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},\qquad
E_{\rm wall,full}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

The extraction layer itself has no further conceptual obstruction. The burden is now proving the joint capped aggregate theorem.

---

# 1. Capped local energies

Let

\[
E_1(m)\ge0
\]

be normalized Gate–1 energy,

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge0
\]

be the local O2/O3 energy, preferably

\[
E_{23}(m)=\mathcal H_{23}(m)
\]

or a quantity polynomially comparable to \(\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2\), and let

\[
E_{\rm wall,full}(m)\ge0
\]

encode downstream eligibility.

Fix polynomial thresholds

\[
\eta_1=Q^{-A_1},\qquad
\eta_{23}=Q^{-A_{23}},\qquad
\eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Define capped factors

\[
F_1(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_1(m)}{\eta_1},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{23}(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_{23}(m)}{\eta_{23}},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{\rm wall}(m)=\min\!\left(\frac{E_{\rm wall,full}(m)}{\eta_{\rm wall}},1\right).
\]

Then

\[
0\le F_1,F_{23},F_{\rm wall}\le1.
\]

---

# 2. Multi-factor extraction lemma

Assume

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
\kappa\,\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m\in\mathcal C:
F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ 
F_{23}(m)\ge\theta,\ 
F_{\rm wall}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal C\setminus\mathcal G_\theta\), at least one capped factor is \(<\theta\), while all factors are at most \(1\). Therefore

\[
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\le\theta
\]

on the complement. Hence

\[
\kappa\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
\theta\mu(\mathcal C)
+
(1-\theta)\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Taking

\[
\theta=\frac{\kappa}{2}
\]

gives

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa}{2}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

On \(\mathcal G_\theta\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge \theta\eta_1,
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge \theta\eta_{23},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall,full}(m)\ge \theta\eta_{\rm wall}.
\]

Since \(\theta,\eta_1,\eta_{23},\eta_{\rm wall}\) are all polynomially small, this gives polynomial pointwise lower bounds for all three quantities.

---

# 3. Definition of full wall eligibility

It is safer not to encode wall-goodness as a single raw scalar. The downstream needs several local conditions. Define

\[
F_{\rm wall}
=
F_{\rm aniso}
F_{\rm Gram}
F_{\rm gauge}
F_{\rm car}
F_{\rm chart}
F_{\rm route}.
\]

Each factor lies in \([0,1]\).

A safe model is:

\[
F_{\rm aniso}
=
\min\!\left(\frac{E_{\rm aniso}}{\eta_{\rm aniso}},1\right),
\]

where

\[
E_{\rm aniso}
\]

is the normalized D4 wall/anisotropy quantity built from

\[
|H|^2(\Gamma_G^2+\Gamma_{G,2}^2).
\]

For binary eligibility conditions, use indicators:

\[
F_{\rm Gram}=1_{\rm Gram-good},
\]

\[
F_{\rm gauge}=1_{\rm gauge-good},
\]

\[
F_{\rm car}=1_{\rm carrier-frame-good},
\]

\[
F_{\rm chart}=1_{\rm chart-good},
\]

\[
F_{\rm route}=1_{\rm carrier-routing-good}.
\]

This product formulation is mathematically safer than hiding all conditions inside one scalar \(E_{\rm wall,full}\), because it makes clear that extraction only succeeds on centers satisfying every downstream local hypothesis.

Equivalently, one may define

\[
E_{\rm wall,full}
=
\min_i E_i
\]

over the required eligibility energies, but capped products are easier to use in aggregate estimates.

---

# 4. Raw product versus capped product

A raw product theorem of the form

\[
\int E_1E_{23}E_{\rm wall,full}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

is usable only if one has polynomial upper bounds

\[
E_1\le Q^{C_1},
\qquad
E_{23}\le Q^{C_{23}},
\qquad
E_{\rm wall,full}\le Q^{C_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Without those bounds, a tiny set of huge outliers can dominate the raw product.

The capped theorem

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

avoids this completely. It is therefore the preferred Stack C2 extraction input.

---

# 5. Measure choice

There are two consistent choices.

## Normalized form

If Gate–1 is stated as

\[
E_1(m)=
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2}{\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2}
\]

and the aggregate theorem is

\[
\int E_1\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C),
\]

then use

\[
d\mu=d\rho.
\]

This is the cleanest downstream measure.

## Unnormalized form

If the available theorem is

\[
\sum_m\rho(m)\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho(m)\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2,
\]

then use

\[
d\mu(m)=\rho(m)\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2,
\]

and define

\[
E_1(m)=
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_{\can}(m)\|^2}{\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2}.
\]

Extraction then gives a \(d\mu\)-large good subset.

To convert a \(d\mu\)-large set into a \(\rho\)-large set, one needs polynomial comparability:

\[
Q^{-C_X}
\le
\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2
\le
Q^{C_X}
\]

on the routed set, or after discarding a negligible tail. Without that comparability, downstream must be written in \(d\mu\)-weight.

Thus the extraction theorem should be stated for a generic measure \(d\mu\), with a separate compatibility condition saying downstream row-energy summation uses the same \(d\mu\).

---

# 6. Local packing and separated extraction

Assume the routed set has polynomial local packing: for every relevant interval \(I\),

\[
\mu(\mathcal C\cap I)
\le
Q^{C_{\rm pack}}
\left(1+\frac{|I|}{\Delta}\right).
\]

Every subset inherits this upper packing.

If downstream requires \(\Delta\)-separated centers, form the graph on \(\mathcal G_\theta\) connecting centers within distance \(\Delta\). The graph has degree at most \(Q^{C_{\rm pack}}\). Coloring gives a separated sub-subset

\[
\mathcal G'\subseteq\mathcal G_\theta
\]

with

\[
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-C_{\rm pack}}\mu(\mathcal G_\theta).
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G')
\ge
Q^{-A-C_{\rm pack}-O(1)}
\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

So separation costs only polynomial weight.

---

# 7. Branchwise form

For branch-complete Stack C2, decompose

\[
\mathcal C
=
\mathcal C_{\rm ord}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm tr}
\sqcup
\mathcal C_{\rm sing}.
\]

Each branch has its own Gate–1 energy:

\[
E_{1,\rm ord},\qquad
E_{1,\rm tr},\qquad
E_{1,\rm sing}.
\]

The O2/O3 and wall quantities must be expressed in branch-compatible local coordinates and then transferred to the same scalar canonical downstream source.

For each branch \(b\in\{\mathrm{ord},\mathrm{tr},\mathrm{sing}\}\), define

\[
F_{1,b}
=
\min(E_{1,b}/\eta_{1,b},1).
\]

Assume the branchwise joint capped aggregate theorem

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_b}
F_{1,b}F_{23,b}F_{{\rm wall},b}\,d\mu_b
\ge
Q^{-A_b}\mu_b(\mathcal C_b).
\]

Then extract

\[
\mathcal G_b\subseteq\mathcal C_b
\]

with

\[
\mu_b(\mathcal G_b)
\ge
Q^{-A_b'}\mu_b(\mathcal C_b),
\]

and pointwise branch-local conditions

\[
E_{1,b},E_{23,b},E_{{\rm wall},b}\ge Q^{-A_b'}
\]

on \(\mathcal G_b\).

The final good set is

\[
\mathcal G
=
\mathcal G_{\rm ord}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm tr}
\cup
\mathcal G_{\rm sing}.
\]

If one branch carries negligible total measure, it can be dropped. If the proof needs full routed coverage, each non-negligible branch requires its own joint capped aggregate input.

---

# 8. Final GoodSubsetExtraction theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\mu)\) be a routed weighted set. Let

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge0
\]

be normalized Gate–1 and O2/O3 energies. Let

\[
F_{\rm wall}
=
F_{\rm aniso}
F_{\rm Gram}
F_{\rm gauge}
F_{\rm car}
F_{\rm chart}
F_{\rm route}
\]

be the capped/indicator product encoding all local downstream eligibility conditions.

Let

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1),
\]

with

\[
\eta_1,\eta_{23}\ge Q^{-A_0}.
\]

Assume

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Then there exists

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

and for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
\boxed{
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
}
\]

and all local downstream eligibility factors are good at polynomial scale or equal to \(1\), depending on their capped/indicator definition.

If \(\mathcal C\) has polynomial local packing, then \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated subset \(\mathcal G'\) with

\[
\boxed{
\mu(\mathcal G')\ge Q^{-A''}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

All losses are polynomial.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

\[
\boxed{
\text{The extraction theorem is closed.}
}
\]

The exact input needed for corrected Stack C2 is:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Here \(F_{\rm wall}\) must include every downstream eligibility condition:

\[
\boxed{
F_{\rm wall}
=
F_{\rm aniso}
F_{\rm Gram}
F_{\rm gauge}
F_{\rm car}
F_{\rm chart}
F_{\rm route}.
}
\]

Given this input, the good subset exists with polynomial weight, can be thinned to a separated subset with polynomial loss, and carries pointwise Gate–1, O2/O3, and downstream-local hypotheses.

The remaining bottlenecks are not extraction. They are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove the joint capped aggregate lower bound,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove branch-to-canonical transfer,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove RowMobility}_{\rm lower},
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove GlobalRowEnergyUpper}_{\rm agg}.
}
\]

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:38:50)

I’ll push extraction into a usable interface for the other agents: when wall/Gram/chart conditions can be appended after a \(F_1F_{23}\) joint theorem, and when they must be included in the joint theorem itself.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: when wall-goodness can be appended

The extraction layer was already reduced to the capped product theorem

\[
\int_{\mathcal C} F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The next question is whether \(F_{\rm wall}\) must be part of the **primary joint aggregate theorem**, or whether it can be appended later from separate bad-set estimates.

The answer is:

\[
\boxed{
F_{\rm wall}\text{ can be appended if wall/Gram/chart bad sets have small absolute }\mu\text{-measure.}
}
\]

But if bad sets are only small with respect to some unrelated measure, or only small on average separately, then wall-goodness must remain inside the joint product.

---

# 1. Appending binary eligibility factors

Suppose we already have

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge \kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Let \(B\subset\mathcal C\) be a bad set, and let

\[
F_B=1_{\mathcal C\setminus B}.
\]

Since

\[
0\le F_1F_{23}\le1,
\]

we have

\[
\int_B F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le \mu(B).
\]

Therefore

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}F_B\,d\mu
=
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
-
\int_BF_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge
(\kappa-\mu(B)/\mu(\mathcal C))\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Thus, if

\[
\mu(B)\le \frac12\kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}F_B\,d\mu
\ge \frac12\kappa\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

So a binary bad condition can be appended after the \(F_1F_{23}\) theorem if its bad set has absolute \(\mu\)-measure at most a polynomial fraction smaller than the joint mass.

---

# 2. Multiple binary bad sets

Let

\[
B_1,\ldots,B_s
\]

be bad sets for Gram, gauge, chart, carrier-frame, carrier-routing, etc. Define

\[
F_{\rm elig}
=
\prod_{j=1}^s1_{\mathcal C\setminus B_j}.
\]

If

\[
\sum_{j=1}^s\mu(B_j)
\le
\frac12\kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm elig}\,d\mu
\ge
\frac12\kappa\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is useful: the purely binary downstream eligibility factors do not need to be built into the primary arithmetic lower-sampling theorem, provided their bad sets have sufficiently small absolute weighted measure.

So for binary conditions, the correct route is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}
+
\mathrm{BadSetSmall}_{\rm elig}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23,\rm elig}.
}
\]

---

# 3. Non-binary wall/aniso energy is different

The actual wall factor is not purely binary. It is a quantitative energy such as

\[
E_{\rm aniso}(m)
\sim
|H(m)|^2(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2)
\]

after normalization.

For this, define

\[
F_{\rm aniso}
=
\min(E_{\rm aniso}/\eta_{\rm aniso},1).
\]

We cannot append \(F_{\rm aniso}\) from a mere average lower bound

\[
\int F_{\rm aniso}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

because it could be large on a region disjoint from the \(F_1F_{23}\)-mass.

We need one of two stronger statements.

## Option A: relative aniso-goodness

Assume

\[
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge \kappa\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

and

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm aniso}\,d\mu
\ge
\kappa_{\rm aniso}
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\]

with

\[
\kappa_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm aniso}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\kappa\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## Option B: one-shot joint theorem

Assume directly

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm aniso}\,d\mu
\ge
Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is cleaner but harder.

Thus the wall/aniso factor cannot be treated like a small bad set unless wall-badness is binary after thresholding.

---

# 4. Thresholded wall as a binary bad set

We can convert wall/aniso into a binary factor by fixing a threshold

\[
\eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}
\]

and defining

\[
B_{\rm wall}
=
\{m:E_{\rm aniso}(m)<\eta_{\rm wall}\}.
\]

If we can prove the relative bad-set estimate

\[
\int_{B_{\rm wall}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
\frac12\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu,
\]

then

\[
\int F_1F_{23}1_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm wall}}\,d\mu
\ge
\frac12\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu.
\]

This yields extraction where

\[
E_{\rm aniso}\ge \eta_{\rm wall}.
\]

This is equivalent to the sequential form:

\[
\boxed{
\text{wall-bad centers carry little }F_1F_{23}\text{-weighted mass.}
}
\]

That is probably easier than proving a fully quantitative wall product theorem.

---

# 5. Best final formulation of \(F_{\rm wall}\)

Split wall eligibility into:

\[
F_{\rm wall}
=
1_{\rm Gram}
1_{\rm gauge}
1_{\rm chart}
1_{\rm carrier}
1_{\rm route}
1_{\rm aniso},
\]

where

\[
1_{\rm aniso}
=
1_{\{E_{\rm aniso}\ge \eta_{\rm aniso}\}}.
\]

Then the joint extraction theorem only needs binary factors:

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is preferable to carrying raw wall energy through the aggregate theorem.

The cost is that we need bad-set estimates:

\[
\int_{\mathrm{bad}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
(1-Q^{-C})
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\]

or, more simply,

\[
\int_{\mathrm{bad}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
\frac12
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu.
\]

So the downstream eligibility problem becomes a relative bad-set exclusion problem, not another product-energy problem.

---

# 6. Refined Stack C2 extraction interface

The extraction layer should be stated in two stages.

## Stage 1: arithmetic joint mass

Prove

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Here:

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1).
\]

This is the main arithmetic lower-sampling theorem.

## Stage 2: downstream eligibility relative to joint mass

Let

\[
B_{\rm dw}
=
B_{\rm Gram}\cup B_{\rm gauge}\cup B_{\rm chart}
\cup B_{\rm carrier}\cup B_{\rm route}\cup B_{\rm wall}.
\]

Prove

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu.
}
\]

Then

\[
\int_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge
\frac12Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Now apply the two-factor extraction lemma on the eligible set.

This gives a polynomial-weight subset

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq \mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}
\]

where

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A'},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'},
\]

and every downstream eligibility condition holds.

This is cleaner than folding every wall/chart/carrier condition into the central arithmetic theorem.

---

# 7. Extraction after eligibility filtering

Let

\[
\mathcal C_{\rm gooddw}
=
\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}.
\]

Assume

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{\rm gooddw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge
\kappa\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
\kappa\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\), define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m\in\mathcal C_{\rm gooddw}:F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{23}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

Then

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Taking \(\theta=\kappa/2\),

\[
\mu(\mathcal G_\theta)\ge\frac{\kappa}{2}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

On \(\mathcal G_\theta\),

\[
E_1\ge \theta\eta_1,
\qquad
E_{23}\ge \theta\eta_{23},
\]

and all downstream eligibility conditions hold by construction.

So the wall-inclusive extraction can be achieved either by product or by relative bad-set filtering.

---

# 8. Which version is better?

The most attackable formulation appears to be:

\[
\boxed{
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

plus

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le
\frac12\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu.
}
\]

This separates arithmetic lower-sampling from local geometric eligibility.

It is weaker and more modular than demanding

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

as one monolithic theorem.

The two formulations are equivalent if

\[
F_{\rm wall}=1_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}},
\]

but the split version better identifies the proof obligations.

---

# 9. Updated Agent 2 endpoint

The extraction layer is now fully modular.

The corrected Stack C2 should require:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}:
\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}:
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\mu
\le \frac12\int F_1F_{23}\,d\mu,
}
\]

then extraction gives

\[
\boxed{
\exists\mathcal G,\quad
\mu(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

with

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

and all downstream eligibility conditions true on \(\mathcal G\).

This is better than forcing \(F_{\rm wall}\) into the main arithmetic aggregate theorem, unless wall/aniso energy itself is deeply coupled with \(E_1E_{23}\) and can be estimated naturally in the same moment calculation.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: measure compatibility and dyadic normalization

The extraction layer is only useful if the same weight can pass through:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}
\longrightarrow
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
\longrightarrow
\mathrm{LocalDownstream}
\longrightarrow
\mathrm{RowMobility}_{\rm lower}.
\]

The unresolved nuisance is that aggregate Gate–1 naturally appears in two different forms:

\[
\sum_m \rho_m \|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\sum_m\rho_m\|X_m\|^2,
\]

whereas downstream row energy may be normalized in \(\rho_m\)-weight rather than \(\rho_m\|X_m\|^2\)-weight.

I attacked this mismatch. The clean solution is a **dyadic normalization decomposition** of \(\|X_m\|^2\).

---

# 1. The measure mismatch

There are two natural measures.

## Normalized measure

Define

\[
E_1(m)
=
\frac{\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2}{\|X_m\|^2}.
\]

Then the clean aggregate theorem is

\[
\int E_1\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C).
\]

This directly extracts a \(\rho\)-large good subset.

## Unnormalized measure

But many second-moment estimates naturally prove only

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

This is equivalent to

\[
\int E_1\,d\mu
\ge Q^{-A}\mu(\mathcal C),
\qquad
d\mu(m)=\rho_m\|X_m\|^2.
\]

Extraction then gives a \(\mu\)-large good subset, not necessarily a \(\rho\)-large one.

This matters because row mobility and global row-energy upper bounds may be formulated in \(\rho\)-weight.

---

# 2. Dyadic decomposition fixes the mismatch

Let

\[
N(m):=\|X_m\|^2.
\]

Assume a polynomial upper bound

\[
0<N(m)\le Q^{C_X}
\]

on the routed set, after discarding zero-mass centers.

Decompose

\[
\mathcal C
=
\bigsqcup_j \mathcal C_j,
\qquad
\mathcal C_j
=
\{m:2^j\le N(m)<2^{j+1}\}.
\]

Since \(N(m)\le Q^{C_X}\), the number of dyadic classes is

\[
O(\log Q).
\]

If the unnormalized aggregate theorem gives

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m\rho_m N(m),
\]

then by dyadic pigeonhole there exists a class \(\mathcal C_j\) such that

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_m\|T_{\can}X_m\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_m N(m).
\]

On \(\mathcal C_j\), \(N(m)\) is comparable:

\[
2^j\le N(m)<2^{j+1}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_m E_1(m)
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_m.
\]

So unnormalized AggG1 becomes normalized AggG1 on one dyadic slice, with only \(Q^{o(1)}\) loss.

This solves the measure problem.

---

# 3. Joint dyadic extraction with \(E_{23}\)

Now include O2/O3.

Let

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1).
\]

Suppose the available joint theorem is unnormalized:

\[
\sum_m \rho_m N(m) F_1(m)F_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_m \rho_m N(m).
\]

Dyadically decompose by \(N(m)\). Then on some slice \(\mathcal C_j\),

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_mF_1(m)F_{23}(m)
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal C_j}\rho_m.
\]

Thus we can run extraction in \(\rho\)-measure on that slice.

This is better than trying to carry the \(\rho N\)-weight through downstream.

The cost is only:

\[
O(\log Q)=Q^{o(1)}.
\]

So the correct extraction theorem should allow a preliminary dyadic normalization step.

---

# 4. Relative downstream bad-set filtering survives dyadic slicing

Let

\[
B_{\rm dw}
\]

be the union of downstream-bad sets.

The relative bad-set condition can be stated on the selected dyadic slice:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}\cap\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho.
\]

If instead one proves it globally in \(\rho N\)-measure,

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d(\rho N)
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d(\rho N),
\]

then dyadic pigeonholing again gives at least one slice where the relative bound holds, up to a harmless \(Q^{o(1)}\) loss.

So the downstream-bad filter is compatible with either normalized or unnormalized aggregate theorems.

---

# 5. Final extraction pipeline with dyadic normalization

The extraction pipeline should be:

## Step 1: prove unnormalized joint mass if necessary

Either prove the normalized theorem

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\rho(\mathcal C),
\]

or prove the unnormalized theorem

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,d(\rho N)
\ge
Q^{-A}\int_{\mathcal C}d(\rho N).
\]

## Step 2: dyadically select a normalization class

If using the unnormalized theorem, choose a dyadic class

\[
\mathcal C_j=\{N(m)\asymp N_j\}
\]

such that

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}\rho(\mathcal C_j).
\]

## Step 3: remove downstream-bad centers

Assume

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}\cap\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho.
\]

Then

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_j\setminus B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}\rho(\mathcal C_j).
\]

## Step 4: extract good centers

For \(\theta=Q^{-A'}\), define

\[
\mathcal G
=
\{m\in \mathcal C_j\setminus B_{\rm dw}:F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{23}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

Then

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A''}\rho(\mathcal C_j),
\]

and on \(\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

while all downstream eligibility conditions hold.

---

# 6. What if \(\|X_m\|^2\) has huge dynamic range?

If

\[
N(m)=\|X_m\|^2
\]

has super-polynomial dynamic range, dyadic decomposition introduces too many slices.

Thus Stack C2 needs a polynomial truncation hypothesis:

\[
\boxed{
\text{after discarding negligible aggregate mass, }
Q^{-C_X}\le \|X_m\|^2\le Q^{C_X}.
}
\]

Equivalently, one needs tail estimates:

\[
\sum_{N(m)>Q^{C_X}}\rho_mN(m)
\le
Q^{-B}\sum_m\rho_mN(m),
\]

and

\[
\sum_{N(m)<Q^{-C_X}}\rho_mN(m)
\le
Q^{-B}\sum_m\rho_mN(m)
\]

if working in \(\rho N\)-measure.

This is a new but probably accessible normalization estimate. It is much weaker than pointwise Gate–1.

---

# 7. Interaction with branchwise extraction

For branch-complete Stack C2, perform the dyadic normalization separately by branch:

\[
\mathcal C_b
=
\bigsqcup_j \mathcal C_{b,j},
\qquad
b\in\{\mathrm{ord},\mathrm{tr},\mathrm{sing}\}.
\]

Each branch has its own natural normalization:

\[
N_{\rm ord}(m)=\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2,
\]

\[
N_{\rm tr}(m)=\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|_{\rm ref}^2
\quad\text{or the branch RHS energy},
\]

\[
N_{\rm sing}(m)=\|S_R(m)\|^2
\quad\text{or the singular source reference energy}.
\]

The extraction theorem works branchwise as long as the branch-to-canonical transfer later preserves the selected weight up to polynomial factors.

So each branch requires:

\[
\boxed{
\text{polynomial dyadic normalization control for its reference energy.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Updated extraction theorem

A robust final extraction theorem should be stated as follows.

Let \((\mathcal C,\rho)\) be a routed weighted set, and let

\[
N(m)>0
\]

be a reference branch energy with polynomial truncation. Suppose

\[
F_1,F_{23}\in[0,1]
\]

and

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}\,N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Then there exists a dyadic class

\[
\mathcal C_j=\{N\asymp N_j\}
\]

such that

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}
\rho(\mathcal C_j).
\]

If, on that dyadic class,

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}\cap\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho,
\]

then there exists

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C_j\setminus B_{\rm dw}
\]

with

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A'}\rho(\mathcal C_j)
\]

such that

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

on \(\mathcal G\), and all downstream eligibility conditions hold.

If local packing holds, \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated sub-subset with another polynomial loss.

---

# 9. New endpoint

The extraction layer now has three precise inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}
\text{ in either normalized or unnormalized measure;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{PolyNorm}
\text{ for the reference energy }N(m);
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}
\text{ on the selected dyadic slice.}
}
\]

Given these, extraction is completely routine and polynomial-loss.

So Agent 2’s final recommendation is to replace the monolithic wall-inclusive theorem with the modular stack:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}
+
\mathrm{PolyNorm}
+
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
}
\]

This is cleaner than forcing \(F_{\rm wall}\) into the arithmetic aggregate theorem and better aligned with the likely proof technologies: arithmetic lower sampling for \(F_1F_{23}\), geometric/local estimates for downstream bad sets.
