## Agent 2 — continued attack: simultaneous dyadic selection and bad-set filtering

The previous extraction pipeline was:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}
+
\mathrm{PolyNorm}
+
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
\]

I now checked a subtle point: if \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}\) is proved in the unnormalized measure

\[
d\mu_N=N(m)\,d\rho(m),
\]

can we select **one dyadic normalization slice** that simultaneously:

1. carries polynomial joint mass;
2. has comparable \(N(m)\);
3. avoids downstream-bad centers;
4. gives a \(\rho\)-weighted good subset suitable for row mobility?

The answer is yes, but the order matters.

The safe order is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{first remove downstream-bad mass in }N\text{-weight, then dyadically pigeonhole.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Setup

Let

\[
N(m)>0
\]

be the branch reference energy:

\[
N_{\rm ord}(m)=\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2
\]

in the ordinary branch, with analogous choices for transition and singular branches.

Let

\[
F(m):=F_1(m)F_{23}(m),
\qquad 0\le F\le1.
\]

Assume the unnormalized joint aggregate theorem

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m)
\ge
\eta
\int_{\mathcal C}N(m)\,d\rho(m),
\]

with

\[
\eta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Let

\[
B_{\rm dw}
\]

be the downstream-bad set: Gram-bad, gauge-bad, chart-bad, carrier-frame-bad, carrier-routing-bad, and wall-bad.

Assume the relative downstream-bad estimate in the same joint mass:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m)
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

Then

\[
\int_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}}F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m)
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2}
\int_{\mathcal C}N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

This is the correct starting point.

---

# 2. Dyadic selection after bad-set removal

Define dyadic slices

\[
\mathcal C_j
=
\{m\in\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}:2^j\le N(m)<2^{j+1}\}.
\]

Assume polynomial normalization range after truncation:

\[
Q^{-C_N}\le N(m)\le Q^{C_N}.
\]

Then the number of dyadic slices is

\[
J=O(\log Q)=Q^{o(1)}.
\]

Let

\[
M_N
=
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Since

\[
\sum_j \int_{\mathcal C_j}F N\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2}M_N,
\]

there exists a slice \(j_*\) such that

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F N\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2J}M_N.
\]

Because \(F\le1\), this also gives

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}N\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2J}M_N.
\]

On \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\), \(N\asymp N_{j_*}\), hence

\[
N_{j_*}\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*})
\asymp
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}N\,d\rho.
\]

So \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\) has polynomially large \(N\)-weighted mass. If \(N\) is polynomially bounded above and below, it also has polynomially large \(\rho\)-mass relative to the appropriate global scale.

Moreover,

\[
\frac{
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F N\,d\rho
}{
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}N\,d\rho
}
\ge
\frac{
\eta/(2J)M_N
}{
M_N
}
\]

is not the right bound; this only gives \(\eta/(2J)\) against the total \(M_N\), not the slice mass. The sharper fact is obtained by selecting a slice with high conditional average.

Since

\[
\sum_j
\left(
\int_{\mathcal C_j}F N\,d\rho
-
\frac{\eta}{4}\int_{\mathcal C_j}N\,d\rho
\right)
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2}M_N-\frac{\eta}{4}M_N
=
\frac{\eta}{4}M_N,
\]

there exists a slice \(j_*\) such that both:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F N\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\eta}{4}
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}N\,d\rho
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}M_N
}
\]

after discarding slices with negligible contribution.

The precise way to phrase this is: there exists a dyadic slice whose \(N\)-weighted mass is polynomially non-negligible and whose conditional \(F\)-average is at least a polynomial fraction of \(\eta\).

Thus on \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\),

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A-o(1)}
\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*}),
\]

because \(N\) is comparable on the slice.

Now extraction can proceed in ordinary \(\rho\)-weight.

---

# 3. Extraction on the selected slice

Recall

\[
F=F_1F_{23},
\]

where

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1).
\]

On the selected eligible dyadic slice \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\),

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
\kappa
\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*})
\]

with

\[
\kappa\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Define

\[
\mathcal G_\theta
=
\{m\in\mathcal C_{j_*}:F_1(m)\ge\theta,\ F_{23}(m)\ge\theta\}.
\]

For \(0<\theta<\kappa\),

\[
\rho(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa-\theta}{1-\theta}
\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*}).
\]

Taking

\[
\theta=\frac{\kappa}{2},
\]

we get

\[
\rho(\mathcal G_\theta)
\ge
\frac{\kappa}{2}\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*}).
\]

On \(\mathcal G_\theta\),

\[
E_1\ge \theta\eta_1,
\qquad
E_{23}\ge \theta\eta_{23}.
\]

And because \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\subseteq\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}\), all downstream eligibility conditions hold.

Thus the extracted set satisfies:

\[
\boxed{
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'},
\quad
m\notin B_{\rm dw},
\quad
N(m)\asymp N_{j_*}.
}
\]

That last comparability is useful downstream because all normalizations are now stable on \(\mathcal G_\theta\).

---

# 4. Why the order matters

If we first select a dyadic slice and only afterward try to remove bad sets, the selected slice might be almost entirely downstream-bad.

The global relative bad-set condition

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12\int F N\,d\rho
\]

does not automatically imply the same inequality on every dyadic slice.

Therefore the safer pipeline is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{remove bad mass globally in }FN\,d\rho
\text{, then select a good dyadic slice.}
}
\]

Equivalently, define the post-filtered measure

\[
d\nu_{\rm gooddw}
=
1_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho,
\]

and dyadically decompose within that.

---

# 5. If bad-set estimates are only absolute

Sometimes one can prove only

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho
\le
\epsilon
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Since

\[
F\le1,
\]

this implies

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F N\,d\rho
\le
\epsilon
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

To make this smaller than half the joint mass

\[
\int FN\,d\rho\ge \eta M_N,
\]

we need

\[
\epsilon\le \frac12\eta.
\]

Thus absolute bad-set estimates must be stronger than the joint aggregate scale. If

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},
\]

then it suffices to prove

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A-C}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

This is often stronger than a qualitative “bad set has small measure” statement.

So the relative bad-set theorem is preferable:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

---

# 6. Compatibility with row mobility

The selected set \(\mathcal G\) has three useful properties:

1. pointwise lower bounds:

   \[
   E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A'};
   \]

2. downstream eligibility:

   \[
   m\notin B_{\rm dw};
   \]

3. normalization comparability:

   \[
   N(m)\asymp N_{j_*}.
   \]

Therefore downstream local row certificates have comparable normalization:

\[
\|v_m\|^2
\asymp
N_{j_*}\cdot Q^{-A'}
\]

or whatever the local theorem dictates.

This is important for row mobility: if the local certificates have wildly varying sizes, row-mobility lower bounds are harder. Dyadic normalization prevents that.

Thus dyadic slicing is not merely a measure trick. It stabilizes the downstream Hilbert-space normalization.

---

# 7. Final refined extraction theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\rho)\) be a routed set, \(N>0\) a reference energy with polynomial dyadic range, and

\[
F_1,F_{23}\in[0,1].
\]

Assume:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
\eta
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho,
\qquad
\eta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Assume downstream-bad relative control:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

Assume polynomial local packing of \((\mathcal C,\rho)\).

Then there exists a dyadic slice

\[
\mathcal C_j=\{N\asymp N_j\}
\]

and a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq \mathcal C_j\setminus B_{\rm dw}
\]

such that:

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A'}\rho(\mathcal C_j),
\]

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_j}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A'}\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho,
\]

\[
E_1(m),E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}
\quad(m\in\mathcal G),
\]

and

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j
\quad(m\in\mathcal G).
\]

Moreover, if separation is needed, \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated subset \(\mathcal G'\) with only another polynomial loss.

---

# 8. Updated Agent 2 endpoint

The extraction layer should now be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
+
\mathrm{Packing}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{GoodSubset}.
}
\]

Where:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}:
\quad
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho,
\]

\[
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}:
\quad
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

This is the cleanest interface for the other agents.

The extraction part is now genuinely closed modulo these two aggregate inputs.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: reducing \(\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}\)

Current extraction interface:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}:
\qquad
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
\eta\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho,
\qquad
\eta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then we need

\[
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}:
\qquad
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

I attacked when \(\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}\) follows from simpler estimates.

Let

\[
F:=F_1F_{23},\qquad 0\le F\le1,
\]

and

\[
M_N:=\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Then the joint aggregate input is

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}FN\,d\rho\ge \eta M_N.
\]

---

# 1. Absolute bad-set control is sufficient but must beat the aggregate scale

If

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{2}M_N,
\]

then, since \(F\le1\),

\[
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}FN\,d\rho
\le
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{2}M_N
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}FN\,d\rho.
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm dw}}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{2}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathrm{RelBadSet}_{\rm dw}^{FN}.
}
\]

This is useful for genuinely rare geometric bad sets: Gram-bad, chart-boundary-bad, carrier-frame-bad, and routing-bad.

But note the strength required. Since

\[
\eta=Q^{-A},
\]

absolute bad mass must be smaller than total \(N\)-mass by a factor at least

\[
Q^{-A}.
\]

A merely qualitative estimate such as “bad set has \(10\%\) of the mass” is not enough if the joint aggregate mass itself is only \(Q^{-A}\).

---

# 2. Union of bad sets

Suppose

\[
B_{\rm dw}=B_1\cup\cdots\cup B_s.
\]

It is enough to prove

\[
\sum_{i=1}^s\int_{B_i}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{2}M_N.
\]

Equivalently, each bad class may be allowed

\[
\int_{B_i}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{2s}M_N.
\]

Since \(s\) is fixed or at most polynomially small, this only changes exponents.

Thus binary local eligibility conditions can be removed by ordinary \(N\)-weighted bad-set estimates:

\[
\boxed{
\text{binary eligibility bad sets do not need to enter the arithmetic joint theorem}
}
\]

provided they are \(Q^{-A}\)-small in \(N\,d\rho\)-mass.

---

# 3. Quantitative wall/aniso condition is different

The anisotropy/wall condition is not naturally binary. It has a local energy, say

\[
W(m)=E_{\rm aniso}(m),
\]

and one wants

\[
W(m)\ge \eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}.
\]

Let

\[
F_{\rm wall}:=\min(W/\eta_{\rm wall},1).
\]

The wall-bad set is

\[
B_{\rm wall}:=\{W<\eta_{\rm wall}\}
=
\{F_{\rm wall}<1\}.
\]

A separate average

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho\ge Q^{-A}M_N
\]

does not help, because wall-good mass can be disjoint from \(F\)-mass.

What is needed is a **relative wall theorem**:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}F F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge
\delta
\int_{\mathcal C}FN\,d\rho,
\qquad
\delta\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This says wall/aniso energy is not systematically absent on Gate–1/O2-O3 joint mass.

---

# 4. Relative wall theorem gives a wall-good subset

Assume

\[
\int F F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge
\delta
\int FN\,d\rho.
\]

Define

\[
G_{\rm wall}(\theta):=\{m:F_{\rm wall}(m)\ge \theta\}.
\]

On the complement, \(F_{\rm wall}<\theta\). Therefore

\[
\int F F_{\rm wall}N
\le
\theta\int_{G_{\rm wall}^c}FN
+
\int_{G_{\rm wall}}FN.
\]

Let

\[
I:=\int FN,\qquad
I_G:=\int_{G_{\rm wall}}FN.
\]

Then

\[
\delta I\le \theta(I-I_G)+I_G
=
\theta I+(1-\theta)I_G.
\]

So

\[
I_G\ge
\frac{\delta-\theta}{1-\theta}I.
\]

Taking

\[
\theta=\delta/2
\]

gives

\[
\boxed{
\int_{F_{\rm wall}\ge \delta/2}FN\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\delta}{2} \int FN\,d\rho.
}
\]

On this wall-good region,

\[
F_{\rm wall}\ge\delta/2,
\]

hence

\[
W(m)\ge
\frac{\delta}{2}\eta_{\rm wall}.
\]

If \(\delta,\eta_{\rm wall}\ge Q^{-A}\), then the wall lower bound remains polynomial.

Thus wall/aniso can be appended by a relative theorem, not necessarily by including it in the first arithmetic theorem.

---

# 5. Best decomposition of downstream eligibility

The downstream-bad set should be split into two classes.

## Class I: rare binary/geometric bad sets

These are:

\[
B_{\rm Gram},\quad
B_{\rm gauge},\quad
B_{\rm chart},\quad
B_{\rm carrier},\quad
B_{\rm route}.
\]

For these, prove absolute \(N\)-mass smallness:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm geom}}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Choose \(A_{\rm geom}\) large enough relative to the joint aggregate exponent \(\eta\), so that

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}FN\,d\rho
\le
\frac14\int FN\,d\rho.
\]

## Class II: quantitative wall/aniso failure

For this, prove the relative wall theorem:

\[
\int F F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}
\int FN\,d\rho.
\]

Then extract a wall-good region that still carries polynomial \(FN\)-mass.

This is cleaner than forcing all wall/Gram/chart conditions into one monolithic joint theorem.

---

# 6. Modular extraction theorem

Assume:

\[
\int FN\,d\rho
\ge
\eta\int N\,d\rho,
\qquad
\eta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Assume rare geometric bad sets satisfy

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac{\eta}{4}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Then

\[
\int_{\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm geom}}FN\,d\rho
\ge
\frac34\int FN\,d\rho.
\]

Assume also the relative wall theorem

\[
\int F F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge
\delta\int FN\,d\rho,
\qquad
\delta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Then the wall-good and geometry-good region

\[
\mathcal C_{\rm elig}
=
(\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm geom})
\cap
\{F_{\rm wall}\ge \delta/4\}
\]

satisfies

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{\rm elig}}FN\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A'}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Then dyadically decompose by \(N\) and extract a subset where

\[
F_1,F_{23}\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

all geometric conditions hold, and

\[
W=E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A''}.
\]

Thus the full extraction pipeline becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
}
\]

This is more modular than the earlier

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}\,d\mu
\]

form.

---

# 7. Which theorem is easier to prove?

The arithmetic-heavy piece should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}:
\quad
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
}
\]

The geometry/local pieces should be:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}:
\quad
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}:
\quad
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

The relative wall theorem is the only one that may still be arithmetically coupled to Gate–1/O2-O3 mass. If wall/aniso is independent enough, it should hold by a second-moment/anti-concentration argument. If not, it becomes a new named aggregate input.

---

# 8. Failure mode: \(F\)-mass can concentrate on wall-bad centers

Without \(\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}\), the following countermodel is possible:

\[
F=1\quad\text{on }B_{\rm wall},
\]

\[
F=0\quad\text{off }B_{\rm wall},
\]

and

\[
B_{\rm wall}
\]

has only \(Q^{-A}\)-fraction of total \(N\)-mass.

Then

\[
\int FN\sim Q^{-A}\int N,
\]

so \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}\) holds, but all joint mass lies on wall-bad centers. Extraction gives no usable downstream subset.

Thus wall/aniso cannot be ignored merely because wall-bad has small absolute measure unless it is smaller than the aggregate joint mass scale.

---

# 9. Updated final interface for other agents

The cleanest Stack C2 interface is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
}
\]

where

\[
F=F_1F_{23}.
\]

Explicitly:

\[
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho,
\]

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le Q^{-A'}\int N\,d\rho,
\]

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A''}\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

Then extraction gives a polynomial-weight dyadic subset where:

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{23}\ge Q^{-C},
\]

\[
E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-C},
\]

and all binary downstream eligibility conditions hold.

This is now the most precise Agent 2 extraction architecture.

## Assistant (2026-04-27 19:45:15)

I’ll look for one more extraction-side simplification: whether the relative wall theorem can be replaced by a distributional small-sublevel estimate for the wall quantity under the \(F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho\) measure.

_Thought for 16s_

## Agent 2 — one step further: reduce relative wall to sublevel control

The current extraction interface is:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
\]

The least clean input is still

\[
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}:
\qquad
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

I pushed this one layer deeper. The useful refinement is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
\text{ is equivalent to a sublevel-distribution estimate for the wall quantity under the joint mass measure.}
}
\]

This gives a more concrete target than an opaque product estimate.

---

# 1. Change measure to the joint mass

Let

\[
F:=F_1F_{23},
\qquad
d\nu := F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

Then

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
\]

says

\[
\nu(\mathcal C)\ge Q^{-A}\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

The wall condition becomes a statement about \(\nu\).

Let

\[
W(m):=E_{\rm aniso}(m)
\]

be the normalized wall/anisotropy energy. Fix threshold

\[
\eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}},
\]

and define

\[
F_{\rm wall}(m)=\min\left(\frac{W(m)}{\eta_{\rm wall}},1\right).
\]

Then

\[
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
\]

is simply

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

So all wall analysis occurs under the probability measure

\[
d\mathbb P_{\nu}
=
\frac{d\nu}{\nu(\mathcal C)}.
\]

---

# 2. Layer-cake formulation

Because

\[
0\le F_{\rm wall}\le1,
\]

we have

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
=
\int_0^1
\nu(F_{\rm wall}\ge s)\,ds.
\]

Since

\[
F_{\rm wall}\ge s
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
W\ge s\eta_{\rm wall},
\]

this becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
=
\int_0^1
\nu(W\ge s\eta_{\rm wall})\,ds.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
=
\nu(\mathcal C)
-
\int_0^1
\nu(W<s\eta_{\rm wall})\,ds.
\]

Thus \(\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}\) follows from any useful sublevel bound for

\[
\nu(W<\varepsilon).
\]

---

# 3. Sufficient sublevel estimate

A clean sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu(W<\varepsilon)
\le
C\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\eta_0}\right)^\beta
\nu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

for

\[
0<\varepsilon\le \eta_0,
\]

where

\[
\eta_0\ge Q^{-A_0},
\qquad
\beta>0,
\qquad
C\le Q^{A_C}.
\]

Then, taking \(\eta_{\rm wall}\le \eta_0\),

\[
\int_0^1
\nu(W<s\eta_{\rm wall})\,ds
\le
C\nu(\mathcal C)
\int_0^1
\left(\frac{s\eta_{\rm wall}}{\eta_0}\right)^\beta ds
\]

\[
=
\frac{C}{\beta+1}
\left(\frac{\eta_{\rm wall}}{\eta_0}\right)^\beta
\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

So if \(\eta_{\rm wall}\) is chosen small enough polynomially, then

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
\ge
\frac12\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is stronger than needed.

Therefore the wall input can be restated as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Under the joint Gate--1/O2-O3 mass }d\nu,
\text{ the D4 wall quantity has polynomial sublevel decay.}
}
\]

This is more geometric and more attackable than the product theorem.

---

# 4. Even weaker condition: one good wall level

We do not actually need full sublevel decay. It is enough to find one polynomial level

\[
\eta_{\rm wall}=Q^{-A_{\rm wall}}
\]

such that

\[
\boxed{
\nu(W\ge \eta_{\rm wall})
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Indeed, on this set,

\[
F_{\rm wall}=1,
\]

so

\[
\int F_{\rm wall}\,d\nu
\ge
Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

This gives extraction with another polynomial loss.

So the minimal relative wall input is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{WallMass}_{\nu}:
\quad
\nu\{E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A}\}
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is strictly weaker than requiring average wall energy.

---

# 5. What failure of wall mass means

If \(\mathrm{WallMass}_{\nu}\) fails, then

\[
\nu
\]

is concentrated near the D4 wall:

\[
E_{\rm aniso}(m)
=
|H(m)|^2(\Gamma_G(m)^2+\Gamma_{G,2}(m)^2)
\approx0.
\]

Thus most of the joint Gate–1/O2-O3 mass lies where at least one of the following is small:

\[
H(m)\approx0,
\]

or

\[
\Gamma_G(m)\approx0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)\approx0.
\]

This gives a sharp alternative:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Either wall-good mass exists, or joint mass concentrates on a low-codimension algebraic wall.}
}
\]

That concentration should be treated as a new bad-set theorem, not as extraction bookkeeping.

---

# 6. Potential interaction with O2/O3

There is a possible extra: if \(E_{23}\) is large, then concentration on

\[
\Gamma_G=\Gamma_{G,2}=0
\]

may be incompatible with the augmented complex-frame condition.

Why? \(E_{23}\) says the augmented curvature moment map

\[
(c,P)\mapsto
\left(
L((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\]

has polynomial lower singular value. If wall quantities

\[
\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

are functionally derived from the same curvature multiplier \(m_{\can}\), then simultaneous smallness of both wall generators may force a low-rank or near-scalar curvature configuration. That should lower \(E_{23}\).

This suggests a possible theorem:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_{\rm aniso}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}
}
\]

outside Gram/chart exceptional sets.

If true, the wall factor is redundant once O2/O3 is good.

But I do **not** see a formal proof yet. It depends on whether \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) are genuinely represented inside the augmented O2/O3 frame or only downstream in D4.

So this becomes a promising target for another agent:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does augmented O2/O3 nondegeneracy imply wall escape?}
}
\]

If yes, \(F_{\rm wall}\) can be removed from the joint aggregate theorem entirely.

---

# 7. Refined modular stack

The extraction side can now be split into three increasingly strong variants.

## Variant A: Direct capped product

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Simple, but monolithic.

## Variant B: Joint mass plus wall sublevel

\[
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho,
\]

and with

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho,
\]

\[
\nu(E_{\rm aniso}<\varepsilon)
\le
Q^C\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\eta_0}\right)^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is more geometric.

## Variant C: O2/O3 implies wall

\[
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

off binary exceptional sets.

This is strongest and would simplify the architecture most.

Current evidence supports Variant B as the most realistic formulation.

---

# 8. Updated final interface

Replace

\[
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
\]

by either of these equivalent targets:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{WallMass}_{\nu}:
\quad
\nu(E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A})\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{WallSublevel}_{\nu}:
\quad
\nu(E_{\rm aniso}<\varepsilon)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

after normalization.

Then extraction proceeds:

1. prove \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}\);
2. define \(d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho\);
3. use \(\mathrm{WallMass}_{\nu}\) or \(\mathrm{WallSublevel}_{\nu}\);
4. dyadically normalize \(N\);
5. extract \(\mathcal G\).

This is a more precise and probably more attackable target than the earlier wall-inclusive product theorem.

## Agent 2 — one step further: split wall into source-size and anisotropy sublevels

The previous endpoint was:

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho,
\]

and wall-goodness should be treated as a sublevel estimate under \(\nu\):

\[
\nu(E_{\rm aniso}\ge Q^{-A})\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

or

\[
\nu(E_{\rm aniso}<\varepsilon)
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

The extra improvement is that the D4 wall quantity should be split. It is not one irreducible object.

At the carrier level, the relevant wall energy has the schematic form

\[
E_{\rm wall}(m)
\sim
|H(m)|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2
\right),
\]

after local normalization.

Thus wall-badness is the union of two different failures:

\[
|H|\ll Q^{-A}
\]

or

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ll Q^{-A}.
\]

These should not be treated identically.

---

# 1. The \(H\)-factor may already be controlled by Gate–1 plus Gram-goodness

In the D4 carrier formulas, \(H\) is a scalar source-amplitude factor. In the prior D4 notation, it is tied to the canonical scalar source

\[
u=q''_{\can}
\]

and the base Gram/phase scalar \(q_0\), schematically

\[
H\sim q_0 u
\]

or an equivalent normalized scalar multiple.

If this is the active normalization, then

\[
|H| \ll Q^{-A}
\]

can happen only if either:

\[
|u|\ll Q^{-A'}
\]

or

\[
|q_0|\ll Q^{-A'}.
\]

But \(u\)-smallness should be incompatible with Gate–1 source energy on the good subset, after the local Gate–1-to-source transfer. And \(q_0\)-smallness should be a Gram/chart/gauge degeneracy, hence part of binary downstream-badness.

So there is a likely simplification:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On Gate--1-good and Gram/chart-good centers, } |H|\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

If true, the wall sublevel theorem only needs to treat

\[
\Gamma_{\rm wall}
:=
\left(
\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}
\right).
\]

Then

\[
E_{\rm wall}\ge Q^{-A}
\]

reduces to

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is a cleaner target.

---

# 2. Refined downstream-bad split

The downstream-bad set should be decomposed as:

\[
B_{\rm dw}
=
B_{\rm geom}
\cup
B_H
\cup
B_\Gamma,
\]

where

\[
B_{\rm geom}
=
B_{\rm Gram}\cup B_{\rm gauge}\cup B_{\rm chart}
\cup B_{\rm carrier}\cup B_{\rm route},
\]

\[
B_H=\{|H|<Q^{-A_H}\},
\]

and

\[
B_\Gamma=
\{|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2<Q^{-A_\Gamma}\}.
\]

Then:

1. \(B_{\rm geom}\) should be controlled by absolute \(N\,d\rho\)-mass estimates.

2. \(B_H\) should be controlled by Gate–1 plus Gram/chart-goodness.

3. \(B_\Gamma\) is the genuine wall sublevel problem.

Thus the extraction stack improves from

\[
\mathrm{RelWall}^{FN}
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^N
+
\mathrm{H\text{-}from\text{-}Gate1}
+
\mathrm{GammaSublevel}_{\nu}.
}
\]

---

# 3. \(H\)-from-Gate–1 lemma

The useful deterministic lemma would be:

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1}
\quad\text{and}\quad
m\notin B_{\rm geom}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|H(m)|\ge Q^{-A_H}.
\]

This is plausible if:

\[
E_1(m)
\]

actually supplies a local lower bound for the scalar source \(u=q''_{\can}\), and if the geometric-good conditions give

\[
|q_0(m)|\ge Q^{-C}
\]

or whatever scalar factor multiplies \(u\) in \(H\).

If \(E_1\) is only a derivative-row lower bound and not a pointwise \(u\)-lower bound, then the lemma needs a local finite-jet-to-value transfer:

\[
\|J_R(u)(m)\|\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sup_{|x|\le Q^{-C}}|u(m+x)|\ge Q^{-A'}.
\]

Then \(H\) may be large after a microscopic row-mobility shift, not necessarily at exactly \(m\). If D4 is strictly pointwise at \(m\), this distinction matters.

So the \(H\)-factor creates a concrete local check:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does Gate--1-good imply }H\text{-good at the same center, or only nearby?}
}
\]

If nearby is enough, row mobility must absorb the shift. If not, \(H\) remains part of the wall theorem.

---

# 4. Genuine remaining wall theorem: \(\Gamma\)-sublevel under joint mass

After removing \(H\), define

\[
G_\Gamma(m)
:=
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2.
\]

Then the remaining relative wall input is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu(G_\Gamma\ge Q^{-A})\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

or stronger,

\[
\boxed{
\nu(G_\Gamma<\varepsilon)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is now an anti-concentration theorem for the pair

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

under the joint Gate–1/O2-O3 mass.

This is cleaner than a wall-energy product theorem because \(H\)-smallness no longer contaminates the estimate.

---

# 5. Possible relation between \(E_{23}\) and \(\Gamma\)-sublevel

There is a promising interaction:

\[
E_{23}\text{ large}
\]

means the augmented curvature-moment map is nondegenerate. If

\[
\Gamma_G\approx0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0,
\]

then the canonical curvature multiplier may satisfy a low-order algebraic relation. That could force the augmented map to become nearly singular.

The possible deterministic implication is:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A'}
}
\]

outside Gram/chart exceptional sets.

If this implication holds, then wall-goodness is not a separate aggregate input at all. It follows from O2/O3 nondegeneracy.

This is worth assigning to a downstream/D4 agent, because it depends on the exact definitions of \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) and the augmented map \(\mathcal A_m\).

If false, the counterexample would identify a regime where O2/O3 is strong but D4 wall escape still fails. That would justify retaining \(\Gamma\)-sublevel as an independent input.

---

# 6. New extraction interface

The most refined version is now:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^N:
\qquad
\int F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Then define

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

Add:

\[
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^N:
\qquad
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le Q^{-A'}\int N\,d\rho.
\]

Add either:

\[
\mathrm{H\text{-}from\text{-}Gate1}
\]

or, if unavailable,

\[
\nu(|H|<Q^{-A_H})\le \frac12\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then add either:

\[
\mathrm{GammaFromE23}
\]

or

\[
\mathrm{GammaSublevel}_\nu:
\qquad
\nu(G_\Gamma<Q^{-A_\Gamma})
\le
(1-Q^{-C})\nu(\mathcal C)
\]

or the stronger polynomial sublevel estimate.

After these removals, dyadic normalization and good-subset extraction run exactly as before.

---

# 7. Why this matters

This split reduces the wall-inclusive aggregate burden from one opaque theorem:

\[
\int F_1F_{23}F_{\rm wall}N\,d\rho\ge Q^{-A}\int N\,d\rho
\]

to smaller components:

\[
\boxed{
\text{arithmetic joint mass: }F_1F_{23},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rare geometric bad sets,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{source-size transfer for }H,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{anisotropy anti-concentration for }(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
}
\]

This is mathematically better because only the last two are genuinely D4-wall-specific, and one of them may already be implied by Gate–1.

---

# 8. Extra possible shortcut

If both deterministic implications hold:

\[
E_1+\text{geom-good}
\Longrightarrow |H|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
E_{23}+\text{geom-good}
\Longrightarrow
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then the extraction theorem no longer needs wall factors at all.

Then Stack C2 becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^N
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^N
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
+
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}
+
\mathrm{LocalDownstream}.
}
\]

This would be a significant simplification.

Current status:

\[
\boxed{
H\text{-from-Gate1 is plausible and should be checked.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma\text{-from-}E_{23}\text{ is unknown and is the real extra target.}
}
\]

So the next best attack is not more extraction. It is to test:

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\Rightarrow \Gamma\text{-wall escape?}
}
\]

If yes, wall-goodness is absorbed. If no, \(\Gamma\)-sublevel under \(\nu\) becomes the exact remaining wall input.

## Agent 2 — testing the shortcut \(E_{23}\Rightarrow\Gamma\)-wall escape

I attacked the proposed simplification:

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A}
\quad\stackrel{?}{\Longrightarrow}\quad
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

outside Gram/chart/gauge bad sets.

This would remove the \(\Gamma\)-wall factor from extraction. The result is negative in the current abstract structure.

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\text{ does not, by itself, imply }\Gamma\text{-wall escape.}
}
\]

The reason is structural: \(E_{23}\) is a **moment-frame nondegeneracy** condition, while \(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\) are **pointwise carrier/jet-wall coordinates**. A finite-dimensional frame can remain well-conditioned while two scalar wall coordinates vanish. That is codimension-two, not rank collapse.

---

# 1. What \(E_{23}\) controls

The augmented O2/O3 map is

\[
\mathcal A_m(c,P)
=
\left(
L_m((c+m_{\can}P)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}.
\]

The local energy is

\[
E_{23}(m)\simeq \sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A_m)^2
\]

or the harmonic exterior proxy

\[
\mathcal H_{23}(m)
=
\frac{\det(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}
{e_{n-1}(\mathcal A_m^*\mathcal A_m)}.
\]

Thus \(E_{23}\) says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the finite moment system } \{1,m_{\can},m_{\can}x,\ldots,m_{\can}x^R\}
\text{ is well-framed under }L_m.
}
\]

It prevents an algebraic dependence of the constant column against the curvature-multiplier Krylov block.

It does **not** directly assert anything about the scalar carrier coordinates

\[
\Gamma_G,\qquad \Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

---

# 2. What \(\Gamma\)-wall escape controls

The D4 wall factor is, schematically,

\[
E_{\rm wall}
\sim
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

After splitting off the likely Gate–1-controlled source factor \(H\), the genuine D4 wall problem is

\[
G_\Gamma(m):=
|\Gamma_G(m)|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2.
\]

This is a pointwise low-jet/carrier condition. It asks that the point does not lie near the D4 wall

\[
\Gamma_G=\Gamma_{G,2}=0.
\]

That wall is codimension two inside the local jet/carrier parameter space.

A lower singular value condition for \(\mathcal A_m\) does not usually exclude a codimension-two wall unless the wall equations are themselves equivalent to frame degeneracy.

---

# 3. Finite-dimensional countermodel

Here is the relevant abstract countermodel.

Let \(L\) be a moment functional on nodes

\[
x_0,\ldots,x_{R+1}
\]

with weights chosen so that the augmented matrix

\[
\mathcal A=
\left(
L((c+mP)x^k)
\right)_{0\le k\le R+1}
\]

is the identity, or at least has

\[
\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A)\asymp1.
\]

This is easy in a phase-relaxed finite model: choose separated nodes, choose \(m(x_j)\) nonconstant, and choose weights/basis dual to the evaluation system.

Now independently impose two scalar wall equations

\[
\Gamma_G=0,\qquad \Gamma_{G,2}=0
\]

on the local carrier parameters. Since these are only two equations and the frame condition is open, one can satisfy the wall equations while keeping \(\sigma_{\min}(\mathcal A)\) bounded below.

Thus the implication

\[
E_{23}\ge c
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
G_\Gamma\ge c'
\]

is false in the abstract local algebra.

To make it true for zeta, one would need an additional identity tying the wall coordinates directly to a null vector or minor of \(\mathcal A_m\). I do not see such an identity in the current formulation.

---

# 4. Why non-scalar \(m_{\can}\) is not enough

One might hope:

\[
\Gamma_G\approx\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0
\]

forces \(m_{\can}\) to be nearly scalar, and then \(E_{23}\) would fail.

But this is not generally valid.

Two scalar relations on low jets can hold while the multiplier \(m_{\can}(x)\) remains highly nonconstant across the packet. In other words,

\[
\Gamma_G=\Gamma_{G,2}=0
\]

does not imply

\[
m_{\can}(x)\approx \text{constant}
\]

as a function on the full finite moment support.

So the analytic chain

\[
\Gamma\text{-wall}
\Rightarrow
m_{\can}\text{ scalar}
\Rightarrow
E_{23}\text{ small}
\]

is missing its first implication.

At best, one may prove a weaker conditional statement:

\[
\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0
+
\text{additional jet recurrence}
\Rightarrow
m_{\can}\text{ approximately low-rank/scalar}
\Rightarrow
E_{23}\text{ small}.
\]

But the “additional jet recurrence” is not currently available.

---

# 5. What \(E_{23}\) may still help with

Although \(E_{23}\not\Rightarrow\Gamma\)-escape, it may still help in a **relative sublevel theorem**.

Recall the joint mass measure

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

Because \(F_{23}\) is part of \(\nu\), the measure already downweights centers where the augmented frame degenerates. Therefore the remaining wall theorem is not arbitrary; it is:

\[
\nu(G_\Gamma<\varepsilon)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta \nu(\mathcal C).
\]

This says:

\[
\boxed{
\text{among centers where Gate--1 and O2/O3 are jointly active, the D4 wall is not overly sticky.}
}
\]

That is much more plausible than a pointwise implication.

---

# 6. Refined wall input

The extraction stack should therefore retain an independent \(\Gamma\)-sublevel input:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GammaSublevel}_\nu:
\quad
\nu(G_\Gamma<\varepsilon)
\le
Q^C
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^\beta
\nu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

for

\[
0<\varepsilon\le \varepsilon_0,
\qquad
\varepsilon_0\ge Q^{-A_0}.
\]

A weaker but sufficient version is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GammaMass}_\nu:
\quad
\nu(G_\Gamma\ge Q^{-A})
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This input replaces the earlier opaque wall product theorem.

---

# 7. Revised decomposition of wall-goodness

The downstream eligibility package should be decomposed as follows.

## Geometric binary bad sets

\[
B_{\rm geom}
=
B_{\rm Gram}\cup B_{\rm gauge}\cup B_{\rm chart}
\cup B_{\rm carrier}\cup B_{\rm route}.
\]

Input:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A}
\int N\,d\rho.
\]

## Source-amplitude factor \(H\)

Likely deterministic:

\[
E_1+\text{geom-good}
\Longrightarrow
|H|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

If this fails, use a separate \(H\)-sublevel input under \(\nu\).

## D4 anisotropy vector

Independent wall input:

\[
G_\Gamma
=
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

Need either:

\[
\nu(G_\Gamma\ge Q^{-A})\ge Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

or polynomial sublevel decay.

---

# 8. Updated extraction architecture

The cleanest current interface is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{H\text{-}from\text{-}Gate1}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathrm{HSublevel}_{\nu}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GammaSublevel}_{\nu}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
}
\]

Then extraction yields a polynomial-weight dyadic subset where:

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
|H|\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
G_\Gamma\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and all geometric conditions hold.

This is enough to feed local Gate2/D4.

---

# 9. Current status of the shortcut

\[
\boxed{
E_{23}\Rightarrow \Gamma\text{-wall escape is false in the abstract model.}
}
\]

So wall-goodness cannot be removed entirely.

But we have reduced the wall obligation to a sharply defined anti-concentration theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(\Gamma\)-sublevel anti-concentration under the joint Gate--1/O2-O3 mass measure.}
}
\]

That is the right remaining wall input.

The next mathematical target should be:

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2<\varepsilon\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is much more concrete than the earlier wall-inclusive product theorem.

## Agent 2 — \(\Lambda\)-compatible GoodSubsetExtraction

I read the update carefully. The main change for Agent 2 is that the extraction layer must no longer treat wall-goodness as raw anisotropy

\[
|H|^2\bigl(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\bigr).
\]

The correct wall factor is now **forbidden-channel visibility** through the global identity operator \(\Lambda\):

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\left|
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)\Gamma(m)
\right|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
\]

This changes extraction materially: the good subset must be good not merely for D4 row energy, but for **D4 energy in the globally forbidden channel**. 

---

# 1. Updated extraction target

Let

\[
\Gamma(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Let

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)
:=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m).
\]

Then

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)
}.
\]

Define capped factors

\[
F_1(m)=\min\left(\frac{E_1(m)}{\eta_1},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{23}(m)=\min\left(\frac{E_{23}(m)}{\eta_{23}},1\right),
\]

\[
F_{\rm forb}(m)
=
\min\left(
\frac{E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)}{\eta_{\rm forb}},
1
\right).
\]

The one-shot extraction input becomes

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1F_{23}F_{\rm forb}\,N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

Given this, extraction is exactly as before: there exists a polynomial-weight dyadic subset \(\mathcal G\) such that

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\ge Q^{-A'}
\]

for every \(m\in\mathcal G\), with

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j
\]

on the selected dyadic class.

---

# 2. Modular version: preferred interface

The one-shot theorem is clean but may be too hard. The modular extraction interface is better:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbWallMass}_{\nu}
\text{ or }
\mathrm{ForbWallSublevel}_{\nu}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
}
\]

Where

\[
F:=F_1F_{23},
\]

and

\[
d\nu:=F\,N\,d\rho.
\]

The core arithmetic joint mass is

\[
\boxed{
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

The forbidden-wall sublevel input is

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}<\varepsilon\right)
\le
Q^C
\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}\right)^\beta
\nu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

for

\[
0<\varepsilon\le \varepsilon_0,
\qquad
\varepsilon_0\ge Q^{-A_0}.
\]

A weaker sufficient version is

\[
\boxed{
\nu\left(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is now the correct replacement for the earlier \(\Gamma\)-sublevel theorem.

---

# 3. Rank cases for \(M_{\rm forb}\)

Extraction depends on the rank of

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)
=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m)
\]

on the live D4 carrier plane.

## Rank two

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=2
\]

with polynomial lower singular value, then

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

Then the earlier raw anisotropy wall factor is sufficient up to polynomial loss.

In this case Agent 2’s previous \(\Gamma\)-sublevel theorem under

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\]

is enough.

## Rank one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}M_{\rm forb}=1,
\]

then the correct wall factor is not

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2.
\]

It is one forbidden linear combination:

\[
|\ell_1(m)\Gamma_G(m)+\ell_2(m)\Gamma_{G,2}(m)|^2.
\]

Thus the wall sublevel theorem becomes

\[
\nu\left(
|\ell_1\Gamma_G+\ell_2\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
<
\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

The extraction layer is unchanged, but the wall quantity is different.

## Rank zero

If

\[
M_{\rm forb}=0
\]

on the live carrier plane, extraction cannot fix the problem. D4 produces row energy, but not in a globally forbidden channel. Then corrected Stack C2 fails downstream unless another \(\Lambda\) or another D4 carrier channel is found.

So Agent 2’s extraction theorem is conditional on

\[
\boxed{
M_{\rm forb}\text{ has nonzero polynomially visible rank on extracted centers.}
}
\]

---

# 4. Geometric bad sets remain separate

Define

\[
B_{\rm geom}
=
B_{\rm Gram}
\cup
B_{\rm gauge}
\cup
B_{\rm chart}
\cup
B_{\rm carrier}
\cup
B_{\rm route}.
\]

These should be removed by absolute \(N\)-weighted smallness:

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm geom}}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

Since

\[
0\le F_1F_{23}\le1,
\]

this implies

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm geom}}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

If

\[
A_{\rm geom}
\]

is chosen larger than the joint aggregate exponent, then geometric bad sets remove at most half of the joint mass.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{binary geometric eligibility is not part of the arithmetic joint theorem.}
}
\]

The forbidden-wall factor is the only downstream eligibility factor that must be coupled to \(F_1F_{23}\)-mass unless it can be proved absolutely rare.

---

# 5. Dyadic normalization with forbidden wall

Let

\[
N(m)>0
\]

be the branch reference energy.

Assume

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
\eta
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho,
\qquad
\eta\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Remove geometric bad mass and forbidden-wall bad mass in the \(F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho\) measure:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}\cup B_{\rm forb}}
F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\le
\frac12
\int_{\mathcal C}
F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho.
\]

Then

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{\rm elig}}
F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
\frac{\eta}{2}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Now dyadically decompose

\[
\mathcal C_{\rm elig}
=
\bigsqcup_j
\mathcal C_j,
\qquad
\mathcal C_j=\{m:N(m)\asymp N_j\}.
\]

After polynomial truncation of \(N\), there are only

\[
Q^{o(1)}
\]

many dyadic classes. Hence there exists a class \(\mathcal C_{j_*}\) with polynomially large \(N\)-mass and conditional joint average:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C_{j_*}}F_1F_{23}\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*}).
\]

Then standard two-factor extraction gives

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq \mathcal C_{j_*}
\]

with

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A''}\rho(\mathcal C_{j_*}),
\]

and on \(\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

\[
N(m)\asymp N_{j_*},
\]

and all geometric conditions hold.

This is the final \(\Lambda\)-compatible extraction pipeline.

---

# 6. Branchwise reference energies

The same extraction theorem applies branchwise, but \(N\) must be branch-specific.

## Ordinary

\[
N_{\rm ord}(m)=\|X_{\can}(m)\|^2.
\]

## Transition

Use a branch reference energy compatible with the weighted transition source, for example

\[
N_{\rm tr}(m)
=
\|\mathcal J_R^{\rm tr}(m)\|^2
\]

or the local scalar \(L^2\)-source energy it controls, depending on the aggregate theorem’s normalization.

## Singular

Use the projected canonical singular energy, not raw diagnostic sine moments:

\[
N_{\rm sing}(m)
=
\left\|
P_R\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\]

or the corresponding branch reference norm.

This aligns Agent 2’s extraction layer with the updated Agent 5 correction: singular branch inputs must be actual canonical projections.

---

# 7. Final \(\Lambda\)-compatible GoodSubsetExtraction theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\rho)\) be one branch of routed centers. Let \(N>0\) be the branch reference energy with polynomial dyadic range. Define

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1).
\]

Let

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\rm D4}
},
\qquad
M_{\rm forb}=\Lambda E_{\rm car}L_{\rm clean}.
\]

Assume:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Assume geometric bad-set smallness:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A'}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Assume forbidden-wall mass or sublevel control under

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho:
\]

\[
\nu(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A'})
\ge
Q^{-A''}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

or stronger polynomial sublevel decay.

Then there exists a dyadic class \(\mathcal C_j\) and a subset

\[
\mathcal G\subseteq\mathcal C_j
\]

such that:

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A'''}\rho(\mathcal C_j),
\]

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j\quad(m\in\mathcal G),
\]

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A'''},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A'''},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\ge Q^{-A'''},
\]

and all geometric eligibility conditions hold.

If local packing holds, \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated sub-subset with another polynomial loss.

---

# 8. What remains outside extraction

The extraction theorem is now fully adapted to the \(\Lambda\)-forbidden downstream architecture.

The remaining inputs are:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenWallSublevel}_{\nu}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{PolyDyadicNorm}
}
\]

and, downstream:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda\text{ exists and }M_{\rm forb}\text{ has nonzero polynomial rank}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbiddenMobility}_{\rm lower}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GlobalForbiddenIdentityUpper}.
}
\]

Thus Agent 2’s work is now consistent with the updated architecture: extraction must target forbidden-channel D4 visibility, not raw D4 anisotropy.

M_{\rm forb}(m)=\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\rm clean}(m),
\qquad
\Gamma(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The next extraction-side issue is that \(M_{\rm forb}(m)\) may vary with \(m\) and may have rank two, rank one, or rank zero. The extraction theorem must therefore be **rank-stratified**.

The main new point:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Forbidden-wall extraction should not use a single global wall condition.}
}
\]

It should first decompose the joint mass according to the finite rank profile of

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m).
\]

---

# 1. Work under the joint mass measure

Let

\[
F=F_1F_{23},
\qquad
d\nu=F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

The core aggregate input is

\[
\nu(\mathcal C)
=
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

The forbidden-wall problem is now:

\[
\nu\left(
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C),
\]

or a sublevel form:

\[
\nu\left(E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}<\varepsilon\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

But this is hard to analyze until \(M_{\rm forb}\) is stratified.

---

# 2. Rank stratification of \(M_{\rm forb}\)

Let

\[
s_1(m)\ge s_2(m)\ge0
\]

be the singular values of

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m):\mathbb C^2\to\mathcal K.
\]

There are three regimes.

## Rank-two visible

\[
s_2(m)\ge Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
\ge
Q^{-2A_2}|\Gamma|^2.
\]

So forbidden wall visibility follows from the raw \(\Gamma\)-wall lower bound:

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

In this regime, the previous \(\Gamma\)-sublevel theorem is sufficient.

## Rank-one visible

\[
s_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\qquad
s_2(m)<Q^{-A_2}.
\]

Then there is a unit vector

\[
\ell(m)\in\mathbb C^2
\]

such that

\[
|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2
\asymp
s_1(m)^2
|\langle \Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2
\]

up to negligible \(s_2\)-errors.

In this regime, raw anisotropy

\[
|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\]

is not enough. The vector \(\Gamma\) could lie almost in the kernel of \(M_{\rm forb}\).

The needed wall theorem becomes a **moving linear-form anti-concentration** statement:

\[
\nu\left(
|\langle \Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C_{\rm rank1}).
\]

## Rank-zero / forbidden-invisible

\[
s_1(m)<Q^{-A_0}.
\]

Then

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)
\]

is small for every \(\Gamma\). No extraction theorem can repair this. If the joint mass concentrates here, the D4 output is globally allowed or invisible to \(\Lambda\).

Thus Stack C2 needs a finite downstream visibility input:

\[
\boxed{
\nu(s_1(M_{\rm forb})\ge Q^{-A_0})
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

If this fails, the problem is downstream, not extraction.

---

# 3. Rank-visible extraction theorem

Define the rank-visible set

\[
\mathcal C_{\rm vis}
=
\{m:s_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_0}\}.
\]

Assume

\[
\nu(\mathcal C_{\rm vis})
\ge
Q^{-A_{\rm vis}}\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

Decompose

\[
\mathcal C_{\rm vis}
=
\mathcal C_2\cup\mathcal C_1,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal C_2=\{s_2\ge Q^{-A_2}\},
\]

and

\[
\mathcal C_1=\{s_1\ge Q^{-A_1},\ s_2<Q^{-A_2}\}.
\]

At least one of \(\mathcal C_1,\mathcal C_2\) carries polynomial \(\nu\)-mass.

### If \(\mathcal C_2\) carries mass

It is enough to prove

\[
\nu_{\mathcal C_2}
\left(
|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C_2).
\]

Then

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A''}
\]

on a polynomial-mass subset, assuming \(H\) and normalization are controlled.

### If \(\mathcal C_1\) carries mass

It is enough to prove

\[
\nu_{\mathcal C_1}
\left(
|\langle \Gamma,\ell\rangle|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C_1).
\]

Then again

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A''}
\]

on a polynomial-mass subset.

So the correct forbidden-wall theorem is rank-dependent:

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank two: raw }\Gamma\text{-anti-concentration;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank one: moving forbidden-linear-form anti-concentration;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{rank zero: downstream no-go unless another }\Lambda\text{ is chosen.}
}
\]

---

# 4. \(H\)-factor and normalization

The forbidden wall energy is

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\frac{|H|^2|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2}
{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}.
\]

Rank visibility only controls the middle factor. We still need

\[
\frac{|H|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}\ge Q^{-A_H}
\]

on the extracted set.

There are two possible treatments.

## Deterministic \(H\)-from-Gate–1

If

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A_1}
\]

and geometric-goodness imply

\[
\frac{|H|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}\ge Q^{-A_H},
\]

then no separate \(H\)-sublevel theorem is needed.

## \(H\)-sublevel under \(\nu\)

If not, add

\[
\nu\left(
\frac{|H|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is weaker than requiring raw \(H\) non-small pointwise.

Current status:

\[
\boxed{
H\text{-from-Gate--1 remains plausible but must be checked against the exact D4 normalization.}
}
\]

Until then, the extraction theorem should include either \(H\)-from-Gate–1 or \(H\)-sublevel as an explicit input.

---

# 5. Moving direction \(\ell(m)\) creates a new issue

In rank one, the forbidden direction

\[
\ell(m)
\]

may move with \(m\). The needed theorem is not fixed linear-form anti-concentration but moving linear-form anti-concentration:

\[
|\langle \Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2
\not\approx0
\]

on a polynomial portion of the \(\nu\)-mass.

This is analogous to Agent 3’s moving Schur-pivot obstruction. The residual direction moves, so fixed-test sampling is not enough.

The wall theorem should therefore be stated as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{ForbLinSublevel}_{\nu}:
\quad
\nu\left(
|\langle \Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C_1).
}
\]

This is the precise rank-one wall input.

---

# 6. Dyadic rank slicing

To make extraction stable, also dyadically slice the singular values of \(M_{\rm forb}\).

Use slices

\[
s_1(m)\asymp S_1,\qquad s_2(m)\asymp S_2
\]

with

\[
S_1,S_2\in[Q^{-A},Q^A]
\]

after polynomial truncation.

There are only \(Q^{o(1)}\) such slices.

On a selected slice, \(M_{\rm forb}\) has stable scale. This prevents normalization mismatch in the downstream forbidden row certificates.

Thus the full dyadic normalization should include:

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\]

\[
s_1(M_{\rm forb}(m))\asymp S_1,
\]

and, in rank-two regimes,

\[
s_2(M_{\rm forb}(m))\asymp S_2.
\]

In rank-one regimes, also fix the direction \(\ell(m)\) up to angular caps if needed.

---

# 7. Angular caps in rank-one case

If \(\ell(m)\) varies too rapidly, anti-concentration is hard to express.

Cover the unit sphere in \(\mathbb C^2\) by polynomially many caps of radius

\[
Q^{-A_{\rm cap}}.
\]

On a cap,

\[
\ell(m)\approx \ell_0
\]

for some fixed unit vector \(\ell_0\). If the cap cover is polynomial, pigeonhole a cap carrying polynomial \(\nu\)-mass.

Then the rank-one wall theorem reduces locally to a fixed linear-form sublevel estimate:

\[
\nu\left(
|\langle \Gamma,\ell_0\rangle|^2<\varepsilon
\right)
\le
Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C_{\ell_0}).
\]

The cap count is polynomial because the space of directions in \(\mathbb C^2\) has fixed dimension. This is important: moving direction can be frozen at polynomial cost.

So rank-one forbidden-wall extraction is not fatal. It requires fixed-direction anti-concentration after angular pigeonholing.

---

# 8. Final rank-stratified extraction stack

The \(\Lambda\)-compatible extraction should be stated as:

\[
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
\]

\[
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
\]

\[
\mathrm{RankVisible}_{\nu}(M_{\rm forb})
\]

\[
\mathrm{HGood}_{\nu}\text{ or }H\text{-from-Gate--1}
\]

\[
\mathrm{ForbiddenWallAntiConc}_{\nu}
\]

\[
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}_{N,s_1,s_2}
\]

\[
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}.
\]

Where

\[
\mathrm{ForbiddenWallAntiConc}_{\nu}
\]

means either:

### Rank two

\[
\nu(|\Gamma|^2<\varepsilon)\le Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu.
\]

### Rank one

After angular cap reduction,

\[
\nu(|\langle\Gamma,\ell_0\rangle|^2<\varepsilon)
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu.
\]

---

# 9. What this gives

Under these inputs, extraction produces a subset \(\mathcal G\) where:

\[
E_1\ge Q^{-A},
\]

\[
E_{23}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

\[
\frac{|H|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and hence

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Additionally:

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\]

\[
s_1(M_{\rm forb})\asymp S_1,
\]

and, if rank two,

\[
s_2(M_{\rm forb})\asymp S_2.
\]

So downstream receives stable forbidden-channel local certificates.

---

# 10. Updated conclusion

The extraction layer is still Green, but the forbidden wall input has sharpened to a finite-rank visibility problem.

The new key downstream/extraction interface is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankVisible}_{\nu}(M_{\rm forb})
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenWallAntiConc}_{\nu}.
}
\]

If \(M_{\rm forb}\) is rank two on polynomial joint mass, raw \(\Gamma\)-anti-concentration suffices.

If \(M_{\rm forb}\) is rank one, we need moving forbidden-linear-form anti-concentration, reducible to fixed linear forms after angular pigeonholing.

If \(M_{\rm forb}\) is rank zero on most joint mass, Stack C2 fails downstream unless Agent 6 finds a different \(\Lambda\) or a different D4 carrier channel.

## Agent 2 — \(\Lambda\)-compatible extraction after rank stratification

I executed the Agent 2 instruction under the updated Stack C2 architecture. The important update is that extraction must target **forbidden-channel D4 visibility**, not raw carrier/D4 anisotropy. The wall factor is now

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{|H(m)|^2\,|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2}
{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)},
\]

where

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)
=
\Lambda E_{\rm car}(m)L_{\clean}(m),
\qquad
\Gamma(m)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

This is exactly the \(\Lambda\)-dependent correction required by the coordinator update. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{GoodSubsetExtraction remains Green, but only after rank-stratifying }M_{\rm forb}.
}
\]

The extraction theorem now requires:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}
+
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N}
+
\mathrm{RankVisible}_\nu(M_{\rm forb})
+
\mathrm{HGood}
+
\mathrm{ForbiddenWallAntiConc}_\nu
+
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}.
}
\]

If \(M_{\rm forb}\) is rank zero on most joint mass, extraction cannot help. That is a downstream no-go, not an extraction failure.

---

# 1. Work under the joint mass measure

Let

\[
F:=F_1F_{23},
\]

where

\[
F_1=\min(E_1/\eta_1,1),
\qquad
F_{23}=\min(E_{23}/\eta_{23},1).
\]

Let \(N(m)>0\) be the branch reference energy. For the ordinary branch,

\[
N_{\ord}(m)=|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

Define the joint measure

\[
d\nu(m):=F(m)N(m)\,d\rho(m).
\]

The aggregate input is

\[
\boxed{
\nu(\mathcal C)
=
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
}
\]

This is the correct pre-extraction measure.

---

# 2. Geometric bad sets are removed separately

Let

\[
B_{\rm geom}
=
B_{\rm Gram}\cup B_{\rm gauge}\cup B_{\rm chart}
\cup B_{\rm carrier}\cup B_{\rm route}.
\]

These are binary downstream eligibility failures. They do not need to enter the arithmetic joint theorem.

It is enough to assume

\[
\boxed{
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A_{\rm geom}}
\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho,
}
\]

with \(A_{\rm geom}\) large relative to the \(\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N}\) exponent. Since \(F\le1\),

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}F N\,d\rho
\le
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho.
\]

So geometric bad sets remove at most a polynomially negligible portion of the joint mass.

After removal, replace \(\mathcal C\) by

\[
\mathcal C^{\rm geom}
=
\mathcal C\setminus B_{\rm geom}.
\]

---

# 3. Rank stratification of \(M_{\rm forb}\)

Let

\[
s_1(m)\ge s_2(m)\ge0
\]

be the singular values of

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m):\mathbb C^2\to\mathcal K.
\]

Define three regimes.

## Rank-two visible

\[
\mathcal C_2
=
\{m:s_2(m)\ge Q^{-A_2}\}.
\]

Then

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
\ge
Q^{-2A_2}|\Gamma|^2.
\]

So raw \(\Gamma\)-anti-concentration is enough.

## Rank-one visible

\[
\mathcal C_1
=
\{m:s_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},\ s_2(m)<Q^{-A_2}\}.
\]

Then there is a unit right singular direction

\[
\ell(m)\in\mathbb C^2
\]

such that

\[
|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2
\asymp
s_1(m)^2|\langle\Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2
\]

up to the negligible \(s_2\)-error.

So the needed wall theorem becomes a forbidden **linear-form** anti-concentration estimate.

## Rank-zero / invisible

\[
\mathcal C_0
=
\{m:s_1(m)<Q^{-A_0}\}.
\]

On this set,

\[
M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)
\]

is small for every \(\Gamma\). If \(\mathcal C_0\) carries essentially all \(\nu\)-mass, then D4 is invisible to the forbidden channel.

Thus Stack C2 requires:

\[
\boxed{
\nu(\mathcal C_1\cup\mathcal C_2)
\ge
Q^{-A_{\rm vis}}\nu(\mathcal C^{\rm geom}).
}
\]

If this fails, the downstream channel \(\Lambda\) does not see the live D4 carrier on the extracted mass.

---

# 4. Rank-two extraction

Assume \(\mathcal C_2\) carries polynomial \(\nu\)-mass:

\[
\nu(\mathcal C_2)\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C^{\rm geom}).
\]

Assume \(H\)-goodness, either deterministically from Gate–1 plus geometry or by a separate sublevel estimate:

\[
\frac{|H(m)|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)}
\ge
Q^{-A_H}
\]

on a polynomial \(\nu\)-portion of \(\mathcal C_2\).

Assume raw \(\Gamma\)-anti-concentration:

\[
\boxed{
\nu_{\mathcal C_2}\left(|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A_\Gamma}\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C_2).
}
\]

Then on the intersection,

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}
=
\frac{|H|^2|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}
\ge
Q^{-A_H}Q^{-2A_2}Q^{-A_\Gamma}
=
Q^{-A''}.
\]

So rank-two extraction succeeds.

---

# 5. Rank-one extraction

Assume \(\mathcal C_1\) carries polynomial \(\nu\)-mass:

\[
\nu(\mathcal C_1)\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C^{\rm geom}).
\]

Then \(M_{\rm forb}\) sees only one forbidden direction. Let \(\ell(m)\) be the visible right singular direction.

The required anti-concentration theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\nu_{\mathcal C_1}
\left(
|\langle\Gamma(m),\ell(m)\rangle|^2
\ge Q^{-A_\ell}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C_1).
}
\]

Then, using \(s_1\ge Q^{-A_1}\),

\[
|M_{\rm forb}\Gamma|^2
\ge
Q^{-2A_1}Q^{-A_\ell}
\]

on a polynomial-mass subset, modulo the \(s_2\)-error. Together with \(H\)-goodness,

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A''}.
\]

So rank-one extraction also succeeds, but the anti-concentration input is a moving linear-form theorem, not raw \(|\Gamma|\)-anisotropy.

---

# 6. Freezing the moving direction by angular caps

The moving direction \(\ell(m)\in\mathbb C^2\) can be reduced to fixed directions at polynomial cost.

Cover the unit sphere in \(\mathbb C^2\) by caps of radius

\[
Q^{-A_{\rm cap}}.
\]

The real dimension is \(3\), so the number of caps is polynomial:

\[
O(Q^{3A_{\rm cap}}).
\]

By pigeonhole, one cap carries polynomial \(\nu\)-mass. On that cap,

\[
\ell(m)=\ell_0+O(Q^{-A_{\rm cap}})
\]

for some fixed unit vector \(\ell_0\). If

\[
|\Gamma(m)|\le Q^C
\]

on the relevant truncated set, then

\[
|\langle\Gamma,\ell(m)\rangle
-
\langle\Gamma,\ell_0\rangle|
\le
Q^{C-A_{\rm cap}}.
\]

Choose \(A_{\rm cap}\) large enough. Then the moving anti-concentration theorem reduces to fixed linear-form anti-concentration:

\[
\boxed{
\nu_{\mathcal C_{1,\ell_0}}
\left(
|\langle\Gamma,\ell_0\rangle|^2\ge Q^{-A}
\right)
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu(\mathcal C_{1,\ell_0}).
}
\]

Thus rank-one is not fatal. It adds only a fixed-direction anti-concentration theorem after angular pigeonholing.

---

# 7. Dyadic normalization

For stable downstream summation, select dyadic slices simultaneously in:

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\]

\[
s_1(M_{\rm forb}(m))\asymp S_1,
\]

and, in rank-two,

\[
s_2(M_{\rm forb}(m))\asymp S_2.
\]

After polynomial truncation, each parameter has only \(O(\log Q)\) relevant dyadic values. So the total number of slices is \(Q^{o(1)}\).

On a selected slice, the local normalization is stable:

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\qquad
s_i(m)\asymp S_i.
\]

Then the usual capped extraction gives a set \(\mathcal G\) with polynomial weight and pointwise lower bounds.

---

# 8. Final rank-stratified extraction theorem

Let \((\mathcal C,\rho)\) be one branch of routed centers. Let \(N>0\) be the branch reference energy with polynomial dyadic range.

Assume:

\[
\int_{\mathcal C}F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\ge
Q^{-A}\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Assume geometric bad-set smallness:

\[
\int_{B_{\rm geom}}N\,d\rho
\le
Q^{-A'}\int_{\mathcal C}N\,d\rho.
\]

Let

\[
d\nu=F_1F_{23}N\,d\rho
\]

on \(\mathcal C^{\rm geom}\).

Assume rank visibility:

\[
\nu(s_1(M_{\rm forb})\ge Q^{-A_0})
\ge
Q^{-A_{\rm vis}}\nu(\mathcal C^{\rm geom}).
\]

Then at least one of the rank-one or rank-two visible regimes carries polynomial \(\nu\)-mass.

In the rank-two case, assume raw \(\Gamma\)-anti-concentration:

\[
\nu(|\Gamma|^2\ge Q^{-A})\ge Q^{-A'}\nu.
\]

In the rank-one case, after angular cap reduction, assume fixed forbidden-linear-form anti-concentration:

\[
\nu(|\langle\Gamma,\ell_0\rangle|^2\ge Q^{-A})
\ge
Q^{-A'}\nu.
\]

Also assume \(H\)-goodness:

\[
\frac{|H|^2}{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}}\ge Q^{-A_H}
\]

on a polynomial \(\nu\)-mass subset, either deterministically from \(E_1+\) geometry or by sublevel control.

Then there exists a dyadic subset

\[
\mathcal G
\]

such that

\[
\rho(\mathcal G)\ge Q^{-A''}\rho(\mathcal C_j)
\]

for a selected dyadic class \(\mathcal C_j\), and for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)\ge Q^{-A''},
\]

\[
N(m)\asymp N_j,
\]

and all geometric conditions hold.

If local packing holds, \(\mathcal G\) contains a separated sub-subset with another polynomial loss.

---

# 9. Branchwise form

The theorem applies branchwise with the appropriate \(N\).

## Ordinary

\[
N_{\ord}(m)=|X_{\can}(m)|^2.
\]

## Transition

Use the weighted transition reference energy, for example

\[
N_{\tr}(m)=\|\mathcal J_R^{\tr}(m)\|^2
\]

or the local scalar \(L^2\)-source energy controlled by \(\mathcal J_R^{\tr}\).

## Singular

Use projected canonical singular energy:

\[
N_{\sing}(m)
=
\left\|
P_R[\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)]
\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2,
\]

or the multiresolution projected version if single \(P_R\) is insufficient.

This avoids raw diagnostic sine moments.

---

# 10. Extraction-side no-go

If

\[
\nu(s_1(M_{\rm forb})<Q^{-A_0})
\]

carries almost all joint mass, then no extraction theorem can produce forbidden-channel D4 energy. The local D4 live carrier is invisible to \(\Lambda\).

In that case the architecture fails downstream unless:

1. a different \(\Lambda\) is identified;
2. another D4 carrier channel is used;
3. or the final contradiction is not actually a forbidden-channel identity.

So the extraction layer clearly hands the decisive finite audit back to Agent 6:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Find }\Lambda\text{ and prove }M_{\rm forb}\text{ is not rank-zero on the live carrier plane.}
}
\]

---

# Final Agent 2 status

The \(\Lambda\)-compatible extraction theorem is now complete modulo sharply isolated inputs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{JointAgg}_{1,23}^{N},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{GeomBadSmall}^{N},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{RankVisible}_{\nu}(M_{\rm forb}),
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{HGood}\text{ or }H\text{-sublevel},
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Gamma\text{-anti-concentration in rank two,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{forbidden linear-form anti-concentration in rank one,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{DyadicNorm}.
}
\]

Given those, extraction produces a polynomial-weight subset with pointwise Gate–1, O2/O3, and forbidden-channel D4 visibility.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: forbidden output atomization after extraction

The current extraction theorem produces a good set \(\mathcal G\) such that, for every \(m\in\mathcal G\),

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A},\qquad
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}(m)
=
\frac{|H(m)|^2|M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)|^2}
{\mathcal N_{\rm D4}(m)}
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This gives local forbidden-channel energy. But it does **not yet** give a global contradiction, because the forbidden outputs

\[
w_m:=\Lambda v_m
\]

may point in different directions and cancel after summation.

So the next extraction-side refinement is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{after extracting forbidden energy, also atomize the forbidden output direction and phase.}
}
\]

This does not solve row mobility by itself, but it gives Agent 6 a much cleaner input.

---

# 1. Local forbidden certificate

On the extracted set,

\[
w_m
=
\Lambda v_m
\approx
H(m)M_{\rm forb}(m)\Gamma(m)
\]

up to controlled quotient/residual errors.

The extraction theorem gives

\[
|w_m|^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_{\rm loc}(m)
\]

for a local normalization \(\mathcal N_{\rm loc}\).

But global contradiction needs something like

\[
\left|\sum_{m\in\mathcal G} \sqrt{\mu_m}\, w_m\right|^2
\gtrsim
Q^{-C}\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2,
\]

or a row-energy lower-frame variant.

This is false for arbitrary phases.

---

# 2. Finite-dimensional direction pigeonholing

The codomain \(\mathcal K\) of \(\Lambda\) should be finite-dimensional at fixed local order. Let

\[
d_{\Lambda}:=\dim\mathcal K.
\]

Assume \(d_\Lambda=Q^{o(1)}\) or polynomially bounded.

Cover the unit sphere in \(\mathcal K\) by caps of radius

\[
\epsilon_{\rm cap}=Q^{-A_{\rm cap}}.
\]

The number of caps is

\[
\ll \epsilon_{\rm cap}^{-O(d_\Lambda)}.
\]

If \(d_\Lambda=O(1)\) or \(Q^{o(1)}\), this is only polynomial or \(Q^{o(1)}\)-loss.

Since each

\[
\widehat w_m:=\frac{w_m}{|w_m|}
\]

lies in one cap, there is a cap centered at \(\omega_0\) carrying polynomial fraction of the weighted forbidden energy:

\[
\sum_{m:\widehat w_m\approx\omega_0}\mu_m|w_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2.
\]

Restrict to that cap and call the subset \(\mathcal G_\omega\).

Then

\[
w_m
=
\alpha_m\omega_0+r_m,
\qquad
|r_m|\le Q^{-A_{\rm cap}}|w_m|.
\]

Now the local forbidden certificates are all essentially scalar multiples of one fixed forbidden direction.

---

# 3. Phase pigeonholing

The scalars

\[
\alpha_m=\langle w_m,\omega_0\rangle
\]

are complex. Cover the unit circle by arcs of length

\[
Q^{-A_{\rm ph}}.
\]

Pigeonhole again to obtain

\[
\mathcal G_{\omega,\phi}\subseteq \mathcal G_\omega
\]

such that

\[
\arg \alpha_m
\]

is nearly constant on \(\mathcal G_{\omega,\phi}\), and

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\omega,\phi}}\mu_m|w_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A''}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2.
\]

On this subset,

\[
\alpha_m=e^{i\phi_0}|\alpha_m|+O(Q^{-A_{\rm ph}}|w_m|).
\]

Therefore

\[
\left|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_{\omega,\phi}}\sqrt{\mu_m}w_m
\right|
\]

has no phase cancellation at the level of the forbidden output vector, up to polynomially negligible cap errors.

---

# 4. What this gives

After direction and phase atomization, we get a subset \(\mathcal G_*\) such that:

\[
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_*}\mu_m|w_m|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2,
\]

and

\[
w_m
=
e^{i\phi_0}a_m\omega_0
+
\mathrm{small},
\qquad
a_m\ge0.
\]

Then

\[
\left|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_*}\sqrt{\mu_m}w_m
\right|^2
\gtrsim
\left(\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_*}\sqrt{\mu_m}|w_m|\right)^2.
\]

By Cauchy,

\[
\left(\sum_m\sqrt{\mu_m}|w_m|\right)^2
\ge
\sum_m\mu_m|w_m|^2.
\]

So on \(\mathcal G_*\),

\[
\boxed{
\left|
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G_*}\sqrt{\mu_m}w_m
\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A'}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2.
}
\]

This is a **coherent forbidden-channel lower bound**.

---

# 5. Caveat: row mobility may not sum certificates directly

The above assumes the global forbidden vector is literally a sum of local forbidden outputs:

\[
\Lambda V_{\rm glob}
\sim
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\sqrt{\mu_m}w_m.
\]

If row mobility maps local certificates into different row atoms, then we need an additional atom selection.

Let

\[
w_m=\sum_a w_{m,a}e_a
\]

in the global forbidden row basis.

If

\[
|w_m|^2=\sum_a|w_{m,a}|^2,
\]

then for each \(m\) choose a dominant atom \(a(m)\) with

\[
|w_{m,a(m)}|^2\ge d_\Lambda^{-1}|w_m|^2.
\]

Pigeonhole \(a(m)=a_0\). If the number of possible atoms is polynomially bounded on the routed set, then a polynomial fraction of forbidden energy lands in one atom.

Then phase-pigeonhole within that atom.

This gives a scalar coherent subcollection:

\[
w_{m,a_0}\approx e^{i\phi_0}|w_{m,a_0}|.
\]

Thus the local forbidden outputs are not only visible to \(\Lambda\), but visible to the **same global forbidden coordinate with the same phase**.

---

# 6. Relation to RowMobility\(_{\rm lower}\)

This atomization gives a sufficient condition for RowMobility\(_{\rm lower}\):

1. local forbidden certificates \(w_m\) are nonzero;
2. the number of possible global forbidden atoms is polynomially bounded;
3. a polynomial-energy subcollection shares one atom;
4. phase-pigeonholing gives coherent sign.

Then

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2.
\]

So instead of asking for a general lower-frame theorem, one can ask for the more concrete:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Dominant forbidden atom extraction + phase coherence.}
}
\]

This is weaker than full Riesz lower-frame mobility and may be easier.

---

# 7. New extraction-plus-mobility interface

The refined chain should be:

\[
\mathrm{GoodSubsetExtraction}^{\Lambda}
\]

gives

\[
\mathcal G,\qquad |w_m|^2\ge Q^{-A}\mathcal N_m.
\]

Then:

\[
\mathrm{ForbiddenAtomBound}
\]

says the local outputs decompose into at most \(Q^C\) global forbidden atoms.

Then:

\[
\mathrm{AtomPigeonhole}
\]

extracts one atom \(a_0\) carrying polynomial forbidden energy.

Then:

\[
\mathrm{PhasePigeonhole}
\]

extracts one phase sector carrying polynomial atom energy.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\left|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}\right|^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\sum_{m\in\mathcal G}\mu_m|w_m|^2.
}
\]

Now the global forbidden upper can contradict this.

---

# 8. When this fails

This refinement fails if any of the following hold.

## Too many atoms

If local certificates spread over super-polynomially many global forbidden row atoms, atom pigeonholing loses too much.

Then one needs true row-mobility lower-frame theory, not just atom extraction.

## No coherent summation

If the global object is not a sum of local forbidden outputs but a more complicated nonlinear or signed assembly, phase pigeonholing may not apply.

Then \(\Lambda\) must be analyzed structurally.

## Phase locked adversarially

If phases are tied to row positions in a way that prevents selecting a useful coherent subcollection without destroying row coverage, then the method fails.

But if only aggregate contradiction is needed, a polynomial-weight coherent subcollection should be enough.

---

# 9. Updated Agent 2 endpoint

The extraction layer can now feed downstream in a stronger form.

Instead of only producing

\[
E_{\rm wall}^{\rm forb}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

we can add two purely finite pigeonholing steps:

\[
\boxed{
\text{forbidden direction/atom selection}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{phase-sector selection}.
}
\]

This yields a subset \(\mathcal G_*\) where local forbidden certificates are coherent:

\[
w_m\approx e^{i\phi_0}a_m\omega_0,
\qquad a_m\ge0.
\]

Then, provided the global forbidden identity actually sums these local contributions,

\[
|\Lambda V_{\rm glob}|^2
\]

gets a polynomial lower bound.

So Agent 2’s refined extraction theorem now outputs not just a good subset, but a **coherent forbidden-channel good subset**, conditional on a polynomial atom-count row mobility model.
