## Agent 2 — continued attack: projection design for carrier-atlas holonomy

The carrier-atlas route now has a sharp criterion:

\[
\Lambda_{\atlas}^{(R)}
=
P_A T_{C\to A}P_C T_{B\to C}P_B T_{A\to B}P_A-P_A
\]

is useful only if:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows have small finite projection holonomy,}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carrier data has nonzero finite projection holonomy.}
}
\]

This is a projection-design problem. The most promising site remains the transition–singular lift.

---

# 1. Reformulate the problem as “smooth rows versus singular carrier defects”

Let the singular chart retained projection be

\[
P_{\sing}: \mathcal H_{\sing}\to R_{\sing},
\]

with discarded complement

\[
C_{\sing}=(I-P_{\sing})\mathcal H_{\sing}.
\]

For actual global rows \(V_{\glob}\), the atlas upper needs

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\|V_{\glob}\|^2
\]

in the aggregate plaquette norm.

For D4 live carriers \(W_{\tr}\), visibility needs

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\neq0,
\]

and after completing the loop,

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\]

must have nonzero projection back into the ordinary/transition live carrier plane.

So the intended separation is:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\glob}\text{ is smooth/coherent and captured by }P_{\sing};
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
W_{\tr}\text{ is a formal local D4 carrier and leaks into }C_{\sing}.
}
\]

This is analogous to a consistency-versus-stability argument in nonconforming discretizations.

---

# 2. What kind of \(P_{\sing}\) can do this?

A projection that is too large kills visibility. A projection that is too small kills the upper.

The correct \(P_{\sing}\) should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{large enough to approximate actual scalar source rows;}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{not closed under transition of formal D4 live carriers.}
}
\]

That suggests a retained singular space built from **actual canonical singular modes**, not arbitrary Taylor carriers.

For example,

\[
R_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\Phi_3,\ldots,\Phi_{R+3}\}
\]

or a multiresolution variant chosen to approximate actual \(q''_{\can}\)-rows in \(L^2\), while the transition image of the formal D4 carrier has components outside that basis.

The crucial distinction:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\sing}\text{ should approximate actual global rows, not all formal carrier jets.}
}
\]

If it approximates all formal jets through order \(9\), then it likely transports \([\mathsf C_7]\) and \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) functorially and gives rank zero.

---

# 3. Candidate: energy-adapted singular projection

Define \(P_{\sing}\) as the \(L^2(I)\)-orthogonal projection onto a singular basis \(R_{\sing}\) optimized for the actual scalar source \(F_m(x)\):

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

Then the upper is:

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing})F_p\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\le Q^{-B}
\sum_p
\|F_p\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

This is an actual-source approximation theorem.

But D4 live carriers are not arbitrary actual source functions. They are finite carrier quotient directions:

\[
[\mathsf C_7],\qquad [\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Their transition image into the singular chart may not lie in the actual-source-adapted retained space.

Thus visibility becomes:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\|+
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is exactly the desired separation.

---

# 4. Risk: if \(R_{\sing}\) is chosen from \(q''_{\can}\), it may also capture D4

The danger is that D4 carriers are not extraneous; they are produced from the same \(q''_{\can}\) source. If the retained singular projection captures actual source energy well, it might also capture the D4 live carrier components.

Then

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}=0
\]

or very small, and the atlas holonomy is rank zero.

So the audit must check whether the D4 live plane is actually contained in the singular retained source basis.

Concretely, test:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\in R_{\sing}?
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\in R_{\sing}?
\]

If both are contained, no transition–singular leakage.

If at least one has a component outside \(R_{\sing}\), atlas D4 visibility is possible.

---

# 5. Leakage must return

Leakage alone is insufficient. The leaked component must return into a forbidden retained coordinate after completing the loop:

\[
W_{\tr}
\xrightarrow{T_{\tr\to\sing}}
C_{\sing}
\xrightarrow{T_{\sing\to\ord}}
\mathcal H_{\ord}
\xrightarrow{P_{\ord}}
W_{\ord}\oplus C_{\ord}.
\]

The relevant block is:

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
\]

If this is zero, leakage is invisible to the loop.

So the finite rank audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}L_{\clean}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

This is more precise than merely asking \(T^{CW}_{\tr\to\sing}\neq0\).

---

# 6. Three possible outcomes

## Outcome A: no leakage

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}=0.
\]

Then the transition–singular projection is functorial on the D4 live plane. Atlas \(\Lambda\) is rank zero.

## Outcome B: leakage without return

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\neq0,
\]

but

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}=0.
\]

Then the leak exists but does not create forbidden holonomy. Still rank zero for contradiction.

## Outcome C: leakage with return

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\neq0.
\]

Then the atlas loop sees D4. Rank one or two depends on the image dimension.

This is the only successful case.

---

# 7. Role of multiresolution singular retained spaces

If the singular retained space is too small, the upper fails. If it is too large, leakage disappears.

A multiresolution retained space

\[
R_{\sing}^{\multi}
=
\bigoplus_j R_{\sing,j}
\]

could be tuned so actual rows are well approximated while still excluding specific formal D4 carrier directions.

But if multiresolution becomes dense enough to capture all order-\(7/9\) carrier directions, it again kills leakage.

Thus the projection design must deliberately retain:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source modes}
}
\]

rather than:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all local polynomial/Taylor carrier modes.}
}
\]

This is subtle. The projection should be source-adapted, not jet-complete.

---

# 8. Possible way to force separation: use orthogonality to actual-source manifold

Let \(\mathcal S_{\actual}\) be the local manifold/subspace of actual canonical source rows in the singular chart.

If actual rows are not arbitrary in \(\mathcal H_{\sing}\), then choose

\[
R_{\sing}\supset \mathcal S_{\actual}
\]

up to small error, but do not include all formal D4 carriers.

Then upper holds by construction, while D4 visibility holds if

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\not\subset R_{\sing}.
\]

This is a powerful conceptual separation:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows live in a small coherent source subspace;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{formal D4 carrier data points partly transverse to that subspace.}
}
\]

The needed theorem is then:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual-source coherence subspace is smaller than formal carrier jet space.}
}
\]

This may be proveable from explicit-formula completion/coherence.

---

# 9. Relation to partial EF residuals

Partial EF residuals can prove actual-source coherence:

\[
q''_{\can}\approx B_2+P_2^{\le X}+D_2^{\le X}
\]

in local \(L^2\) branch norms.

Thus actual global rows lie near the finite EF model subspace.

If D4 formal carrier directions include components outside that finite EF model subspace after transition–singular projection, they create atlas holonomy.

So partial EF residuals can support the atlas upper:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing})V_{\glob}\|^2\ll1,
\]

while leaving D4 leakage if D4 is not in the EF coherent subspace.

This is a hidden interaction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{partial EF coherence may prove the atlas upper.}
}
\]

---

# 10. Updated finite audit target

The transition–singular audit should not merely ask whether \(T^{CW}\neq0\). It should define a retained singular source-coherence space \(R_{\sing}\) and test:

## Upper

\[
\sum_p\|(I-P_{\sing})V_{\glob,p}\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## Leakage

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\neq0.
\]

## Return

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\neq0.
\]

This is the precise finite carrier atlas test.

---

# 11. Final Agent 2 endpoint

The carrier atlas route is viable only if we can choose singular retained projections that are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{accurate for actual global source rows}
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
\text{not functorial on formal D4 live carriers.}
}
\]

The strongest current opportunity is to define \(P_{\sing}\) as an actual-source coherence projection, backed by partial EF residual estimates, rather than as a jet-complete projection.

Then D4 visibility becomes a transverse-defect test:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\not\subset R_{\sing}.
\]

If the D4 live carrier plane lies inside the actual-source coherence subspace, atlas holonomy is rank zero. If it has transverse components that return around the loop, finite D4 finally has a Type I forbidden contradiction channel.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: actual-source coherence space versus formal D4 carriers

The carrier-atlas route now hinges on a very specific separation:

\boxed{
\text{actual global rows are well approximated by }R_{\sing},

but

T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\not\subset R_{\sing}.

I pushed this into a linear algebra test. The key idea is to define the singular retained space \(R_{\sing}\) not as “low modes” or “Taylor jets,” but as an **actual-source coherence space** coming from the canonical explicit formula.

---

# 1. Define the actual-source coherence space

Let

\[
F_m(x)=\lambda^3 q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\]

in a singular chart.

A partial explicit-formula approximation gives

\[
F_m(x)
=
F_m^{\mathrm{coh}}(x)+R_m(x),
\]

where

\[
F_m^{\mathrm{coh}}
\in R_{\sing},
\]

and

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|R_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\le Q^{-B}\sum_m\rho_m\|F_m\|_{L^2(I)}^2.
\]

Then \(R_{\sing}\) is the retained singular carrier/source-coherence space.

The atlas upper becomes immediate:

\[
\sum_m\rho_m\|(I-P_{\sing})F_m\|^2
=
\sum_m\rho_m\|R_m\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\sum_m\rho_m\|F_m\|^2.
\]

So the upper is not mysterious if \(R_{\sing}\) is the coherent EF model space.

---

# 2. The D4 visibility test becomes orthogonality to coherence constraints

Let

\[
W_{\tr}
=
\operatorname{span}\{[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\}.
\]

Define

\[
L_{\tr\to\sing}:=T_{\tr\to\sing}\big|_{W_{\tr}}.
\]

Then atlas D4 visibility is equivalent to

\[
(I-P_{\sing})L_{\tr\to\sing}\neq0.
\]

Equivalently, there exists a linear functional

\[
\ell\in R_{\sing}^{\perp}
\]

such that

\[
\ell\bigl(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}\bigr)\neq0
\]

or

\[
\ell\bigl(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}\bigr)\neq0.
\]

Thus the finite audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Find a coherence annihilator }\ell
\text{ that kills actual EF-coherent rows but not the D4 live carrier.}
}
\]

This is much sharper than checking leakage abstractly.

---

# 3. What are the coherence annihilators?

If \(R_{\sing}\) is generated by explicit-formula coherent modes, then \(R_{\sing}^{\perp}\) consists of tests orthogonal to all retained prime/dual/gamma/tail coherent modes.

For example, if

\[
R_{\sing}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\phi_n(x):n\le X\}
+
\operatorname{span}\{\phi_n^\vee(x):n\le X\}
+
R_{\gamma}
+
R_{\tail},
\]

then \(\ell\in R_{\sing}^{\perp}\) satisfies

\[
\langle \ell,\phi_n\rangle=0,
\quad
\langle \ell,\phi_n^\vee\rangle=0,
\quad
\langle \ell,R_{\gamma}\rangle=0,
\quad
\cdots
\]

for the retained coherent family.

D4 visibility requires that the singular image of the formal carrier is not in that span.

So the question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\text{ representable by retained explicit-formula modes?}
}
\]

and similarly for

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If yes, no leakage. If no, we get a forbidden atom.

---

# 4. Expected outcome depends on the size of \(R_{\sing}\)

There is a tradeoff.

## If \(R_{\sing}\) is too large

Suppose \(R_{\sing}\) contains all local singular functions up to order \(9\), or all modes capable of representing order-\(7/9\) carrier behavior. Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\subset R_{\sing}
\]

and the atlas rank is zero.

## If \(R_{\sing}\) is too small

Then D4 leaks, but actual global rows may also leak:

\[
(I-P_{\sing})F_m
\]

may be too large, destroying the atlas upper.

## Correct balance

\(R_{\sing}\) should retain actual coherent EF modes but not arbitrary formal carrier directions.

So the existence of a useful \(\Lambda_{\atlas}\) depends on a structural fact:

\[
\boxed{
\text{formal D4 live carriers are transverse to the actual EF-coherent singular source manifold.}
}
\]

This is now the exact finite theorem.

---

# 5. Return map condition

Leakage alone does not give loop holonomy. Need return:

\[
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\neq0.
\]

In functional terms:

1. \(\ell\in R_{\sing}^{\perp}\) detects leakage in the singular chart.
2. The corresponding leaked mode, when transported to ordinary chart, must have nonzero retained ordinary carrier component.

Equivalently, there exists an ordinary retained test \(r_{\ord}\in W_{\ord}^*\) such that

\[
r_{\ord}
\left(
T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}w
\right)\neq0
\]

for some \(w\in W_{\tr}\).

So the atlas atom is a pair:

\[
(\ell_{\sing},r_{\ord})
\]

with nonzero transition pairing.

This gives a concrete finite matrix entry to compute:

\[
\boxed{
r_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}w.
}
\]

---

# 6. Possible link to \(M_A/M_C\)

Earlier, \(M_A/M_C\) identities were likely rank-zero as \(\Lambda\). But they may still help here.

They describe local connection identities and pre-trivialization closures. If they characterize exactly which formal carrier directions are EF-coherent, then they can identify \(R_{\sing}\) or its annihilator.

Thus \(M_A/M_C\) may be useful not as the forbidden identity, but as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a characterization of the coherent subspace }R_{\sing}.
}
\]

Then D4 visibility reduces to checking whether \([\mathsf C_7]\) or \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) violates those connection identities after transition to singular coordinates.

This is a valuable reuse.

---

# 7. Candidate no-go

There is a strong possible no-go:

If the actual EF-coherent space \(R_{\sing}\) is defined broadly enough to include all allowed local source jets of \(q''_{\can}\), then D4 carriers are probably inside it, because D4 is generated by actual source geometry.

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\subset R_{\sing}
\]

and atlas holonomy is rank zero.

To avoid this, \(R_{\sing}\) must be an approximation space for **global coherent rows**, not the full local solution space.

So the distinction is:

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}\neq
\text{all local jets compatible with }q''_{\can};
}
\]

rather,

\[
\boxed{
R_{\sing}=
\text{finite global EF coherent subspace under the chosen cutoff/regularization.}
}
\]

This leaves room for D4 formal local carriers to be transverse.

---

# 8. Updated theorem target

A viable finite carrier atlas theorem should be stated as:

## Actual-source coherence upper

There exists a finite singular retained space \(R_{\sing}\) such that actual global rows satisfy

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}V_{\glob,p}\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## D4 transversality

For the live D4 plane \(W_{\tr}\),

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1.
\]

This is the exact theorem.

If both hold, the atlas loop gives a Type I forbidden channel.

---

# 9. What to compute first

The fastest finite audit is:

1. Choose candidate \(R_{\sing}\) from the EF-coherent approximation used for \(q''_{\can}\).

2. Compute the two vectors:

\[
v_7=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
v_9=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

3. Project:

\[
v_7^\perp=(I-P_{\sing})v_7,
\]

\[
v_9^\perp=(I-P_{\sing})v_9.
\]

4. If both are zero, atlas route fails for this \(R_{\sing}\).

5. If not, apply return map:

\[
u_7=P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}v_7^\perp,
\]

\[
u_9=P_{\ord}T_{\sing\to\ord}v_9^\perp.
\]

6. Compute rank of

\[
[u_7\ u_9].
\]

That rank is the forbidden D4 rank.

This is the concrete downstream finite computation.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 conclusion

The carrier-atlas route can be reframed as a clean transversality problem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers must be transverse to the actual EF-coherent singular subspace.}
}
\]

The upper is supplied by partial EF coherence:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual global rows lie close to }R_{\sing}.
}
\]

The contradiction is supplied if:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\not\subset R_{\sing}
}
\]

and the leaked component returns nontrivially around the atlas loop.

This is now the most concrete finite-D4 downstream audit I can formulate.

## Agent 6 — ForbiddenSingularDiscardD4 theorem

I executed the Agent 6 instruction: assemble the downstream contradiction theorem assuming the local singular-discard operator

\[
\Lambda_p=(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}
\]

is available. This is now the preferred finite-D4 forbidden channel because it is local, atomic, and avoids complement cancellation. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the singular-discard upper and D4 transversality rank hold, downstream closes.}
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}
}
\]

is a Type-I positive forbidden channel. Therefore local D4 energy gives a positive lower bound for

\[
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2,
\]

and this can directly contradict the singular-discard upper

\[
\sum_p\|\Lambda_p J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

No phase-coherence or complement-cancellation theorem is needed.

---

# 1. Forbidden operator and global upper

For each local transition–singular plaquette \(p\), define

\[
\Lambda_p
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}.
\]

Here:

* \(P_{\tr,p}\) projects the transition local row/source representation to the retained transition carrier/source part;
* \(T_{\tr\to\sing,p}\) transports transition data to the singular chart;
* \(P_{\sing,p}\) projects to the retained EF-coherent singular subspace \(R_{\sing,p}\);
* \(I-P_{\sing,p}\) extracts the discarded singular component.

The forbidden upper is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
\Lambda_p J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This upper says: actual global rows from \(q''_{\can}\) are singular-coherent after transition to the singular chart.

This is Type-I because the left side is a positive sum over local atoms \(p\).

---

# 2. Local D4 visibility

The transition-chart D4 live output has the form

\[
v_m^{\live}
=
H(m)\,
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}(m)
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}(m)
\Gamma(m),
\]

where

\[
\Gamma(m)
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\Gamma_G(m)\\
\Gamma_{G,2}(m)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Define the finite singular-discard D4 matrix

\[
K_p
:=
\Lambda_p
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}.
\]

Then the forbidden wall energy is

\[
\boxed{
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\|K_p\Gamma(m)\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
}.
}
\]

The rank of \(K_p\) gives the relevant wall condition.

---

# 3. Rank cases

## Rank two

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_p=2
\]

with lower singular value

\[
s_2(K_p)\ge Q^{-A_K},
\]

then

\[
\|K_p\Gamma\|^2
\ge
Q^{-2A_K}
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right).
\]

So ordinary raw anisotropy is enough.

The wall factor can be taken as

\[
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
/\mathcal N_{\D4}.
\]

## Rank one

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_p=1
\]

with

\[
s_1(K_p)\ge Q^{-A_K},
\]

then there is a visible linear functional

\[
\ell_p(\Gamma)
=
\ell_{1,p}\Gamma_G+\ell_{2,p}\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

such that

\[
\|K_p\Gamma\|^2
\asymp
s_1(K_p)^2
|\ell_p(\Gamma)|^2.
\]

Then the correct wall factor is

\[
\frac{|H|^2|\ell_p(\Gamma)|^2}{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

Raw anisotropy is too strong and not necessary.

## Rank zero

If

\[
K_p=0
\]

on the D4 live plane, then this local singular-discard operator is D4-invisible.

No extraction or wall anti-concentration theorem can repair rank zero. A different \(P_{\sing}\), different chart transition, or different \(\Lambda\) is needed.

---

# 4. Good-subset extraction input

Assume the upstream/extraction layers produce a polynomial-weight good set \(G\) such that for each \(m\in G\):

\[
E_1(m)\ge Q^{-A_1},
\]

\[
E_{23}(m)\ge Q^{-A_{23}},
\]

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)\ge Q^{-A_w},
\]

and all geometric eligibility conditions hold:

\[
\text{Gram-good},\quad
\text{gauge-good},\quad
\text{chart-good},\quad
\text{carrier-good},\quad
\text{routing-good}.
\]

Then local Gate–2/D4 gives

\[
\|\Lambda_p v_m^{\live}\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_{\D4}}\mu_m
\]

after normalization, where \(\mu_m\) is the selected branch/dyadic measure weight.

Equivalently,

\[
\|H(m)K_p\Gamma(m)\|^2
\ge
Q^{-A_{\D4}}\mu_m\mathcal N_{\D4}(m).
\]

---

# 5. Atomic lower bound

Because \(\Lambda_p\) is local and atomic, define the global forbidden energy

\[
\mathcal E_{\forb}
:=
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2.
\]

The D4 local certificates contribute to these same atoms.

Assume the local-to-atom assignment has polynomial multiplicity:

* each extracted center \(m\in G\) contributes to at most \(Q^{C_{\mob}}\) atoms \(p\);
* each atom receives contributions from at most \(Q^{C_{\mob}}\) centers;
* the local D4 contribution is not canceled inside the same atom because the energy is measured after applying \(\Lambda_p\) and squared atomwise.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_{\forb}
\ge
Q^{-C_{\mob}}
\sum_{m\in G}
\mu_m
\|\Lambda_p v_m^{\live}\|^2.
}
\]

Using the local lower bound,

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal E_{\forb}
\ge
Q^{-A_*}\mu(G).
}
\]

This is the positive atomic mobility theorem. It is much easier than the earlier global phase-coherence problem.

---

# 6. Contradiction against the forbidden upper

The forbidden upper gives

\[
\mathcal E_{\forb}
=
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_p J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

The extracted set satisfies

\[
\mu(G)\ge Q^{-A_G}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Therefore the local D4 lower bound gives

\[
\mathcal E_{\forb}
\ge
Q^{-A_*-A_G}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If

\[
B>A_*+A_G
\]

with enough margin, contradiction.

Thus the downstream theorem is clean:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular-discard upper}
+
\text{D4 transversality}
+
\text{good subset}
\Longrightarrow
\text{contradiction}.
}
\]

---

# 7. Residual bad sets

The theorem requires excluding or controlling:

## Rank-zero patches

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_p=0.
\]

These are fatal locally. Need extraction to avoid them or a rank-visible theorem.

## Gram/gauge/chart bad sets

These invalidate local Gate–2/D4 or transition maps.

## Singular projection upper failure

If

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\]

is not small for actual rows, \(\Lambda_p\) is not forbidden.

## O2/O3 tail-bad patches

If \(E_{23}\) is measured on a finite approximation but the full canonical \(A_m\) differs by large tail/completion error, local D4 may not be valid.

## Branch coverage holes

If good centers do not have transition–singular overlap plaquettes, \(\Lambda_p\) cannot be applied.

---

# 8. Exact theorem statement

A downstream theorem can be stated as follows.

Assume:

1. **Singular-discard forbidden upper**

   \[
   \sum_p
   \|\Lambda_p J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
   \le
   Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
   \]

2. **Good subset**

   There exists \(G\subseteq\mathcal C\) with

   \[
   \mu(G)\ge Q^{-A_G}\mu(\mathcal C)
   \]

   and on \(G\),

   \[
   E_1,E_{23},E_{\wall}^{\forb}\ge Q^{-A}.
   \]

3. **D4 local theorem**

   On \(G\),

   \[
   E_1+E_{23}+E_{\wall}^{\forb}
   \Longrightarrow
   \|\Lambda_p v_m^{\live}\|^2\ge Q^{-A_D}\mu_m.
   \]

4. **Atomic mobility/multiplicity**

   Local forbidden certificates contribute to the positive atomic energy with loss at most \(Q^{C_{\mob}}\).

Then

\[
Q^{-A'}\mu(\mathcal C)
\le
\sum_p
\|\Lambda_p V_{\glob}\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

a contradiction for \(B>A'\).

---

# 9. What remains to prove

The downstream proof now depends on two concrete finite/analytic inputs.

## Input A: forbidden upper

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is an actual-source singular coherence theorem, likely proved via partial EF residual/coherence.

## Input B: D4 transversality rank

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1
}
\]

on polynomial-weight patches.

This is a finite carrier computation.

Everything else is bookkeeping/extraction/local D4.

---

# Final verdict

The singular-discard operator

\[
\Lambda_{\tr,\sing,p}
=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}P_{\tr,p}
\]

is the cleanest downstream contradiction channel found so far.

It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{local/atomic,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{positive-energy,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{immune to complement cancellation,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite-auditable on }[\mathsf C_7],[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
}
\]

If the singular-discard upper and D4 transversality rank are true, the finite-D4 downstream mechanism closes.

\text{the forbidden upper and the D4 lower must use the same transition representative.}
}
\]

If the upper is proved for the actual transition row

\[
J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
\]

but D4 produces a carrier-quotient representative only defined up to nuisance terms, then

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

may kill the actual row but not be well-defined on the D4 quotient, or vice versa.

So the next finite audit is **representative compatibility**.

---

# 1. The required commutative diagram

We need the following diagram to be well-defined up to controlled errors:

\[
\begin{array}{ccc}
\text{transition actual row} & \longrightarrow & \text{transition carrier quotient} \\
\downarrow T_{\tr\to\sing} & & \downarrow \widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing} \\
\text{singular actual row} & \longrightarrow & \text{singular retained/discarded split}.
\end{array}
\]

The forbidden upper is about

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can}).
\]

The D4 lower is about

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma.
\]

For the theorem to be valid, \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) must act consistently on both:

1. actual transition rows;
2. D4 carrier representatives.

So we need:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ descends to the transition carrier quotient and respects nuisance equivalence.}
}
\]

If not, the singular-discard operator is not a well-defined \(\Lambda\) on the D4 output.

---

# 2. Nuisance invariance condition

Let

\[
\mathcal N_{\tr}
\]

be the transition nuisance submodule being quotiented out before the D4 live plane is identified.

The singular-discard operator descends to the quotient only if

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}
\subseteq
\text{singular-forbidden-null space}
}
\]

or, more strongly,

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}=0.
}
\]

If this fails, then the value of

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}[C_7]^{\tr}
\]

depends on the representative chosen for \([C_7]^{\tr}\).

That would invalidate the finite rank audit.

So the first required finite lemma is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NuisanceCompatibility}_{\tr\to\sing}:
\quad
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ annihilates transition nuisance directions, or sends them to controlled singular nuisance.}
}
\]

---

# 3. If nuisance compatibility fails

If

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}\neq0,
\]

there are two options.

## Option A: enlarge \(P_{\sing}\)

Add

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}
\]

to the singular retained space:

\[
R_{\sing}^{+}
=
R_{\sing}
+
T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}.
\]

Then the map descends to the quotient.

But this may also absorb the D4 live leakage:

\[
(I-P_{\sing}^{+})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\]

could become zero.

So enlarging \(P_{\sing}\) may restore well-definedness but kill visibility.

## Option B: work before quotient

Do not quotient transition nuisance before applying \(\Lambda\). Instead define the D4 output at representative level and prove nuisance terms are small separately.

This is dangerous because D4 was repaired specifically by routing nuisance rows away. Reopening representative-level nuisances may reintroduce old downstream residual problems.

Thus the cleaner route is Option A, but it requires the transversality audit after enlargement.

---

# 4. Refined rank test

The correct rank test is not merely

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\neq0.
\]

It is:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
W_{\tr}\neq0,
}
\]

where

\[
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}}
=
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}}
+
T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}
+
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{retained}}.
\]

So define

\[
P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}}
\]

as projection onto the singular EF-coherent space plus all transition-nuisance images that must be killed for quotient well-definedness.

Then compute

\[
K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}
=
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}.
\]

The downstream route succeeds only if

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}\ge1.
}
\]

This is the real finite transversality matrix.

---

# 5. Upper after quotient-compatible enlargement

If we enlarge

\[
R_{\sing}
\]

to

\[
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}},
\]

the forbidden upper becomes easier, not harder:

\[
\|(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|
\le
\|(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|.
\]

So the upper survives enlargement.

The only cost is possible loss of D4 visibility.

Thus the finite audit has a clean monotonicity:

\[
\boxed{
\text{larger retained singular space improves upper but weakens D4 visibility.}
}
\]

The optimal retained space is the smallest one that contains:

1. actual EF-coherent rows;
2. transition nuisance images;
3. singular nuisance/representative ambiguities.

Then test whether D4 live carriers remain transverse.

---

# 6. Another hidden issue: \(P_{\tr}\) itself

The operator is

\[
\Lambda_p=(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}.
\]

If \(P_{\tr}\) projects onto transition retained carrier space, then actual rows satisfy

\[
J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
P_{\tr}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
+
(I-P_{\tr})J_{\tr}(q''_{\can}).
\]

The forbidden upper only controls

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\]

if the discarded transition component is either small or harmless.

So we also need:

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-P_{\tr})J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|
\text{ is small in the atlas plaquette norm.}
}
\]

Or include it in the upper:

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\]

without \(P_{\tr}\), and define D4 on the \(P_{\tr}\)-retained part.

The cleanest choice is:

\[
\Lambda_p^{\rm actual}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

on actual rows,

but

\[
\Lambda_p^{\rm D4}
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}P_{\tr}
\]

on carrier outputs.

Then prove

\[
(I-P_{\tr})J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\]

does not affect the upper/lower comparison.

Again, this is a finite projection compatibility issue.

---

# 7. Full set of finite compatibility conditions

The singular-discard route now requires four finite/analytic conditions.

## C1. Actual-row coherence upper

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## C2. Transition projection coherence

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\tr})J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
\]

or otherwise ensure \(P_{\tr}\) is unnecessary in the actual upper.

## C3. Nuisance compatibility

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}=0
\]

or after enlargement of \(R_{\sing}\),

\[
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\mathcal N_{\tr}=0.
\]

## C4. D4 transversality after quotient enlargement

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1.
\]

If all four hold, the downstream singular-discard theorem is well-defined and contradiction-capable.

---

# 8. Possible no-go

There is a plausible no-go:

The transition nuisance module \(\mathcal N_{\tr}\) may be so large that its singular image contains the D4 live plane:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\subseteq
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}}
+
T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}.
\]

Then after quotient-compatible enlargement,

\[
K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}=0.
\]

This would mean D4 live carriers are not distinguishable from transition nuisance once viewed in the singular discarded channel.

In that case singular-discard \(\Lambda\) fails.

So the decisive finite question becomes:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}
\subset
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}}+T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}
\quad\text{or not?}
}
\]

This is sharper than the earlier condition

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}W_{\tr}\subset R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}}.
\]

---

# 9. Updated downstream theorem

Assume \(R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}}\) is defined as above and

\[
K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}
=
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\]

has rank at least \(1\).

Then the forbidden wall energy is

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}(m)
=
\frac{
|H(m)|^2
\|K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}\Gamma(m)\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}(m)
}.
\]

If extraction gives a set where

\[
E_1,E_{23},E_{\wall}^{\forb}\ge Q^{-A},
\]

then local D4 yields positive forbidden singular-discard energy.

The global upper is

\[
\sum_p
\|(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Contradiction follows exactly as before.

---

# 10. Final Agent 6 update

The singular-discard route remains the best finite-D4 contradiction channel, but the finite rank test must be performed **after quotient/nuisance compatibility enlargement**.

The real rank matrix is:

\[
\boxed{
K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}
=
(I-P_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}.
}
\]

Where

\[
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{quot}}
=
R_{\sing}^{\mathrm{EF}}
+
T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathcal N_{\tr}
+
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{retained}}.
\]

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}\ge1,
\]

finite D4 has a genuine Type-I forbidden channel.

If

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}^{\mathrm{quot}}=0,
\]

then the singular-discard route fails: D4 is indistinguishable from actual EF coherence plus transition nuisance in the singular discarded projection.

## Assistant (2026-04-28 04:12:17)

I’ll execute this as Agent 2 by attacking the singular-discard upper, since that is the current extraction/downstream interface issue: whether \(L^2\) EF coherence is strong enough, or whether a stronger transition-to-singular jet/coherence estimate is required.

_Thought for 18s_

## Agent 2 — EF coherence strength needed for the singular-discard upper

I read the update as changing Agent 2’s task: extraction is no longer the hard part. The active question is whether the singular-discard forbidden upper follows from the same EF-completion estimates needed for \(L^2\)-Gate–1, or whether it needs a strictly stronger transition-to-singular coherence theorem. The update explicitly identifies this as a hidden risk: \(L^2\)-completion may be too weak for the \(\tr\to\sing\) discarded-channel upper. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}\text{ is not automatically strong enough for the singular-discard upper.}
}
\]

The forbidden upper needs a stronger estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{\tr\to\sing\text{-jet/projection}}
}
\]

or, more invariantly,

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)\|^2
\text{ must be small in the local plaquette norm.}
}
\]

So the singular-discard route remains viable, but the upper is not free from the \(L^2\)-Gate–1 residual theorem.

---

# 1. The two residual estimates are different

For Gate–1, the needed residual theorem is roughly

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\obs}\vee W_{\ref})}^2
\le
Q^{-A}\|F_X\|_{L^2(W_{\ref})}^2,
\]

where

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X,
\qquad
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}.
\]

This controls the scalar source in an \(L^2\) observation norm.

But the singular-discard upper needs

\[
\sum_p
\left\|
(I-P_{\sing,p})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})
\right\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If \(F_X\) is constructed so that

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)\in R_{\sing},
\]

then

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
=
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

Thus the required upper is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left\|
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is stronger than plain

\[
\sum_p \|R_X\|_{L^2(I_p)}^2\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

unless the operator

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}
\]

is uniformly bounded from the \(L^2\) residual norm to the discarded singular norm with only polynomial loss.

---

# 2. Exact operator condition needed

Define

\[
\mathcal L_p
:=
(I-P_{\sing,p})T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}.
\]

Then the singular-discard upper follows from \(L^2\)-EF coherence if we have a uniform operator bound

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p\|\mathcal L_p f\|^2
\le Q^C
\|f\|_{L^2(W_{\tr\to\sing})}^2
}
\]

for all relevant residuals \(f\), together with

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\tr\to\sing})}^2
\le Q^{-B-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

If \(\mathcal L_p\) contains derivatives, jet extraction, singular rescaling, or high-mode tests, this operator norm may be much larger than polynomial unless \(R_X\) has extra smoothness/bandwidth control.

So the needed strengthening is:

\[
\boxed{
R_X\text{ must be small not only in }L^2,\text{ but in the norm seen by }\mathcal L_p.
}
\]

---

# 3. When \(L^2\)-coherence is sufficient

Plain \(L^2\)-coherence is enough if the transition-to-singular discarded operator is \(L^2\)-bounded:

\[
\|\mathcal L_p f\|
\le Q^C\|f\|_{L^2(I_p)}
\]

with polynomial \(Q^C\), uniformly over plaquettes.

This is plausible if:

1. \(J_{\tr,p}\) is not a high-order point jet but an \(L^2\)-localized transition representative;

2. \(T_{\tr\to\sing,p}\) is a bounded \(L^2\)-chart map;

3. \(I-P_{\sing,p}\) is an orthogonal projection or uniformly bounded projection.

In that case, singular-discard upper is just a corollary of stronger residual smallness:

\[
\|R_X\|_{L^2(W_{\tr\to\sing})}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

Then the same partial EF residual theorem can serve both:

\[
\mathrm{Agg}L^2(q''_{\can})
\]

and

\[
\mathrm{ForbiddenSingularDiscardUpper}.
\]

This is the good case.

---

# 4. When \(L^2\)-coherence is not sufficient

If

\[
J_{\tr,p}
\]

contains pointwise derivatives or finite jets, then \(L^2\) control is not enough. A residual \(R_X\) can be small in \(L^2\) but large in a derivative/jet norm.

Then the upper needs a Sobolev/bandlimited residual estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\mathcal L_p R_X\|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

proved directly from the explicit-formula tail, not inferred from \(L^2\).

A sufficient condition would be

\[
\|R_X\|_{H^s(W_{\tr\to\sing})}^2
\le Q^{-B_s}\mu(\mathcal C)
\]

for \(s\) at least the order of the transition/singular jet map.

But this may be much harder than the \(L^2\) residual estimate.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{singular-discard upper strongly prefers }J_{\tr}\text{ to be an }L^2\text{-localized representative, not a point jet.}
}
\]

That is a design constraint.

---

# 5. Defining \(R_{\sing}\) to make the upper true

The retained singular space should be

\[
R_{\sing}
=
\overline{
\{T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta):\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}
}
\]

or a finite-dimensional approximation thereof, where \(\Theta_{\arith}\) is the actual finite EF parameter family, not arbitrary coefficients.

Then for the actual coherent approximation \(F_X\),

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X)=0
\]

or is \(Q^{-B}\)-small.

Therefore the upper reduces exactly to the residual term:

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X).
\]

This is the right construction. It prevents \(P_{\sing}\) from being arbitrary, but also prevents it from being jet-complete.

---

# 6. D4 visibility after defining \(R_{\sing}\)

With this choice,

\[
R_{\sing}
\]

contains actual EF-coherent variations but need not contain formal D4 carrier directions.

The finite rank test is:

\[
K_{\D4}
=
(I-P_{\sing})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}.
\]

The route succeeds only if

\[
\operatorname{rank}K_{\D4}\ge1.
\]

But one must be careful: if \(R_{\sing}\) includes all first-order variations of \(F_X^\theta\) under the allowed EF parameters \(\theta\), then the tangent space is

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}_j
\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

The D4 rank should be computed modulo this tangent space, not merely modulo the zeroth-order model space.

The correct rank test is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}
L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1.
}
\]

This is stronger and more invariant than projection onto a fixed \(R_{\sing}\).

---

# 7. \(\alpha\)-admission risk

If \(\alpha\) is included among the actual EF parameters \(\theta\), then

\[
\partial_\alpha F_X^\theta
\]

belongs to the tangent space.

This may absorb part of the D4 live plane.

Therefore the downstream rank test depends on whether \(\alpha\) is admitted into the actual-source manifold.

The dangerous case is:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_\alpha F_X^\theta,\partial_mF_X^\theta,\partial_\lambda F_X^\theta,\ldots\}.
\]

If this happens, the best rank-one D4 channel becomes tangent and disappears.

So the \(\alpha\)-twist used for O2/O3 can weaken the D4 normality theorem.

This is a genuine cross-thread coupling.

---

# 8. Preferred theorem formulation

The singular-discard upper and D4 lower should be combined geometrically.

Let

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
=
\{T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(F_X^\theta):\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}.
\]

Assume actual rows satisfy

\[
\sum_p
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can}),
\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}
\right)^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the upper.

Assume D4 normality:

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\left(
(I-P_{T_u\mathcal M_{\sing,p}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
E_{\car,p}^{\tr}
L_{\clean,p}^{\tr}
\right)
\ge1.
\]

This is the lower.

Then extraction plus local D4 gives a contradiction.

This avoids an arbitrary choice of \(P_{\sing}\) and makes the theorem coordinate-invariant.

---

# 9. The first finite computation: \(Y12\) normality

The most promising rank-one test remains:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\neq0.
}
\]

Equivalently, using an order-nine connection residual

\[
\mathcal K_9,
\]

test

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right)
\neq0.
}
\]

If this is nonzero, then the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) D4 channel gives a clean rank-one forbidden direction.

If it is zero, test \([\mathsf C_7]\). But \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is the best first target because it naturally contributes at order nine without needing to be generated by order-seven source variation.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 conclusion

The singular-discard upper is not automatically supplied by \(L^2\)-EF coherence. It requires EF coherence in the norm induced by

\[
(I-P_{\sing})T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}.
\]

The best invariant formulation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{actual rows lie close to an arithmetic singular coherence manifold }
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith},
}
\]

while

\[
\boxed{
\text{D4 live carriers have nonzero normal projection to this manifold.}
}
\]

The decisive finite computation is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ tangent or normal to }
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}?
}
\]

If normal, the singular-discard \(\Lambda\) route is real. If tangent, finite D4 remains local visibility only.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: tangent-space audit for the \(Y12\) channel

The current best downstream formulation is:

\[
\text{actual rows lie near }\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith},
\]

while D4 should push normally to that manifold:

\[
(I-P_{T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}
\neq 0.
\]

The most promising rank-one test is:

\[
(I-P_{T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}})
T_{\tr\to\sing}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\neq0.
\]

I now pushed this by analyzing what tangent directions can plausibly absorb the \(Y12\) carrier.

---

# 1. What is the tangent space?

Let the finite EF-coherent model be

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta,
\]

transported into the singular chart:

\[
u^\theta_{\sing}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{u^\theta_{\sing}:\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}.
\]

At the actual source \(u=u^{\theta_0}_{\sing}\),

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\partial_{\theta_j}u^\theta_{\sing}\big|_{\theta=\theta_0}
\right\}.
\]

The allowed parameters \(\theta\) matter. Likely candidates are:

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \alpha\text{ if admitted},\quad
\text{cutoff/regularization parameters},\quad
\text{finite EF amplitude parameters constrained by actual arithmetic}.
\]

But **not** arbitrary local coefficients. If arbitrary prime/dual coefficients are allowed, the tangent space becomes too large and will swallow D4.

So the downstream theorem depends critically on a narrow definition:

\[
\boxed{
\Theta_{\arith}
=
\text{actual global EF parameters only, not arbitrary packet amplitudes.}
}
\]

---

# 2. The dangerous tangent directions

The D4 carrier

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is an order-nine mixed carrier. It can be tangent only if some allowed EF parameter derivative creates the same singular order-nine residual.

The dangerous tangent directions are:

## A. \(m\)-translation

\[
\partial_m u_{\sing}
\]

raises singular order by one through differentiation.

A translation derivative of a lower-order singular coefficient can create order-nine terms. So one must check whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m u_{\sing},\partial_m^2u_{\sing},\ldots\}
\]

under the allowed finite jet depth.

However, \(m\)-translation derivatives are not free parameters if \(m\) is fixed at the zero center. The tangent vector \(\partial_m u\) is a genuine source-family tangent only if the manifold includes nearby centers as the same coherence family. It probably does.

So \(m\)-translation must be included in the tangent space.

## B. Scale \(\lambda\)-variation

\[
\partial_\lambda
\left[
\lambda^3q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\right]
=
3\lambda^2q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
+
\lambda^3 x q'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

This mixes source orders and may create order-nine singular components. Thus \(\lambda\)-variation may also absorb some order-nine carriers.

## C. \(\alpha\)-tilt

If \(\alpha\) is admitted,

\[
\partial_\alpha F_X^\theta
\]

changes plus/dual branch weights and phases. This can create mixed plus/dual carrier directions. Therefore it may be particularly dangerous for \(Y12\).

If

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in \operatorname{span}\{\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}\}
\]

or in the span of \(\partial_\alpha,\partial_m,\partial_\lambda\) directions, then the rank-one \(Y12\) channel disappears.

Thus \(\alpha\) may help O2/O3 but hurt downstream normality.

## D. Cutoff/regularization variation

Changing \(X\), smoothing cutoffs, or regularization parameters may create high-order tail-like singular components. If these parameters are included in \(\Theta_{\arith}\), they can enlarge the tangent space drastically.

For downstream normality, cutoff/regularization parameters should probably be fixed, not treated as free source parameters.

Otherwise the tangent manifold may absorb D4.

---

# 3. Order-nine connection residual as tangent annihilator

The clean way to test tangency is to construct a residual functional

\[
\mathcal K_9
\]

that annihilates all tangent directions:

\[
\mathcal K_9
\left(
\partial_{\theta_j}u^\theta_{\sing}
\right)=0
\quad
\text{for all allowed }\theta_j.
\]

Then D4 normality is simply:

\[
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)\neq0.
\]

The residual has schematic form:

\[
\mathcal K_9(s)
=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(s_{<7}).
\]

This says: actual EF-coherent singular data have their order-nine coefficient determined by lower-order singular data and source parameters.

The \(Y12\) carrier should be normal if it changes \(s_9\) in a way not induced by lower-order variations.

---

# 4. Why \(Y12\) is the best channel

The clean D4 order-nine term contains

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal K_9([\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0,
\]

then the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel alone gives a forbidden rank-one direction. We do not need raw two-dimensional anisotropy.

By contrast, the order-seven carrier

\[
[\mathsf C_7]
\]

may plausibly be tangent because actual EF singular data can vary at order seven under \(m,\lambda,\alpha\) or lower source parameters.

So the first test should be \(Y12\), not \(C_7\).

---

# 5. Tangency no-go if \(Y12\) is a second derivative of allowed deformation

There is one serious possible no-go.

If the \(Y12\) carrier is exactly the second variation of the actual EF source family under two allowed parameters, for example

\[
\partial_{\theta_a}\partial_{\theta_b}u^\theta_{\sing},
\]

then it may belong to the second-order tangent/curvature of the manifold.

For a linear tangent-space test, second-order variations are not tangent. But if the actual row is allowed to move nonlinearly by a finite amount, a second-order displacement can be absorbed by moving along the manifold with parameter size \(\sqrt{\varepsilon}\).

This is a tubular-radius issue.

D4 produces a small displacement of size roughly

\[
\varepsilon.
\]

If manifold curvature is \(Q^C\), then second-order absorption is of size

\[
Q^C|\delta\theta|^2.
\]

Normality gives a contradiction only if the D4 displacement exceeds the manifold's quadratic absorption scale.

So one needs a polynomial tubular theorem:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u+D,\mathcal M)
\ge
\|(I-P_T)D\|
-
Q^C\|D\|^2.
\]

As long as

\[
\|D\|\le Q^{-C'}
\]

with \(C'\) large enough, the linear normal component dominates. If \(D\) is too large, nonlinear absorption could matter.

Thus D4 normality requires both:

\[
\boxed{
\text{linear normal rank}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{polynomial tubular radius}.
}
\]

---

# 6. How \(\alpha\)-admission changes the tangent test

If \(\alpha\) is used for determinant branch survival, we must decide whether \(\alpha\) belongs to \(\Theta_{\arith}\).

## If \(\alpha\) is an averaging/proof parameter only

Then \(\partial_\alpha u\) may not be tangent to the fixed-\(\alpha\) actual-source manifold. D4 normality is less threatened.

But then \(\alpha\)-averaged determinant survival needs a separate specialization/persistence theorem.

## If \(\alpha\) is admitted as a genuine source-family parameter

Then \(\partial_\alpha u\in T_u\mathcal M\). This may enlarge the tangent space and potentially absorb \(Y12\).

So there is a tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-admissibility helps O2/O3 survival but may hurt D4 normality.}
}
\]

The exact audit:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(\partial_\alpha u_{\sing})=0?
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0?
}
\]

If both hold, \(\alpha\) is safe. If the second vector lies in the span generated by \(\partial_\alpha u\), \(\alpha\) kills the channel.

---

# 7. Proposed finite theorem: \(Y12\)-normality

The clean theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Y12Normality}:
\quad
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right)
\neq0
}
\]

where \(\mathcal K_9\) annihilates

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

More quantitatively:

\[
\left|
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}
\]

after normalization.

If this holds, then

\[
K_{\D4}^{\quot}
\]

has rank at least one through the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel.

The forbidden wall factor becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{\forb}
\sim
\frac{
|H|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\left|
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])
\right|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

This is extremely clean.

---

# 8. If \(Y12\)-normality fails

If

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])=0,
\]

then test the full D4 combination:

\[
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}
(
\beta_9[\mathsf C_7]\Gamma_G
+
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
)
\right).
\]

Maybe the combination is normal even if \(Y12\) alone is tangent, but then the forbidden channel depends on a mixed linear form in

\[
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

If both \(C_7\) and \(Y12\) are tangent, then singular-discard D4 is rank zero.

At that point finite D4 likely cannot close through this \(\Lambda\).

---

# 9. Updated downstream rank audit

The rank audit should proceed in this order:

1. Define \(\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\) with allowed parameters \(\theta\).

2. Construct annihilators of \(T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\), especially \(\mathcal K_9\).

3. Compute

   \[
   \mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
   \]

4. If nonzero: rank-one D4 channel found.

5. If zero, compute

   \[
   \mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]).
   \]

6. If at least one nonzero, define the forbidden linear form in \(\Gamma\).

7. If both zero, singular-discard channel fails unless higher-order residuals \(\mathcal K_{11},\mathcal K_{13},\ldots\) detect D4.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 conclusion

The best finite-D4 downstream test has become very specific:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the order-nine }Y12\text{ carrier violate the singular EF connection relation?}
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0?
}
\]

This is the cleanest current opportunity for a rank-one forbidden channel.

The main caveats are:

1. \(\mathcal K_9\) must annihilate the actual EF tangent space, including \(m,\lambda\), and possibly \(\alpha\) if admitted;

2. the actual-source manifold must have polynomial tubular radius;

3. the EF residual must be small in the \(\mathcal K_9\)-induced norm;

4. if \(Y12\) is tangent, test \(C_7\) and then higher singular residuals.

This gives a finite, actionable downstream audit.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: \(Y12\)-normality versus osculating absorption

The last attack reduced the downstream finite-D4 channel to the order-nine residual test

\[
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0.
\]

That is the correct first-order tangent test. But there is a hidden danger:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ may fail to be tangent but still lie in the second-order osculating cone of the actual EF manifold.}
}
\]

If so, D4 might be absorbed by a nonlinear change of actual-source parameters, and the singular-discard contradiction would fail unless the D4 displacement scale is below the tubular radius.

So the right downstream normality theorem is not merely first-order normality. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{\(Y12\) has a polynomially non-small component normal to the actual EF manifold at the D4 scale.}
}
\]

---

# 1. Linear tangent residual

Let singular coefficients be

\[
s=(s_0,s_1,\ldots,s_{R_*})
\]

in the singular chart. The actual EF-coherent source manifold is

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{s(\theta):\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}.
\]

Suppose the order-nine coherence relation is locally

\[
s_9
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}(s_7)
+
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(s_{<7})
+
\mathcal T_9(\theta),
\]

where \(\mathcal T_9\) records explicit allowed parameter dependence not already expressed through lower singular coefficients.

Linearizing at the actual point \(s_0=s(\theta_0)\), a tangent vector \(\delta s\) satisfies

\[
\delta s_9
=
D\mathcal R_{7\to9}(\delta s_7)
+
D\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(\delta s_{<7})
+
D\mathcal T_9(\delta\theta).
\]

So the correct linear residual is

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(\delta s)
=
\delta s_9
-
D\mathcal R_{7\to9}(\delta s_7)
-
D\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(\delta s_{<7})
-
D\mathcal T_9(\delta\theta).
\]

For all tangent vectors,

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(\delta s)=0.
\]

The \(Y12\) test is

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
\]

If \(Y12\) is a pure order-nine injection with no matching lower-order variation, then this should reduce to checking whether its \(s_9\)-component is outside the allowed \(D\mathcal T_9\)-directions.

---

# 2. The osculating-space risk

Even if

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(Y12)\neq0,
\]

the actual manifold may bend toward \(Y12\). For a parameter displacement \(\delta\theta\),

\[
s(\theta_0+\delta\theta)
=
s_0
+
Ds\,\delta\theta
+
\frac12D^2s(\delta\theta,\delta\theta)
+
O(|\delta\theta|^3).
\]

The second-order term

\[
\frac12D^2s(\delta\theta,\delta\theta)
\]

may have a \(Y12\)-like component.

This matters because \(Y12\) itself arises from a quadratic mixed-block mechanism. It is structurally plausible that it corresponds to a second derivative of the source family rather than an arbitrary normal vector.

So first-order normality alone proves only

\[
Y12\notin T_s\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

For contradiction we need something stronger:

\[
Y12
\notin
T_s\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
+
\text{quadratic osculating cone at the relevant scale}.
\]

---

# 3. Tubular estimate needed

Let \(D\) be the D4 displacement in singular coordinates:

\[
D
=
H\,T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma.
\]

Let \(P_T\) be projection onto the tangent space. Suppose

\[
\|(I-P_T)D\|\ge Q^{-A}\|D\|.
\]

A standard tubular estimate gives

\[
\operatorname{dist}(s_0+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
\ge
\|(I-P_T)D\|
-
C_{\mathrm{II}}\|D\|^2,
\]

where \(C_{\mathrm{II}}\) is a bound for the second fundamental form of the manifold.

Thus first-order normality is useful only when

\[
C_{\mathrm{II}}\|D\|^2
\le
\frac12\|(I-P_T)D\|.
\]

If

\[
C_{\mathrm{II}}\le Q^C
\]

and

\[
\|(I-P_T)D\|\ge Q^{-A}\|D\|,
\]

then it is enough that

\[
\|D\|\le Q^{-A-C-10}
\]

after normalization, or more generally that the D4 displacement scale lies inside a polynomial tubular radius.

So the singular-discard theorem needs:

\[
\boxed{
\text{polynomial tubular radius for }\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
}
\]

This is a finite geometric condition on the EF parameterization.

---

# 4. When osculating absorption is harmless

Osculating absorption is harmless if the actual source manifold is parameterized by **linear EF coefficients fixed by arithmetic**, not by free local amplitudes.

If the allowed parameters are only

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{ cutoff/regularization scales}
\]

with dimension \(O(1)\) or \(Q^{o(1)}\), then the second-order osculating cone is small. A generic \(Y12\)-carrier will not lie in it.

But if the allowed parameters include many independent prime/dual coefficients, then the manifold becomes high-dimensional and may absorb \(Y12\). That would destroy the forbidden channel.

Thus we need the actual-source manifold to be **arithmetic-parametric**, not packet-universal:

\[
\boxed{
\Theta_{\arith}\text{ must not include arbitrary local AFE amplitudes.}
}
\]

This is the same “avoid universal EF atom span” trap in tangent form.

---

# 5. \(Y12\) as second fundamental form: good or bad?

There is a subtle possibility:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

may actually equal a second fundamental form component of the actual-source manifold. That is,

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\sim
\mathrm{II}(\xi_1,\xi_2)
\]

for some tangent directions \(\xi_1,\xi_2\).

If so, \(Y12\) is not tangent, but it is osculating.

Does that kill the argument?

Not necessarily. A second fundamental direction is still normal at first order. If D4 produces a displacement of order \(\varepsilon\), while movement along the manifold producing that second-order displacement requires parameters of size \(\sqrt{\varepsilon}\), the first-order tangent displacement \(Ds\,\delta\theta\) would also appear and must be canceled. Unless the proof allows simultaneous tangent cancellation, the normal component remains detectable.

But to be rigorous, the forbidden channel should project not merely away from \(T_s\mathcal M\), but measure the distance to \(\mathcal M\) itself:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(s_0+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}).
\]

So the safest formulation is distance-to-manifold, not fixed linear projection.

The linear residual \(\mathcal K_9^{(1)}\) is the first test; the final theorem is nonlinear distance lower bound.

---

# 6. Refined \(Y12\)-normality theorem

The theorem should be stated as follows.

Let

\[
D_{Y12}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

Assume:

1. **Linear normality**

   \[
   \|(I-P_T)D_{Y12}\|
   \ge
   Q^{-A}\|D_{Y12}\|.
   \]

2. **Polynomial tubular radius**

   For all \(D\) in the D4 scale range,

   \[
   \operatorname{dist}(s_0+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
   \ge
   \frac12\|(I-P_T)D\|.
   \]

Then \(Y12\) gives a forbidden rank-one channel.

Equivalently, in residual form:

\[
\left|
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(D_{Y12})
\right|
\ge Q^{-A},
\]

and the nonlinear correction to \(\mathcal K_9\) is \(Q^{-A}\)-smaller at the D4 scale.

---

# 7. How to test this finitely

The finite audit should compute these objects.

## Step 1: tangent generators

List the allowed EF parameters:

\[
\theta_j\in
\{m,\lambda,\alpha?,X?,\text{regularization?},\ldots\}.
\]

Compute

\[
v_j=
\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

These span the tangent space.

## Step 2: order-nine residual

Construct \(\mathcal K_9^{(1)}\) annihilating all \(v_j\).

## Step 3: D4 carriers

Compute

\[
d_7=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr},
\]

\[
d_Y=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

Evaluate

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(d_Y),
\qquad
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(d_7).
\]

## Step 4: tubular bound

Bound second derivatives

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)
\]

in the singular norm.

Need

\[
\|\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}u^\theta\|
\le Q^C.
\]

Then the tubular radius is polynomial.

---

# 8. Interaction with \(\alpha\)

If \(\alpha\) is included as an allowed parameter, add

\[
v_\alpha=
\partial_\alpha T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)
\]

to the tangent set.

Then recompute

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}(d_Y).
\]

If it vanishes only after including \(v_\alpha\), then \(\alpha\)-admissibility kills the \(Y12\) forbidden channel.

This gives a precise tradeoff:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-twist for O2/O3 must be checked against }Y12\text{-normality.}
}
\]

If \(\alpha\) kills \(Y12\), one may still use \(\alpha\) as an averaging tool but not admit it into \(\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\) for the downstream normality theorem. That would require a careful fixed-\(\alpha\) specialization theorem.

---

# 9. If \(Y12\) fails, try higher residuals

If

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=0
\]

and

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_7)=0,
\]

then order-nine singular discard is rank-zero.

But the D4 live carrier may still violate higher singular coherence relations:

\[
\mathcal K_{11},\quad
\mathcal K_{13},\ldots
\]

The next possible channel is

\[
\mathcal K_{11}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
\]

However, using higher residuals moves the downstream D4 channel to higher-order singular projections and may demand stronger EF residual/tail estimates.

So order-nine is best; higher orders are fallback.

---

# 10. Updated conclusion

The finite-D4 singular-discard channel is now reduced to a precise differential-geometric audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Is }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ linearly normal to the actual EF singular manifold, with polynomial tubular radius?}
}
\]

If yes, rank-one downstream D4 works through the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel.

If no, test \(C_7\) and then higher residuals.

The most valuable immediate computation is still:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right).
}
\]

But it must be evaluated after including all allowed actual-source tangent directions, especially \(m,\lambda\), and possibly \(\alpha\) if the proof admits it.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: minimal tangent set for the \(K_9(Y12)\) test

The current finite-D4 downstream question is:

\[
\mathcal K_9^{(1)}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\right)
\stackrel{?}{\neq}0.
\]

But \(\mathcal K_9^{(1)}\) must annihilate the tangent space of the actual EF singular manifold. So the decisive issue is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Which source-family parameters are genuinely allowed tangents?}
}
\]

If the tangent space is too small, \(Y12\) is normal. If it is too large, \(Y12\) may be absorbed and finite D4 becomes invisible.

---

# 1. Split tangent parameters into three classes

The actual-source singular manifold is

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)\}.
\]

The allowed parameter set \(\Theta_{\arith}\) should be split into:

## Class A — structural parameters that must be tangent

These are unavoidable if the manifold represents actual nearby source rows:

\[
m,\qquad \lambda,
\]

and possibly local normalization/scaling parameters forced by the chart.

So

\[
\partial_m u_{\sing},\qquad \partial_\lambda u_{\sing}
\]

must be included in

\[
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}.
\]

Any \(K_9\) residual must annihilate them.

## Class B — fixed construction parameters, probably not tangent

These include cutoff \(X\), smoothing profile, regularization convention, and truncation scheme.

They are chosen by the proof, not varied by the actual zeta source. Therefore they should **not** automatically be tangent directions.

If we include them, the tangent space may become artificially too large.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
X,\ \text{cutoff shape},\ \text{regularization choices}
\text{ should be fixed unless the proof explicitly averages over them.}
}
\]

## Class C — optional deformation parameters

The main one is

\[
\alpha.
\]

If the proof runs over an \(\alpha\)-family, then

\[
\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}
\]

may need to be included. If \(\alpha\) is only an auxiliary analytic trick, it should not be part of the downstream singular EF manifold at fixed target.

This is a critical fork.

---

# 2. Minimal tangent annihilator

The first \(K_9\) test should use the minimal honest tangent space

\[
T_{\min}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{
\partial_m u_{\sing},
\partial_\lambda u_{\sing},
\partial_{\mathrm{norm}}u_{\sing}
\}.
\]

Construct

\[
\mathcal K_{9,\min}^{(1)}
\]

so that

\[
\mathcal K_{9,\min}^{(1)}(v)=0
\qquad
(v\in T_{\min}).
\]

Then test

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_{9,\min}^{(1)}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
}
\]

If nonzero, \(Y12\) is normal to the unavoidable actual-source manifold.

This is the best-case downstream result.

---

# 3. Optional \(\alpha\)-enlarged tangent test

If the proof must admit \(\alpha\) as a genuine family parameter, enlarge the tangent space:

\[
T_{\alpha}
=
T_{\min}
+
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}\}.
\]

Then define

\[
\mathcal K_{9,\alpha}^{(1)}
\]

annihilating \(T_\alpha\).

The key test becomes

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_{9,\alpha}^{(1)}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
}
\]

There are three possible outcomes.

## Outcome 1: nonzero even after \(\alpha\)

Then \(\alpha\)-twisting is compatible with the downstream D4 channel. This is the ideal case.

## Outcome 2: nonzero for \(T_{\min}\), zero for \(T_\alpha\)

Then \(\alpha\)-admission kills the \(Y12\) forbidden channel.

This creates a strategic conflict:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{-twist helps O2/O3 but destroys D4 normality.}
}
\]

The proof must either avoid admitting \(\alpha\) downstream or find another forbidden channel.

## Outcome 3: zero already for \(T_{\min}\)

Then \(Y12\) is tangent even without \(\alpha\). Test \(C_7\), mixed combinations, or higher residuals.

---

# 4. Why \(Y12\) might survive \(m,\lambda\) tangents

The \(m\)- and \(\lambda\)-tangent directions are generated by differentiating the actual scalar source:

\[
\partial_mF_m(x)
=
\lambda^3q'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x),
\]

\[
\partial_\lambda F_m(x)
=
3\lambda^2q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
+
\lambda^3xq'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x).
\]

These are linear differential consequences of the same scalar source.

The \(Y12\) carrier, however, is a promoted quadratic mixed-block remainder:

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
=
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

coupled through same-point whitening and order-nine carrier extraction.

That structure is not obviously a first derivative of \(q''_{\can}\) with respect to \(m\) or \(\lambda\).

So there is a plausible reason

\[
Y12\notin T_{\min}.
\]

This is encouraging.

---

# 5. Why \(\alpha\) is more dangerous

The \(\alpha\)-derivative changes the relative plus/dual amplitudes and phases. Since \(Y12\) is mixed-block in nature, it may resemble a second-order branch-mixing response.

A first \(\alpha\)-derivative may not equal \(Y12\), but if the EF manifold includes nonlinear \(\alpha\)-variation, its second-order osculating directions may include \(Y12\)-like terms.

Thus even if

\[
Y12\notin T_\alpha,
\]

one must still check the second fundamental form involving \(\alpha\):

\[
\mathrm{II}(\partial_\alpha,\partial_\alpha),
\qquad
\mathrm{II}(\partial_\alpha,\partial_m),
\qquad
\mathrm{II}(\partial_\alpha,\partial_\lambda).
\]

This is the deepest \(\alpha\)-D4 interaction.

---

# 6. Refined tubular theorem with optional \(\alpha\)

Let

\[
T
\]

be either \(T_{\min}\) or \(T_\alpha\). Suppose

\[
D_{Y}
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Assume linear normality:

\[
\|(I-P_T)D_Y\|
\ge Q^{-A}\|D_Y\|.
\]

Let

\[
\mathrm{II}_T
\]

be the second fundamental form of the corresponding EF manifold. If

\[
\|\mathrm{II}_T\|\le Q^C
\]

and the D4 displacement scale \(\epsilon\) satisfies

\[
Q^C\epsilon^2
\le
\frac12Q^{-A}\epsilon,
\]

then

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u+\epsilon D_Y,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
\ge
\frac12Q^{-A}\epsilon.
\]

So the actual theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{linear }Y12\text{-normality}
+
\text{polynomial tubular bound}
\Longrightarrow
\text{forbidden singular-discard lower bound.}
}
\]

This makes nonlinear osculating absorption explicit.

---

# 7. Finite residual hierarchy

If \(K_9\) fails, do not immediately abandon finite D4. Move up the residual hierarchy.

Define residuals

\[
\mathcal K_9,\quad
\mathcal K_{11},\quad
\mathcal K_{13},\ldots
\]

where \(\mathcal K_{2r+1}\) measures whether the order-\((2r+1)\) singular coefficient is determined by lower coherent EF data.

D4 may be tangent at order \(9\) but normal at order \(11\). However, higher residuals require:

1. stronger singular EF residual estimates;
2. higher-order carrier transition data;
3. possibly higher D4 algebra.

So the priority order remains:

\[
\boxed{
K_9(Y12)\text{ first;}
}
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
K_9(C_7);}
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
K_{11}(Y12)\text{ as fallback.}
}
\]

---

# 8. A concrete linear algebra formulation

Let singular coefficient coordinates be split as

\[
s=(s_{<9},s_9,s_{>9}).
\]

Let tangent generators form a matrix

\[
T=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_{<9}\\
T_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Let the \(Y12\) vector be

\[
d_Y=
\begin{pmatrix}
d_{Y,<9}\\
d_{Y,9}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The order-nine residual annihilating tangents is equivalent to a row vector

\[
k_9=(k_{<9},1)
\]

such that

\[
k_{<9}T_{<9}+T_9=0.
\]

This is solvable if \(T_{<9}\) has enough rank to express \(T_9\) over lower coordinates.

Then

\[
K_9(d_Y)=k_{<9}d_{Y,<9}+d_{Y,9}.
\]

If \(d_Y\) is pure order-nine, then

\[
d_{Y,<9}=0,
\]

and

\[
K_9(d_Y)=d_{Y,9}.
\]

So if the \(Y12\) carrier truly has no lower-order singular components, it is automatically \(K_9\)-normal unless its \(s_9\)-component lies in optional parameter directions included directly in \(T_9\).

This is an excellent simplification.

Thus a key finite question is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ has lower-order singular components or is pure order-nine?}
}
\]

If pure order-nine, \(Y12\)-normality is very likely.

---

# 9. What could make \(Y12\) non-pure?

Transition to singular coordinates may mix orders. A transition map could send transition order-nine data into a combination of singular orders:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
=
d_{<9}+d_9+d_{>9}.
\]

If \(d_{<9}\neq0\), then \(K_9(d_Y)\) may cancel:

\[
d_9
=
-\mathcal R(d_{<9}).
\]

So the finite transition map matters. If it is order-preserving up to lower triangular terms, then \(Y12\) might have lower-order leakage. If it is order-filtered, \(Y12\) stays pure.

Therefore another finite audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{order filtration of }T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ on }[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
}
\]

If transition is upper triangular by singular order and preserves leading order, then the leading order-nine component should survive the residual.

---

# 10. Updated action target

The next finite computation should output four items:

1. The singular order decomposition:

   \[
   T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
   =
   d_{<9}+d_9+d_{>9}.
   \]

2. The tangent generator matrix for allowed parameters:

   \[
   T_{\min}
   \quad\text{and optionally}\quad
   T_\alpha.
   \]

3. The residual row \(k_9\) annihilating tangents.

4. The scalar

   \[
   k_9\cdot d_Y.
   \]

If

\[
|k_9\cdot d_Y|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

The \(Y12\)-normality problem has become a concrete finite singular-order calculation.

The key simplifying possibility is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ is pure order-nine in singular coordinates.}
}
\]

If so, and if allowed EF tangents determine \(s_9\) from lower orders, then \(Y12\) should be normal almost automatically.

The main dangers are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{transition lower-order mixing cancels the residual;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{ or other optional parameters add direct order-nine tangents;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{second-order osculating absorption at D4 scale.}
}
\]

But the finite audit is now sharply specified.
