## Agent 2 — covariant construction of \(\mathcal K_9\) and finite rank audit

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: construct \(\mathcal K_9\) as a **covariant singular connection residual**, not a naive Taylor recurrence, and use it to test \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) and \([\mathsf C_7]\). The prompt’s target is the rank-one downstream channel

\[
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0
\]

modulo legitimate actual-source parameter tangents and lower-order connection effects. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9\text{ should be defined as a Schur-complement normal covector to the actual EF tangent space.}
}
\]

With that definition:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\text{ gives a rank-one D4 channel if its singular order-nine component is not generated by allowed parameter tangents or lower-order connection.}
}
\]

The strongest current result is conditional but sharp:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ is pure order-nine with }Y12\text{-signature, then }\mathcal K_9(Y12)\neq0.
}
\]

The remaining finite audit is whether transition-to-singular coordinates introduce lower-order pieces or whether optional parameters, especially \(\alpha\), create a tangent with the same \(Y12\)-signature.

---

# 1. Singular coordinates through order nine

Let the singular coefficient vector be decomposed as

\[
s=(s_{<7},s_7,s_9),
\]

where:

\[
s_{<7}
\]

collects all singular coherent coefficients of order below seven,

\[
s_7\in \mathcal Y_7
\]

is the order-seven carrier coordinate, and

\[
s_9\in \mathcal Y_9
\]

is the order-nine carrier/residual coordinate.

For an actual finite EF source

\[
F_X^\theta=B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta,
\]

transported from transition to singular coordinates, define

\[
s(\theta)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

The actual EF singular manifold is

\[
\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
=
\{s(\theta):\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}.
\]

The tangent matrix is

\[
T_p
=
\left[
\partial_{\theta_j}s(\theta)
\right]_{\theta=\theta_0}.
\]

Split it compatibly:

\[
T_p=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_{<7}\\
T_7\\
T_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The legitimate parameter set should include

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \text{mandatory chart/normalization parameters},
\]

and include \(\alpha\) only if the proof genuinely runs over an \(\alpha\)-family. It should not include arbitrary prime/dual coefficients or arbitrary cutoff perturbations; including those would make the manifold packet-universal and likely swallow D4.

---

# 2. Schur-complement construction of \(\mathcal K_9\)

The covariant residual is a row functional

\[
\mathcal K_9=(K_{<7},K_7,I_9)
\]

on

\[
\mathcal Y_{<7}\oplus\mathcal Y_7\oplus\mathcal Y_9
\]

such that

\[
\mathcal K_9 T_p=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
K_{<7}T_{<7}+K_7T_7+T_9=0.
\]

This is the finite Schur-complement normal covector to the actual EF tangent space, normalized to have identity coefficient on the chosen forbidden component of \(s_9\).

In scalar notation, this has the schematic form

\[
\mathcal K_9(s;p)
=
s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}(p)s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(p)s_{<7},
\]

but the correct object is the covariant differential:

\[
D_s\mathcal K_9(\delta s)+D_p\mathcal K_9(\delta p)=0
\]

for every legitimate actual-source parameter tangent.

The \(p\)-dependence is essential. Without it, ordinary variation of the singular connection coefficients would produce false positives.

---

# 3. Covariance check

For an allowed parameter \(p_j\),

\[
\delta s=\partial_{p_j}s.
\]

Covariance requires

\[
D_s\mathcal K_9(\partial_{p_j}s)
+
D_{p_j}\mathcal K_9
=
0.
\]

Using

\[
\mathcal K_9=s_9-\mathcal R_{7\to9}(p)s_7-\mathcal R_{<7\to9}(p)s_{<7},
\]

this expands to

\[
\partial_{p_j}s_9
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}\partial_{p_j}s_7
-
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}\partial_{p_j}s_{<7}
\]

\[
-
(\partial_{p_j}\mathcal R_{7\to9})s_7
-
(\partial_{p_j}\mathcal R_{<7\to9})s_{<7}
=
0.
\]

This is the correct connection-residual identity for actual EF-coherent singular data.

So the residual must annihilate not merely fixed-coordinate tangent vectors, but also the change of the singular connection itself.

---

# 4. Application to \(Y12\)

Let

\[
d_Y
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}.
\]

Decompose

\[
d_Y=(d_{Y,<7},d_{Y,7},d_{Y,9}).
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)
=
d_{Y,9}
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}d_{Y,7}
-
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}d_{Y,<7}.
\]

The cleanest case is:

\[
d_{Y,<7}=0,\qquad d_{Y,7}=0,\qquad d_{Y,9}\neq0.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=d_{Y,9}\neq0.
}
\]

This is why \(Y12\) is the preferred D4 carrier: it is expected to be a promoted order-nine mixed-block carrier with no order-seven antecedent.

The main finite risks are:

\[
d_{Y,<7}\neq0
\]

or

\[
d_{Y,7}\neq0
\]

through transition-to-singular lower-order mixing, allowing cancellation

\[
d_{Y,9}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}d_{Y,7}
+
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}d_{Y,<7}.
\]

Thus the key finite audit is the singular-order filtration of

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

---

# 5. Application to \([\mathsf C_7]\)

Let

\[
d_7
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]^{\tr}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_7)
=
d_{7,9}
-
\mathcal R_{7\to9}d_{7,7}
-
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}d_{7,<7}.
\]

If \([\mathsf C_7]\) is an actual coherent order-seven carrier, then it may satisfy

\[
d_{7,9}
=
\mathcal R_{7\to9}d_{7,7}
+
\mathcal R_{<7\to9}d_{7,<7},
\]

and hence

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_7)=0.
\]

That is not a problem. The desired rank-one channel is through \(Y12\), not necessarily through \(C_7\).

If \(\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=0\) but \(\mathcal K_9(d_7)\neq0\), then the forbidden linear form would involve \(\Gamma_G\) rather than \(\Gamma_{G,2}\). But this is a fallback.

---

# 6. D4 rank consequence

The clean D4 contribution in the transition chart is triangular:

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7 & 0\\
\beta_9 & \alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
c_7\alpha_9\neq0.
\]

The order-nine D4 displacement seen by \(\mathcal K_9\) is

\[
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}^{\tr}L_{\clean}^{\tr}\Gamma
\right)
\]

\[
=
H\left[
\Gamma_G
\left(
\beta_9\,\mathcal K_9(d_Y)
+
c_7\,\mathcal K_9(d_7)
\right)
+
\Gamma_{G,2}
\left(
\alpha_9\,\mathcal K_9(d_Y)
\right)
\right].
\]

Therefore:

## If \(\mathcal K_9(d_Y)\neq0\)

Then the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) coefficient is nonzero:

\[
\alpha_9\mathcal K_9(d_Y)\neq0.
\]

So we get a rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

The wall factor becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
\asymp
\frac{
|H|^2
|\alpha_9\mathcal K_9(d_Y)|^2
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
},
\]

possibly plus the \(\Gamma_G\)-mixed term.

## If \(\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=0\) but \(\mathcal K_9(d_7)\neq0\)

Then the channel is through \(\Gamma_G\):

\[
Hc_7\mathcal K_9(d_7)\Gamma_G.
\]

## If both vanish

The order-nine residual does not see finite D4. One must try higher residuals such as

\[
\mathcal K_{11}
\]

or another forbidden channel.

---

# 7. Effect of \(\alpha\)-admission

If \(\alpha\) is part of \(\Theta_{\arith}\), then

\[
\partial_\alpha s
\]

is included in \(T_p\), and \(\mathcal K_9\) must annihilate it.

This can alter the Schur-complement coefficients

\[
\mathcal R_{7\to9},\quad
\mathcal R_{<7\to9},
\]

or add a direct parameter tangent in \(\mathcal Y_9\).

The audit must compare:

\[
\mathcal K_{9,0}(d_Y)
\]

using the fixed-\(\alpha\) tangent space, and

\[
\mathcal K_{9,\alpha}(d_Y)
\]

using the enlarged \(\alpha\)-tangent space.

There are three cases:

1. \(\mathcal K_{9,\alpha}(d_Y)\neq0\): \(\alpha\)-twisting is compatible with D4.

2. \(\mathcal K_{9,0}(d_Y)\neq0\) but \(\mathcal K_{9,\alpha}(d_Y)=0\): \(\alpha\) kills the \(Y12\) channel.

3. \(\mathcal K_{9,0}(d_Y)=0\): \(Y12\) is tangent even at fixed \(\alpha\).

This is now the precise \(\alpha\)-D4 compatibility test.

---

# 8. Signature shortcut

A full coordinate computation may be avoidable if there is a signature projection

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

such that

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\left(
T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7}
\right)=0,
\]

but

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}d_Y\neq0.
\]

The relevant \(Y12\)-signature is:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
\left(
\text{quadratic source-Hessian},
\text{mixed branch},
\text{same-point},
\text{one whitening slot},
\text{reflection/source-grade parity}
\right).
\]

Legitimate tangents

\[
\partial_m,\quad \partial_\lambda,\quad \partial_\alpha
\]

are first-order EF deformations unless the parameter changes the nonlinear source map itself. They should not carry the quadratic Hessian/same-point whitening signature.

If this signature separation is valid, then

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)\neq0
\]

follows immediately.

This is probably the fastest proof route.

---

# 9. Required tubular condition

The residual detects linear normality. To get distance from the actual nonlinear EF manifold, need a polynomial tubular estimate:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u+D,\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith})
\ge
\|(I-P_T)D\|
-
Q^C\|D\|^2.
\]

In \(\mathcal K_9\)-coordinates, this means:

\[
|\mathcal K_9(u+D)|
\ge
|\mathcal K_9(D)|
-
Q^C\|D\|^2
-
\text{EF residual error}.
\]

Thus D4 normality is valid at the D4 displacement scale if

\[
Q^C\|D\|^2
\ll
|\mathcal K_9(D)|.
\]

This condition must be included in the final NormalD4 theorem.

---

# 10. Final theorem statement

The order-nine D4 channel is valid if the following hold.

## Covariant residual

There exists a covariant residual

\[
\mathcal K_9
\]

annihilating the actual EF tangent space:

\[
D_s\mathcal K_9(\delta s)+D_p\mathcal K_9(\delta p)=0.
\]

## \(Y12\)-normality

\[
\left|
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\right|
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

## EF residual upper

\[
\sum_p
\left|
\mathcal K_{9,p}
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## Tubular radius

\[
\|\mathrm{II}_{\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}}\|\le Q^C.
\]

Then finite D4 produces a rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

The construction of \(\mathcal K_9\) is now reduced to finite Schur-complement linear algebra against the actual-source tangent matrix.

The decisive computation is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal K_9
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
}
\]

The best proof route is signature separation: show that the \(Y12\) carrier has a quadratic mixed-Hessian same-point whitening signature absent from all legitimate actual-source tangents and lower-order connection images.

If that succeeds, finite D4 has a clean rank-one forbidden channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

## Agent 2 — continued attack: conditioning of the \(K_9\) Schur residual

The current downstream target is:

\[
\mathcal K_9\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0.
\]

The last step defined \(\mathcal K_9\) as a Schur-complement normal covector annihilating the actual-source tangent space. I now attack the next issue:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Even if }Y12\text{ is formally normal, is the residual }\mathcal K_9\text{ polynomially conditioned?}
}
\]

If \(\mathcal K_9\) has super-polynomial coefficients, the rank-one channel is unusable. It would turn a nonzero residual into a numerically meaningless obstruction.

---

# 1. Tangent matrix formulation

Write singular coordinates through order nine as

\[
s=(s_L,s_9),
\]

where

\[
s_L=(s_{<7},s_7)
\]

collects all lower and order-seven coherent coordinates, and \(s_9\) is the order-nine coordinate.

Let the tangent matrix be

\[
T=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_L\\
T_9
\end{pmatrix},
\]

where columns are allowed actual-source tangents:

\[
\partial_{\theta_j}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

A residual row of the form

\[
\mathcal K_9(s)=s_9-Rs_L
\]

annihilates tangents if

\[
T_9=R T_L.
\]

Thus \(R\) is obtained by solving a finite regression/interpolation problem:

\[
R=T_9T_L^\dagger
\]

on the tangent span, or by choosing any \(R\) satisfying \(RT_L=T_9\).

The conditioning issue is:

\[
\boxed{
\|R\|\le Q^A?
}
\]

If \(\|R\|\) is super-polynomial, then \(\mathcal K_9\) is not a usable forbidden functional.

---

# 2. The real condition: lower-order tangent observability

A polynomially conditioned \(\mathcal K_9\) exists if the lower-coordinate projection

\[
T_L
\]

is well-conditioned on the actual-source tangent space.

More precisely, for every tangent vector \(v=T a\), one needs

\[
\|T_La\|
\ge
Q^{-A}\|Ta\|.
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\text{no allowed actual-source tangent is almost purely order-nine.}
}
\]

If there exists a parameter tangent with

\[
T_La\approx0,\qquad T_9a\neq0,
\]

then order-nine is an independent tangent direction. In that case \(\mathcal K_9\) must annihilate it, forcing the residual to lose sensitivity to pure order-nine carriers. This could kill \(Y12\).

So the \(K_9\) route needs:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LowerOrderTangentObservability}:
\quad
\|T_La\|\ge Q^{-A}\|T_9a\|
\quad\text{for all allowed }a.
}
\]

This is a finite tangent-space theorem.

---

# 3. Why this condition is plausible

Allowed actual-source parameters are expected to move the singular source coherently. Variations in \(m\), \(\lambda\), and normalization typically affect low and intermediate singular coefficients before they affect order nine.

For example:

\[
\partial_m F_m(x)=\lambda^3q'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\]

changes the whole local expansion, not only \(s_9\).

Similarly:

\[
\partial_\lambda F_m(x)
=
3\lambda^2q''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
+
\lambda^3xq'''_{\can}(m+\lambda x)
\]

also changes lower singular modes.

Thus it is plausible that no structural parameter produces a pure order-nine tangent.

The dangerous exception is \(\alpha\): if \(\partial_\alpha\) changes a high-order mixed branch term with little lower-order footprint, it could become order-nine-like. That is another way \(\alpha\) may kill \(Y12\).

---

# 4. \(Y12\)-normality after conditioning

Let

\[
d_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
=
(d_{Y,L},d_{Y,9}).
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=d_{Y,9}-R d_{Y,L}.
\]

There are three cases.

## Case A: \(d_{Y,L}=0\)

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)=d_{Y,9}.
\]

If

\[
d_{Y,9}\neq0,
\]

we have a clean channel, and conditioning of \(R\) is irrelevant for \(Y12\) itself.

## Case B: \(d_{Y,L}\neq0\), but lower-order piece has different signature

If

\[
R d_{Y,L}
\]

lies in a different signature sector from

\[
d_{Y,9},
\]

then cancellation is impossible. Signature separation again proves normality.

## Case C: possible cancellation

If

\[
d_{Y,9}\approx R d_{Y,L},
\]

then \(Y12\) is coherent with the actual-source order-nine connection and the \(K_9\) channel fails.

Thus the finite computation should not merely test whether \(d_{Y,9}\neq0\). It must test whether

\[
d_{Y,9}
\notin
R\operatorname{span}(d_{Y,L})
\]

in the relevant signature/quotient.

---

# 5. Avoiding coordinate artifacts

The residual

\[
s_9-Rs_L
\]

depends on the chosen splitting into lower and order-nine coordinates. A bad coordinate choice could create artificial large \(R\).

The invariant formulation is:

\[
\mathcal Q_9^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9 /
\pi_9(T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}\cap \ker \pi_L^\perp)
\]

more practically:

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
d_Y,
T_u\mathcal M_{\sing}^{\arith}
\right)
\]

in a Riesz-normalized singular coefficient norm.

So the finite audit should be performed in a Riesz-conditioned singular basis:

\[
\|s\|_{\sing}^2
\asymp
\|F_s\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\]

with condition number at most \(Q^A\).

Then the normal projection is coordinate-invariant up to polynomial factors.

Thus add:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{SingularRieszBasis}:
\quad
\text{singular coefficient coordinates through order nine have polynomial condition number.}
}
\]

Without this, \(\mathcal K_9\)-normality may be a coordinate artifact.

---

# 6. Practical finite audit

The \(K_9\) rank audit should proceed as follows.

## Step 1: choose Riesz singular coordinates

Pick a singular basis through order nine with

\[
Q^{-A}\|s\|^2
\le
\left\|\sum_j s_j\Phi_j\right\|_{L^2(I)}^2
\le
Q^A\|s\|^2.
\]

## Step 2: build tangent matrix

Compute columns:

\[
T_m,\quad T_\lambda,\quad T_{\mathrm{norm}},\quad
T_\alpha\text{ if admitted}.
\]

Split:

\[
T=
\begin{pmatrix}
T_L\\
T_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

## Step 3: test lower-order tangent observability

Check

\[
\sigma_{\min}(T_L|_{\operatorname{ran}T})
\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

Equivalently, no tangent is invisible below order nine.

## Step 4: compute Schur residual

Form

\[
R=T_9T_L^\dagger
\]

on the tangent image and evaluate

\[
K_9(d_Y)=d_{Y,9}-R d_{Y,L}.
\]

## Step 5: rank result

If

\[
\|K_9(d_Y)\|\ge Q^{-A}\|d_Y\|,
\]

then \(Y12\) gives a polynomial rank-one channel.

If not, evaluate \(d_7\) and higher residuals.

---

# 7. Interaction with tubular radius

A well-conditioned tangent projection also helps the tubular estimate. If the singular coordinate chart is Riesz and the actual-source parameterization has derivatives bounded by \(Q^A\), then the second fundamental form satisfies

\[
\|\mathrm{II}\|\le Q^A
\]

unless there are near-singular parameter coordinates.

So the same finite conditioning package should include:

\[
\boxed{
\|T^\dagger\|\le Q^A,
\qquad
\|D^2s(\theta)\|\le Q^A.
}
\]

Then:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(s_0+D,\mathcal M)
\ge
\|(I-P_T)D\|-Q^A\|D\|^2.
\]

This turns the linear \(K_9\) residual into a nonlinear distance lower bound.

---

# 8. New risk: tangent dimension may be too large

If \(\Theta_{\arith}\) contains many parameters, then \(T_L\) may be wide and the tangent space may become nearly all of \((s_L,s_9)\)-space. Then the normal dimension vanishes.

This is why actual-source parameters must remain restricted.

Allowed:

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha\text{ if necessary},\text{ finite chart normalizations}.
\]

Not allowed:

\[
\{a_n\}_{n\le X},\quad \{b_n\}_{n\le X},\quad
\text{arbitrary branch amplitudes}.
\]

If arbitrary EF coefficients are allowed, then \(Y12\)-normality likely fails.

So the finite theorem should explicitly state:

\[
\boxed{
\dim\Theta_{\arith}=Q^{o(1)}
\text{ or at least much smaller than singular coefficient dimension,}
}
\]

and the actual source manifold is not packet-universal.

---

# 9. What this gives for the wall factor

If \(K_9(d_Y)\) is polynomially nonzero, then D4 seen by \(K_9\) is

\[
K_9(D_{\D4})
=
H\left[
\Gamma_G
\left(
\beta_9K_9(d_Y)+c_7K_9(d_7)
\right)
+
\Gamma_{G,2}\alpha_9K_9(d_Y)
\right].
\]

If

\[
K_9(d_Y)\neq0,
\]

define the forbidden linear form:

\[
\ell_{\forb}(\Gamma)
=
\alpha_9K_9(d_Y)\Gamma_{G,2}
+
\left(
\beta_9K_9(d_Y)+c_7K_9(d_7)
\right)\Gamma_G.
\]

Then

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|\ell_{\forb}(\Gamma)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

If the \(\Gamma_G\) coefficient is nuisance or poorly controlled, one can still use the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) term provided

\[
\alpha_9K_9(d_Y)
\]

is separated from cancellation in the moving linear form after rank stratification.

Thus the wall anti-concentration becomes a moving linear-form theorem in \((\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\), but with a fixed nonzero \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) component.

---

# 10. Final updated \(K_9\) theorem

A robust NormalD4\(_{K9,Y12}\) theorem needs:

1. **Singular Riesz coordinates**

   \[
   \operatorname{cond}\le Q^A.
   \]

2. **Restricted actual-source tangent set**

   no arbitrary EF coefficients.

3. **Lower-order tangent observability**

   \[
   \|T_La\|\ge Q^{-A}\|T_9a\|.
   \]

4. **Polynomial Schur residual**

   \[
   \|R\|\le Q^A.
   \]

5. **\(Y12\)-normality**

   \[
   \|d_{Y,9}-Rd_{Y,L}\|\ge Q^{-A}\|d_Y\|.
   \]

6. **Tubular bound**

   \[
   \|\mathrm{II}\|\le Q^A.
   \]

Then finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel through

\[
\ell_{\forb}(\Gamma).
\]

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

The \(K_9(Y12)\) problem is no longer just “is the residual nonzero?” It is a conditioned Schur-complement normality test.

The most important added input is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{LowerOrderTangentObservability}:
\text{no actual-source tangent is almost purely order-nine.}
}
\]

If this holds and \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is pure or signature-separated at order nine, then \(Y12\)-normality is very likely.

The finite audit should now compute:

\[
d_Y=(d_{Y,L},d_{Y,9}),
\qquad
R=T_9T_L^\dagger,
\qquad
d_{Y,9}-Rd_{Y,L}.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: splitting \(K_9(Y12)\) into pure order-nine and cancellation tests

The current decisive downstream quantity is

\[
K_9(d_Y)
=
d_{Y,9}-R\,d_{Y,L},
\]

where

\[
d_Y=T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr},
\qquad
d_Y=(d_{Y,L},d_{Y,9}),
\]

and

\[
R=T_9T_L^\dagger
\]

is the covariant lower-to-order-nine connection induced by actual-source tangents.

The next useful split is:

\[
\boxed{
K_9(Y12)\neq0
}
\]

is really two tests:

\[
\boxed{
\text{(A) }d_{Y,9}\neq0\text{ in the forbidden signature sector;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{(B) }R\,d_{Y,L}\text{ has no component in that same sector.}
}
\]

If both hold, then \(K_9(Y12)\neq0\) without computing the full Schur complement.

---

# 1. Forbidden signature sector

Let

\[
\Sigma_Y
\]

denote the structural \(Y12\)-signature:

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(\text{quadratic source-Hessian},
\text{mixed branch},
\text{same-point},
\text{one whitening slot},
\text{source-grade/reflection parity}).
\]

Let

\[
\Pi_Y
\]

be the projection to this signature sector inside \(\mathcal Y_9\).

Then a sufficient condition for \(Y12\)-normality is

\[
\Pi_Y d_{Y,9}\neq0
\]

and

\[
\Pi_Y R d_{Y,L}=0.
\]

Indeed,

\[
\Pi_Y K_9(d_Y)
=
\Pi_Y d_{Y,9}
-
\Pi_Y R d_{Y,L}
=
\Pi_Y d_{Y,9}.
\]

So the target becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Yd_{Y,9}\neq0,
\qquad
\Pi_YR d_{Y,L}=0.
}
\]

This is likely easier than computing \(K_9\) completely.

---

# 2. Why \(\Pi_Y d_{Y,9}\neq0\) is plausible

By construction,

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

comes from the promoted mixed-block carrier

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

with one same-point whitening slot.

Its whole purpose in the D4 architecture is to contribute a distinct order-nine carrier. If transition to singular coordinates preserves source-grade and polarization signatures, then

\[
d_{Y,9}
\]

must have a nonzero \(\Sigma_Y\)-component.

Thus the finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ preserves the }Y12\text{ signature through order nine.}
}
\]

If transition mixes signatures, the signature proof may fail, but the raw residual may still be nonzero.

---

# 3. Why \(\Pi_Y R d_{Y,L}=0\) is plausible

The term

\[
R d_{Y,L}
\]

is produced by lower-order connection data. It lies in the image of actual-source tangent-induced order-nine reconstruction from

\[
s_L=(s_{<7},s_7).
\]

If lower-order data do not contain the \(Y12\)-signature, then

\[
R d_{Y,L}
\]

cannot generate a \(\Sigma_Y\)-component.

So it is enough to show:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y R(\mathcal Y_L)=0
}
\]

or more weakly,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y R(d_{Y,L})=0.
}
\]

This follows if the actual-source connection

\[
R
\]

preserves source degree / polarization / same-point whitening count and lower-order spaces lack \(\Sigma_Y\).

Thus the core signature theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the actual EF singular connection cannot create a quadratic mixed-Hessian same-point whitening signature from lower-order data.}
}
\]

That is a structural statement, not a numerical one.

---

# 4. Lower-order contamination danger

The transition map could send

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\]

to lower singular orders:

\[
d_{Y,L}\neq0.
\]

If those lower components already carry a hidden \(Y12\)-like label, then the connection could reconstruct the order-nine \(Y12\) component and cancel.

This would mean \(Y12\) is not genuinely new at order nine in the singular chart; it already appears in lower singular data.

That is unlikely if the singular order filtration is compatible with source-grade and mixed-block degree, but it must be checked.

The finite test is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma_Y}^{L}d_{Y,L}=0?
}
\]

where

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_Y}^{L}
\]

is the corresponding signature projection in lower singular orders.

If

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_Y}^{L}d_{Y,L}=0,
\]

then lower-order cancellation cannot occur through same-signature reconstruction.

---

# 5. Relation to \(C_7\)

The order-seven carrier

\[
[\mathsf C_7]
\]

may naturally lie in the actual-source connection pathway:

\[
s_7\mapsto s_9.
\]

So

\[
K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7])
\]

may vanish. That is expected and not harmful.

What matters is that \(Y12\) is not in the same connection image as \(C_7\).

In D4, the \(\Gamma_G\) order-nine component includes

\[
\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_G
\]

as well as the connection from \(C_7\). But the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) component is

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Thus if \(Y12\) survives \(K_9\), the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel is clean even if \(C_7\) is tangent.

---

# 6. Signature versus \(\alpha\)-tangent

The dangerous optional tangent is

\[
\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}.
\]

If

\[
\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}
\]

has only source-linear branch phase/weight signature, then

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}=0.
\]

Then including \(\alpha\) in the tangent set does not affect the \(Y12\) signature sector, and

\[
\Pi_YK_9(d_Y)
\]

remains nonzero.

But if

\[
\partial_\alpha
\]

changes the nonlinear source map or induces second-order mixed branch variations at the same singular order, then

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}
\]

may be nonzero and could kill \(Y12\).

So the \(\alpha\)-compatibility audit reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y(\partial_\alpha u_{\sing})=0?
}
\]

This is much sharper than comparing the full \(\partial_\alpha u\) vector with \(Y12\).

---

# 7. Signature theorem package

A clean finite theorem would be:

## Signature preservation

The transition map

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

preserves the \(\Sigma_Y\)-signature through order nine:

\[
\Pi_Y d_{Y,9}
=
c_Y[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\qquad
|c_Y|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

## Tangent signature exclusion

For all legitimate actual-source tangents,

\[
\Pi_Y T_p=0.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\Pi_Y T_9=0
\]

on the tangent matrix.

## Lower connection exclusion

The lower-to-nine connection has no \(\Sigma_Y\)-component:

\[
\Pi_Y R=0
\]

on lower-order spaces.

Then:

\[
\Pi_YK_9(d_Y)=\Pi_Yd_{Y,9}\neq0.
\]

This proves \(Y12\)-normality.

---

# 8. If signature theorem fails

If the signature theorem fails, there are levels of fallback.

## Failure 1: \(\Pi_Yd_{Y,9}=0\)

Then transition to singular chart kills or reclassifies the \(Y12\) order-nine signature. Try a different singular chart or higher residual \(K_{11}\).

## Failure 2: tangents have \(\Sigma_Y\)

Then \(Y12\) may be actual-source tangent. Need to see whether the tangent coefficient is constrained enough that \(Y12\) still has a normal component after quotienting.

## Failure 3: lower connection creates \(\Sigma_Y\)

Then \(Y12\) may be coherent with lower data. Need to use a higher residual or a refined signature that separates promoted \(Y12\) from lower-induced \(Y12\)-like terms.

---

# 9. Current strongest result

The strongest conditional statement is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the }Y12\text{ source-Hessian/same-point whitening signature is absent from actual-source tangents and lower singular connection images, then finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.}
}
\]

The wall form becomes

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y}
\asymp
\frac{
|H|^2
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

This is the cleanest downstream statement so far.

---

# 10. Immediate next finite audit

Compute or prove by representation/signature:

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0,
\]

\[
\Pi_YT_p=0
\quad\text{for allowed tangents }p,

## Agent 2 — continued attack: filtration proof of \(Y12\)-normality

The current target is the signature separation package:

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0,
\]

\[
\Pi_YT_p=0
\quad\text{for legitimate actual-source tangents},
\]

\[
\Pi_YR=0
\quad\text{on lower connection images}.
\]

I now push this into a graded/filtered statement. The goal is to avoid coefficient computation by proving that \(Y12\) lives in a graded component unavailable to tangents and lower-order connection terms.

---

# 1. Define a signature filtration

Introduce a finite signature tuple

\[
\Sigma
=
(h,\mu,\omega,\pi,\rho),
\]

where:

\[
h=\text{source-Hessian degree},
\]

\[
\mu=\text{mixed-branch degree},
\]

\[
\omega=\text{same-point whitening-slot count},
\]

\[
\pi=\text{source-grade/reflection parity},
\]

\[
\rho=\text{singular order}.
\]

For the \(Y12\) carrier,

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

with one same-point whitening slot, so schematically

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(Y12)=(2,\mu_{\mathrm{mix}},1,\pi_Y,9).
}
\]

The exact parity label \(\pi_Y\) depends on the transition-to-singular convention, but the important components are:

\[
h=2,
\qquad
\omega=1,
\qquad
\rho=9.
\]

Now decompose the singular order-nine target space as

\[
\mathcal Y_9
=
\bigoplus_{\Sigma:\rho=9}\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma}
\]

up to nuisance/quotient identifications. Define

\[
\Pi_Y:\mathcal Y_9\to\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}.
\]

The desired finite result is:

\[
\Pi_Yd_{Y,9}\neq0,
\qquad
\Pi_YR d_{Y,L}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_YT_9=0.
\]

---

# 2. Actual-source tangents should have \(h\le1\)

Legitimate actual-source parameters are

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \text{chart normalization},\quad \alpha\text{ if admitted}.
\]

Their first variations act on the explicit-formula source by:

* translating \(m\);
* rescaling \(\lambda\);
* multiplying by log-frequency weights;
* changing AFE branch weights/phases;
* changing fixed background/gamma terms.

These are first-order source deformations. They do not introduce a new quadratic source-Hessian term

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Thus for all first-order actual-source tangent vectors,

\[
\boxed{
h\le1.
}
\]

Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y T_p=0
}
\]

provided \(\Pi_Y\) really isolates \(h=2\) Hessian terms.

This remains true even if \(\alpha\) is admitted, **provided** \(\partial_\alpha\) only changes branch weights/phases and does not vary the nonlinear source map \(N_0\) itself.

So the \(\alpha\)-compatibility condition sharpens to:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha N_0=0
\quad\text{at the nonlinear source-map level.}
}
\]

If \(\alpha\) changes only AFE coefficients, it is safe for the \(Y12\)-signature. If it changes the nonlinear source map or whitening geometry in a way that creates \(D^2N_0\)-terms, it may kill the channel.

---

# 3. Lower connection maps should preserve \(h\)

The lower connection map

\[
R=R_{7\to9}+R_{<7\to9}
\]

is generated by singular coordinate changes, chart transport, and coherent EF relations. It is linearized along the actual source.

Therefore it should be linear in the source variation. It may:

* raise singular order;
* multiply by log-frequency or curvature weights;
* mix parity sectors;
* transport order-seven coefficients to order nine.

But it should not create a quadratic Hessian term from lower-order linear source data.

Thus

\[
\boxed{
h(Rs_L)\le h(s_L).
}
\]

If lower singular data \(s_L\) contains no \(h=2\), same-point \(Y12\)-signature component, then

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YR s_L=0.
}
\]

For \(d_Y\), this gives

\[
\Pi_YR d_{Y,L}=0
\]

provided any lower-order leakage of \(d_Y\) does not already carry the \(h=2,\omega=1\) signature.

So the key finite filtration check is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y^L d_{Y,L}=0.
}
\]

If transition-to-singular sends \(Y12\) into lower singular orders with the same \(h=2,\omega=1\) signature, then lower connection could reconstruct the order-nine \(Y12\)-component and cancellation is possible.

---

# 4. Whitening-slot count gives an independent separator

Even if source-Hessian degree is ambiguous, the whitening-slot count helps.

The \(Y12\) carrier has one same-point whitening slot:

\[
\omega=1.
\]

Actual EF tangents from \(m,\lambda,\alpha\) usually have

\[
\omega=0
\]

unless the gauge/whitening map itself is varied.

Gauge or Gram normalization tangents can introduce whitening derivatives, but typically of the form

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[A],
\]

not

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

coupled to the same-point whitening slot.

So a refined signature can separate:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(h=2,\omega=1,\text{same-point})
\]

from

\[
(h=1,\omega=1)
\]

gauge tangents.

This is useful because if chart normalization tangents introduce \(\omega=1\), they still should not have \(h=2\).

Thus the robust forbidden projection is not just \(h=2\), but

\[
\boxed{
(h,\omega,\mathrm{point})=(2,1,\mathrm{same}).
}
\]

---

# 5. Source-grade/reflection parity as a backup separator

If \(h\) and \(\omega\) are insufficient, use the source-grade reflection identity.

Earlier structure gave:

\[
(Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle})^T
=
(-1)^{g+e}
Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle}.
\]

The \(Y12\) carrier has a definite source-grade/reflection parity determined by:

* mixed branch bidegree;
* same-point whitening slot;
* quadratic source Hessian;
* order-nine singular target.

Actual-source tangents and lower-order connection terms may occupy different parity sectors.

Thus another sufficient separation is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\pi_Y}
(T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\pi_Y}d_Y\neq0.
}
\]

This is weaker than full signature separation but may be easier to verify using existing reflection/complement-parity machinery.

---

# 6. The finite theorem

The clean finite theorem should be stated as:

There exists a signature projection

\[
\Pi_Y:\mathcal Y_9\to\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

such that:

## Y12 survives

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]^{\tr}
\neq0.
}
\]

## Tangents vanish

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_p=0
}
\]

for every legitimate actual-source tangent parameter \(p\).

## Lower connection vanishes

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YR_{7\to9}=0,
\qquad
\Pi_YR_{<7\to9}=0
}
\]

on lower-order spaces.

Then

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal K_9(d_Y)
=
\Pi_Yd_Y\neq0.
\]

Therefore

\[
\mathcal K_9(d_Y)\neq0.
\]

This proves rank-one D4 normality through \(Y12\).

---

# 7. What if lower-order \(Y12\)-signature appears?

If

\[
\Pi_Y^L d_{Y,L}\neq0,
\]

then the simple signature proof fails. But not all is lost.

One can refine the residual to quotient out lower \(Y12\)-signature components only if they are actual-source coherent. Define

\[
R_Y:\mathcal Y_{L,\Sigma(Y12)}\to \mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma(Y12)}.
\]

Then test

\[
\Pi_Yd_{Y,9}-R_Y\Pi_Y^Ld_{Y,L}\neq0.
\]

This is a smaller Schur-complement calculation restricted to the \(Y12\)-signature sector.

So even if transition produces lower \(Y12\)-signature pieces, the audit remains finite and manageable.

---

# 8. Consequence for wall factor

If the signature theorem holds, then the forbidden wall factor becomes essentially

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

The \(\Gamma_G\) part may also contribute, but it is not needed. The rank-one channel is already present through

\[
\alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Thus downstream wall anti-concentration reduces to

\[
|\Gamma_{G,2}|\not\approx0
\]

under the extracted measure, rather than full two-dimensional anisotropy.

This is a significant simplification.

---

# 9. Remaining analytic inputs

Even after signature separation, three analytic inputs remain:

## EFCompletion\(_{K9}\)

Need

\[
\sum_p
|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X)|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the singular discarded residual upper in the \(Y12\)-signature norm.

## Tubular radius

Need the actual EF singular manifold to have polynomial tubular radius in the \(\Pi_Y\)-normal direction:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(u+D,\mathcal M)
\ge
|\Pi_YD|-Q^C\|D\|^2.
\]

## \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-sublevel

Need

\[
\nu(|\Gamma_{G,2}|<\varepsilon)
\le Q^C\varepsilon^\beta\nu(\mathcal C)
\]

or a polynomial mass statement.

These are now the clean downstream analytic inputs.

---

# 10. Final conclusion

The \(Y12\)-normality problem can likely be reduced to a signature-filtration theorem.

The best finite statement is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7})=0,
\qquad
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

If this holds, finite D4 has a rank-one normal channel through \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

The main finite check is whether transition-to-singular preserves the \(Y12\) signature at order nine and does not create a lower-order coherent \(Y12\)-signature that cancels through the connection.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: coordinate-free \(Y12\)-signature obstruction

The last step proposed a signature projection

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
\]

with

\[

(T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7})=0,

\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}

\]

I now push this one level deeper. The key risk is that “signature” could be a coordinate artifact. To make the proof usable, the \(Y12\)-signature must be defined as an invariant graded quotient, not as a label attached to a chosen basis.

---

# 1. Define an invariant filtration, not just a projection

Let \(\mathcal Y_{\le 9}\) be the singular coefficient/carrier space through order nine after transition to the singular chart.

Introduce a filtration

\[
0\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}
\subset
\mathcal F^{\le Y}
\subset
\mathcal Y_{\le 9}
\]

where:

\[
\mathcal F^{<Y}
=
\text{span of all components with signature strictly below }Y12,
\]

and

\[
\mathcal F^{\le Y}
=
\mathcal F^{<Y}
+
\text{span of the }Y12\text{-signature sector}.
\]

Then define the invariant graded piece

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}
\mathcal Y_{\le 9}
:=
\mathcal F^{\le Y}/\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

The \(Y12\)-normality test should be:

\[
\boxed{
[T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]]
\neq0
\quad
\text{in }
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}\mathcal Y_{\le9}.
}
\]

And the tangent/lower-connection condition should be:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7}
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}.
}
\]

This avoids dependence on a noncanonical projection \(\Pi_Y\). Any projection realizing the filtration gives the same nonzero graded class.

---

# 2. What order should the filtration use?

The filtration should be ordered lexicographically by structural invariants:

\[
\Sigma=(h,\omega,\mu,\pi,\rho),
\]

where:

* \(h\) = source-Hessian degree;
* \(\omega\) = same-point whitening-slot count;
* \(\mu\) = mixed-branch bidegree;
* \(\pi\) = source-grade/reflection parity;
* \(\rho\) = singular order.

For \(Y12\),

\[
\Sigma_Y=(2,1,\mu_{\rm mix},\pi_Y,9).
\]

The most robust ordering is:

\[
(h,\omega,\mu,\pi,\rho),
\]

with \(h\) first. This is because actual-source tangents are first-order in the EF source and should satisfy

\[
h\le1,
\]

while \(Y12\) has

\[
h=2.
\]

Thus even if transition-to-singular mixes singular orders, it should not change the source-Hessian degree \(h\). So \(h=2\) is the strongest invariant.

A safer filtration is therefore:

\[
\mathcal F^{<Y}
=
\{h<2\}
+
\{h=2,\omega<1\}
+
\{h=2,\omega=1,\mu<\mu_Y\}
+\cdots.
\]

Then \(Y12\) is detected at the first point where a quadratic same-point mixed-Hessian whitening component appears.

---

# 3. Transition-to-singular must preserve the filtration

The signature argument requires

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\mathcal F^{\le Y}_{\tr}
\subset
\mathcal F^{\le Y}_{\sing},
\]

and

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\mathcal F^{<Y}_{\tr}
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}_{\sing}.
\]

That is, the chart transition may mix coordinates and singular orders, but it must not lower the signature filtration.

If transition can send \(Y12\)-signature into lower filtration or create \(Y12\)-signature from lower filtration, the argument fails.

So we need a finite transition theorem:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ is filtered with respect to }
(h,\omega,\mu,\pi).
}
\]

This is plausible because chart transition is built from re-expansion and scaling of the same local source expression. It should not change source-Hessian degree or whitening-slot count; it may only redistribute singular order \(\rho\).

Thus the filtered statement should be easier than tracking every singular coefficient.

---

# 4. Lower connection maps must be filtered

The lower-to-nine connection

\[
R=R_{7\to9}+R_{<7\to9}
\]

must also respect the filtration:

\[
R(\mathcal F^{<Y}_{L})
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}_{9}.
\]

Equivalently, lower-order coherent data cannot generate the \(Y12\)-graded component.

This is stronger than saying \(R\) is linear. It says \(R\) is a filtered connection.

The relevant theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{Y} R =0
\quad
\text{on lower-order actual-source coherent data.}
}
\]

If this holds, then

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}K_9(d_Y)
=
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}d_{Y,9}.
\]

So \(K_9(Y12)\neq0\) follows from survival of the \(Y12\)-graded component.

---

# 5. Actual-source tangents are filtered below \(Y12\)

For legitimate parameters

\[
m,\lambda,\text{normalization},\alpha?
\]

we need

\[
T_{\par}
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

This becomes a clean theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_{\theta}
F_X^\theta
\text{ has no }h=2,\omega=1,\mu_{\rm mix}\text{ component}
}
\]

for every allowed actual-source parameter \(\theta\).

This is clear for \(m,\lambda\), provided they only differentiate the already-built scalar EF source. It is also clear for phase/amplitude variations of plus/dual coefficients.

It is not automatic for parameters that vary the nonlinear map \(N_0\), the whitening construction, or the source model itself. Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{ is safe iff }\partial_\alpha
\text{ does not vary }N_0\text{ or the whitening map in a quadratic way.}
}
\]

This is the precise alpha-D4 compatibility criterion.

---

# 6. \(Y12\)-survival in the graded quotient

The finite D4 carrier must satisfy

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
\]

Because \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is defined by

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}
=
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with a same-point whitening slot, its class should be nonzero in the \(h=2,\omega=1\) graded piece unless the quotient conventions explicitly kill that carrier.

Thus the core finite check becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the carrier quotient identify }
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\text{ same-point whitening terms with nuisance?}
}
\]

If not, \(Y12\) survives.

If yes, D4 is rank-zero in this channel.

---

# 7. Interaction with nuisance quotient

The filtration must be applied after quotienting nuisance directions. Let

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

be the singular nuisance submodule. The graded target is actually

\[
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}
(\mathcal Y_{\le9}/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
\]

So survival means

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal F^{<Y}
+
\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

This is important. A nuisance convention could kill \(Y12\) even if the raw signature is present.

The precise theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad
\text{in }
\operatorname{gr}_{Y}
(\mathcal Y_{\le9}/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
}
\]

This is the invariant downstream rank test.

---

# 8. What if \(Y12\) is killed by nuisance?

If

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\mathcal F^{<Y}+\mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

then the rank-one \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel fails.

Fallbacks:

1. test the full D4 combination:

   \[
   \beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_G
   +
   \alpha_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\Gamma_{G,2}
   +
   c_7[\mathsf C_7]\Gamma_G
   \]

   in a different graded sector;

2. test \([\mathsf C_7]\) in an order-seven normal quotient;

3. test a higher residual

   \[
   \mathcal K_{11}
   \]

   where the image of \(Y12\) may reappear;

4. change the singular retained/nuisance split, if possible, without destroying the actual-row upper.

But the current best route is still \(Y12\) at order nine.

---

# 9. Coordinate-free theorem statement

The downstream finite theorem can now be stated cleanly.

Let

\[
\mathcal G_Y
:=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}
(\mathcal Y_{\le9}/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
\]

Assume:

1. **Filtered transition**

   \[
   T_{\tr\to\sing}
   \text{ induces a map on }\mathcal G_Y.
   \]

2. **Filtered actual tangents**

   \[
   T_{\par}\mapsto0
   \quad\text{in }\mathcal G_Y.
   \]

3. **Filtered lower connection**

   \[
   R_{7\to9},R_{<7\to9}
   \mapsto0
   \quad\text{in }\mathcal G_Y.
   \]

4. **\(Y12\)-survival**

   \[
   [T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]
   \neq0
   \quad\text{in }\mathcal G_Y.
   \]

Then

\[
\mathcal K_9(T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12})
\neq0
\]

for any covariant residual \(\mathcal K_9\) realizing the normal quotient.

Thus finite D4 has rank at least one through the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel.

---

# 10. Why this is better

This theorem avoids:

* choosing a specific Taylor coefficient basis;
* solving a large Schur complement explicitly;
* worrying about lower-order transition mixing, as long as it is filtered;
* false positives from coordinate changes;
* ambiguity about \(\alpha\), reduced to whether it preserves the filtration.

So the new finite audit should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{construct }\mathcal G_Y
\text{ and check }[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

This is the cleanest possible formulation of the \(Y12\)-normality test.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

The \(Y12\)-normality problem should be attacked through an invariant graded quotient:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal Y_{\le9}/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
}
\]

The key theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad
\text{in }\mathcal G_Y,
}
\]

while actual-source tangents and lower connection images vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

If true, finite D4 has a coordinate-free rank-one forbidden channel via \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

So the old first-order normality test was insufficient. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ remains the best finite-D4 channel, but only if its primitive source-Hessian signature is absent from }E_2\text{ and }\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
}
\]

More precisely:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0\text{ in }\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
}
\]

follows if the following four signature exclusions hold:

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}=0,
\]

while

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

Here \(\Pi_Y\) is the invariant projection to the primitive \(Y12\)-signature sector:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\text{source-Hessian degree }2,\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point},\ \text{one whitening slot},\ \text{order }9).
\]

The only genuinely dangerous new pieces are:

\[
\boxed{\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\mathcal T_{E_2}}.
\]

The first can absorb \(Y12\) if the actual EF family has a legitimate second-order deformation with the same primitive \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\) signature. The second can absorb \(Y12\) if the regularized tail/completion \(E_2\) contains a free or tangent \(Y12\)-signature component.

---

# 1. Refined quotient

The correct forbidden quotient is

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
\mathcal A_9,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal A_9
=
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

The terms mean:

\[
\mathcal T_{\par}
=
\text{first-order actual-source parameter tangents};
\]

\[
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}
=
\text{order-nine data coherently forced by order-seven/lower singular data};
\]

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
=
\text{second fundamental-form directions of the actual EF singular manifold at order nine};
\]

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2}
=
\text{order-nine directions supplied by the canonical completion/tail }E_2.
\]

The downstream channel exists iff

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin \mathcal A_9.
\]

Equivalently, there exists a covariant residual functional

\[
\mathcal K_{9,2}
\]

annihilating \(\mathcal A_9\) such that

\[
\mathcal K_{9,2}
\left(
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\right)
\neq0.
\]

---

# 2. First-order tangents remain harmless

The first-order parameter directions are still unlikely to contain \(Y12\).

For

\[
m,\lambda,\alpha
\]

one obtains translations, rescalings, phase/log-frequency changes, and AFE weight deformations. These are source-linear:

\[
\deg_{\src}\le1.
\]

But \(Y12\) has primitive source-Hessian degree

\[
\deg_{\src}=2.
\]

Thus, provided \(\alpha\) is only an AFE tilt and does not deform \(N_0\) or the whitening geometry,

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{\par}=0.
}
\]

This is a strong point in favor of \(Y12\)-normality.

If \(\alpha\) changes the nonlinear source map \(N_0\), then this fails. But in the current use of \(\alpha\), it appears to change branch phase/weight structure, not the nonlinear source Hessian itself.

---

# 3. Lower connection terms are likely harmless

The lower connection space

\[
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}
\]

contains order-nine data forced by coherent lower singular coefficients.

A filtered connection should preserve source-Hessian degree and same-point whitening count. If lower singular data do not already contain the \(Y12\)-signature, then

\[
\Pi_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0.
\]

Thus the main finite checks here are:

\[
\Pi_Y^L
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]_{<9}
=0,
\]

and

\[
\operatorname{gr}_Y R_{7\to9}
=
\operatorname{gr}_Y R_{<7\to9}
=0.
\]

If transition-to-singular sends \(Y12\) into lower-order slots with the same primitive signature, lower connection cancellation becomes possible. Otherwise lower connection cannot kill the \(Y12\) class.

So this is a finite filtration audit, not a broad analytic issue.

---

# 4. The new hard term: \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\)

Because \(Y12\) is quadratic, second-order source-manifold curvature must be included.

Let

\[
u(\theta)
=
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)
\]

be the actual EF singular model. Then

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
\Pi_9
D^2_{\theta_i\theta_j}u(\theta_0)
\right\}
\]

modulo tangent and lower-connection components.

The dangerous equality is

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+
\mathcal T_{\par}
+
\mathcal T_7
+
\mathcal T_{<7}
+
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

This can happen only if a legitimate second variation of the actual EF model produces the same primitive nonlinear source-Hessian/same-point whitening signature.

The key distinction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{EF parameter curvature is usually second derivative of source-linear EF atoms;}
}
\]

whereas

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ is the second derivative of the nonlinear source map }N_0.
}
\]

These are not the same unless the allowed EF parameters directly deform the input variables \(\xi_1,\xi_2\) inside \(N_0\) or the whitening map.

So a sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

Expanded:

\[
\Pi_Y D^2_{\theta_i\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta)=0
\]

for all legitimate \(\theta_i,\theta_j\).

This includes the dangerous checks:

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha^2 u=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_m\partial_\alpha u=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\lambda\partial_\alpha u=0.
\]

If these vanish, \(\alpha\)-twisting remains compatible with D4.

---

# 5. The other hard term: \(E_2\)

The completion/tail \(E_2\) must be handled explicitly.

There are four possible statuses for

\[
\Pi_YE_2.
\]

## Case A: absent

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0.
\]

Then \(E_2\) does not affect the \(Y12\) channel.

## Case B: fixed but not tangent

\[
\Pi_YE_2\neq0
\]

but it is a fixed inhomogeneous offset, not a freely variable tangent direction. Then it modifies the basepoint of the singular manifold but does not kill the normal direction.

This is acceptable if the residual is covariant around the shifted basepoint.

## Case C: nuisance

If \(E_2\)'s \(Y12\)-component is declared nuisance and quotiented out, then it may also quotient out the \(Y12\) D4 carrier. This is dangerous.

Need to verify:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin \mathcal N_{E_2,Y}.
\]

## Case D: free/tangent

If \(E_2\) contributes a freely adjustable \(Y12\)-signature tangent, then the \(Y12\) forbidden channel dies.

Thus the necessary audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}=0
\quad\text{or at least}\quad
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin \Pi_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}.
}
\]

My expectation is that \(E_2\) is a deterministic regularized completion, not a free local deformation. If so, it should be a basepoint/background correction, not a tangent direction. But this must be verified.

---

# 6. Signature proof package

A clean theorem proving \(Y12\)-survival would be:

There exists a graded component

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\]

such that:

\[
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal G_Y,
\]

and

\[
\mathcal T_{\par},\quad
\mathcal T_7,\quad
\mathcal T_{<7},\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\]

all map to zero in

\[
\mathcal G_Y.
\]

Then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

This is the best route because it avoids solving a full coordinate Schur-complement system.

---

# 7. Consequence if \(Y12\) survives

If

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb},
\]

then the D4 order-nine component gives:

\[
\mathcal K_{9,2}(\delta s_{\D4})
=
H\,
\mathcal K_{9,2}([\mathsf C_{Y12}])
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
+
\text{possibly }C_7\text{ terms}.
\]

Since

\[
\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

we get a rank-one wall channel with moving linear form

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)
=
\beta_9(m)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9(m)\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

or a simplification to \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) if the covariant residual removes the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) part.

The forbidden wall energy is

\[
E_{\wall}^{K9}
=
\frac{
|H|^2|\ell_m(\Gamma)|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

So downstream requires anti-concentration for

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma),
\]

not necessarily full raw anisotropy.

---

# 8. Consequence if \(Y12\) dies

If

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb},
\]

then the clean \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) channel disappears.

Next options:

1. test

   \[
   [\mathsf C_7]\neq0
   \]

   in an order-seven or order-nine residual quotient;

2. test the full \(\Gamma_G\)-combination

   \[
   \beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}]+c_7[\mathsf C_7]
   \]

   for normality;

3. move to higher residuals

   \[
   \mathcal K_{11},\mathcal K_{13},\ldots;
   \]

4. abandon finite order \(7/9\) D4 as a contradiction channel.

Given the current architecture, option 2 is the immediate fallback, but it is less clean than \(Y12\).

---

# 9. Refined finite audit checklist

The next concrete audit should compute or prove:

## A. \(Y12\)-survival

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

## B. Actual-source first tangents vanish

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta u=0
\]

for

\[
\theta=m,\lambda,\alpha?,\text{normalization/chart}.
\]

## C. Actual-source second curvature vanishes

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}u=0
\]

for all legitimate pairs.

## D. Lower connection vanishes

\[
\Pi_YR_{7\to9}=0,\qquad \Pi_YR_{<7\to9}=0.
\]

## E. \(E_2\) status

\[
\Pi_YE_2
\]

is absent/fixed/nuisance/free. Free is bad; absent or fixed is acceptable.

If A is true and B-E are harmless, then \(Y12\)-normality is proved.

---

# 10. Agent 2 conclusion

The current downstream yes/no test is:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\text{ in }
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}).
\]

The best proof route is a source-signature filtration:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\deg_{\src}=2,\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point},\ \text{one whitening slot},\ \rho=9).
\]

If this signature is absent from legitimate parameter tangents, second EF curvature, lower connection, and \(E_2\)-tangents, while present in \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\), then finite D4 has a real rank-one forbidden channel.

## Agent 2 — stress test of \(Y12\)-survival against \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) and \(E_2\)

The current target is:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}
=
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}).
\]

The two hardest possible absorbers are:

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
\]

and

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2}.
\]

I stress-tested both.

---

# 1. Actual EF second curvature: what can it contain?

The actual finite EF model is

\[
F_X^\theta
=
B_2^\theta+P_{2,\le X}^\theta+D_{2,\le X}^\theta.
\]

The second fundamental directions are generated by

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}
T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(F_X^\theta).
\]

For the usual actual parameters

\[
\theta\in\{m,\lambda,\alpha,\text{normalization/chart}\},
\]

these second derivatives have a specific structure: they are still derivatives of explicit EF atoms. They can introduce:

* additional logarithmic weights;
* phase factors;
* scale factors;
* derivatives of the chart basis;
* products of first-order scalar weights.

But they do **not** introduce a new primitive nonlinear source-Hessian term of the form

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

So, under the assumption that the actual EF parameter family does not vary the nonlinear source map \(N_0\) itself, we get

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.
}
\]

This is the main positive finding.

---

# 2. The dangerous exception: whitening-geometry curvature

There is one possible exception. If the chart normalization / whitening map is treated as part of the actual-source parameter family, then second derivatives of the whitening operation can produce terms with one or more whitening slots.

A second derivative of a whitening map can look like

\[
D^2(G^{-1/2})[A,B],
\]

or mixed terms

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[A]\cdot D(\text{source})[B].
\]

These may have \(\omega=1\) or \(\omega=2\)-type whitening signatures.

But \(Y12\) is not merely “has whitening.” It is specifically:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

coupled with one same-point whitening slot.

So the refined exclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
D^2(\text{whitening/chart})=0
}
\]

unless the chart curvature includes the primitive source-Hessian \(D^2N_0\).

Thus whitening curvature is dangerous only if it couples to the same quadratic source-Hessian carrier, not merely because it has a whitening slot.

This suggests a sharper signature:

\[
\Sigma(Y12)
=
(\text{primitive }D^2N_0,\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point whitening count }1,\ \rho=9).
\]

With “primitive \(D^2N_0\)” included, ordinary chart/whitening curvature should not absorb \(Y12\).

---

# 3. \(\alpha\)-second derivatives

If \(\alpha\) is admitted, the second derivative

\[
\partial_\alpha^2F_X^\theta
\]

may create branch products in the sense of log-weight or phase-weight squares:

\[
(\log n)^2 a_n n^{-im},
\]

or second variation of AFE weights.

But these are still source-linear at the EF atom level. They do not create

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

unless \(\alpha\) modifies \(N_0\), \(\xi_1,\xi_2\), or the nonlinear whitening map.

Thus \(\alpha\)-twisting remains compatible with \(Y12\)-normality if:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha N_0=0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha \text{ does not vary the same-point whitening map in a }D^2N_0\text{-coupled way.}
}
\]

This is a more precise condition than merely saying “\(\partial_\alpha\) is source-linear.”

---

# 4. \(E_2\): absent, fixed, nuisance, or free

The completion term \(E_2\) is more delicate because it is part of the **canonical source**, not just a proof artifact.

The relevant question is:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{E_2}
\]

where \(\mathcal T_{E_2}\) denotes the order-nine tangent directions supplied by the \(E_2\) completion family.

There are four cases.

## Case A: \(E_2\) has no \(Y12\)-signature

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}E_2=0.
\]

Then harmless.

## Case B: \(E_2\) has a fixed \(Y12\)-signature component

This changes the basepoint of the actual EF manifold but not its tangent space.

Then harmless, provided \(\mathcal K_9\) is constructed covariantly at the shifted basepoint.

## Case C: \(E_2\) has a nuisance \(Y12\)-component

If the \(Y12\)-component of \(E_2\) is quotiented as nuisance, this may kill the D4 \(Y12\) class too.

Need to check whether

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

is identified with the \(E_2\)-nuisance direction. If yes, the channel dies.

## Case D: \(E_2\) has a free/tangent \(Y12\)-component

If regularization/tail completion can vary freely in the \(Y12\)-signature sector, then

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}\mathcal T_{E_2}
\]

contains the D4 carrier and the \(Y12\) channel dies.

The route needs \(E_2\) to be in Case A or B, not C/D.

---

# 5. Why \(E_2\) is likely fixed, not free

In the current canonical setup, \(E_2\) is a regularized tail/completion term determined by \(m,\alpha\), cutoff convention, and the canonical source definition. It is not a freely adjustable local parameter.

Therefore its variation should be included only through actual parameters already counted:

\[
\partial_mE_2,\quad
\partial_\lambda E_2,\quad
\partial_\alpha E_2.
\]

If those are source-linear tail variations, they should not have primitive \(Y12\)-signature.

So the expected status is:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ is fixed/coherent, not a free }Y12\text{ tangent.}
}
\]

But this must be audited because if the regularized tail includes nonlinear local correction terms with the same \(D^2N_0\)-signature, the channel may be compromised.

---

# 6. Refined signature theorem

The most robust theorem is:

There exists a graded quotient

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}
\left(
\mathcal Y_9/
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\right),
\]

where

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(\text{primitive }D^2N_0,\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point whitening }1,\ \rho=9).
\]

Then:

## Survival

\[
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\neq0
\quad\text{in }\mathcal G_Y.
\]

## Absence from actual model directions

\[
\mathcal T_{\par},\quad
\mathcal T_7,\quad
\mathcal T_{<7},\quad
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},\quad
\mathcal T_{E_2}
\]

all map to zero in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

Then:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

This is the cleanest current finite-D4 theorem.

---

# 7. What could still kill \(Y12\)?

There are now only three credible killers.

## Killer 1: singular nuisance quotient kills primitive \(D^2N_0\)

If

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

contains the primitive \(D^2N_0\) same-point whitening sector, then

\[
\mathcal G_Y=0.
\]

This would make finite D4 invisible by convention.

## Killer 2: actual EF second curvature contains primitive \(D^2N_0\)

This would mean actual source parameters vary the nonlinear source map itself. Then \(Y12\) is allowed curvature of the actual-source manifold.

## Killer 3: \(E_2\) supplies free/tangent primitive \(D^2N_0\)

This would make the completion term absorb the D4 \(Y12\) carrier.

If none of these holds, \(Y12\)-normality is very plausible.

---

# 8. Wall factor consequence

If \(Y12\) survives in \(\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}\), D4 gives:

\[
K_{9,2}(\delta s_{\D4})
=
Hc_Y
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

with

\[
c_Y\neq0.
\]

So the wall factor is

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|c_Y|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

If \(\beta_9\) is absorbed by the lower \(C_7\)-connection in the covariant residual, it may reduce to

\[
\frac{|H|^2|c_Y|^2|\alpha_9|^2|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2}
{\mathcal N_{\D4}}.
\]

Either way, rank one is enough.

---

# 9. Updated final audit checklist

To settle \(Y12\), the finite audit should answer:

1. Does

   \[
   T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
   \]

   have nonzero primitive \(D^2N_0\), mixed-branch, same-point-whitening, order-nine class?

2. Does the singular nuisance quotient kill that class?

3. Do first-order actual parameters \(m,\lambda,\alpha\) have that class?

4. Do second-order actual parameter curvatures have that class?

5. Does \(E_2\) have a free/tangent component in that class?

If answers are:

\[
\text{yes, no, no, no, no},
\]

then \(Y12\)-normality holds.

---

# 10. Agent 2 conclusion

After adding the second-order and \(E_2\) absorbers, the \(Y12\) route still looks viable. The decisive invariant should be refined to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{primitive }D^2N_0
+
\text{mixed branch }12
+
\text{same-point whitening slot}
+
\text{order }9.
}
\]

Actual EF parameter tangents and second curvature are likely source-linear, not primitive source-Hessian. \(E_2\) is likely fixed/coherent, not a free \(Y12\)-tangent.

Therefore the best current finite theorem remains:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
}
\]

This is the most promising downstream finite-D4 obstruction.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: polarization test for primitive \(D^2N_0\) versus parameter curvature

The current finite-D4 target is

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

The strongest proposed invariant is the primitive signature

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(\text{primitive }D^2N_0,\ \text{mixed branch }12,\ \text{same-point whitening }1,\ \rho=9).
\]

I now push the distinction between:

\[
\boxed{
\text{primitive source-Hessian }D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary second parameter curvature }D_\theta^2F_X^\theta.
}
\]

This matters because the quotient now includes

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\]

so \(Y12\) survives only if it is not just a second derivative of the actual EF parameter family.

---

# 1. Primitive Hessian versus parameter Hessian

Let the nonlinear source map be

\[
N_0(\xi).
\]

The \(Y12\) carrier contains

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Now take an actual EF family

\[
\xi=\xi(\theta),
\]

and differentiate

\[
N_0(\xi(\theta)).
\]

Then

\[
\partial_i N_0
=
DN_0[\partial_i\xi],
\]

and

\[
\partial_i\partial_j N_0
=
D^2N_0[\partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi]
+
DN_0[\partial_i\partial_j\xi].
\]

Thus a second parameter derivative **can** contain a primitive \(D^2N_0\) term if the source variable \(\xi\) itself varies with the parameter.

This is the dangerous case.

So the previous statement “EF second curvature is source-linear” is only true if the actual EF family is already defined after applying the source map, or if \(\partial_i\xi\) has no component in the \(Y12\) slots.

The right test is therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do allowed source variations } \partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi
\text{ span the two }Y12\text{ inputs }\xi_1,\xi_2?
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) may be actual EF second curvature. If no, \(Y12\) is primitive-normal.

---

# 2. Polarization criterion

The primitive Hessian term is bilinear:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Let

\[
V_{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\theta\xi:\theta\in\Theta_{\arith}\}
\]

be the allowed first-variation space of the actual EF source input.

Then

\[
\mathcal{II}_{\arith}^{\mathrm{Hess}}
\]

contains only

\[
D^2N_0[v,w],
\qquad v,w\in V_{\arith}.
\]

Therefore \(Y12\) is absorbed by second curvature only if

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{\arith}]
+
\text{linear-source terms}.
\]

So define the Hessian-polarization quotient

\[
\mathcal H_Y
=
D^2N_0[\mathcal V_1,\mathcal V_2]
\Big/
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{\arith}],
\]

with branch/same-point/whitening restrictions imposed.

Then \(Y12\)-survival follows if

\[
[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal H_Y.
\]

This is the key refined audit.

---

# 3. Why \(Y12\) may survive ordinary parameters

For ordinary actual parameters:

\[
m,\quad \lambda,\quad \alpha,
\]

the induced source-input variations \(\partial_\theta\xi\) are typically **diagonal** or **same-branch** variations:

* \(m\)-variation multiplies each EF atom by \(-i\log n\);
* \(\lambda\)-variation rescales \(x\) and differentiates the same local source;
* \(\alpha\)-variation reweights plus/dual branches.

These variations act on existing branch packets. They do not necessarily produce two independent collision inputs

\[
\xi_1,\xi_2
\]

with the \(Y12\) mixed-block structure.

The \(Y12\) carrier, however, is a promoted quadratic mixed-block remainder from two independent source losses:

\[
Y_{12}^{(2)}=D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

Thus if

\[
V_{\arith}
\]

is generated only by global scalar deformations of the actual EF packet, it may be too small to span both \(\xi_1\) and \(\xi_2\).

Then

\[
Y12\notin \mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

This is favorable.

---

# 4. Dangerous case: \(\alpha\) as independent branch split

If \(\alpha\)-variation gives independent plus/dual source directions, then

\[
\partial_\alpha\xi
\]

may have a mixed branch component.

But a single \(\alpha\)-direction still gives only

\[
D^2N_0[\partial_\alpha\xi,\partial_\alpha\xi],
\]

not necessarily the polarized mixed term

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

unless

\[
\partial_\alpha\xi
\]

contains both \(\xi_1\) and \(\xi_2\) components.

By polarization,

\[
D^2N_0[v_1+v_2,v_1+v_2]
-
D^2N_0[v_1-v_2,v_1-v_2]
=
4D^2N_0[v_1,v_2].
\]

So if the allowed tangent space contains both

\[
v_1=\xi_1
\quad\text{and}\quad
v_2=\xi_2,
\]

then the mixed Hessian is absorbed.

But if \(\alpha\) supplies only one fixed branch-weight direction, the span may still be one-dimensional and not enough to generate arbitrary \(Y12\).

Thus the \(\alpha\)-risk becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\dim \Pi_{\{\xi_1,\xi_2\}}V_{\arith}\ge2?
}
\]

If yes, \(Y12\) may die. If no, \(Y12\) likely survives.

---

# 5. Same-point whitening as an extra separator

Even if

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

is generated by parameter curvature, the D4 carrier includes one same-point whitening slot.

So the absorbed second curvature must also have the **same whitening placement**.

Allowed parameter second derivatives may produce:

\[
D^2N_0[\partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi]
\]

but not necessarily coupled to

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[A]
\]

at the same point.

Thus the true dangerous space is not merely

\[
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{\arith}],
\]

but

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[\cdot]_{\mathrm{same}}
\otimes
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{\arith}].
\]

If the actual EF parameter family does not vary the whitening slot independently, then this same-point whitening-coupled Hessian sector remains absent.

So the refined condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\omega=1,\mathrm{same}}
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{\arith}]
=0
}
\]

or at least does not contain \(Y12\).

This makes \(Y12\)-survival more plausible.

---

# 6. Finite test in terms of input-slot projections

Let

\[
U_1,U_2
\]

be the two source-input slot spaces whose mixed Hessian produces \(Y12\):

\[
Y12\in D^2N_0[U_1,U_2]\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}.
\]

Let

\[
P_i:V_{\arith}\to U_i
\]

be projections of actual first-variation directions to these slots.

Then the allowed Hessian-generated \(Y12\)-type space is

\[
D^2N_0[P_1V_{\arith},P_2V_{\arith}]
\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}.
\]

The finite test is:

\[
\boxed{
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\notin
D^2N_0[P_1V_{\arith},P_2V_{\arith}]
}
\]

in the relevant mixed-branch/reflection quotient.

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
(\xi_1,\xi_2)\notin
P_1V_{\arith}\times P_2V_{\arith}
}
\]

modulo the kernel of \(D^2N_0\).

This is concrete. It reduces \(Y12\)-normality against second curvature to a rank problem in the source-input slot spaces.

---

# 7. Interaction with \(E_2\)

The same polarization test applies to \(E_2\).

If \(E_2\) is generated by tail variation of the same source input family, then its dangerous \(Y12\)-component lies in

\[
D^2N_0[V_{E_2},V_{E_2}]
\]

or

\[
D^2N_0[V_{\arith},V_{E_2}].
\]

If \(V_{E_2}\) is not free and is determined by the actual EF tail, then it contributes a fixed basepoint correction, not a tangent.

But if regularization allows \(V_{E_2}\) to vary freely in the \(Y12\) slots, then it can absorb D4.

So classify \(E_2\) by its source-input variation space:

\[
V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}}.
\]

Need:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\notin
D^2N_0[V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}},V_{\arith}+V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}}]
\]

in the \(Y12\)-signature quotient.

If \(V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}}=0\), \(E_2\) is harmless.

---

# 8. Updated theorem: Hessian-slot separation

A sharper theorem replacing vague signature separation is:

Let \(V_{\arith}\) be the actual first-variation source-input space, and let \(V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}}\) be the free completion variation space. Define

\[
V_{\mathrm{free}}
=
V_{\arith}+V_{E_2}^{\mathrm{free}}.
\]

Then \(Y12\)-normality holds if

\[
\boxed{
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}
\notin
D^2N_0[V_{\mathrm{free}},V_{\mathrm{free}}]\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}
+
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

This is the finite Hessian-slot separation theorem.

It is stronger and more precise than saying “\(Y12\) has source-Hessian signature.”

---

# 9. Practical audit steps

To test this:

1. Identify the source-input slots \(\xi_1,\xi_2\) for \(Y12\).

2. Compute projections of actual parameter variations:

   \[
   P_iV_{\arith}\subseteq U_i.
   \]

3. Compute free \(E_2\) variations:

   \[
   P_iV_{E_2}^{\free}.
   \]

4. Check whether

   \[
   \xi_i\in P_i(V_{\arith}+V_{E_2}^{\free})
   \]

   for both \(i=1,2\).

5. Check same-point whitening slot availability.

6. Check reflection/source-grade parity.

If at least one slot direction is unavailable, or if the same-point whitening coupling is unavailable, \(Y12\) survives.

---

# 10. Current conclusion

The refined survival condition is no longer simply:

\[
\deg_{\src}(Y12)=2.
\]

Because actual second parameter curvature can also produce degree \(2\) Hessian terms.

The correct condition is:

\[
\boxed{
Y12\text{ uses a primitive Hessian polarization in source-input slots not spanned by actual/free EF variations, with the same-point whitening coupling.}
}
\]

This is now a precise finite rank/polarization test.

If it holds, then:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb},
\]

and finite D4 has the rank-one \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-visible downstream channel.
