## Agent 2 — continued attack: finite rank test for Hessian-slot separation

The current \(Y12\)-normality condition is:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}
\notin
D^2N_0[V_{\mathrm{free}},V_{\mathrm{free}}]\otimes W_{\mathrm{same}}
+
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

This is still abstract. I now reduce it to a finite rank test on three ingredients:

\[
\boxed{
\text{slot 1 availability, slot 2 availability, and same-point whitening availability.}
}
\]

If any one of these is missing from the actual/free EF variation space, \(Y12\) survives.

---

# 1. Decompose the \(Y12\) carrier into three factors

At the signature level, write

\[
Y12
\sim
D(G^{-1/2})[A_{\mathrm{same}}]\,
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\]

where:

* \(\xi_1\in U_1\) is the first mixed-block source input;
* \(\xi_2\in U_2\) is the second mixed-block source input;
* \(A_{\mathrm{same}}\in W_{\mathrm{same}}\) is the same-point whitening input.

Thus the forbidden primitive tensor is

\[
y
=
A_{\mathrm{same}}\otimes \xi_1\otimes \xi_2
\]

modulo the bilinear map \(D^2N_0\) and the whitening derivative.

Let

\[
V_{\free}
=
V_{\arith}+V_{E_2}^{\free}
\]

be the total legitimate first-variation space after including actual EF parameter variations and genuinely free completion variations.

Project it to the three slots:

\[
V_1=P_1V_{\free}\subseteq U_1,
\]

\[
V_2=P_2V_{\free}\subseteq U_2,
\]

\[
V_W=P_WV_{\free}\subseteq W_{\mathrm{same}}.
\]

Then legitimate second curvature can produce only

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[V_W]\,
D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

So \(Y12\) can be absorbed only if, modulo kernels and lower connection/nuisance,

\[
A_{\mathrm{same}}\in V_W,\qquad
\xi_1\in V_1,\qquad
\xi_2\in V_2
\]

in the relevant coupled sense.

---

# 2. Sufficient rank-separation criteria

The following are each sufficient for \(Y12\)-normality.

## Criterion A: missing source slot

If

\[
\xi_1\notin V_1
\]

or

\[
\xi_2\notin V_2
\]

modulo the kernel of \(D^2N_0\), then

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\notin
D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

Hence \(Y12\) survives.

## Criterion B: missing same-point whitening slot

If

\[
A_{\mathrm{same}}\notin V_W
\]

modulo the kernel of \(D(G^{-1/2})\), then the actual EF second curvature cannot produce the same same-point whitening-coupled carrier.

Hence \(Y12\) survives.

## Criterion C: branch/polarization mismatch

Even if

\[
\xi_i\in V_i
\]

abstractly, the actual variations may generate only symmetric or diagonal polarizations, e.g.

\[
D^2N_0[v,v],
\]

whereas \(Y12\) requires a mixed polarization

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2],
\qquad \xi_1\not\parallel \xi_2.
\]

If

\[
(\xi_1,\xi_2)
\notin
\operatorname{SymSpan}(V_{\free})
\]

in the required branch-labeled polarization, then \(Y12\) survives.

This matters especially for \(\alpha\), which may provide only one branch-weight direction.

---

# 3. The actual rank matrix

Define matrices:

\[
M_1=\bigl[P_1\partial_{\theta_j}\xi\bigr]_j,
\]

\[
M_2=\bigl[P_2\partial_{\theta_j}\xi\bigr]_j,
\]

\[
M_W=\bigl[P_W\partial_{\theta_j}\xi\bigr]_j,
\]

where columns range over all legitimate first-variation directions \(\theta_j\), including free \(E_2\) directions if any.

Then test:

\[
\operatorname{rank}[M_1\ \xi_1]
>
\operatorname{rank}M_1,
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}[M_2\ \xi_2]
>
\operatorname{rank}M_2,
\]

\[
\operatorname{rank}[M_W\ A_{\mathrm{same}}]
>
\operatorname{rank}M_W.
\]

If any one rank increases, \(Y12\) is normal.

If all three do not increase, proceed to the coupled bilinear test.

---

# 4. Coupled bilinear test

Even if each slot is individually available, \(Y12\) may still survive if the required coupled tensor is not available.

Let

\[
\mathcal B_{\free}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
D(G^{-1/2})[w]\,
D^2N_0[v_1,v_2]
:
w\in V_W,\ v_1\in V_1,\ v_2\in V_2
\right\}.
\]

Then test whether

\[
D(G^{-1/2})[A_{\mathrm{same}}]
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
\mathcal B_{\free}
+
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

This is a finite membership test in the \(Y12\)-signature graded piece.

In practice, because the \(Y12\) signature includes branch labels, same-point labels, and reflection parity, \(\mathcal B_{\free}\) may be much smaller than the naive tensor product.

---

# 5. Application to \(m,\lambda,\alpha\)

## \(m\)-variation

\[
\partial_m
\]

usually multiplies EF atoms by log frequencies and differentiates the same scalar source. It likely projects to diagonal variations in the same existing branch packets.

Expected:

\[
P_1\partial_m\xi,\ P_2\partial_m\xi
\]

do not independently span both \(Y12\) slots.

So \(m\) alone should not absorb \(Y12\).

## \(\lambda\)-variation

\[
\partial_\lambda
\]

adds scaling and \(x\partial_x\)-type deformation.

Again, likely source-linear and diagonal across slots. It should not create the required independent mixed polarization unless the two \(Y12\) slots are simply scale derivatives of the same source mode.

Expected safe.

## \(\alpha\)-variation

This is the dangerous one. If \(\alpha\) provides only one plus/dual contrast direction, then

\[
\dim P_1V_\alpha+\dim P_2V_\alpha\le1
\]

in the coupled mixed slot, and \(Y12\) survives.

If \(\alpha\) plus \(m\) or \(\lambda\) span both input slots, then second curvature

\[
D^2N_0[\partial_\alpha\xi,\partial_m\xi]
\]

could absorb \(Y12\).

Thus the actual alpha-D4 compatibility check is:

\[
\boxed{
(\xi_1,\xi_2)
\in
(P_1\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m,\partial_\lambda,\partial_\alpha\})
\times
(P_2\operatorname{span}\{\partial_m,\partial_\lambda,\partial_\alpha\})
?
}
\]

with same-point whitening included.

This is a finite rank test.

---

# 6. Role of \(E_2\)

If \(E_2\) is fixed/coherent, then

\[
V_{E_2}^{\free}=0.
\]

Then it does not enlarge \(V_{\free}\). It may shift the basepoint but does not absorb \(Y12\).

If \(E_2\) has free regularization/tail parameters, include their variations in \(M_1,M_2,M_W\). The risk is:

\[
V_{E_2}^{\free}
\]

may be high-dimensional enough to contain \(\xi_1,\xi_2,A_{\mathrm{same}}\), killing \(Y12\).

Therefore the route requires:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ has no free }Y12\text{-slot variation.}
}
\]

A deterministic tail depending on \(m,\lambda,\alpha\) is acceptable; free local tail amplitudes are not.

---

# 7. Lower connection and nuisance terms

After the Hessian-slot rank test, still quotient by:

\[
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

These could kill \(Y12\) even if actual parameter curvature does not.

But their ability to do so must respect the same slot signature.

So perform the same graded membership test:

\[
y_Y
\in
\operatorname{gr}_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal N_{\sing})?
\]

If no, \(Y12\) survives.

If yes, need a refined signature, likely involving:

* promoted \(D^2N_0\) versus connection-induced \(D^2N_0\);
* same-point versus transported whitening;
* reflection/source-grade parity.

---

# 8. Outcome classification

The finite audit can return:

## Green

At least one slot rank increases, or coupled bilinear membership fails:

\[
Y12\notin D^2N_0[V_{\free},V_{\free}]\otimes D(G^{-1/2})[V_W]
+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then \(Y12\) is forbidden-normal.

## Yellow

All slots are individually available, but coupled tensor membership is unresolved.

Then need explicit tensor/rank computation.

## Red

The \(Y12\) tensor lies in the legitimate second curvature or nuisance/lower connection space.

Then order-nine \(Y12\) D4 channel fails.

---

# 9. Refined theorem statement

A precise \(Y12\)-normality theorem is:

Let

\[
V_{\free}
=
V_{\arith}+V_{E_2}^{\free}.
\]

Let

\[
y_Y=
D(G^{-1/2})[A_{\mathrm{same}}]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

be the \(Y12\)-signature class of

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

If

\[
y_Y
\notin
D(G^{-1/2})[P_WV_{\free}]
D^2N_0[P_1V_{\free},P_2V_{\free}]
+
\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

then

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

This is now a fully finite rank/polarization theorem.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 conclusion

The \(Y12\)-survival problem has reduced to a finite slot-rank test.

The fastest sufficient checks are:

\[
\xi_1\notin P_1V_{\free}
\]

or

\[
\xi_2\notin P_2V_{\free}
\]

or

\[
A_{\mathrm{same}}\notin P_WV_{\free}.
\]

If any holds, \(Y12\) survives.

If all fail, perform the coupled bilinear membership test.

This is the sharpest current finite formulation of \(Y12\)-normality in \(\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}\).

## Agent 2 — \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\) upper for the forbidden channel

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: isolate the actual-row upper needed for the \(Y12\)-forbidden channel and decide whether ordinary \(L^2\) EF completion is enough. The current architecture uses \(Y12\)-normality as the preferred finite-D4 channel, with the downstream upper only needing residual control in the \(Y12\)-graded sector, not the full \(K_9\)-norm. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\text{ is strictly weaker than full }\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{K9},
\text{ but stronger than plain }\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}.
}
\]

The required estimate is

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_{Y12}\,
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C),
}
\]

where

\[
q''_{\can}=F_X+R_X.
\]

This is the correct actual-row upper for the rank-one \(Y12\)-forbidden channel.

Plain \(L^2\) residual control implies this only if the operator

\[
L_{Y12,p}
=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}
\]

is polynomially bounded from the \(L^2\)-residual norm to the \(Y12\)-graded singular norm.

That operator-bound condition is the main audit.

---

# 1. Correct residual after the \(E_2\) honesty check

Do not define

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-(B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X})
\]

blindly. The correct residual depends on the \(Y12\)-status of \(E_2\).

## Case A: \(E_2\) has no \(Y12\)-signature

Then set

\[
F_X=B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X},
\]

\[
R_X=q''_{\can}-F_X.
\]

The \(Y12\)-upper becomes

\[
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)
\right|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

## Case B: \(E_2\) has fixed coherent \(Y12\)-signature

Then include the coherent part in the model:

\[
F_X^{Y}
=
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
+
E_{2,\le X}^{Y\text{-coh}},
\]

and define

\[
R_X^Y=q''_{\can}-F_X^Y.
\]

The upper becomes

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\left|
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X^Y)
\right|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

## Case C: \(E_2\) has free/tangent \(Y12\)-signature

Then the \(Y12\)-channel is probably dead before residual estimates matter, because \(E_2\) belongs in

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2^{Y12}}
\]

and may absorb the D4 carrier in

\[
\mathcal Q_{9,2}^{\forb}.
\]

So Agent 1’s \(E_2\)-classification is logically prior to the final definition of \(R_X\).

---

# 2. The exact operator to bound

Define

\[
L_{Y12,p}
:=
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\]

is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
|L_{Y12,p}R_X^Y|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is a plaquette-local norm. It is not automatically the same as

\[
\|R_X^Y\|_{L^2(W_{\obs}\vee W_{\ref})}^2.
\]

The implication

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}
\Longrightarrow
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\]

requires

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p |L_{Y12,p}f|^2
\le Q^C\|f\|_{L^2(W_{Y12})}^2.
}
\]

If this operator bound holds, then ordinary residual smallness with exponent \(B+C\) implies the \(Y12\)-upper.

---

# 3. When \(L^2\)-completion is enough

Plain \(L^2\) completion is enough if all three maps are polynomially bounded in \(L^2\):

\[
J_{\tr,p}:L^2(I_p)\to \mathcal H_{\tr,p},
\]

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}:\mathcal H_{\tr,p}\to \mathcal H_{\sing,p},
\]

\[
\Pi_{Y12}:\mathcal H_{\sing,p}\to \mathcal G_{Y12,p}.
\]

The clean sufficient estimate is

\[
\sum_p
\|\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}f\|^2
\le
Q^C
\int |f(t)|^2 W_{Y12}(t)\,dt.
\]

This is plausible if:

\[
J_{\tr,p}
\]

is an \(L^2\)-localized representative or projection, not a pointwise high-order jet;

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing,p}
\]

is a bounded chart map;

\[
\Pi_{Y12}
\]

is a bounded projection to an invariant graded quotient.

In that favorable case:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\text{ follows from a sufficiently strong }
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{L^2}.
}
\]

---

# 4. When stronger residual estimates are needed

If

\[
J_{\tr,p}
\]

extracts derivatives, point jets, high singular coefficients, or sharp Taylor data, then \(L^2\)-small residuals may have large \(Y12\)-coefficients.

Then the needed estimate is Sobolev/jet-level:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
|L_{Y12,p}R_X^Y|^2
\le
Q^C
\|R_X^Y\|_{H^s(W_{Y12})}^2,
}
\]

with \(s\) at least the effective order of \(J_{\tr}\) and the singular projection.

Then one must prove

\[
\boxed{
\|R_X^Y\|_{H^s(W_{Y12})}^2
\le
Q^{-B-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is a stronger tail/completion theorem than Gate–1 needs.

So the route strongly prefers defining \(J_{\tr}\) and \(\Pi_{Y12}\) through bounded \(L^2\)-projection functionals rather than point-jet extraction.

---

# 5. Why the \(Y12\)-sector may be easier than full \(K_9\)

The full \(K_9\)-upper asks for control of all order-nine connection residuals:

\[
\sum_p
|K_{9,p}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)|^2.
\]

The \(Y12\)-upper only asks for the primitive signature sector:

\[
\sum_p
|\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X)|^2.
\]

This should be easier because many residual pieces have the wrong signature:

\[
P_2\text{-tail},\quad D_2\text{-tail},\quad B_2\text{-background},\quad \text{ordinary gamma terms}
\]

are source-linear or fixed coherent pieces, not primitive

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

same-point whitening carriers.

Thus the \(Y12\)-sector residual may be zero or much smaller for most completion terms.

This gives a targeted theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(P_2-P_{2,\le X})=0
}
\]

and similarly for \(D_2\)-tail, unless the tail contains primitive nonlinear Hessian data.

If true, \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\) is far easier than full residual completion.

---

# 6. Term-by-term residual classification

For the residual

\[
R_X^Y
=
q''_{\can}-F_X^Y,
\]

classify each contribution.

## Prime tail

\[
P_2-P_{2,\le X}.
\]

Likely source-linear and wrong signature:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(P_2-P_{2,\le X})=0
\]

unless the transition/singular map itself applies a nonlinear source Hessian, which it should not.

## Dual tail

\[
D_2-D_{2,\le X}.
\]

Same as prime tail: source-linear, wrong primitive Hessian signature.

## Gamma/background mismatch

\[
B_2\text{ residual or gamma correction}.
\]

This is fixed/coherent background. It should not produce primitive \(D^2N_0\) mixed-branch same-point whitening.

## Regularized completion \(E_2\)

This is the only dangerous term. It must be audited separately:

\[
\Pi_{Y12}E_2=0
\]

or included in the coherent model.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\text{ likely reduces almost entirely to the }E_2\text{ honesty check.}
}
\]

That is a major simplification if true.

---

# 7. Proposed theorem: signature-null residual

A strong theorem would be:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}
\left(
q''_{\can}
-
F_X^Y
\right)
=
0
}
\]

identically up to negligible regularization error.

Equivalently:

\[
R_X^Y
\]

has no primitive \(Y12\)-signature component.

If exact zero is too strong, prove

\[
\sum_p
|\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(R_X^Y)|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
\]

This is the precise \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\) theorem.

---

# 8. Relationship to \(Y12\)-normality

Do not confuse the two statements.

## \(Y12\)-normality

\[
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

This says D4 has a forbidden normal component.

## \(Y12\)-upper

\[
\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})
\approx0.
\]

This says actual global rows do not have that forbidden component.

Both are needed.

They are compatible because:

* actual rows are EF-coherent and lack primitive \(Y12\)-signature;
* formal D4 carriers have primitive \(Y12\)-signature.

That is the core normality contradiction.

---

# 9. Failure modes

\[
\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}
\]

fails if any of the following occur.

## Failure 1: \(E_2\) has free \(Y12\)-signature

Then actual rows are not forbidden in the \(Y12\)-channel.

## Failure 2: transition map creates \(Y12\)-signature from source-linear residuals

Then \(\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}\) is not a filtered operator.

This would undermine the whole signature approach.

## Failure 3: \(J_{\tr}\) uses unbounded jets

Then residuals small in \(L^2\) may be large in the \(Y12\)-projection norm.

Need stronger Sobolev tail estimates.

## Failure 4: singular quotient kills or conflates \(Y12\)

Then either the D4 lower vanishes or the actual-row upper becomes ill-defined.

---

# 10. Final theorem statement

A clean Agent-2 theorem is:

Let

\[
F_X^Y
=
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}
+
E_{2,\le X}^{Y\text{-coh}}
\]

where the last term is included only if \(E_2\) has a fixed coherent \(Y12\)-signature component.

Let

\[
R_X^Y=q''_{\can}-F_X^Y.
\]

Assume:

1. **Filtered transition**

   \[
   L_{Y12,p}=\Pi_{Y12}T_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}
   \]

   is a well-defined filtered operator.

2. **Polynomial operator bound**

   \[
   \sum_p |L_{Y12,p}f|^2
   \le Q^C\|f\|_{\mathcal R}^2
   \]

   for an appropriate residual norm \(\mathcal R\).

3. **Residual smallness**

   \[
   \|R_X^Y\|_{\mathcal R}^2
   \le Q^{-B-C}\mu(\mathcal C).
   \]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
|L_{Y12,p}R_X^Y|^2
\le
Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\).

---

# Agent 2 conclusion

The \(Y12\)-upper is not automatic from \(L^2\)-Gate–1 residual control, but it is likely much easier than full \(K_9\)-completion.

The decisive checks are:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{Y12}E_2
\text{ classification,}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
L_{Y12,p}
\text{ polynomial operator bound,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
R_X^Y
\text{ residual smallness in that operator norm.}
}
\]

If the transition/singular machinery is filtered and \(E_2\) has no free \(Y12\)-signature, then \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\) should follow from a targeted, possibly much weaker, residual estimate.

\[

If the filtered vanishing theorem is exact, we do not need a strong operator bound for \(L_Y\) on generic residuals.

Three structural compatibilities are needed.

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}

must not create primitive \(D^2N_0\) signatures from source-linear inputs.

T_{\tr\to\sing}
(\text{source-linear})=0.

## Filtered transition representative \(J_{\tr}\)

\[
J_{\tr}

must not itself apply a nonlinear operation that turns source-linear residuals into \(Y12\)-signature.

\[
\Pi_Y
\]

must be defined after quotienting nuisance directions but before collapsing the primitive \(D^2N_0\)-same-point sector.

If nuisance quotient kills the sector, both upper and lower vanish. That is the rank-zero case.

---

# 6. Consequence for \(F_X^Y\)

The finite coherent model for the downstream \(Y12\)-upper should be:

\[
F_X^Y
=
F_X
+
E_2^{Y,\mathrm{coh}},

not necessarily all of \(E_2\).

But for Gate–1 \(L^2\), \(E_2\) remains part of the full canonical source. This means different modules can use different coherent finite approximations:

\[

for Gate–1, and

\[
F_X^Y

for the \(Y12\)-upper.

This is acceptable as long as both approximate the same actual source in their own norms.

\[

}

Do not include free \(Y12\)-directions as coherent model directions, or the forbidden channel disappears.

# 7. Updated downstream theorem

Assume:

1. \(Y12\)-survival:

   \[
   \Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.

   is zero or \(Q^{-B}\)-small.

after subtracting coherent model/basepoint contributions, while D4 gives nonzero \(Y12\)-normal output.

---

# 8. Failure modes

The \(Y12\)-upper fails only if:

A source-linear residual produces primitive \(Y12\)-grade after \(T_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}\). That would undermine the signature architecture.

\Pi_YJ_{\sing}(q''_{\can})\approx0,
\qquad
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

This is the cleanest current downstream form.

---

# 1. Minimal algebraic axioms for \(\mathcal G_Y\)

We need \(\mathcal G_Y\) to satisfy five properties.

## Axiom 1: \(Y12\)-survival

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

Quantitatively,

\[
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is the finite D4 rank-one source.

## Axiom 2: source-linear annihilation

For every source-linear EF piece \(S_{\lin}\),

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(S_{\lin})=0.
\]

This should cover:

\[
B_2,\quad P_2,\quad D_2,
\]

and their truncation tails, provided they remain source-linear.

This axiom is what makes the \(Y12\)-upper easy.

## Axiom 3: legitimate first tangents vanish

For every allowed actual-source first parameter tangent,

\[
\partial_\theta u_{\sing},
\]

we have

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\theta u_{\sing}=0.
\]

This includes:

\[
\theta=m,\lambda,\text{normalization/chart},
\]

and \(\alpha\) if admitted, provided \(\alpha\) is source-linear.

## Axiom 4: legitimate second curvature vanishes

For every legitimate actual-source second parameter curvature,

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}u_{\sing},
\]

we have

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}u_{\sing}=0.
\]

This is the key second-order correction. It says actual EF curvature is not primitive \(D^2N_0\)-same-point-whitening.

## Axiom 5: \(E_2\)-honesty

Either

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0,
\]

or the fixed coherent \(Y12\)-part of \(E_2\) is included in the base model and the residual satisfies

\[
\sum_p\|\Pi_Y(E_2-E_2^{Y,\coh})\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu.
\]

No free \(Y12\)-tangent from \(E_2\) is allowed.

---

# 2. What these axioms buy immediately

Let

\[
F_X^Y=
B_2+P_{2,\le X}+D_{2,\le X}+E_2^{Y,\coh}
\]

and

\[
R_X^Y=q''_{\can}-F_X^Y.
\]

If Axioms 2 and 5 hold, then

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(R_X^Y)
\]

is zero or \(Q^{-B}\)-small.

Therefore the forbidden upper is:

\[
\boxed{
\sum_p
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing,p}J_{\tr,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu(\mathcal C)
}
\]

after subtracting the fixed coherent basepoint.

This is exactly the Type-I atomic upper.

On the other hand, D4 produces

\[
\delta s_{\D4}
=
H\,T_{\tr\to\sing}E_{\car}L_{\clean}\Gamma.
\]

Since

\[
L_{\clean}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad \alpha_9\neq0,
\]

Axiom 1 gives

\[
\Pi_Y\delta s_{\D4}
=
H\,\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
+\text{possible }C_7\text{ contribution}.
\]

Thus the local forbidden energy is rank one, with wall form

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

This is the downstream lower.

---

# 3. Why this avoids the \(K_9\)-conditioning problem

The earlier \(K_9\) construction required a Schur complement

\[
K_9=s_9-Rs_L
\]

and therefore needed conditioning of

\[
R=T_9T_L^\dagger.
\]

With the graded quotient \(\mathcal G_Y\), the lower-order connection and tangent spaces are killed structurally by filtration:

\[
\operatorname{gr}_Y(T_{\par}+T_7+T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0.
\]

So no explicit ill-conditioned Schur complement is needed.

The conditioning problem becomes simpler:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_Y\text{ must have a Riesz norm with polynomial conditioning.}
}
\]

That is, the quotient norm must satisfy

\[
\|\Pi_Y v\|\le Q^A\|v\|
\]

and the surviving \(Y12\) class must have

\[
\|\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\|\ge Q^{-A}.
\]

This is easier than controlling a large Schur complement.

---

# 4. The key finite check becomes quotient nontriviality

The central finite computation is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_Y\neq0
\quad\text{and}\quad
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0\text{ in }\mathcal G_Y.
}
\]

This includes checking that the singular nuisance quotient does not kill the \(Y12\)-signature.

If

\[
\mathcal G_Y=0,
\]

the entire \(Y12\)-route fails.

If

\[
\mathcal G_Y\neq0
\]

but \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) maps to zero, the route fails in the transition-to-singular chart.

If the class survives, downstream has a forbidden channel.

---

# 5. \(E_2\) can now be audited with one question

Previously \(E_2\) had many possible statuses. In this quotient framework, the key question is simply:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(E_2)
\text{ is fixed/coherent/small, or free?}
}
\]

If fixed, include it in the basepoint.

If small, absorb into the upper.

If free, it belongs in the tangent/model space and may kill the forbidden channel.

So the \(E_2\)-honesty audit becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }E_2\text{ create a free direction in }\mathcal G_Y?
}
\]

That is the right formulation.

---

# 6. How this interacts with \(\alpha\)

Similarly, \(\alpha\)-compatibility reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha u_{\sing}=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha^2u_{\sing}=0.
}
\]

If both vanish, admitting \(\alpha\) does not affect \(Y12\)-normality.

If not, \(\alpha\) enlarges the model space in \(\mathcal G_Y\) and may kill the D4 channel.

Thus the alpha audit is fully localized to \(\mathcal G_Y\).

---

# 7. The next useful theorem: filtered functoriality

Everything depends on the filtered functoriality of the transition map:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}.
\]

We need:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\left(\text{source-linear pieces}\right)
\subset \ker\Pi_Y,
\]

but

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\left([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)
\not\subset \ker\Pi_Y.
\]

This is the exact “singular-discard” distinction.

So the next finite theorem should be:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ preserves the source-Hessian/whitening filtration.}
}
\]

If it preserves the filtration, all source-linear pieces vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\), and \(Y12\) survives if its signature is not quotiented.

---

# 8. Resulting proof skeleton for downstream

Assume \(\mathcal G_Y\) satisfies the five axioms.

Then:

1. Actual rows are forbidden-small:

   \[
   \sum_p
   \|\Pi_YJ_{\sing,p}(q''_{\can})\|^2
   \le Q^{-B}\mu.
   \]

2. Good-subset extraction supplies centers with:

   \[
   E_1,E_{23}\ge Q^{-A},
   \]

   and wall anti-concentration for

   \[
   \ell(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
   \]

3. Local D4 gives:

   \[
   \|\Pi_Y\delta s_{\D4}\|^2
   \ge Q^{-A}\mu(G).
   \]

4. Atomic positivity gives a lower bound for the same sum as in (1).

5. Contradiction.

This is now a clean Type-I downstream theorem.

---

# 9. Remaining wall issue

The remaining nontrivial downstream input is anti-concentration of

\[
\ell(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If the covariant quotient removes \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\), then

\[
\ell(\Gamma)=\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

which is simpler.

But in the graded quotient formulation, \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) remains unless the \(C_7\)-induced part is killed in \(\mathcal G_Y\). So we need to know whether

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]=0.
\]

If yes, wall reduces to \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

If no, wall is a moving linear form.

This is a small but important finite audit.

---

# 10. Final current state

The downstream target has simplified to one graded quotient:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}
(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
}
\]

Required:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0,
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\text{ kills source-linear pieces, actual tangents, second EF curvature, lower connections, and free }E_2\text{ directions.}
}
\]

If so, \(Y12\) gives a rank-one forbidden channel and avoids the full \(K_9\)-conditioning problem.

This is the cleanest downstream formulation so far.

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The filtered-transition theorem is plausible, but it is a finite structural audit, not yet proved.}
}
\]

The route succeeds if

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

is filtered with respect to the refined signature

\[
\Sigma=(\deg_{\src},\operatorname{origin},\operatorname{branch},\operatorname{point},w,\pi,\rho,\operatorname{carrier}),
\]

and if the singular nuisance quotient does **not** kill the primitive \(Y12\)-graded class.

Under those assumptions:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
}
\]

and all source-linear pieces vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

The first hard yes/no is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in \mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal F^{<Y}
\quad\text{or not?}
}
\]

If yes, this \(Y12\) channel is rank-zero. If no, finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden target.

---

# 1. Refined filtration

Define the signature tuple

\[
\Sigma
=
(d,o,b,p,w,\pi,\rho,c)
\]

with:

\[
d=\text{source-Hessian degree},
\]

\[
o=\text{origin label},
\]

\[
b=\text{branch/mixed type},
\]

\[
p=\text{same-point versus transported},
\]

\[
w=\text{whitening-slot count},
\]

\[
\pi=\text{reflection/source-grade parity},
\]

\[
\rho=\text{singular order},
\]

\[
c=\text{carrier class}.
\]

For the \(Y12\) carrier,

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,\mathrm{same},1,\pi_Y,9,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Define a filtration

\[
\mathcal F^{<Y}\subset\mathcal F^{\le Y}\subset \mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

and the graded quotient

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\mathcal F^{\le Y}/\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

The projection

\[
\Pi_Y
\]

means the canonical map to this graded piece.

The origin label

\[
o=D^2N_0
\]

is essential. It separates \(Y12\) from superficially similar degree-two terms such as moving-whitening corrections or second parameter derivatives of EF-linear atoms.

---

# 2. Required filtered transition theorem

The transition map must satisfy:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}(\mathcal F^{<Y}_{\tr})
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}_{\sing},
\]

and

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}(\mathcal F^{\le Y}_{\tr})
\subset
\mathcal F^{\le Y}_{\sing}.
\]

In words:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ may mix singular orders and coordinates, but it must not change the origin label }D^2N_0
\text{ or create primitive }Y12\text{ signature from lower signatures.}
}
\]

This is plausible if the transition is built from:

* re-expansion;
* rescaling;
* chart transport;
* linear source representation changes;
* singular basis conversion.

These operations should not create a primitive nonlinear source-Hessian term

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

But this must be checked: if the transition map includes whitening/gauge differentiation in a way that applies \(D^2N_0\), it could contaminate the \(Y12\)-graded sector.

---

# 3. Source-linear annihilation

Under filtered transition,

\[
P_2,\quad D_2,\quad B_2
\]

and their source-linear tails have origin labels:

\[
o=\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear}
\quad\text{or}\quad
o=\gamma/\mathrm{background}.
\]

Therefore:

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(P_2)=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(D_2)=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(B_2)=0.
\]

Similarly,

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(P_2-P_{2,\le X})=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(D_2-D_{2,\le X})=0,
\]

provided the tails remain source-linear and do not pass through a nonlinear operation with \(D^2N_0\)-origin.

This is the key simplification behind \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\): most residuals vanish by signature, not by size.

---

# 4. \(Y12\)-survival

The desired survival statement is

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

The transition map may rescale or re-expand \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\), but if it is filtered it should preserve its primitive origin:

\[
o=D^2N_0,
\]

mixed branch \(12\), same-point whitening slot, and carrier identity.

Thus

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

should survive unless one of the following occurs:

1. the singular nuisance quotient contains the \(Y12\)-graded sector;
2. the transition map sends \(Y12\) entirely into lower filtered pieces;
3. the carrier identity \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) is reclassified as a coherent/tangent actual-source direction;
4. the chosen singular chart does not retain an order-nine \(Y12\)-graded coordinate at all.

So the finite rank-zero condition is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal F^{<Y}.
}
\]

If that does not happen, \(Y12\) gives the downstream channel.

---

# 5. Lower connection effects

We also need:

\[
\Pi_Y R_{7\to9}=0,
\]

\[
\Pi_Y R_{<7\to9}=0.
\]

Under the origin filtration, this holds if the lower connection maps are constructed from linear chart transport, coherent source recurrence, and singular re-expansion. Such maps may raise order seven to order nine, but should not create a primitive

\[
D^2N_0
\]

mixed branch same-point whitening class.

A failure would mean:

\[
R_{7\to9}
\]

or

\[
R_{<7\to9}
\]

contains an explicit primitive \(D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\)-origin correction. In that case \(Y12\) might be coherent with lower data rather than forbidden.

So the exact lower-connection audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}R_{7\to9}=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}R_{<7\to9}=0.
}
\]

This should be easier than full \(K_9\) computation.

---

# 6. Nuisance quotient risk

The most serious finite risk is the singular nuisance quotient.

If

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

contains primitive \(D^2N_0\), mixed \(12\), same-point, one-whitening, order-nine carriers, then

\[
\mathcal G_Y=0.
\]

Then both actual rows and D4 vanish in the quotient.

So the singular quotient must be designed to kill source-linear nuisance, chart artifacts, and completion/tail ambiguity, but **not** the primitive \(Y12\)-carrier class.

This gives a sharp constraint:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\notin\mathcal N_{\sing}.
}
\]

If the current singular quotient was defined too broadly and kills all quadratic mixed Hessian terms, the \(Y12\)-route fails by construction.

---

# 7. Moving-whitening contamination

A subtle contaminant is a term like

\[
D(W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]\,E_2(\xi_2),
\]

which may have:

* source degree two;
* one whitening slot;
* same-point structure.

But its origin is not

\[
D^2N_0.
\]

It is

\[
D(W^{-1/2})\cdot E_2.
\]

Therefore, with the refined origin label, it lies outside \(\mathcal G_Y\).

This is why origin tracking is essential. Without it, the \(Y12\)-graded sector would be polluted by ordinary moving-whitening/tail terms.

---

# 8. Consequence for \(E_2\)

With filtered transition, the \(E_2\) honesty audit becomes localized:

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}J_{\tr}(E_2)
\]

is dangerous only if \(E_2\) has origin

\[
D^2N_0
\]

with mixed \(12\), same-point, one-whitening slot.

If \(E_2\) is EF-linear, regularization, gamma/background, chart curvature, or moving-whitening times a tail, it should vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{only primitive }D^2N_0\text{-origin components of }E_2\text{ threaten }Y12.
}
\]

That narrows Agent 1’s \(E_2\) audit considerably.

---

# 9. The resulting downstream wall

If

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0,
\]

then

\[
\Pi_YD_{\D4}
=
H\,y_Y\,
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

where

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

Thus the forbidden wall factor is

\[
E_{\wall}^{Y}
=
\frac{
|H|^2
|y_Y|^2
|\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
}{
\mathcal N_{\D4}
}.
\]

The \(\Gamma_G\) term remains unless there is an additional quotient reason why

\[
\Pi_Y(\beta_9[\mathsf C_{Y12}])=0
\]

for the \(\Gamma_G\) branch. In the current filtration, no such reason is automatic.

So the honest wall is the moving linear form

\[
\ell_m(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

---

# 10. Final statement of the filtered-transition theorem

A clean theorem would be:

Let

\[
\mathcal G_Y=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\]

with

\[
\Sigma(Y12)=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,\mathrm{same},1,\pi_Y,9,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Assume:

1. \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) is filtered for \(\Sigma\);

2. all source-linear EF pieces lie in \(\ker \Pi_Y\);

3. lower connection maps satisfy

   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}R_{7\to9}
   =
   \operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}R_{<7\to9}=0;
   \]

4. the singular nuisance quotient does not kill \([\mathsf C_{Y12}]\);

5. \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) has nonzero \(\Sigma_Y\)-graded component.

Then:

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0,
\]

and source-linear actual rows vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

This establishes the filtered part of the \(Y12\)-forbidden channel.

---

# Final Agent 2 conclusion

The \(Y12\)-quotient route reduces to a finite filtered-transition audit.

The key yes/no is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal F^{<Y}.
}
\]

If true, and if the lower connections and \(E_2\) lack primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin \(Y12\) components, then finite D4 has a rank-one forbidden channel.

The origin label \(D^2N_0\) is the essential refinement that prevents moving-whitening, \(E_2\)-tail, and source-linear EF terms from polluting the \(Y12\)-graded quotient.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: invariance of the \(D^2N_0\)-origin label under \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\)

The previous step reduced the downstream channel to the filtered transition test

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal F^{<Y},
\]

where the \(Y12\)-graded signature is

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(2,D^2N_0,\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,\mathrm{same},1,\pi_Y,9,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

I now stress-test the most delicate part: whether the **origin label**

\[
o=D^2N_0
\]

is actually invariant under the transition-to-singular map.

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The }D^2N_0\text{-origin label is invariant if }T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ is built from linear re-expansion, scaling, and frozen whitening.}
}
\]

But:

\[
\boxed{
\text{it may fail if }T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ differentiates the whitening/source map or re-solves a nonlinear normal form.}
}
\]

So the filtered-transition theorem needs a structural hypothesis:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ is source-linear on origins and does not apply }D^2N_0.
}
\]

If this holds, then \(Y12\)-survival is likely. If not, \(Y12\) can be mixed into lower/tangent sectors and the quotient becomes ambiguous.

---

# 1. Decompose \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\)

Write the transition schematically as a composition

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
=
S_{\sing}\circ R_{\lambda}\circ C_{\tr},
\]

where:

* \(C_{\tr}\) extracts the transition local representative;
* \(R_{\lambda}\) rescales/re-expands the local variable into singular coordinates;
* \(S_{\sing}\) expresses the result in the singular basis or quotient.

The good case is when all three are **linear** on the already-formed source expression.

Then for any term \(Z\),

\[
\operatorname{Origin}(T_{\tr\to\sing}Z)
=
\operatorname{Origin}(Z).
\]

In particular,

\[
\operatorname{Origin}\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)
=
D^2N_0.
\]

And for any source-linear term \(L\),

\[
\operatorname{Origin}(T_{\tr\to\sing}L)
=
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\]

so

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}L=0.
\]

This gives the filtered theorem.

---

# 2. Bad case: transition recomputes normal form

If the singular chart is not just a re-expression of the transition source, but recomputes a normal form using the nonlinear map \(N_0\), then \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) can introduce terms like

\[
D^2N_0[\delta\xi_1,\delta\xi_2]
\]

from source-linear input variations.

Then a source-linear residual could acquire \(D^2N_0\)-origin **during transition**, and the statement

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}(\text{source-linear})=0
\]

would fail.

This is the main no-go mechanism for the signature proof.

So we need to know:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }T_{\tr\to\sing}\text{ merely transport a formed source, or does it recompute }N_0?
}
\]

If it recomputes \(N_0\), the \(Y12\)-origin label is not protected.

---

# 3. Whitening commutator contamination

Even if \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) is linear on the source, moving whitening can contaminate the signature.

Suppose the singular representative is whitened by

\[
\mathcal W_{\sing}^{-1/2}.
\]

Then

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}Z
=
\mathcal W_{\sing}^{-1/2} R_\lambda Z.
\]

If whitening is frozen at the basepoint, this is linear and origin-preserving.

But if the transition differentiates or varies the whitening map with the source,

\[
\delta(\mathcal W^{-1/2}Z)
=
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\delta \mathcal W]Z
+
\mathcal W^{-1/2}\delta Z.
\]

The first term has one whitening derivative slot. If \(Z\) is a source-linear residual, it can resemble:

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]\,Z(\xi_2).
\]

This has degree two and one whitening slot, but origin

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})\cdot \mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\]

not

\[
D^2N_0.
\]

So the refined origin label still separates it **provided the quotient remembers origin**.

Thus the signature must distinguish:

\[
\boxed{
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]\otimes D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})_{\same}
}
\]

from

\[
\boxed{
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]\otimes \mathrm{EF\text{-}linear}(\xi_2).
}
\]

If the singular quotient forgets this origin distinction, whitening commutator contamination can kill the \(Y12\) channel.

---

# 4. Required refinement of \(\Sigma_Y\)

The \(Y12\)-signature should be refined from

\[
(\deg_{\src}=2,\ w=1)
\]

to

\[
\boxed{
(\operatorname{origin}=D^2N_0,\ \operatorname{whitening\ role}=\text{external same-point slot}).
}
\]

That is, \(Y12\) is:

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})_{\same}
\left[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right],
\]

not:

\[
D^2(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1,\xi_2]\,N_0
\]

and not:

\[
D(\mathcal W^{-1/2})[\xi_1]\,DN_0[\xi_2].
\]

These three are all “degree two with whitening,” but they should occupy different graded pieces.

So define an origin-pair:

\[
(o_N,o_W),
\]

where:

\[
o_N=\text{nonlinear source origin},
\]

\[
o_W=\text{whitening origin}.
\]

For \(Y12\):

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,\ D\mathcal W_{\same}).
\]

This is more robust than a single origin label.

---

# 5. Filtered transition theorem in refined form

The desired theorem becomes:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

preserves the pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)
\]

up to lower filtration.

Specifically:

1. If \(o_N\neq D^2N_0\), then

   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_{Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}Z=0.
   \]

2. If \(o_W\neq D\mathcal W_{\same}\), then

   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_{Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}Z=0.
   \]

3. If \(Z=[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\), then

   \[
   \operatorname{gr}_{Y}T_{\tr\to\sing}Z\neq0.
   \]

This is the coordinate-free filtered transition theorem needed.

---

# 6. Interaction with \(E_2\)

This refinement makes the \(E_2\) audit sharper.

An \(E_2\) term is dangerous only if it has the same pair:

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,\ D\mathcal W_{\same})
\]

and the same mixed-branch \(12\), parity, and carrier class.

If \(E_2\) has:

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},D\mathcal W),
\]

or

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(DN_0,D^2\mathcal W),
\]

it is not \(Y12\).

So the no-free-\(E_2^{Y12}\) theorem can be narrowed to:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ has no free component with }
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same})
\text{ and mixed branch }12.
}
\]

This is much easier than excluding all degree-two completion effects.

---

# 7. Interaction with \(\alpha\)

Similarly, \(\alpha\)-variation is safe unless it changes either:

\[
o_N
\]

or

\[
o_W
\]

in the \(Y12\) way.

If

\[
\alpha
\]

only changes AFE weights/phases, then

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},0)
\]

for \(\partial_\alpha u\), and perhaps

\[
(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},0)
\]

again for \(\partial_\alpha^2u\). Then

\[
\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha u=\Pi_Y\partial_\alpha^2u=0.
\]

If \(\alpha\) changes whitening geometry, then \(\partial_\alpha^2u\) could have \(D\mathcal W\)-origin, but still not \(D^2N_0\)-origin unless it also changes \(N_0\).

Thus \(\alpha\) is compatible if:

\[
\boxed{
\partial_\alpha N_0=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\partial_\alpha D\mathcal W_{\same}
\text{ is not coupled to }D^2N_0\text{ mixed }12.
}
\]

---

# 8. A possible no-go: whitening is part of \(N_0\)

If the formalism treats the whitened nonlinear source as a single composite map

\[
\widetilde N_0
=
\mathcal W^{-1/2}N_0,
\]

then the distinction between

\[
D^2N_0\cdot D\mathcal W
\]

and

\[
DN_0\cdot D^2\mathcal W
\]

may be lost.

Then \(Y12\)-origin may not be invariant.

To preserve the downstream channel, the formalism must factor the composite source into:

\[
\boxed{
\text{nonlinear source map }N_0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\text{whitening map }\mathcal W^{-1/2}
}
\]

before taking the graded quotient.

If the draft’s carrier quotient works only with the composite \(\widetilde N_0\), then the origin filter may be unavailable.

This is a serious formal-structure audit.

---

# 9. Updated finite check

The finite transition audit should now test:

## C1. Source/whitening factorization

Can every order-nine carrier be assigned a pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)?
\]

## C2. Transition preservation

Does

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

preserve \((o_N,o_W)\) up to lower filtration?

## C3. \(Y12\)-survival

Does

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

have nonzero class with

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same})?
\]

## C4. Source-linear annihilation

Do \(P_2,D_2,B_2,E_2\)-linear tails have different \((o_N,o_W)\)?

## C5. Nuisance quotient

Does \(\mathcal N_{\sing}\) preserve the \((o_N,o_W)\) grading and avoid killing the \(Y12\) class?

If all pass, filtered transition is Green.

---

# 10. Final conclusion

The \(Y12\)-filtered transition theorem is viable if the formalism retains a refined origin pair:

\[
\boxed{
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,\ D\mathcal W_{\same}).
}
\]

This origin-pair label separates the true \(Y12\) carrier from:

* source-linear tails,
* moving-whitening times linear tails,
* chart curvature,
* second parameter curvature,
* gamma/background pieces,
* most \(E_2\) terms.

The main risk is that the current carrier formalism may have already collapsed \(N_0\) and whitening into a composite map, losing the invariant distinction. If so, the \(Y12\)-quotient must be refined before the normal-D4 route can be made rigorous.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: quotient compatibility of the \((o_N,o_W)\) origin-pair

The current proposed \(Y12\)-graded channel depends on retaining the refined origin-pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,\ D\mathcal W_{\same}).
\]

This separates the true \(Y12\) carrier from superficially similar terms such as

\[
D\mathcal W[\xi_1]\cdot E_2(\xi_2),
\qquad
D^2\mathcal W[\xi_1,\xi_2]\cdot N_0,
\qquad
D N_0[\xi]\text{ with log/phase weights}.
\]

The next question is whether this origin-pair survives the **singular nuisance quotient**. If the nuisance quotient identifies all degree-two whitening-bearing terms, then the \(Y12\) channel collapses. If it preserves origin-pair grading, the route remains viable.

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The }Y12\text{ route requires the nuisance quotient to be origin-pair filtered.}
}
\]

More precisely, the singular nuisance module must satisfy

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
=
\bigoplus_{(o_N,o_W)}
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{(o_N,o_W)}
\]

or at least

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap \mathcal F^{\le Y}
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}
\quad\text{unless it is explicitly intended to kill }Y12.
\]

If the nuisance quotient is not filtered, then

\[
[D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same}]
\]

may be identified with

\[
[DN_0,D^2\mathcal W],
\qquad
[\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},D\mathcal W],
\]

and the \(Y12\)-graded quotient is not well-defined.

So this is the next finite audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }\mathcal N_{\sing}\text{ preserve the }(o_N,o_W)\text{ filtration?}
}
\]

---

# 1. The quotient must be filtered, not merely linear

Let

\[
\mathcal Y_9
\]

be the raw order-nine singular carrier space. We want a quotient

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}
(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}).
\]

This is only well-defined if the nuisance module respects the filtration:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal F^{\le Y}
\]

has a controlled image in

\[
\mathcal F^{\le Y}/\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

There are three cases.

## Case A: filtered nuisance

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal F^{\le Y}
\subset
\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

Then \(Y12\) is not killed by nuisance, and

\[
\mathcal G_Y
\]

is well-defined.

## Case B: nuisance has a proper \(Y12\)-graded component

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

contains some, but not all, \(Y12\)-signature directions. Then the quotient must be refined to

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}/
(\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}).
\]

The route survives only if

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}.
\]

## Case C: nuisance kills the \(Y12\) sector

\[
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}
\subseteq \mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal G_Y=0,
\]

and the \(Y12\)-route is dead by definition.

So the finite question is sharpened to:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}.
}
\]

---

# 2. What nuisance terms are likely safe?

The following nuisance classes should be safe if the origin-pair filtration is respected.

## Source-linear nuisance

These have

\[
o_N=\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear}
\]

or

\[
o_N=DN_0,
\]

not

\[
D^2N_0.
\]

They lie below the \(Y12\) graded piece.

## Chart-coordinate nuisance

Coordinate changes and re-expansions may alter singular order, but they should not alter primitive nonlinear origin:

\[
o_N\neq D^2N_0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
o_N'\neq D^2N_0.
\]

Safe if transitions are filtered.

## Pure whitening nuisance

Terms such as

\[
D^2\mathcal W\cdot N_0,
\qquad
D\mathcal W\cdot DN_0
\]

have whitening nonlinearity but not primitive \(D^2N_0\) origin. They are safe only if the quotient remembers the \(o_N/o_W\) split.

## Tail/completion nuisance

Most tail and regularization terms are EF-linear or fixed background. Safe unless \(E_2\) has a free primitive \(D^2N_0\)-same-point component.

---

# 3. The dangerous nuisance class

The only dangerous nuisance class is

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y}
:=
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\cap
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}.
\]

If this contains the image of \(Y12\), then

\[
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]=0.
\]

Where could such a nuisance come from?

## A. If \(Y12\) was declared “source-model ambiguity”

If the singular chart treats primitive \(D^2N_0\) mixed Hessian terms as part of a flexible source-model ambiguity, then \(Y12\) is nuisance. This kills the channel.

## B. If all same-point whitening corrections are quotiented out

If the quotient says “ignore same-point whitening derivatives” wholesale, then \(Y12\) dies.

The quotient must instead kill only the coherent/tangent whitening corrections, not the primitive \(D^2N_0\)-coupled one.

## C. If \(E_2\)-free directions are included in nuisance

If \(E_2\) has an allowed free component in the same sector and the quotient absorbs it, \(Y12\) dies.

So the nuisance quotient must be narrower than “all residual order-nine same-point whitening terms.”

---

# 4. A safe quotient design

Define the raw order-nine space as a graded direct sum:

\[
\mathcal Y_9
=
\mathcal Y_9^{Y}
\oplus
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{lin}}
\oplus
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{whit}}
\oplus
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{bg}}
\oplus
\cdots
\]

where

\[
\mathcal Y_9^{Y}
=
\mathcal Y_9^{(D^2N_0,D\mathcal W_{\same},\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,[\mathsf C_{Y12}])}.
\]

Then impose

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
=
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{lin}}
\oplus
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{whit}}
\oplus
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{\mathrm{bg}}
\oplus
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y,\mathrm{coh}},
\]

where

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y,\mathrm{coh}}
\subsetneq
\mathcal Y_9^Y
\]

contains only actual-source coherent \(Y\)-directions, if any.

Then

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\mathcal Y_9^Y/\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y,\mathrm{coh}}.
\]

This makes the audit precise:

\[
[T_{\tr\to\sing}\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Y_9^Y/\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y,\mathrm{coh}}.
\]

---

# 5. Connection to actual-source tangent and curvature

The coherent \(Y\)-nuisance should be exactly

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}^{Y,\mathrm{coh}}
=
\Pi_Y
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}
\right).
\]

So instead of adding those terms externally, incorporate them into the \(Y\)-sector nuisance:

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\mathcal Y_9^Y/
\Pi_Y
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}
\right).
\]

Then the finite D4 question is simply:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\Pi_Y
\left(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}
\right).
}
\]

This is the final coordinate-free version.

---

# 6. Why origin labels help with \(E_2\)

The \(E_2\) term can now be audited inside \(\mathcal Y_9^Y\) only.

We do not care if \(E_2\) has large components in:

\[
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{lin}},
\quad
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{whit}},
\quad
\mathcal Y_9^{\mathrm{bg}},
\]

because \(\Pi_Y\) kills them.

We only care about:

\[
\Pi_Y E_2.
\]

Then:

* if \(\Pi_YE_2=0\), safe;
* if \(\Pi_YE_2\) is fixed, include it in the basepoint;
* if \(\Pi_YE_2\) is small, absorb it in \(\mathrm{EFCompletion}_{Y12}\);
* if \(\Pi_YE_2\) is free and spans \(\Pi_YC_{Y12}\), fatal.

This is much cleaner than trying to classify all of \(E_2\).

---

# 7. Does \(Y12\) survive if nuisance kills only tangent \(Y\)-directions?

Suppose

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y
\]

is one-dimensional and generated by

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Then if any coherent \(Y\)-nuisance is nonzero in that same one-dimensional space, it kills the channel.

So dimension matters.

If

\[
\dim\mathcal Y_9^Y=1,
\]

then the route needs:

\[
\Pi_Y(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free})=0.
\]

If

\[
\dim\mathcal Y_9^Y>1,
\]

then \(Y12\) may survive even if there are other coherent \(Y\)-directions.

Thus a useful finite check is:

\[
\boxed{
\dim\mathcal Y_9^Y
\quad\text{and}\quad
\operatorname{rank}
\Pi_Y
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}).
}
\]

If the rank is less than the dimension and \(Y12\) is outside the image, the channel survives.

---

# 8. Wall form after quotienting

Let

\[
\bar y_Y
\]

be the class of

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\]

in \(\mathcal G_Y\).

Then

\[
\Pi_YD_{\D4}
=
H\bar y_Y(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
+
H\bar y_7(c_7\Gamma_G)
\]

where

\[
\bar y_7
\]

is the class of \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7]\), if any.

If

\[
\bar y_7=0
\]

and

\[
\bar y_Y\neq0,
\]

then wall is clean:

\[
\ell(\Gamma)=\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

If additionally the \(\Gamma_G\)-associated \(Y12\) coefficient is killed by a refined carrier label, then

\[
\ell(\Gamma)=\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

But absent that extra refinement, keep the moving line.

---

# 9. Final finite audit list

To settle filtered transition and quotient compatibility, compute:

1. The \(Y\)-sector

   \[
   \mathcal Y_9^Y.
   \]

2. The coherent \(Y\)-nuisance image

   \[
   \mathcal C_Y
   =
   \Pi_Y
   (\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}).
   \]

3. The D4 \(Y12\)-class

   \[
   y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
   \]

4. The membership test

   \[
   y_Y\notin\mathcal C_Y.
   \]

This is the finite algebraic heart of the downstream route.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 status

The refined origin-pair grading is viable only if the singular nuisance quotient respects it.

The downstream \(Y12\) channel is equivalent to:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\Pi_Y
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}).
}
\]

This is the sharpest finite statement yet. It replaces generic \(\Lambda\), \(K_9\), and projection language with a single graded membership test.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: \(Y\)-sector rank test

The current downstream condition is the finite membership test

\[
y_Y
:=
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal C_Y,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal C_Y
=
\Pi_Y
(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free})
\subseteq
\mathcal Y_9^Y.
\]

I now reduce this to a rank audit inside the \(Y\)-sector.

---

# 1. The finite rank formulation

Let

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\]

with

\[
d_Y=\dim \mathcal Y_9^Y.
\]

Choose any \(Q^A\)-conditioned basis of \(\mathcal Y_9^Y\). Let

\[
M_Y
\]

be the matrix whose columns are the coherent \(Y\)-sector images:

\[
M_Y
=
\left[
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{\par}\ \big|\ 
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_7\ \big|\ 
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{<7}\ \big|\ 
\Pi_Y\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\ \big|\ 
\Pi_Y\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}
\right].
\]

Let

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Then the condition is

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}[M_Y\ y_Y]>\operatorname{rank}M_Y.
}
\]

Quantitatively, need

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(y_Y,\operatorname{ran}M_Y)
\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
}
\]

This is the complete finite downstream rank test.

---

# 2. One-dimensional \(Y\)-sector

If

\[
d_Y=1,
\]

then the test simplifies drastically.

Either:

\[
M_Y=0
\]

and

\[
y_Y\neq0,
\]

so the channel survives;

or

\[
M_Y\neq0,
\]

in which case

\[
\operatorname{ran}M_Y=\mathcal Y_9^Y
\]

and the channel dies.

Thus if the \(Y\)-sector is one-dimensional, the only acceptable outcome is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free})=0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
}
\]

So in dimension one, even a single free/coherent \(Y\)-direction kills the channel.

This makes the \(E_2\)-honesty and \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) audits especially critical.

---

# 3. Higher-dimensional \(Y\)-sector

If

\[
d_Y>1,
\]

then the channel can survive even if some coherent \(Y\)-directions exist.

For example, \(E_2\) might contribute a fixed/free \(Y\)-direction

\[
e_Y
\]

but if

\[
y_Y\notin \operatorname{span}\{e_Y\},
\]

then the D4 channel survives in the quotient

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y/\operatorname{span}\{e_Y\}.
\]

This is important because it weakens the \(E_2\)-risk.

The real criterion is not:

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0,
\]

but:

\[
\boxed{
y_Y\notin\operatorname{span}\Pi_Y(E_2^{\free},\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},\ldots).
}
\]

So the finite audit must compute dimensions and alignments, not merely nonzero/zero.

---

# 4. Natural decomposition of \(\mathcal Y_9^Y\)

The \(Y\)-sector may decompose further by labels:

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y
=
\bigoplus_{\eta}
\mathcal Y_9^{Y,\eta},
\]

where \(\eta\) could include:

* ordered polarization \(12\) versus symmetrized \(12+21\);
* same-point whitening side;
* branch orientation;
* source-grade parity;
* carrier subtype;
* \(\Gamma_G\)-slot versus \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-slot, if retained.

If

\[
y_Y
\]

lies in a sublabel

\[
\eta_0
\]

that coherent terms do not enter, then survival follows immediately.

Thus refine the rank test by projecting first to the smallest invariant sublabel containing \(Y12\):

\[
\mathcal Y_9^{Y,\eta_0}.
\]

Then test:

\[
\operatorname{rank}[M_{Y,\eta_0}\ y_Y]>
\operatorname{rank}M_{Y,\eta_0}.
\]

This may avoid a false failure caused by unrelated \(Y\)-origin directions.

---

# 5. The \(\Gamma\)-slot refinement

The D4 contribution through \(Y12\) is

\[
H\,y_Y
(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

If the \(Y\)-sector can distinguish the \(\Gamma_G\)-induced \(Y12\) occurrence from the \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-induced occurrence, then we can further split:

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y
=
\mathcal Y_{9,G}^Y\oplus \mathcal Y_{9,G2}^Y.
\]

Then the preferred channel is:

\[
y_{Y,G2}\neq0
\quad\text{mod coherent directions}.
\]

This would reduce the wall from moving form

\[
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\]

to the cleaner

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

But this refinement is valid only if \(\Gamma_G\)- and \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-slots are invariant labels of the carrier construction, not just coefficients multiplying the same vector.

If they are merely scalar coefficients on the same \(Y12\)-graded vector, they cannot be separated. Then the moving linear form is unavoidable.

So the finite question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }\mathcal G_Y\text{ distinguish the D4 source slot that multiplies }\Gamma_{G,2}\text{ from the one multiplying }\Gamma_G?
}
\]

If yes, wall simplifies. If no, use moving line.

---

# 6. Quantitative conditioning

The rank test needs polynomial conditioning.

Let

\[
P_{\mathcal C_Y}
\]

be orthogonal projection to the coherent subspace inside a Riesz basis for \(\mathcal Y_9^Y\). Need:

\[
\|(I-P_{\mathcal C_Y})y_Y\|\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
\]

If the angle is super-polynomially small, the quotient exists but is unusable.

Thus add:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{Angle}_{Y12}:
\quad
\angle(y_Y,\mathcal C_Y)\ge Q^{-A}.
}
\]

This is the quantitative version of survival.

Signature separation gives a strong angle, usually \(O(1)\), if it applies.

---

# 7. What can be proved by signature alone?

Signature alone proves survival if there is an invariant sublabel \(\eta_0\) such that:

\[
y_Y\in \mathcal Y_9^{Y,\eta_0},
\]

but

\[
\mathcal C_Y\cap \mathcal Y_9^{Y,\eta_0}=0.
\]

Then angle is automatically bounded below by the Riesz conditioning.

If coherent terms share the same \(\eta_0\), signature alone is insufficient, and one must compute actual coefficients/ranks.

So the practical path is:

1. refine signature labels as much as invariantly possible;
2. check whether coherent terms enter the same label;
3. only if they do, compute rank.

---

# 8. Application to \(E_2\)

For \(E_2\), the question is not simply:

\[
\Pi_YE_2\neq0?
\]

It is:

\[
\Pi_{Y,\eta_0}E_2^{\free}\neq0?
\]

where \(\eta_0\) is the \(Y12\)-carrier sublabel.

If \(E_2\) has \(Y\)-origin but different sublabel, it does not kill the channel.

If \(E_2\) has the same sublabel but fixed coefficient, it shifts basepoint but does not add to \(M_Y\). If free, it adds a column. Then test angle.

So \(E_2\) audit should return:

\[
\boxed{
\text{none / fixed same-label / free same-label / other-label.}
}
\]

Only free same-label is fatal in dimension one.

---

# 9. Application to actual second curvature

Similarly, \(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}\) may have primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin but different polarization or whitening sublabel.

Only the same sublabel matters.

So the second-curvature audit should check:

\[
\Pi_{Y,\eta_0}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}.
\]

If zero, safe.

If nonzero but not spanning \(y_Y\), rank may survive.

If spanning \(y_Y\), channel dies.

---

# 10. Refined theorem statement

Let

\[
\mathcal Y_{Y0}
\]

be the smallest invariant graded subspace containing

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

Let

\[
M_{Y0}
\]

be the coherent column matrix projected to \(\mathcal Y_{Y0}\).

If

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_Y,\operatorname{ran}M_{Y0})
\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|,
\]

then \(Y12\) survives with polynomial rank.

If

\[
\operatorname{ran}M_{Y0}=0,
\]

signature proves survival immediately.

If not, the finite rank test decides.

---

# Final Agent 2 status

The \(Y12\)-survival problem is now a finite rank/angle test inside the smallest invariant \(Y12\)-sublabel.

The decisive quantity is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\Pi_Y(\mathcal T_{\par}+\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free})
\right).
}
\]

Signature filtration may make this distance automatically polynomial by showing the coherent image is zero in the \(Y12\)-sublabel. If not, a concrete finite rank computation is needed.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: decision tree for \(Y12\)-survival

The current finite survival condition is

\[
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
y_Y,
\mathcal C_Y
\right)
\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|,
\]

where

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}],
\]

and

\[
\mathcal C_Y=
\Pi_Y(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}
).
\]

I now turn this into a practical decision tree. The point is to avoid doing full coefficient computations unless the signature filtration fails to separate the \(Y12\) class.

---

# 1. Stage 0: confirm the \(Y12\) sector exists

First check:

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma(Y12)}(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\neq0.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal Y_9^Y=0,
\]

the singular quotient kills the entire primitive \(Y12\) sector and the route is dead.

So the first yes/no is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does the singular nuisance quotient preserve a nonzero primitive }Y12\text{ sector?}
}
\]

If no, stop. Try another residual/order.

---

# 2. Stage 1: confirm \(Y12\) enters the sector

Check:

\[
y_Y=\Pi_YT_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0.
\]

If

\[
y_Y=0,
\]

then either:

* \(T_{\tr\to\sing}\) kills the carrier;
* it lowers it into a non-\(Y12\) signature;
* it sends it into singular nuisance;
* the transition chart is not suitable.

Then the \(Y12\)-route fails in this transition/singular pair.

If

\[
\|y_Y\|\ge Q^{-A},
\]

continue.

So second yes/no:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\text{ have a polynomially nonzero }Y12\text{-graded image?}
}
\]

---

# 3. Stage 2: same-sublabel coherent image

Let

\[
\mathcal Y_{Y0}
\subseteq\mathcal Y_9^Y
\]

be the smallest invariant sublabel containing \(y_Y\). For example, this might include:

* ordered mixed branch \(12\) rather than symmetrized \(12+21\);
* same-point side;
* whitening side;
* reflection/source-grade parity;
* carrier subtype;
* possibly \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-slot if invariant.

Project coherent directions to this sublabel:

\[
\mathcal C_{Y0}
=
P_{Y0}\mathcal C_Y.
\]

If

\[
\mathcal C_{Y0}=0,
\]

then survival is immediate:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(y_Y,\mathcal C_Y)=\|y_Y\|.
\]

So the easiest proof is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all coherent/tangent/}E_2\text{ directions miss the exact }Y12\text{ sublabel.}
}
\]

This is the signature-only green case.

---

# 4. Stage 3: dimension-one danger

If

\[
\dim\mathcal Y_{Y0}=1,
\]

then any nonzero coherent vector in \(\mathcal C_{Y0}\) spans the same line as \(y_Y\). Therefore:

\[
\mathcal C_{Y0}\neq0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
y_Y\in\mathcal C_Y.
\]

So in a one-dimensional \(Y12\)-sublabel, the route survives only if

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal C_{Y0}=0.
}
\]

This is especially relevant if the \(Y12\) sector has only one primitive carrier class. Then even a single free \(E_2\)-direction or actual second-curvature direction with the same sublabel kills the channel.

Thus if \(d_{Y0}=1\), prioritize proving:

\[
P_{Y0}\mathcal T_{\par}=0,
\]

\[
P_{Y0}\mathcal T_7=P_{Y0}\mathcal T_{<7}=0,
\]

\[
P_{Y0}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0,
\]

\[
P_{Y0}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}=0.
\]

---

# 5. Stage 4: higher-dimensional rank test

If

\[
d_{Y0}>1
\]

and

\[
\mathcal C_{Y0}\neq0,
\]

then perform the finite rank test.

Let

\[
M_{Y0}
\]

be a matrix whose columns span \(\mathcal C_{Y0}\) in a \(Q^A\)-conditioned basis.

Compute

\[
\Delta_Y
=
\|(I-P_{\operatorname{ran}M_{Y0}})y_Y\|.
\]

The survival condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\Delta_Y\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|.
}
\]

If the angle is polynomial, \(Y12\) survives.

If

\[
\Delta_Y=0
\]

or super-polynomially small, the \(Y12\)-route is unusable.

This is the coefficient/rank computation case.

---

# 6. Stage 5: identify the killer if rank fails

If

\[
y_Y\in\mathcal C_{Y0},
\]

do not just declare failure. Identify the component causing it.

## Killer A: \(E_2^{\free}\)

If

\[
y_Y\in P_{Y0}\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free},
\]

then completion supplies the same free direction. The \(Y12\) channel is not forbidden.

Possible response: refine canonical completion so that \(E_2\)'s \(Y12\)-piece is fixed, not free.

## Killer B: actual second curvature

If

\[
y_Y\in P_{Y0}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9},
\]

then \(Y12\) is legitimate actual-source curvature.

Possible response: check whether this curvature requires optional \(\alpha\). If yes, avoid admitting \(\alpha\) downstream.

## Killer C: lower connection

If

\[
y_Y\in P_{Y0}(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7}),
\]

then \(Y12\) is coherent with lower singular data.

Possible response: move to a higher residual or refine the carrier signature.

## Killer D: first tangent

If

\[
y_Y\in P_{Y0}\mathcal T_{\par},
\]

then \(Y12\) is first-order tangent. This is fatal for this channel.

---

# 7. Relation to the wall form

If \(Y12\) survives, the wall form is determined by the image of D4 in the quotient.

Let

\[
y_7=
P_{Y0}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_7],
\qquad
y_Y=
P_{Y0}T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}].
\]

The D4 quotient image is

\[
H\left[
\Gamma_G(c_7y_7+\beta_9y_Y)
+
\Gamma_{G,2}\alpha_9y_Y
\right].
\]

If

\[
y_7=0,
\]

then

\[
H y_Y(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2})
\]

is the wall form.

If additionally the \(\Gamma_G\)-slot is separated or \(\beta_9=0\), it reduces to \(\Gamma_{G,2}\).

If

\[
y_7\neq0
\]

and not collinear with \(y_Y\), the image may actually be rank two in \((\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})\), which is better than expected.

Thus after \(Y12\)-survival, compute:

\[
\operatorname{rank}[c_7y_7+\beta_9y_Y,\ \alpha_9y_Y].
\]

This decides whether wall anti-concentration is rank one or rank two.

Important: \(Y12\)-survival guarantees at least rank one because \(\alpha_9y_Y\neq0\).

---

# 8. Updated best-case scenario

The ideal outcome is:

\[
\mathcal C_{Y0}=0,
\]

\[
y_Y\neq0,
\]

and

\[
y_7=0.
\]

Then the quotient sees only:

\[
H y_Y(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

If a refined slot label kills the \(\beta_9\Gamma_G\) occurrence, even better:

\[
H y_Y\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}.
\]

Then the downstream wall theorem is just \(\Gamma_{G,2}\)-anti-concentration.

---

# 9. Worst-case survival with moving line

If

\[
Y12
\]

survives but

\[
y_7
\]

is collinear with

\[
y_Y,
\]

then wall is rank one with moving linear form:

\[
\ell(\Gamma)
=
(c_7\kappa_7+\beta_9)\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2},
\]

where

\[
y_7=\kappa_7y_Y.
\]

This is still usable if one proves moving-line anti-concentration.

So collinearity is not fatal.

The only fatal outcomes are:

\[
y_Y=0
\]

or

\[
y_Y\in\mathcal C_{Y0}.
\]

---

# 10. Final decision tree

The downstream finite audit should run:

1. Is \(\mathcal Y_{Y0}\neq0\)?
   * no: route dead.
   * yes: continue.

2. Is \(y_Y\neq0\)?
   * no: route dead.
   * yes: continue.

3. Is \(\mathcal C_{Y0}=0\)?
   * yes: route green.
   * no: continue.

4. Is \(\dim \mathcal Y_{Y0}=1\)?
   * yes: route dead unless \(\mathcal C_{Y0}=0\).
   * no: finite rank test.

5. Does \(y_Y\notin\mathcal C_{Y0}\) with polynomial angle?
   * yes: route green.
   * no: identify killer and either refine quotient or abandon \(Y12\).

6. Compute wall rank:
   \[
   \operatorname{rank}[c_7y_7+\beta_9y_Y,\alpha_9y_Y].
   \]

This is the practical finite program.

---

# Final Agent 2 status

The \(Y12\)-survival question has been reduced to a finite rank/angle decision tree in the smallest invariant \(Y12\)-sublabel.

The most important simplification:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If no coherent direction enters the exact }Y12\text{ sublabel, survival is automatic.}
}
\]

If coherent directions do enter, the dimension of the sublabel determines whether the channel is automatically killed or requires a finite rank computation.

## Agent 2 — signature-faithfulness of the singular quotient

I executed the Agent 2 instruction: test whether the refined \(Y12\) signature is an actual invariant of the singular quotient, rather than merely formal bookkeeping. The current prompt identifies this as the key guardrail: the singular nuisance quotient must preserve the origin-pair grading

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

or the \(Y12\)-channel may collapse with ordinary degree-two whitening or tail terms. 

# Verdict

\[
\boxed{
\text{The }Y12\text{ route is viable only if }\mathcal N_{\sing}\text{ is filtered/homogeneous near }\Sigma(Y12).
}
\]

The exact finite condition is:

\[
\boxed{
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\notin
\mathcal N_{\sing}
+
\mathcal F^{<Y}.
}
\]

Equivalently, there must exist a covector

\[
\ell_Y
\]

such that

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal F^{<Y})=0,
\]

but

\[
\ell_Y\!\left(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\right)\neq0.
\]

This is the signature-faithfulness theorem required before \(Y12\)-normality can be used downstream.

---

# 1. Formal signature decomposition

Define the formal order-nine carrier algebra

\[
\mathcal Y_9^{\rm form}
\]

with signature tuple

\[
\Sigma
=
(d,o_N,o_W,b,p,w,\pi,\rho,c),
\]

where:

\[
d=\text{source-Hessian degree},
\]

\[
o_N=\text{nonlinear source origin},
\]

\[
o_W=\text{whitening origin/role},
\]

\[
b=\text{branch/mixed type},
\]

\[
p=\text{same-point versus transported},
\]

\[
w=\text{whitening-slot count},
\]

\[
\pi=\text{reflection/source-grade parity},
\]

\[
\rho=\text{singular order},
\]

\[
c=\text{carrier class}.
\]

The \(Y12\) signature is

\[
\Sigma_Y
=
(2,D^2N_0,DW_{\same},\mathrm{mixed}\ 12,\mathrm{same},1,\pi_Y,9,[\mathsf C_{Y12}]).
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\rm form}
\]

be the formal \(Y12\)-sector.

The realization map is

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}:
\mathcal Y_9^{\rm form}
\to
\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Signature-faithfulness near \(\Sigma_Y\) means:

\[
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\]

does not identify the \(\Sigma_Y\)-sector with lower/different-origin sectors.

---

# 2. What signature-faithfulness requires

A sufficient condition is:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

is filtered with respect to \(\Sigma\). Concretely, if

\[
v_Y+v_{<Y}\in\mathcal N_{\sing},
\]

where

\[
v_Y\in\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\rm form},
\]

and

\[
v_{<Y}\in\mathcal F^{<Y},
\]

then

\[
v_Y\in\mathcal N_{\sing}
\quad\text{and}\quad
v_{<Y}\in\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

This prevents cancellation between a true \(Y12\)-origin carrier and unrelated degree-two/whitening terms.

The quotient then induces a well-defined graded piece

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}
(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\simeq
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\rm form}/
(\mathcal N_{\sing}\cap\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\rm form}).
\]

The downstream test becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\bigl(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\bigr)
\neq0
\quad\text{in }\mathcal G_Y.
}
\]

---

# 3. Dangerous quotient collapses

The route fails if the singular quotient identifies \(Y12\) with any of the following.

## A. Moving-whitening tail terms

Terms like

\[
D(W^{-1/2})[\xi]\,E_2(\eta)
\]

may have degree two and one whitening slot, but their origin-pair is

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},DW).
\]

They must not be identified with

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

## B. Pure whitening curvature

Terms like

\[
D^2(W^{-1/2})[\xi_1,\xi_2]\,N_0
\]

have origin-pair

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(N_0,D^2W),
\]

not

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

If the quotient only sees “degree two with whitening,” it over-collapses.

## C. \(\alpha\)- or \(\lambda\)-second derivatives of EF atoms

These are EF-parameter curvature terms:

\[
\partial_\alpha^2(\text{EF atom}),
\qquad
\partial_\lambda^2(\text{EF atom}),
\]

with origin

\[
o_N=\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear}.
\]

They should not enter the \(Y12\)-graded sector.

## D. Chart curvature

Coordinate curvature can be second-order, but unless the chart transition recomputes

\[
D^2N_0
\]

with mixed branch \(12\) and same-point whitening, it is not \(Y12\).

Thus the quotient must remember origin-pair, not merely degree/order.

---

# 4. Does Bottleneck C live-ness suffice?

No. Bottleneck C live-ness says that the \(Y12\)-type carrier is nonzero in a local D4/carrier quotient.

That gives something like:

\[
[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\neq0
\quad
\text{in the local live carrier quotient}.
\]

But the current downstream quotient is stricter:

\[
\mathcal Y_9/
(\mathcal N_{\sing}
+\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}).
\]

So Bottleneck C live-ness does **not** automatically imply \(Y12\)-forbiddenness. We still need to show \(Y12\) is not equivalent to an actual-source coherent/tangent direction after transition to the singular chart.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Bottleneck C live-ness is necessary but not sufficient.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Direct separating covector as fallback

If proving full quotient homogeneity is too hard, construct a covector directly:

\[
\ell_Y:\mathcal Y_9^{\rm form}\to\mathbb C
\]

such that:

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal T_{\par})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal T_7+\mathcal T_{<7})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_Y(\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free})=0,
\]

but

\[
\ell_Y(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}])\neq0.
\]

This is equivalent to the Schur separation test, but often easier than proving the whole quotient is graded.

A natural candidate is:

\[
\ell_Y=\text{“primitive }D^2N_0\text{-origin, mixed-12, same-point, one-whitening coefficient.”}
\]

If this functional is well-defined and kills nuisance, the channel survives.

---

# 6. Filtered transition interaction

Signature-faithfulness of the quotient is not enough. We also need

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\]

to be filtered.

That is:

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}
\left(\text{non-}D^2N_0\text{ origin}\right)
\subset
\text{non-}D^2N_0\text{ origin}
+
\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

If transition recomputes the nonlinear normal form \(N_0\), it can generate \(D^2N_0\)-origin terms from source-linear inputs and the filtration fails.

Thus the combined structural theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\circ T_{\tr\to\sing}
\text{ is filtered near }\Sigma_Y.
}
\]

This is the exact filtered-transition requirement.

---

# 7. The \(E_2\) implication

Under signature-faithfulness, the \(E_2\) audit simplifies.

We only need to inspect:

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma_Y} E_2.
\]

All other degree-two or whitening-like pieces with different origin-pair vanish automatically in

\[
\mathcal G_Y.
\]

So the no-free-\(E_2^{Y12}\) theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma_Y}E_2
\text{ is absent, fixed, or residual-controlled, not free.}
}
\]

If \(E_2\)'s apparent \(Y12\)-like terms have origin

\[
D(W^{-1/2})\cdot E_2
\]

rather than

\[
D^2N_0\cdot DW_{\same},
\]

they are harmless.

---

# 8. What would be a no-go?

The \(Y12\) route fails if any of these holds.

## No-go 1: quotient over-collapse

\[
\mathcal Y_{9,\Sigma_Y}^{\rm form}
\subseteq
\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

Then \(\mathcal G_Y=0\).

## No-go 2: transition kills \(Y12\)

\[
T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]
\in
\mathcal N_{\sing}+\mathcal F^{<Y}.
\]

Then \(y_Y=0\).

## No-go 3: actual-source coherent directions span \(Y12\)

Even if \(\mathcal G_Y\neq0\),

\[
y_Y\in
\Pi_Y(
\mathcal T_{\par}
+\mathcal T_7
+\mathcal T_{<7}
+\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}
+\mathcal T_{E_2}^{\free}).
\]

Then \(Y12\) is tangent/coherent, not forbidden.

## No-go 4: quotient not origin-pair filtered

If the quotient cannot distinguish

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

from other degree-two whitening origins, then the \(Y12\)-graded object is not well-defined. One would need a different invariant.

---

# 9. Finite theorem statement

A usable theorem is:

**SignatureFaithfulness\(_Y\).** There exists a finite quotient

\[
\mathcal G_Y
=
\operatorname{gr}_{\Sigma_Y}(\mathcal Y_9/\mathcal N_{\sing})
\]

such that:

1. \(\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}\circ T_{\tr\to\sing}\) is filtered near \(\Sigma_Y\);

2. \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\) has nonzero image in \(\mathcal G_Y\);

3. all non-\(Y12\) origin terms vanish in \(\mathcal G_Y\);

4. actual-source coherent/tangent/free-\(E_2\) \(Y\)-sector directions do not span the image of \(T_{\tr\to\sing}[\mathsf C_{Y12}]\).

Then the finite-D4 \(Y12\) forbidden channel is real.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 verdict

The refined \(Y12\)-signature is a valid downstream route only if the singular quotient is origin-pair faithful.

The next finite audit is not analytic; it is algebraic:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\text{ must preserve the }(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})\text{ graded sector.}
}
\]

If that holds, most contaminants vanish structurally, and the \(Y12\)-channel reduces to a finite Schur/angle test against actual-source coherent \(Y\)-directions.
