## Agent 2 — continued attack: local-vs-coherent test for \(E_2^Y\) and \(\mathfrak C_Y\)

For rank-two D4 we need the local fiber matrix

\[
B_Y^{\mathrm{matrix}}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
\ell_7(u_1)&\cdots&\ell_7(u_M)\\
\ell_9^Y(u_1)&\cdots&\ell_9^Y(u_M)
\end{pmatrix}
\]

to vanish on all actual-source/nuisance/local generators \(u_j\). Agent 2’s row is the \(s_9^Y\)-row:

\[
\ell_9^Y(u_j)=0.
\]

The remaining likely failure points are not ordinary parameters; they are:

\[
E_2^Y
\]

and the labelled-realization commutator

\[
\mathfrak C_Y.
\]

These can look like \(Y12\), but only kill rank two if they create **local fiber freedom**. Fixed or finite-coherent contributions are amplitude-level issues, not \(B_Y\)-columns.

---

# 1. Local versus amplitude-level criterion

A primitive \(Y12\)-like term \(Z_Y(p)\) should be routed as follows.

## Local fiber contaminant

It belongs to \(B_Y\) only if its coefficient can vary as a local tangent at the extraction scale:

\[
Z_Y(p)=a_p\,y_Y(p)
\]

with \(a_p\) independently variable across atoms or small blocks.

Then it contributes a column to

\[
B_Y.
\]

For rank two, this must be zero.

## Finite coherent amplitude

If

\[
Z_Y(p)=\sum_{k=1}^{K} a_k\phi_k(p)y_Y(p),
\qquad K\le Q^C,
\]

with the same coefficients \(a_k\) over a block or globally, then it does **not** contribute to \(B_Y\).

It belongs instead to

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}.
\]

This requires amplitude Schur later, but does not collapse local rank two.

## Fixed basepoint

If \(Z_Y\) is fixed, it just shifts the basepoint and does not enter \(B_Y\).

## Residual-controlled

If

\[
\sum_p |Z_Y(p)|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu,
\]

it enters \(R_X^Y\), not \(B_Y\).

So for the rank-two local audit:

\[
\boxed{
\text{finite coherent }Y12\text{ terms are not local }B_Y\text{ contaminants.}
}
\]

---

# 2. \(E_2^Y\) coefficient map

Let

\[
c_E(\theta;p)
=
\frac{\Pi_Y E_{\model}(\theta;p)}{y_Y(p)}
\]

where \(y_Y(p)\neq0\).

For fiber-local contamination, study derivatives with respect to actual local degrees of freedom:

\[
D_{\loc}c_E,
\qquad
D_{\loc}^2c_E.
\]

The rank-two target is:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}c_E=0,
\qquad
D_{\loc}^2c_E=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

If this holds, \(E_2^Y\) cannot produce a local \(s_9^Y\)-column.

---

# 3. Cases for \(E_2^Y\)

## Case A: absent

\[
\Pi_YE_2=0.
\]

Then:

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local})=0.
\]

Rank-two safe.

## Case B: fixed

\[
\Pi_YE_2=c_{\fixed}(p)y_Y(p)
\]

with \(c_{\fixed}\) determined by canonical data, not variable. This shifts the basepoint.

No \(B_Y\)-column.

Rank-two locally safe.

## Case C: finite coherent

\[
\Pi_YE_2=\sum_{k=1}^K a_k\phi_k(p)y_Y(p),
\qquad K\le Q^C.
\]

This enters

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}.
\]

No local \(B_Y\)-column unless the basis \(\phi_k\) has atom-local leverage.

Rank-two locally safe but amplitude Schur required.

## Case D: residual-controlled

\[
\Pi_YE_2=R_E^Y,
\qquad
\sum_p |R_E^Y(p)|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu.
\]

No \(B_Y\)-column.

Rank-two locally safe.

## Case E: locally free

\[
\Pi_YE_2(p)=a_p y_Y(p)
\]

with \(a_p\) free per atom/block at extraction scale.

Then:

\[
(0,1)\in B_Y
\]

unless tied to \(s_7\) through the same graph line. Either way, rank two fails. If vertical, current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y\text{ is rank-two safe iff it is absent/fixed/finite coherent/residual, not locally free.}
}
\]

---

# 4. What makes \(E_2^Y\) locally free?

Local freedom requires one of:

1. a local regularization parameter assigned independently per atom;
2. a tail completion basis with dimension comparable to atom count;
3. a data-dependent fitting rule using the local \(Y12\)-coefficient;
4. a nuisance convention that allows arbitrary primitive \(Y12\)-amplitude.

If \(E_2\) is canonical, deterministic, and depends only on global cutoff/regularization parameters, then it should be finite coherent or fixed, not local.

So the no-local-free theorem for \(E_2^Y\) is plausible if:

\[
\boxed{
E_2\text{ has no extraction-scale free parameters in the primitive }Y12\text{ sector.}
}
\]

---

# 5. Labelled commutator coefficient map

The labelled commutator is

\[
\mathfrak C_Y
=
\mathfrak C_Y^L+\mathfrak C_Y^N+\mathfrak C_Y^W.
\]

For each component, define a local coefficient

\[
c_{\mathfrak C}^{\bullet}(p)
=
\frac{\Pi_Y\mathfrak C_Y^\bullet(p)}{y_Y(p)}
\]

when meaningful.

Rank-two requires no local derivatives:

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^{\bullet}=0,
\qquad
D_{\loc}^2c_{\mathfrak C}^{\bullet}=0,
\]

or the component is zero/residual.

---

# 6. \(\mathfrak C_Y^L\): nonlinear source-input transition

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^L
=
\Pi_Y DN_{0,\sing}[D^2L(\xi_1,\xi_2)].
\]

If

\[
L
\]

is affine or linear in source variables, then

\[
D^2L=0
\]

and:

\[
\boxed{\mathfrak C_Y^L=0.}
\]

If

\[
D^2L\neq0,
\]

then the term has \(DN_0\)-origin, not \(D^2N_0\)-origin, unless the quotient forgets origin. Under origin-pair faithfulness, it should still vanish:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_YDN_0[D^2L]=0.
}
\]

Thus \(\mathfrak C_Y^L\) is expected rank-two safe.

Fatal only if \(D^2L\) plus realization is mislabelled as primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin and locally free.

---

# 7. \(\mathfrak C_Y^N\): tensorial normal-form defect

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(\xi_1,\xi_2)
=
\Pi_Y\left(
D^2N_{0,\sing}[L\xi_1,L\xi_2]
-
S D^2N_{0,\tr}[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right).
\]

This is the most serious labelled transition defect.

Outcomes:

## Zero

If \(D^2N_0\) is naturally transported:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N=0.
\]

Rank-two safe.

## Fixed/finitely coherent

If the defect is determined by fixed chart data and spans a finite-dimensional function family:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N\in\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}.
\]

No \(B_Y\)-column. Rank-two locally safe, amplitude Schur later.

## Local free

If the defect depends on local source variables enough to vary independently per atom:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(p)=a_p y_Y(p),
\]

then rank-two fails. If vertical, current \(Y12\) route may die locally.

Thus the precise audit is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^N=0\text{ or finite coherent, not locally free.}
}
\]

---

# 8. \(\mathfrak C_Y^W\): whitening transport defect

This term captures mismatch between transported \(DW_{\same}\) and raw singular whitening.

Moving-whitening artifacts include:

\[
DW[\eta]DN_0[\xi],
\]

\[
D^2W[\eta_1,\eta_2]N_0,
\]

or

\[
DW[\eta]E_2.
\]

These are not primitive

\[
DW_{\same}[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]]
\]

unless the quotient collapses origin labels.

Therefore under origin-pair faithfulness:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^W=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

If nonzero finite coherent, it joins

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}.
\]

If locally free, rank-two fails.

---

# 9. Combined local-zero theorem

A rank-two-safe theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}=0
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}=0.
}
\]

Expanded:

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=D_{\loc}^2c_E=0,
\]

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=
D_{\loc}^2c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=0,
\]

or the corresponding terms vanish/residual-control.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}+\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local})=0.
}
\]

If Agent 1 also proves the \(s_7\)-row vanishes, then these terms do not contribute to \(B_Y\).

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 update

The two most likely spoilers for the \(s_9^Y\)-row are \(E_2^Y\) and \(\mathfrak C_Y\).

They are rank-two safe if they are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{absent, fixed, finite coherent, or residual-controlled.}
}
\]

They are rank-two fatal if they are:

\[
\boxed{
\text{extraction-locally free in the primitive }Y12\text{ fiber.}
}
\]

Thus the concrete next tests are:

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=D_{\loc}^2c_E=0
\]

and

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=
D_{\loc}^2c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=0
\]

or finite-coherent/residual classification.

If these pass, Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row vanishing is strongly supported, leaving the decisive rank-two fork to Agent 1’s \(s_7\)-actualness audit.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: eliminating local freedom via “canonical dependence” tests

The previous step reduced the \(s_9^Y\)-row risk to the two plausible contaminants:

\[
E_2^Y
\]

and

\[
\mathfrak C_Y=\mathfrak C_Y^L+\mathfrak C_Y^N+\mathfrak C_Y^W.
\]

For rank-two D4, graph-like contamination is not enough. We need these local contaminant spaces to vanish in \(B_Y\). Finite coherent or fixed components may remain, but they must be routed to the amplitude layer \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), not to the local fiber \(B_Y\). This is aligned with the current rank-two fork: \(B_Y=0\) gives a vector D4 wall, while \(B_Y=\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y)\) collapses to the rank-one moving wall. 

The new question is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{How do we prove that an apparent }Y12\text{ component is finite/coherent rather than locally free?}
}
\]

The answer is to test whether its coefficient is a **canonical function of global/low-complexity data**, not an independently assignable local parameter.

---

# 1. Local freedom versus canonical dependence

Let \(Z_Y(p)\) be any \(Y12\)-graded contaminant, for example from \(E_2^Y\) or \(\mathfrak C_Y\). Write

\[
Z_Y(p)=c_Z(p)y_Y(p)
\]

where \(y_Y(p)\) is the live primitive \(Y12\) carrier.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
Z_Y\text{ is locally free}
}
\]

if the values

\[
c_Z(p)
\]

can vary independently across extraction atoms.

It is **not** locally free if

\[
c_Z(p)=\Phi_Z(\zeta(p))
\]

where \(\zeta\) is a fixed low-dimensional or smooth global data vector, and \(\Phi_Z\) is fixed before extraction.

Thus the actual test is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}_{\mathrm{local}}\{c_Z(p)\}_{p\in\Omega}
\ll |\Omega|
}
\]

on every relevant block \(\Omega\).

A sufficient condition is:

\[
c_Z\in \operatorname{span}\{\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_K\},
\qquad K\le Q^C,
\]

with bounded leverage on blocks. Then \(Z_Y\) belongs to \(\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\), not \(B_Y\).

---

# 2. \(E_2^Y\): canonical-tail dependence

For \(E_2^Y\), the coefficient is

\[
c_E(p)=\frac{\Pi_YE_{\model}(p)}{y_Y(p)}.
\]

A rank-two-safe theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
c_E\text{ is absent, fixed, residual-small, or belongs to a finite coherent space.}
}
\]

The no-local-free criterion is:

\[
\boxed{
c_E(p)\text{ is determined by canonical global tail/regularization data, not by an atom-local }Y12\text{ amplitude.}
}
\]

Concretely, if \(E_2\) is built from:

* fixed cutoff choice;
* fixed smoothing kernel;
* deterministic gamma/background correction;
* deterministic regularized tail;
* global finite-dimensional tail parameters;

then

\[
c_E\in\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}
\]

with finite/block-coherent dimension.

It is locally free only if \(E_2\) contains something like:

\[
\sum_p a_p\,\mathbf 1_p\,y_Y(p)
\]

or an equivalent atom-local basis.

So the \(E_2\) theorem should be phrased as:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{NoLocalFreeE2Y12}:
\quad
\dim \mathcal C_Y^{E_2}|_\Omega \le Q^C
\quad\text{and}\quad
\operatorname{lev}_\Omega(\mathcal C_Y^{E_2})\le Q^C.
}
\]

For rank two, this is enough to keep \(E_2^Y\) out of \(B_Y\). It may still create an amplitude Schur problem, but not a local-fiber problem.

---

# 3. \(E_2^Y\) derivatives: what must vanish locally

If \(E_2^Y\) is parameterized by actual local variables \(\theta\), then a local \(B_Y\)-column appears only if

\[
D_{\loc} c_E\neq0
\]

or

\[
D_{\loc}^2c_E\neq0
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

Thus the rank-two condition is:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}c_E=D_{\loc}^2c_E=0
}
\]

for extraction-local parameters.

But derivatives with respect to **global** cutoff or smoothing parameters do not create local fiber directions. They create finite coherent amplitude directions.

So we must distinguish:

\[
D_{\loc}
\]

from

\[
D_{\mathrm{global}}.
\]

The safe classification is:

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=0,
\qquad
D_{\mathrm{global}}c_E\in \mathcal C_Y^{E_2}.
\]

This is an important refinement. It prevents incorrectly treating global coherent \(E_2^Y\)-families as local \(B_Y\)-columns.

---

# 4. \(\mathfrak C_Y^L\): source-transition nonlinearity

Recall:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^L
=
\Pi_YDN_{0,\sing}[D^2L(\xi_1,\xi_2)].
\]

This term should vanish in the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber for either of two reasons.

## Reason A: \(L\) is affine

If

\[
D^2L=0,
\]

then

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^L=0.
\]

## Reason B: wrong origin

Even if

\[
D^2L\neq0,
\]

the term has \(DN_0\)-origin, not \(D^2N_0\)-origin.

So under origin-pair faithfulness,

\[
\Pi_YDN_0[D^2L]=0.
\]

Thus the theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^L=0
}
\]

unless the realization forgets the distinction between \(DN_0\)-origin and \(D^2N_0\)-origin.

If that distinction is lost, the quotient over-collapses and the \(Y12\)-route becomes suspect.

---

# 5. \(\mathfrak C_Y^N\): tensorial naturality defect

The central labelled-transition commutator is:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(\xi_1,\xi_2)
=
\Pi_Y
\left(
D^2N_{0,\sing}[L\xi_1,L\xi_2]
-
S D^2N_{0,\tr}[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right).
\]

This has the correct origin, so origin filtering alone cannot kill it.

The rank-two-safe options are:

## Zero tensorial defect

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N=0.
\]

This is the ideal labelled naturality theorem.

## Fixed/coherent tensorial defect

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(p)=\sum_{k=1}^K a_k\phi_k(p)y_Y(p),
\qquad K\le Q^C.
\]

Then it belongs to

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
\]

and does not enter \(B_Y\).

## Local-free tensorial defect

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(p)=a_p y_Y(p)
\]

with atom-local \(a_p\). Fatal for rank two; likely fatal for the rank-one \(Y12\) route unless graph-tied.

Thus the concrete test is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^N
\text{ has finite coherent coefficient rank over blocks.}
}
\]

If the transition \(L,S\) are fixed chart maps independent of atom-local source data, \(\mathfrak C_Y^N\) should be fixed or finite coherent.

If \(L,S\) are data-dependent per atom, local freedom becomes possible.

---

# 6. \(\mathfrak C_Y^W\): whitening defect

The whitening defect is subtle because it can have one whitening slot.

But the primitive \(Y12\) origin is specifically:

\[
DW_{\same}[D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]].
\]

Moving-whitening artifacts are typically:

\[
DW[\eta]DN_0[\xi],
\]

\[
D^2W[\eta_1,\eta_2]N_0,
\]

or

\[
DW[\eta]E_2.
\]

These have wrong origin pair.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^W=0
}
\]

if the quotient distinguishes:

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

from

\[
(DN_0,DW),
\quad
(N_0,D^2W),
\quad
(E_2,DW).
\]

If there is a true defect in transporting the same-point \(DW_{\same}\) slot attached to \(D^2N_0\), then it must be classified as finite coherent or local-free. Again, fixed chart whitening should make it finite coherent, not atom-local.

---

# 7. Rank-two-safe labelled theorem

A rank-two-safe version of LabelledRealizationConsistency\(_{Y12}\) is stronger than the rank-one version.

It requires:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}=0.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^L
=
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^N
=
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^W
=0
}
\]

and similarly for second local derivatives.

But it allows:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^\bullet\in \mathcal C_Y^{\lab}
\]

finite coherent.

So the exact rank-two-safe theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y
=
c_{\mathrm{fixed}}
+
c_{\mathrm{coh}}
+
c_{\small},
\quad
\text{with no local tangent component}.
}
\]

This keeps \(B_Y=0\) possible but may leave amplitude Schur.

---

# 8. Combined rank-two local-zero criterion

For Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row, a complete rank-two local-zero theorem is:

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{\par})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{<7}^{\arith})=0,
\]

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=D_{\loc}^2c_E=0,
\]

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=
D_{\loc}^2c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=0,
\]

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0.
\]

Then

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{s_9^Y}B_Y=0.
}
\]

If Agent 1 proves the corresponding \(s_7\)-row zero, then

\[
B_Y=0
\]

and rank-two D4 survives.

---

# 9. If zero fails, classify precisely

If a local contaminant is nonzero, classify its fiber vector.

For any local contaminant \(u\):

\[
\mathbf b_Y(u)=
(\ell_7(u),\ell_9^Y(u)).
\]

## Graph

\[
\mathbf b_Y(u)\in\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Rank two collapses to rank one, but \(Y12\) may survive.

## Vertical

\[
\ell_7(u)=0,\quad \ell_9^Y(u)\neq0.
\]

Local vertical freedom. Current \(Y12\) route dies.

## Second slope

There exists another contaminant \(v\) such that

\[
\Delta_Y(u,v)\neq0.
\]

Local fiber absorbed. Current route dies.

## Finite coherent

If the apparent nonzero term is not local but finite coherent over blocks, keep \(B_Y=0\) and route it to amplitude Schur.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 update

The main remaining \(s_9^Y\)-row threats are not ordinary source parameters. They are:

\[
E_2^Y
\]

and

\[
\mathfrak C_Y.
\]

Both are rank-two safe if their primitive \(Y12\) coefficients are canonical/fixed/finite coherent/residual-small rather than extraction-local.

The concrete tests are:

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=D_{\loc}^2c_E=0,
\]

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=
D_{\loc}^2c_{\mathfrak C}^{L,N,W}
=0.
\]

If these hold, Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row vanishes. The rank-two decision then reduces primarily to Agent 1’s question:

\[
\ell_7(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})=0\quad\text{or not?}
\]

## Agent 2 — continued attack: proving local-zero from “canonical coefficient” structure

The previous reduction was:

\[
\Pi_{s_9^Y}B_Y=0
\]

will follow if the only possible \(s_9^Y\)-contributors, namely

\[
E_2^Y
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathfrak C_Y,
\]

are canonical/fixed/finite-coherent/residual-small rather than extraction-local.

The current question is how to prove that. I now push the proof criterion one level lower.

---

# 1. Local freedom requires local parameters

A \(Y12\)-coefficient

\[
c_Z(p)
\]

is locally free only if the construction contains a parameter or representative choice that can vary independently with the extraction atom \(p\).

So for both

\[
E_2^Y
\]

and

\[
\mathfrak C_Y,
\]

the key test is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{does the construction contain atom-indexed free data?}
}
\]

If every ingredient is determined by:

\[
m,\lambda,\text{fixed cutoff},\text{fixed smoothing},\text{fixed chart},\text{fixed global EF/gamma data},
\]

then \(c_Z(p)\) is a deterministic function of low-complexity variables. It may be nonzero, but it is not locally free.

Thus the no-local-free theorem should be proved by a **parameter-count / dependence audit**, not by estimating sizes.

---

# 2. Canonical coefficient lemma

A useful lemma:

**CanonicalCoefficient Lemma.**  
Let

\[
c_Z(p)
\]

be the primitive \(Y12\)-coefficient of a contaminant \(Z\). Suppose:

1. \(Z\) is determined by a fixed formula from a finite list of canonical variables

   \[
   \zeta(p)=(\zeta_1(p),\ldots,\zeta_K(p)),
   \qquad K=O(1)\text{ or }Q^{o(1)}.
   \]

2. Each \(\zeta_j\) has bounded branch-charge / bounded local complexity.

3. No coefficient in the formula is chosen after extraction.

Then

\[
c_Z(p)=\Phi_Z(\zeta(p))
\]

spans a finite coherent amplitude space, not a local fiber tangent.

Therefore

\[
Z
\]

does not contribute to \(B_Y\). It contributes only to

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
\]

or to a residual term.

This lemma is exactly what we need for \(E_2^Y\) and \(\mathfrak C_Y\).

---

# 3. Apply to \(E_2^Y\)

Write

\[
E_2^Y(p)=c_E(p)y_Y(p).
\]

The dangerous case is:

\[
c_E(p)=a_p
\]

with \(a_p\) freely assignable per atom.

But if \(E_2\) is a canonical completion, then \(c_E\) should be determined by:

\[
m_p,\quad \lambda_p,\quad Q_p,\quad \text{fixed cutoff},\quad \text{fixed smoothing},\quad \text{gamma/background data}.
\]

So

\[
c_E(p)=\Phi_E(m_p,\lambda_p,Q_p,\ldots).
\]

That is coherent.

Therefore the rank-two local-zero statement for \(E_2^Y\) becomes:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y\text{ has no atom-indexed free coefficient.}
}
\]

Not:

\[
\boxed{
E_2^Y=0.
}
\]

The sharper routing is:

\[
E_2^Y=0
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{no amplitude issue;}
\]

\[
E_2^Y=\text{fixed/coherent}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\mathcal C_Y^{E_2}\text{ amplitude model;}
\]

\[
E_2^Y=\text{locally free}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
B_Y\text{ contamination or route death.}
\]

So the immediate proof target is:

\[
\boxed{
D_{\loc}c_E=0
}
\]

where \(D_{\loc}\) means derivative with respect to extraction-local representative choices, not derivative with respect to \(m\) or global canonical variables.

---

# 4. Apply to \(\mathfrak C_Y^N\)

Recall:

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N
=
\Pi_Y
\left(
D^2N_{0,\sing}[L\xi_1,L\xi_2]
-
S D^2N_{0,\tr}[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\right).
\]

Let

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^N(p)=c_N(p)y_Y(p).
\]

If \(L\) and \(S\) are fixed labelled chart maps, then

\[
c_N(p)
\]

is determined by the chart transition and canonical variables. It is not locally free.

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathfrak C_Y^N\text{ contributes to }\mathcal C_Y^{\lab}\text{ at worst, not }B_Y.
}
\]

The only dangerous case is if \(L\) or \(S\) is fitted locally from the actual row in a way that can adjust \(c_N(p)\) per atom.

So the finite audit is:

\[
\boxed{
L,S\text{ fixed before extraction?}
}
\]

If yes, no local \(B_Y\)-column.

If no, classify the local fitting dimension.

---

# 5. Apply to \(\mathfrak C_Y^L\)

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^L
=
\Pi_YDN_{0,\sing}[D^2L(\xi_1,\xi_2)].
\]

There are two independent reasons this should not enter \(B_Y\).

First, if

\[
L
\]

is affine in source variables, then

\[
D^2L=0
\]

and

\[
\mathfrak C_Y^L=0.
\]

Second, even if

\[
D^2L\neq0,
\]

this term has

\[
DN_0
\]

origin, not

\[
D^2N_0
\]

origin. If origin-pair labels are faithful, then

\[
\Pi_Y\mathfrak C_Y^L=0.
\]

So this term is dangerous only under simultaneous failure of:

\[
D^2L=0
\]

and

\[
\text{origin-pair faithfulness}.
\]

That makes \(\mathfrak C_Y^L\) a lower-priority threat than \(\mathfrak C_Y^N\).

---

# 6. Apply to \(\mathfrak C_Y^W\)

\(\mathfrak C_Y^W\) is the whitening transport defect.

Moving-whitening terms can look close to \(Y12\), but their origin is usually one of:

\[
(DN_0,DW),
\]

\[
(N_0,D^2W),
\]

\[
(E_2,DW).
\]

The true \(Y12\) origin pair is:

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_Y\mathfrak C_Y^W=0
}
\]

if the quotient remembers origin-pair.

If it does not, this is not merely a labelled-realization defect. It is a quotient-faithfulness failure.

So \(\mathfrak C_Y^W\) reduces to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Does }\Pi_Y\text{ distinguish }DW_{\same}[D^2N_0]\text{ from }DW[DN_0]\text{ and }D^2W[N_0]?
}
\]

If yes, no local \(B_Y\)-column.

---

# 7. Rank-two-safe theorem for contaminants

A combined theorem:

**NoLocalY12Contaminants.**  
Assume:

1. \(E_2^Y\) has no extraction-local free coefficient:

   \[
   D_{\loc}c_E=0.
   \]

2. \(L,S\) in the normal-form commutator are fixed labelled chart maps before extraction.

3. \(L\) is affine in source variables, or \(DN_0[D^2L]\) has wrong origin.

4. The singular quotient preserves the origin pair

   \[
   (D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
   \]

5. Truncation/tail \(Y12\)-remainders are residual-controlled.

Then

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}
(\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
+
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local})
=0.
}
\]

Thus these terms do not contribute to \(B_Y\). They contribute only to:

\[
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}
\]

or

\[
R_X^Y.
\]

---

# 8. Opportunity: rank-two with finite coherent amplitude

If this theorem holds but \(E_2^Y\) or \(\mathfrak C_Y\) is finite coherent, then rank-two local D4 still survives:

\[
B_Y=0
\]

may hold.

The downstream wall becomes vector-valued:

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
\]

modulo a finite coherent amplitude space

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}.
\]

So the needed amplitude theorem is vector Schur:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}_{L^2(\nu)}
(F_{2Y},\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith})^2
\ge
Q^{-A}\|F_{2Y}\|^2.
}
\]

This is still much easier than the rank-one moving-line trap, because the target is a two-component anisotropy rather than a scalar cancellation line.

---

# 9. What remains for Agent 2

Agent 2’s part of \(B_Y=0\) is now reduced to the following claims.

## Ordinary actual parameters

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{\par})=0.
\]

## Actual second curvature

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0
\]

under fixed-\(\alpha\) slot-rank exclusion.

## Lower \(<7\)

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{<7}^{\arith})=0
\]

by first-live primitive \(Y12\).

## \(E_2^Y\)

\[
D_{\loc}c_E=0
\]

or finite coherent/residual routing.

## Labelled commutator

\[
D_{\loc}c_{\mathfrak C}=0
\]

or finite coherent/residual routing.

## Nuisance

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal N_{\sing})=0
\]

by quotient faithfulness.

If all hold, the \(s_9^Y\)-row of \(B_Y\) vanishes.

---

# 10. Final status

The rank-two route depends on proving that all apparent \(Y12\)-contaminants are **canonical**, not **local**.

The criterion is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{canonical/fixed/finite coherent/residual }Y12
\Rightarrow
\mathcal C_Y^{\arith}\text{ or }R_X^Y;
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{extraction-local free }Y12
\Rightarrow
B_Y\text{ contaminant/no-go.}
}
\]

This makes the \(s_9^Y\)-row vanishing theorem plausible. The remaining decisive fork for rank two is whether the \(s_7\)-row also vanishes:

\[
\ell_7(T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith})=0.
\]

## Agent 2 — continued attack: a rank-two-safe upper/lower package

The \(s_9^Y\)-row audit now suggests a favorable route:

\[
\Pi_{s_9^Y}B_Y=0
\]

if all primitive \(Y12\)-looking contaminants are canonical/fixed/finite coherent/residual rather than extraction-local. The decisive remaining rank-two fork is the \(s_7\)-row:

\[
\Pi_{s_7}B_Y=0?
\]

Assume for this attack that both rows vanish:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Then the local D4 channel is rank two. I now push the downstream package in that branch.

---

# 1. Rank-two local D4 output

With

\[
B_Y=0,
\]

nothing is quotiented out in the live two-dimensional fiber

\[
(s_7,s_9^Y).
\]

D4 gives

\[
s_7^{D4}=Hc_7\Gamma_G,
\]

\[
s_9^{Y,D4}=H(\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}).
\]

So the vector wall is

\[
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Because

\[
c_7\alpha_9\neq0,
\]

and assuming coefficient conditioning

\[
|c_7|,|\alpha_9|\ge Q^{-A},
\qquad
|c_7|,|\alpha_9|,|\beta_9|\le Q^A,
\]

we have

\[
\boxed{
\|F_{2Y}\|^2
\asymp_{Q^A}
|H|^2\left(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2\right).
}
\]

So the moving-line obstruction disappears.

No \(\rho_Y\), no \(\rho_\Gamma\), no scalar wall ODE is needed in the rank-two branch.

---

# 2. Rank-two contradiction-ready operator

Let

\[
\Pi_{2Y}
\]

denote the two-coordinate live-fiber projection

\[
v\mapsto
(\ell_7(v),\ell_9^Y(v)).
\]

Define the raw rank-two operator

\[
\Lambda_{2Y}^{0}
=
\Pi_{2Y}
\operatorname{Real}_{\sing}
\widetilde T_{\tr\to\sing}^{\lab}
P_{\tr}.
\]

If there are finite coherent rank-two amplitude spaces, define

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
=
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{E_2}
+
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\lab}
+
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\param}
+\cdots.
\]

Then define a pre-extraction projection

\[
P_{2Y}^0:L^2(\nu_0;\mathbf C^2)\to\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}.
\]

The final contradiction-ready rank-two operator is

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{2Y}^{\eff}
=
(I-P_{2Y}^0)\Lambda_{2Y}^{0}.
}
\]

This mirrors the rank-one construction, but now the target is vector-valued.

---

# 3. Actual-row upper

The actual-row upper should be:

\[
\boxed{
\|\Lambda_{2Y}^{\eff}J_{\tr}(q''_{\can})\|_{L^2(\nu_0)}^2
\le Q^{-B}\mu.
}
\]

It follows if:

1. source-linear/gamma/background pieces have no live \(C_7\) or primitive \(Y12\) coordinate;

2. labelled-realization mismatch satisfies

   \[
   \Pi_{2Y}R_{\lab}
   \in \mathcal C_{2Y}^{\lab}+\text{small};
   \]

3. \(E_2^{2Y}\) satisfies

   \[
   \Pi_{2Y}E_2
   \in \mathcal C_{2Y}^{E_2}+\text{small};
   \]

4. the residual small part obeys targeted completion:

   \[
   \sum_p\|\Pi_{2Y}R_{X,p}\|^2\le Q^{-B}\mu.
   \]

This is stronger than the scalar \(Y12\) upper only insofar as it also includes \(s_7\). But if \(s_7\) has no actual-source component, its upper should be a structural zero.

---

# 4. D4 lower after projection

The raw D4 lower is

\[
F_{2Y}.
\]

After coherent amplitude projection:

\[
F_{2Y}^{\eff}
=
(I-P_{2Y}^{0})F_{2Y}.
\]

The amplitude Schur theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\|F_{2Y}^{\eff}\|_{L^2(\nu_0)}^2
\ge
Q^{-A}
\|F_{2Y}\|_{L^2(\nu_0)}^2.
}
\]

If

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0
\]

or only fixed basepoint, then

\[
F_{2Y}^{\eff}=F_{2Y}
\]

and no amplitude Schur is needed.

If

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
\]

is finite coherent, then amplitude Schur is a vector finite-model nonabsorption theorem.

If it is locally free, rank-two route dies at amplitude level.

---

# 5. Rank-two wall theorem

In the best case,

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0,
\]

the wall theorem is the aggregate anisotropy lower:

\[
\boxed{
\int |H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
\,d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

Or capped:

\[
\boxed{
\int
\min\left(
Q^A|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right),
1
\right)d\nu
\ge Q^{-A}\nu(\mathcal C).
}
\]

This is easier than the scalar moving-wall theorem because it requires both \(\Gamma_G\) and \(\Gamma_{G,2}\) to be small to fail.

The failure condition is now:

\[
\Gamma_G\approx0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0,
\]

not

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G\approx0.
\]

Thus no forced fourth-order scalar wall ODE is created at this layer.

---

# 6. What replaces the ODE trap?

Rank-two failure produces a codimension-two degeneracy:

\[
\Gamma_G\approx0,
\qquad
\Gamma_{G,2}\approx0.
\]

If this occurs on large mass, it is a stronger structural lock. It likely corresponds to simultaneous low-order curvature/torsion vanishing.

The possible fallback theorem is not the scalar wall ODE trap; it is a **joint-vanishing anti-lock** theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{D4VectorWallAntiLock}:
(\Gamma_G,\Gamma_{G,2})
\text{ cannot both be small on polynomial mass.}
}
\]

This may be much closer to existing raw anisotropy or Gate–2 extraction estimates.

So rank two replaces:

\[
\text{moving wall finite type / forced ODE}
\]

with:

\[
\text{joint sublevel / raw anisotropy}.
\]

This is a major simplification.

---

# 7. O2/O3 under rank two

If \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}=0\), the wall weight for O2/O3 can be

\[
W_{\wall}^{2Y}
=
|H|^2
\left(
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\right)
\]

up to coefficient conditioning.

Then

\[
d_0^{(2Y)}
=
d_N+2d_H+2d_{\Gamma,2Y},
\]

where

\[
d_{\Gamma,2Y}
=
\max\{d(\Gamma_G),d(\Gamma_{G,2})\}.
\]

No \(\rho_\Gamma\) enters.

If \(\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}\neq0\), use the raw vector wall for O2/O3, then apply vector amplitude Schur before extraction, just as in the scalar case.

The conservative central transition requirement

\[
\mathbb C_{d_{\max}}[y]\subset V_{\tr}
\]

with \(d_{\max}=14\) remains enough if

\[
d_0^{(2Y)}\le d_{\max}.
\]

---

# 8. Relation to existing raw anisotropy

Earlier raw anisotropy

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

was insufficient for rank one because a moving line could cancel:

\[
\Gamma_{G,2}+\rho_\Gamma\Gamma_G\approx0.
\]

In rank two, raw anisotropy is exactly the wall.

So any existing theorem/experiment/extraction lemma proving polynomial mass for

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

becomes directly useful.

This is the most important practical benefit of rank two.

---

# 9. Remaining blockers in rank-two branch

Rank two still requires:

## A. Live dual pair

\[
e_7,e_Y
\]

must survive with polynomial conditioning.

## B. \(B_Y=0\)

No actual-source/nuisance/local fiber motion in either coordinate.

## C. Coefficient conditioning

\[
c_7,\alpha_9
\]

nonzero and bounded.

## D. No locally free vector amplitude space

\[
\mathcal C_{2Y}^{\arith}
\]

must be zero/fixed or finite coherent, not local.

## E. Vector wall anisotropy

\[
|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2
\]

must have polynomial mass.

These are all more concrete than the rank-one wall tower.

---

# 10. Final status

If Agent 1 proves the \(s_7\)-row zero and Agent 2 proves the \(s_9^Y\)-row zero, then

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Then the downstream D4 route should switch to rank two:

\[
\boxed{
F_{2Y}
=
H
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7\Gamma_G\\
\beta_9\Gamma_G+\alpha_9\Gamma_{G,2}
\end{pmatrix}
}
\]

with wall energy equivalent to

\[
\boxed{
|H|^2(|\Gamma_G|^2+|\Gamma_{G,2}|^2).
}
\]

This bypasses \(\rho_\Gamma\), scalar moving-wall finite type, and forced ODE trap.

So the current highest-value proof target remains:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

Agent 2’s contribution is that the \(s_9^Y\)-row vanishes if \(E_2^Y\) and \(\mathfrak C_Y\) are canonical/coherent rather than locally free, and if fixed-\(\alpha\) actual second curvature fails the primitive slot-rank membership.

not generic Taylor or jet motion.

# 1. Fixed-target actual parameters

# 2. First tangents avoid the live fiber

For first tangents

\[
u_\theta=\partial_\theta q''_{\can},

we need

\[
\ell_7(u_\theta)=0,
\qquad
\ell_9^Y(u_\theta)=0.

## 2.1 The \(\ell_7\)-row

\[
\ell_7
\]

does not read an arbitrary order-seven Taylor coefficient. It reads the live \(C_7\)-carrier class in the two-grade quotient.

\[
[z^7]\partial_\theta q''_{\can}\neq0,

that does not imply

\[
\ell_7(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})\neq0.

The actual first tangents are EF-linear, gamma/background, chart, or normalization-origin motions. They should lie in the order-seven denominator

\[
\mathcal D_7
=
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}}
+
\mathcal N_7
+\cdots.

\boxed{

\quad\text{in}\quad

}

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})=0.

\boxed{
\text{raw order-seven source drift}
\neq
\text{live }C_7\text{ carrier motion}.

## 3.1 The \(\ell_7\)-row

Second parameter curvature can create raw order-seven terms, but those are still EF-curvature, chart-curvature, normalization-curvature, or gamma/background-curvature terms.

+

+

\mathcal D_7.

\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.

So actual second curvature does not generate live \(C_7\) motion unless the actual-source model includes free corrected-package coordinates, which it should not.

## 3.2 The \(\ell_9^Y\)-row

The dangerous possibility is:

\[
u_{ij}
\]

has primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin in the \(Y12\) slots.

V_1=P_1V_{\arith},
\qquad
V_2=P_2V_{\arith},
\qquad
V_W=P_WV_{\arith}.
\]

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

For fixed-\(\alpha\), actual variations are diagonal/source-linear deformations of the same EF/gamma/chart data. They should not independently span:

\[
a_Y,\quad \xi_1,\quad \xi_2.
\]

Thus the expected finite slot-rank result is:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin V_W
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_1\notin V_1
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_2\notin V_2.
}

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(u_{ij})=0.
}
\]

This gives:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}=0.

# 4. The \(\alpha\)-fork

If \(\alpha\) is only an O2/O3 proof-thickening parameter, then it is excluded from

\[
V_{\arith}

and from

T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}.

\notin
DW_{\same}[P_WV_{\arith}^{+\alpha}]
D^2N_0[P_1V_{\arith}^{+\alpha},P_2V_{\arith}^{+\alpha}].
\]

Then \(\alpha\) does not hit the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber.

## Graph-like

The resulting fiber image is contained in

\[
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Then rank two collapses to rank one, but \(Y12\) may survive.

## Vertical or second slope

The local \(Y12\) route dies.

The recommended strategy remains:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Do not include }\alpha\text{ in }T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
\text{ unless the final contradiction genuinely varies over }\alpha.
}
\]

---

# 5. The theorem

**CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber.**  
Assume:

1. the actual-source model is generated by fixed-target EF/gamma/tail/chart/background data and not by free corrected-package carrier coordinates;

2. the \(C_7\)-carrier quotient

   \[
   \mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7
   \]

   places EF-linear, chart, gamma/background, normalization, EF-curvature, chart-curvature, and normalization-curvature order-seven terms in the denominator;

3. the primitive \(Y12\)-origin quotient distinguishes

   \[
   (D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
   \]

   from EF-linear, \(DN_0\), chart, gamma/background, and moving-whitening terms;

4. fixed-\(\alpha\) actual first-variation slot spaces do not span

   \[
   DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
   \]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}=0.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0,
\qquad
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0.
}
\]

---

# 6. Failure modes

The theorem fails if any of the following holds.

## A. Free package coordinates in actual source

If the actual-source model includes local corrected-package coordinates, then it may directly move \(e_7\) or \(e_Y\). That kills rank two and may kill \(Y12\) entirely.

## B. \(C_7\)-quotient over-identifies EF drift with \(C_7\)

If ordinary EF-linear or chart/gamma order-seven drift survives in \(\mathcal Q_7^C\), then:

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal T_{\par})\neq0
\]

and rank two may fail.

## C. Fixed-\(\alpha\) slot-rank fails

If actual fixed-target variations span the primitive \(Y12\) slots, then

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})\neq0.
\]

Classify resulting image:

\[
0,\quad \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),\quad \mathbf C(0,1),\quad \mathbb C^2.
\]

## D. \(\alpha\) is internal source deformation

If \(\alpha\) is admitted as an actual-source variable inside \(N_0\), it may create primitive \(Y12\) curvature.

## E. Origin-pair quotient fails

If the quotient cannot distinguish primitive \(Y12\) from moving-whitening or EF-linear degree-two terms, \(\ell_9^Y\) is not faithful.

---

# 7. Consequence for \(B_Y\)

If CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber holds, then canonical actual-source parameter directions do not contribute to \(B_Y\):

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith,\mathrm{canonical}}=0.
\]

So

\[
B_Y
\]

can only come from:

\[
\mathcal N_{\sing}
\]

if it over-collapses the live pair,

\[
\mathcal T_{E_2^Y}^{\local}
\]

if completion has extraction-local fiber freedom,

\[
\mathcal T_{\lab,Y}^{\local}
\]

if labelled transition mismatch is locally free.

If those are also zero, then:

\[
\boxed{
B_Y=0.
}
\]

Rank-two D4 becomes available.

---

# Final status

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber}
\]

is plausible and is the correct theorem for Agent 2.

The main proof strategy is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Use the }C_7\text{-carrier quotient for the }s_7\text{ row,}
}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{

}
\]

Under fixed-\(\alpha\), actual EF/gamma/chart/tail source motion should avoid the live two-grade fiber. This supports the rank-two route \(B_Y=0\).

# 1. Split the proof into two independent rows

# 2. \(s_7\)-row: the \(C_7\)-carrier quotient proof

Define:

\[
\mathcal D_7
=
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal N_7.
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7.
\]

The live carrier class is

\[
[C_7]\in\mathcal Q_7^C.
\]

The finite theorem needed is:

\[
\boxed{
[C_7]\neq0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C,
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
[\partial_\theta q''_{\can}]_7=0,
\qquad
[\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}]_7=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.
}
\]

This is exactly the distinction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{raw order-seven Taylor drift}
\neq
\text{live }C_7\text{ carrier motion}.
}
\]

So to prove the \(s_7\)-row vanishing, one does **not** need actual parameters to have zero order-seven Taylor coefficients. One needs their order-seven pieces to lie in \(\mathcal D_7\).

---

# 3. Concrete \(s_7\)-row finite matrix test

Choose a basis of \(\mathcal J_7\). Let columns of \(D_7\) span \(\mathcal D_7\).

Let

\[
c_7^{\live}
\]

be the coordinate vector of \(C_7\).

Let

\[
a_{\theta,7}
\]

be the coordinate vector of

\[
[\partial_\theta q''_{\can}]_7.
\]

Let

\[
a_{\theta_i\theta_j,7}
\]

be the coordinate vector of

\[
[\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}]_7.
\]

Then the finite tests are:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(c_7^{\live},\operatorname{ran}D_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|c_7^{\live}\|,
\]

and

\[
a_{\theta,7}\in \operatorname{ran}D_7,
\]

\[
a_{\theta_i\theta_j,7}\in \operatorname{ran}D_7.
\]

Equivalently, any covector \(\ell_7\) annihilating \(\mathcal D_7\) and satisfying

\[
\ell_7(C_7)=1
\]

also satisfies:

\[
\ell_7(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can})=0.
\]

This is a direct Schur/cokernel computation.

---

# 4. Why ordinary parameters should lie in \(\mathcal D_7\)

For fixed-target actual parameters:

\[
m,\lambda,\mathrm{phase},\mathrm{normalization},\mathrm{chart},\mathrm{background/gamma},
\]

the order-seven components of first and second derivatives are generated by:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background},
\quad
\mathrm{normalization},
\quad
\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv},
\quad
\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv},
\quad
\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}.
\]

These are exactly placed in \(\mathcal D_7\).

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}]_7=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C.
}
\]

The only way this fails is if the actual-source model includes free corrected-package carrier coordinates, or if \(\mathcal D_7\) is missing some legitimate EF/chart/gamma curvature direction.

---

# 5. \(s_9^Y\)-row: primitive origin proof

For first tangents:

\[
\partial_\theta q''_{\can}
\]

has origin:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background},
\quad
\mathrm{normalization}.
\]

It is not primitive

\[
D^2N_0
\]

origin. Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})=0.
}

For second curvatures:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}
\]

contains terms of two kinds:

\[
DN_0[\partial_{ij}\xi]
\]

and

\[
D^2N_0[\partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi].
\]

The first has \(DN_0\)-origin and is killed.

The second can hit \(Y12\) only if:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

Thus the primitive \(Y12\) row vanishes if:

\[
a_Y\notin V_W
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_1\notin V_1
\quad\text{or}\quad
\xi_2\notin V_2.
\]

This is the finite slot-rank criterion.

---

# 6. Concrete \(s_9^Y\)-row finite matrix test

For each fixed-target parameter \(\theta\), let

\[
v_\theta=\partial_\theta\xi.
\]

Build projection matrices:

\[
M_1=[P_1v_\theta]_\theta,
\]

\[
M_2=[P_2v_\theta]_\theta,
\]

\[
M_W=[P_Wv_\theta]_\theta.
\]

Then test whether:

\[
\xi_1\in\operatorname{ran}M_1,
\]

\[
\xi_2\in\operatorname{ran}M_2,
\]

\[
a_Y\in\operatorname{ran}M_W.
\]

If any membership fails, then:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0.
}
\]

\[
a_Y\otimes\xi_1\otimes\xi_2
\in
\operatorname{span}\{
P_Wv_i\otimes P_1v_j\otimes P_2v_k
\}
\]

with the parameter-pair constraints relevant to actual second derivatives.

For fixed-\(\alpha\), expected result: at least one membership fails.

---

# 7. The fixed-\(\alpha\) policy is essential

If \(\alpha\) is only proof-thickening for O2/O3, exclude it from:

\[
\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}.
\]

Then fixed-target actual variations are diagonal/source-linear enough that the slot-rank exclusion is plausible.

If \(\alpha\) is included as an actual-source deformation, the matrices become:

\[
M_1^{+\alpha},\quad M_2^{+\alpha},\quad M_W^{+\alpha}.
\]

Then the \(Y12\) row may become nonzero.

Thus the theorem has two versions:

## Fixed-\(\alpha\)

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0
}
\]

expected.

## Actual \(\alpha\)-family

Need a new slot-rank computation. If nonzero, classify as:

\[
0,\quad \mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),\quad \mathbf C(0,1),\quad \mathbb C^2.
\]

---

# 8. Final theorem statement

**CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber.**  
Assume:

1. the \(C_7\)-carrier quotient \(\mathcal Q_7^C\) has denominator \(\mathcal D_7\) containing all EF-linear, chart, gamma/background, normalization, and parameter-curvature order-seven actual-source directions;

2. \(C_7\) survives in \(\mathcal Q_7^C\);

3. the primitive \(Y12\)-origin quotient is faithful;

4. fixed-\(\alpha\) actual-source first-variation slot spaces do not span the \(Y12\) input slots plus same-point whitening slot.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}=0.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0,
\qquad
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0.
}
\]

---

# 9. Remaining vulnerabilities

The theorem can fail only through specific mechanisms.

## \(s_7\)-row failures

* \(\mathcal D_7\) omits actual EF/chart/gamma curvature directions.
* \(C_7\) is not separated from actual-source drift.
* Actual source includes free corrected-package coordinates.

## \(s_9^Y\)-row failures

* fixed-target actual variations span the primitive \(Y12\) slots;
* \(\alpha\) is internal and must be included;
* origin-pair quotient collapses \(DN_0\)/EF-linear/moving-whitening into \(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}\).

These are finite and auditable.

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 status

The proof skeleton for

\[
\mathrm{CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber}
\]

is now concrete:

\[
\boxed{
s_7\text{-row}
=
C_7\text{-carrier quotient membership;}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
s_9^Y\text{-row}
=
Y12\text{-slot-rank/origin membership.}
}
\]

If both pass under fixed-\(\alpha\), then canonical actual-source motion does not enter the live two-grade fiber.

That is one of the key ingredients for proving:

\[

\]

and unlocking the rank-two D4 route.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: strengthening the \(s_7\)-row quotient test

The current proof skeleton for

\[
\mathrm{CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber}

splits into two rows:

[\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}]_7=0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7.
\]

I now push the \(s_7\)-row harder, because this is the fork that decides whether rank-two D4 survives.

---

# 1. What \(\ell_7\) is allowed to detect

The live covector

\[
\ell_7
\]

must detect the corrected package carrier \(C_7\), not raw order-seven Taylor drift.

So the quotient must be arranged so that:

\[
\ell_7(C_7)=1,
\]

but

\[
\ell_7(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathrm{chart})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\gamma/\mathrm{background})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathrm{normalization})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathrm{EF\text{-}curvature})=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathrm{chart\text{-}curvature})=0.
\]

Thus \(s_7\) is not:

\[
[z^7]\partial_\theta q''_{\can}.
\]

It is:

\[
[z^7]\partial_\theta q''_{\can}
\quad\text{modulo the canonical actual-source denominator}.
\]

That distinction is the core rank-two guardrail.

---

# 2. Candidate \(s_7\)-row leakage sources

For fixed-\(\alpha\), actual canonical image leakage into \(s_7\) can only come from one of these:

\[
\partial_m q''_{\can},
\quad
\partial_\lambda q''_{\can},
\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{phase}}q''_{\can},
\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{chart}}q''_{\can},
\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{norm}}q''_{\can},
\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{bg}}q''_{\can},
\]

and second derivatives of these.

The rank-two theorem needs all of these to land in \(\mathcal D_7\).

So define

\[
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}
=
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
[\partial_\theta q''_{\can}]_7,
[\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}]_7
\right\}.
\]

Then the target is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}\subseteq \mathcal D_7.
}
\]

This is stronger and cleaner than checking individual coefficients.

---

# 3. Why \(m\)-translation should vanish in \(\mathcal Q_7^C\)

Translation gives:

\[
\partial_m q''_{\can}=q'''_{\can}.
\]

Raw local expansion shifts coefficients. In Taylor language, it certainly moves order-seven data.

But this is precisely a chart/center-motion effect.

The \(C_7\)-carrier is supposed to detect a corrected package direction independent of center reparametrization. Therefore:

\[
[\partial_m q''_{\can}]_7
\in
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}}
\subseteq
\mathcal D_7.
\]

So

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_m q''_{\can})=0.
}
\]

Similarly,

\[
\partial_m^2q''_{\can}=q^{(4)}_{\can}
\]

is still center/jet curvature, not corrected package \(C_7\)-motion. It should lie in

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}}
\subseteq \mathcal D_7.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_m^2q''_{\can})=0.
}
\]

The key check is that \(\mathcal D_7\) explicitly includes translation-generated order-seven drift and its second-order curvature.

---

# 4. Why \(\lambda\)-scaling should vanish in \(\mathcal Q_7^C\)

Scaling gives:

\[
\partial_\lambda\left(\lambda^3q''(m+\lambda x)\right)
=
3\lambda^2q''(m+\lambda x)+\lambda^3xq'''(m+\lambda x).
\]

This is a chart-scale deformation and EF-linear reweighting of the same local source.

Its order-seven contribution is generated by:

\[
q'',
\quad
xq''',
\]

and therefore should be in

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}}.
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_\lambda q''_{\can})=0.
}
\]

Second \(\lambda\)-derivatives are chart/norm curvature, hence lie in:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}}.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_\lambda^2 q''_{\can})=0.
}
\]

Mixed \(m,\lambda\) terms are covered by the same denominator.

---

# 5. Phase, normalization, chart, and background

Phase and normalization changes are representative choices. They should be killed by:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}}.
\]

Chart changes are killed by:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}}.
\]

Gamma/background variations are killed by:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}}.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_7(\partial_{\mathrm{phase/chart/norm/bg}}q''_{\can})=0,
}
\]

and similarly for their second derivatives.

Again, this is not because raw coefficients vanish. It is because they belong to the denominator defining the live \(C_7\)-carrier quotient.

---

# 6. The crucial finite quotient condition

The \(s_7\)-row proof can now be stated as a finite quotient theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}
\subseteq
\mathcal D_7.
}
\]

where

\[
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{
[\partial_\theta q''_{\can}]_7,
[\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}]_7
\}.
\]

This should be checked by expressing the canonical image generators in the denominator basis.

Equivalently, with denominator matrix \(D_7\) and canonical generator matrix \(A_7\),

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}(D_7,A_7)=\operatorname{rank}(D_7).
}
\]

The live carrier condition is the complementary Schur test:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}(D_7,c_7^{\live})>\operatorname{rank}(D_7),
}
\]

with polynomial angle

\[
\operatorname{dist}(c_7^{\live},\operatorname{ran}D_7)
\ge Q^{-A}\|c_7^{\live}\|.
\]

Together these give:

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0,
\qquad
\ell_7(C_7)=1.
\]

---

# 7. What would break the \(s_7\)-row theorem?

There are only a few failure modes.

## Failure A: actual source includes corrected package coordinates

If the actual-source manifold is allowed to vary in the corrected package coordinate \(C_7\), then:

\[
C_7\in T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith},
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{s_7}T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}\neq0.
\]

This is a definition-level failure for rank two.

## Failure B: denominator \(\mathcal D_7\) is too small

If \(\mathcal D_7\) fails to include legitimate chart/EF/gamma/norm curvature terms, then ordinary actual-source motion may survive artificially in \(\mathcal Q_7^C\).

This is repairable by enlarging \(\mathcal D_7\), provided \(C_7\) remains nonzero.

## Failure C: \(C_7\) is not distinct from EF/chart drift

If

\[
C_7\in\mathcal D_7,
\]

then the live pair fails and rank-two D4 loses the \(s_7\) coordinate.

This is fatal for the rank-two route unless another independent carrier replaces \(C_7\).

## Failure D: nuisance overcollapse

If

\[
\mathcal N_7
\]

kills \(C_7\) or identifies it with canonical drift, then the live quotient is not faithful.

---

If the \(s_7\)-row theorem holds, then:

\[
\Pi_{s_7}\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}=0.
\]

If Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row theorem also holds, then canonical actual-source motion contributes no local fiber column.

Then:

\[

\]

can only receive local contributions from:

\[

\quad
\mathfrak C_Y,
\quad
\mathcal N_{\sing}.
\]

If those are finite coherent/residual/zero rather than local, then:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

So the \(s_7\)-quotient theorem is one of the last pieces needed for rank two.

\[

\quad\text{in}\quad

with polynomial separation, and

}
\]

This proves the \(s_7\)-row of \(\mathrm{CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber}\).

---

The \(s_7\)-row is now a clean finite linear-algebra problem:

\quad\text{but}\quad

The \(s_7\)-row theorem now has the finite form

\[
A_7\subseteq \operatorname{ran}D_7,

That is the next finite obstruction.

---

# 1. The saturation problem

The desired quotient is

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7.
\]

The denominator should include:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm}},

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal N_7.
\]

But if these spaces collectively span all of \(\mathcal J_7\), then

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=0
\]

and \(C_7\) dies.

So the actual test is the Schur complement

\[
\delta_7^2
=
\|c_7^{\live}\|^2
-
(c_7^{\live})^*
D_7(D_7^*D_7)^\dagger D_7^*c_7^{\live}.

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\delta_7^2\ge Q^{-A}\|c_7^{\live}\|^2.
}

This is the finite \(C_7\)-survival condition.

Let

\[
A_7=
[
a_{\theta,7},
a_{\theta_i\theta_j,7}
]

be the matrix of actual canonical first and second parameter images.

Need:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(A_7,\operatorname{ran}D_7)=0
}
\]

or quantitatively

\[
\boxed{
\|(I-P_{D_7})A_7\|\le Q^{-B}.
}

Thus the two simultaneous requirements are:

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{D_7})A_7\approx0,
}
\]

but

\[
\boxed{
(I-P_{D_7})c_7^{\live}\neq0\text{ at polynomial scale.}
}
\]

This is the exact “denominator saturation versus survival” problem.

---

# 3. How to prove \(C_7\)-survival by origin labels

The best proof is not numerical but typed.

Canonical drift terms have origins:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background},
\quad
\mathrm{normalization},
\quad
\mathrm{EF\text{-}curvature},
\quad
\mathrm{chart\text{-}curvature},
\quad
\mathrm{norm\text{-}curvature}.
\]

The \(C_7\) carrier should have a distinct corrected-package origin:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{origin}(C_7)=\mathrm{corrected\ D4/package\ carrier}.
}
\]

If the type system distinguishes this origin from all denominator origins, then

\[
c_7^{\live}\notin \operatorname{ran}D_7
\]

automatically.

So the desired theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal J_7
=
\mathcal D_7
\oplus
\mathbf C C_7
\oplus
\mathcal R_7
}
\]

at least after restricting to the live two-grade carrier sector, with polynomial Riesz conditioning.

Then \(\ell_7\) is just the coordinate functional on the \(C_7\)-summand.

---

# 4. The dangerous denominator terms

Some denominator terms are obviously safe:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}lin}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart}}.
\]

The dangerous ones are:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}},
\quad
\mathcal N_7.
\]

These are dangerous because they are already second-order or quotient-like and might accidentally contain the same corrected-package carrier.

So the finite audit should inspect these four first.

---

# 5. EF-curvature denominator

EF-curvature terms come from second derivatives of canonical EF atoms:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}F_X^\theta.
\]

For fixed-\(\alpha\), these remain EF-generated:

\[
\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}
=
\text{log-weighted/scale-weighted/source-linear curvature}.
\]

They should not create the corrected D4 package carrier \(C_7\), unless actual EF parameters include package coordinates.

So the theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{origin}(\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv})\neq \operatorname{origin}(C_7).
}
\]

If \(\alpha\) is internal source deformation, re-audit. But fixed-\(\alpha\) should be safe.

---

# 6. Chart-curvature and norm-curvature denominator

Chart/norm curvature can be tricky because it can create nonlinear-looking corrections.

But their origin is still:

\[
\mathrm{chart\text{-}curvature}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\mathrm{norm\text{-}curvature},
\]

not corrected D4 package carrier.

So the required typed condition is:

\[
\boxed{
C_7\notin
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{chart\text{-}curv}}
+
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{norm\text{-}curv}}.
}
\]

A practical way to check this is to define a carrier label that chart/norm operations cannot create, analogous to the \(Y12\) origin pair.

For \(C_7\), the label might be:

\[
\Sigma(C_7)
=
(\mathrm{D4/package},\mathrm{live}\ C_7,\mathrm{order}\ 7,\mathrm{specific\ parity/carrier}).
\]

Then chart/norm curvature lacks \(\Sigma(C_7)\).

---

# 7. Nuisance denominator \(\mathcal N_7\)

This is the biggest risk.

If

\[
C_7\in\mathcal N_7,
\]

then the live \(s_7\) coordinate is killed.

If

\[
\mathcal N_7
\]

identifies \(C_7\) with EF/chart drift, then \(\ell_7\) is not well-defined.

So require:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_7\cap \mathbf C C_7=0
}
\]

and preferably the stronger filtered condition:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal N_7\text{ is homogeneous with respect to }\Sigma(C_7).
}
\]

If full homogeneity is too strong, construct a direct covector:

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal N_7)=0,\qquad \ell_7(C_7)=1.
\]

This is the finite separating-functional route.

---

# 8. The strengthened \(C_7\)-carrier theorem

The theorem needed for rank two is:

**C7CarrierFaithfulness.** There exists a finite-dimensional quotient

\[
\mathcal Q_7^C=\mathcal J_7/\mathcal D_7
\]

and a covector

\[
\ell_7:\mathcal J_7\to\mathbf C
\]

such that:

\[
\ell_7(C_7)=1,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal D_7)=0,
\]

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)})=0,
\]

and

\[
\|\ell_7\|\le Q^A.
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\operatorname{dist}(C_7,\mathcal D_7)\ge Q^{-A}\|C_7\|,
\]

and

\[
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}\subseteq\mathcal D_7.
\]

This proves the \(s_7\)-row for canonical actual-source motion.

---

# 9. Interaction with rank-two D4

If C7CarrierFaithfulness holds and Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row theorem holds, then canonical source motion avoids both live fiber coordinates.

If \(E_2^Y\), labelled commutator, and nuisance also have no local fiber component, then:

\[
B_Y=0.
\]

Then the D4 matrix is rank two:

\[
\begin{pmatrix}
c_7&0\\
\beta_9&\alpha_9
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Thus the entire rank-two branch rests locally on:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{C7CarrierFaithfulness}
+
\mathrm{Y12OriginFaithfulness}
+
\mathrm{NoLocalContaminants}.
}
\]

---

# 10. Final Agent 2 update

The \(s_7\)-row is not just “put actual drift in the denominator.” It requires a balanced quotient:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I_{\can,7}^{(2)}\subseteq\mathcal D_7
\quad\text{but}\quad
C_7\notin\mathcal D_7.
}
\]

This is the finite denominator-saturation problem.

The next concrete audit is to test the dangerous denominator pieces:

\[
\mathcal J_7^{\mathrm{EF\text{-}curv}},
\quad

\quad

\quad
\mathcal N_7
\]

against the \(C_7\)-carrier label.

If those do not contain \(C_7\), then C7CarrierFaithfulness is plausible, and rank-two D4 remains alive.

None contains primitive source Hessian

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with same-point whitening.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\partial_\theta q''_{\can})=0
}
\]

for every fixed-target first parameter \(\theta\).

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal T_{\par})=0.
}
\]

This does not require raw order-nine Taylor coefficients to vanish. It only requires their typed carrier class to be outside \(\Sigma(Y12)\).

---

# 3. Second curvatures: split by origin

For second parameter variations:

\[
\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can},
\]

the chain rule gives two kinds of terms if the source is represented as

\[
N_0(\xi(\theta)).
\]

\[
\partial_i\partial_jN_0(\xi)
=
D^2N_0[\partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi]
+
DN_0[\partial_i\partial_j\xi].
\]

The term

\[
DN_0[\partial_i\partial_j\xi]
\]

has \(DN_0\)-origin, not primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin, so:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(DN_0[\partial_i\partial_j\xi])=0.
}
\]

Only

\[
D^2N_0[\partial_i\xi,\partial_j\xi]
\]

can possibly hit the primitive \(Y12\) coordinate.

Thus the entire second-curvature audit reduces to a slot-rank test for the first-variation space.

---

# 4. Slot-rank test

Let

\[
V_{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\theta\xi\}
\]

for fixed-\(\alpha\) parameters.

Project to the two \(Y12\) source slots and the same-point whitening slot:

\[
V_1=P_1V_{\arith},
\]

\[
V_2=P_2V_{\arith},
\]

\[
V_W=P_WV_{\arith}.
\]

The dangerous membership is:

\[
\boxed{
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
}
\]

A sufficient vanishing condition is that at least one slot is missing:

\[
a_Y\notin V_W,
\]

or

\[
\xi_1\notin V_1,
\]

or

\[
\xi_2\notin V_2.
\]

If any one holds, then:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0.
}
\]

---

# 5. Fixed-\(\alpha\) expected slot result

For fixed-\(\alpha\), the allowed variations are diagonal/source-linear deformations of the same canonical EF/gamma/chart source.

They are generated by operations such as:

\[
\log n\text{-multiplication},
\]

\[
x\partial_x\text{-scaling},
\]

\[
\text{phase reweighting},
\]

\[
\text{chart/norm/gamma background variation}.
\]

These do not independently generate:

\[
\xi_1,
\quad
\xi_2,
\quad
a_Y
\]

with the primitive mixed-branch \(12\), same-point, one-whitening-slot labels.

So the expected finite result is:

\[
\boxed{
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\notin
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
}
\]

Hence:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0.
}
\]

---

# 6. Concrete matrix test

For implementation or finite verification, choose bases of the slot spaces and build:

\[
M_1=[P_1\partial_\theta\xi]_\theta,
\]

\[
M_2=[P_2\partial_\theta\xi]_\theta,
\]

\[
M_W=[P_W\partial_\theta\xi]_\theta.
\]

Then check:

\[
\xi_1\in\operatorname{ran}M_1?
\]

\[
\xi_2\in\operatorname{ran}M_2?
\]

\[
a_Y\in\operatorname{ran}M_W?
\]

If any answer is no, the \(Y12\)-origin row vanishes.

If all three are yes, run the coupled bilinear test:

\[
a_Y\otimes\xi_1\otimes\xi_2
\in
\operatorname{span}
\left\{
P_Wv_i\otimes P_1v_j\otimes P_2v_k
\right\},
\]

with the parameter-pair restrictions that actual second derivatives impose.

For fixed-\(\alpha\), I expect the test to fail before the coupled stage, because at least one primitive slot is absent.

---

# 7. The \(\alpha\)-fork

If \(\alpha\) is only a proof-thickening or O2/O3 averaging device, exclude it from:

\[
V_{\arith}
\]

and from:

\[
T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}.
\]

Then the fixed-\(\alpha\) theorem applies.

If \(\alpha\) is an actual-source parameter, include:

\[
v_\alpha=\partial_\alpha\xi
\]

in \(V_{\arith}\) and rerun the slot-rank test.

The result can be:

## Zero

Still safe for rank two.

## Graph-like

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\alpha\text{-curvature}
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Rank two collapses to rank one.

## Vertical

\[
(0,1)\in B_Y.
\]

The current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

## Rank two actual model

The local live fiber is fully absorbed.

So the policy remains:

\[
\boxed{
\alpha\text{ should stay outside }T\mathcal M_Y^{\arith}
\text{ unless the final contradiction genuinely uses an actual }\alpha\text{-family.}
}
\]

---

# 8. Failure modes

Y12OriginFaithfulness fails only through specific mechanisms.

## A. Origin-pair collapse

The quotient fails to distinguish:

\[
(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})
\]

from:

\[
(DN_0,DW),
\quad
(N_0,D^2W),
\quad
(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},DW).
\]

Then \(\ell_9^Y\) is not a faithful primitive \(Y12\)-coordinate.

## B. Actual fixed-target slot span is too large

The fixed-target actual first-variation space spans:

\[
a_Y,\xi_1,\xi_2.
\]

Then actual second curvature may create \(Y12\).

## C. \(\alpha\) is internal

If \(\alpha\) is promoted to a true source deformation, it may generate primitive \(Y12\) curvature.

## D. Free package coordinates

If actual source is allowed to vary in corrected-package coordinates, the quotient theorem fails by definition.

---

# 9. The theorem

**Y12OriginFaithfulness.**  
Assume:

1. fixed-\(\alpha\) downstream;

2. actual first variations are EF-linear/chart/gamma/background/norm-origin and do not include free package coordinates;

3. the primitive \(Y12\) quotient is origin-pair faithful;

4. the fixed-target slot-rank test misses at least one of the \(Y12\) input/whitening slots.

Then:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0.
}
\]

Equivalently:

\[
\boxed{
[\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}]_Y=0
}
\]

in the primitive \(Y12\)-origin quotient.

---

# Final status

Agent 2’s \(s_9^Y\)-row reduces to a finite slot-rank and origin-faithfulness test.

Under fixed-\(\alpha\), the expected result is:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0.
}
\]

This supplies the second row of:

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}=0.
\]

Combined with Agent 1’s \(C_7\)-carrier faithfulness for the \(s_7\)-row, this supports the typed exactness theorem:

\[
B_Y=0,
\]

which unlocks the rank-two D4 vector wall.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: strengthening \(Y12\)-slot exclusion by a diagonal-module argument

We have reduced the \(s_9^Y\)-row to:

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0,
\]

where

\[
\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{\partial_\theta q''_{\can},\partial_{\theta_i}\partial_{\theta_j}q''_{\can}\}
\]

for fixed-target actual parameters.

The remaining finite test is whether actual second curvature can generate the primitive carrier:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

I now push the slot-rank argument into a stronger representation/module statement.

---

# 1. Actual fixed-target variations form a diagonal module

For fixed-\(\alpha\), the actual first variations are generated by operations on the same canonical EF packet:

\[
\partial_m,\quad
\partial_\lambda,\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{phase}},\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{chart}},\quad
\partial_{\mathrm{norm}},\quad
\partial_{\gamma/\mathrm{bg}}.
\]

These act by:

\[
\text{translation derivative},
\]

\[
\text{scale derivative},
\]

\[
\text{phase/log-frequency multiplier},
\]

\[
\text{chart or normalization linearization},
\]

\[
\text{background/gamma drift}.
\]

Thus the actual first-variation source-input space should be a diagonal/coherent module:

\[
V_{\arith}
\subseteq
\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi
+
V_{\mathrm{bg}}
+
V_{\mathrm{chart}},
\]

where \(\mathcal M_{\diag}\) is generated by fixed multipliers such as

\[
1,\quad \log n,\quad x\partial_x,\quad \text{phase weights},\quad \text{scale weights}.
\]

The point is:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\arith}
\text{ is generated from one canonical source packet, not from independent package slots.}
}
\]

So actual second curvature can only generate:

\[
D^2N_0[M_1\xi,M_2\xi]
\]

with \(M_1,M_2\in\mathcal M_{\diag}\), plus wrong-origin terms.

The primitive \(Y12\) carrier, by contrast, uses independently labelled mixed-block source-loss slots:

\[
D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\]

with same-point whitening.

Thus the stronger exclusion target is:

\[
\boxed{
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\notin
DW_{\same}[P_W\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi]\,
D^2N_0[P_1\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi,P_2\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi].
}
\]

---

# 2. Label-module separation

Define the labelled slot spaces:

\[
U_1^{Y},\quad U_2^{Y},\quad W_{\same}^{Y}.
\]

The primitive \(Y12\) triple is:

\[
(a_Y,\xi_1,\xi_2)
\in
W_{\same}^{Y}\times U_1^{Y}\times U_2^{Y}.
\]

The diagonal module projects to:

\[
P_W\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi,\quad
P_1\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi,\quad
P_2\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi.
\]

The best possible proof is a representation-label separation:

\[
\boxed{
\xi_1\notin P_1\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\xi_2\notin P_2\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin P_W\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi.
}
\]

This is stronger than coefficient checking. It says the actual variation module does not even enter the labelled slot where \(Y12\) lives.

The plausible representation reason is:

\[
\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi
\]

preserves the underlying source packet and branch labelling, while \(Y12\) is a promoted Bottleneck-C mixed-block Hessian carrier with a same-point whitening slot. The latter is not obtained by multiplying or differentiating the canonical packet.

---

# 3. Parity/branch shortcut

There is an even sharper possible shortcut.

The \(Y12\) carrier has a definite source-grade/reflection parity inherited from:

\[
(Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle})^T
=
(-1)^{g+e}Y_{g,\beta}^{\langle e\rangle}.
\]

If diagonal actual variations preserve source-grade parity differently from the primitive mixed \(12\) Hessian carrier, then:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
D^2N_0[\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi,\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi]
=0
}
\]

by parity alone.

This would be the cleanest proof.

The finite check is:

1. assign each diagonal variation \(M\xi\) a source-grade label;
2. compute the parity of \(D^2N_0[M_i\xi,M_j\xi]\);
3. compare to \(\pi_Y\), the parity of \(Y12\);
4. verify same-point whitening placement.

If the parity differs, then:

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9})=0
\]

without solving the rank problem.

---

# 4. Same-point whitening as final guard

Even if the source-Hessian slots are not separated by branch or parity, the whitening slot remains a separate blocker.

Actual fixed-target variations change whitening through global Gram/normalization changes:

\[
D W[\delta G_{\theta}]
\]

with \(\delta G_{\theta}\) determined by global source variations.

The \(Y12\) carrier requires:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]
\]

where \(a_Y\) is tied to the same-point Bottleneck-C carrier.

Thus a sufficient theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}.
}
\]

This may be easier to prove than the source-slot separation because ordinary \(m,\lambda,\) phase, and gamma variations do not create the specific same-point whitening input.

So the proof should try the blockers in this order:

\[
\boxed{
\text{same-point whitening slot missing}}
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\text{branch/source slot missing}}
\]

then

\[
\boxed{
\text{parity mismatch}}
\]

then only if all fail:

\[
\boxed{
\text{coupled bilinear coefficient test}.}
\]

---

# 5. Coupled bilinear fallback

If all individual slot tests fail, we still need not conclude absorption. Individual membership

\[
a_Y\in V_W,\quad \xi_1\in V_1,\quad \xi_2\in V_2
\]

is necessary but not sufficient.

The actual second derivatives only produce paired combinations:

\[
D^2N_0[v_i,v_j]
\]

where \(v_i,v_j\) come from actual parameter pairs \((\theta_i,\theta_j)\).

So the real image is:

\[
\mathcal B_{\arith}
=
\operatorname{span}_{i,j}
DW_{\same}[a_{ij}]D^2N_0[P_1v_i,P_2v_j],
\]

not the full tensor product

\[
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

Thus the final finite fallback is:

\[
\boxed{
y_Y\notin \mathcal B_{\arith}.
}
\]

This can be checked by a Schur residual:

\[
\delta_{Y,\II}^2
=
\operatorname{dist}
\left(
y_Y,\mathcal B_{\arith}
\right)^2.
\]

For the \(s_9^Y\)-row to vanish, need:

\[
\delta_{Y,\II}^2\ge Q^{-A}\|y_Y\|^2,
\]

or simply zero image in the labelled quotient.

If instead:

\[
y_Y\in\mathcal B_{\arith},
\]

then actual second curvature hits the primitive \(Y12\)-fiber. Then classify the resulting fiber image:

\[
0,\quad
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y),\quad
\mathbf C(0,1),\quad
\mathbb C^2.
\]

---

# 6. Fixed-\(\alpha\) theorem in final form

The strongest useful theorem is:

**FixedAlphaY12SlotExclusion.**  
For the fixed-target actual parameter set

\[
\Theta_{\fixed}
=
\{m,\lambda,\mathrm{phase},\mathrm{chart},\mathrm{norm},\gamma/\mathrm{bg}\},
\]

the first-variation module

\[
V_{\arith}^{\fixed}
=
\operatorname{span}\{\partial_\theta\xi:\theta\in\Theta_{\fixed}\}
\]

satisfies at least one of:

\[
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed},
\]

\[
\xi_1\notin P_1V_{\arith}^{\fixed},
\]

\[
\xi_2\notin P_2V_{\arith}^{\fixed},
\]

or

\[
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
D^2N_0[V_{\arith}^{\fixed},V_{\arith}^{\fixed}]=0
\]

by branch/parity.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{\fixed})=0.
}
\]

Together with first-tangent origin filtering:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2),\fixed})=0.
}
\]

This is the \(s_9^Y\)-row of \(B_Y=0\).

---

# 7. If \(\alpha\) is internal

If the proof later requires an actual \(\alpha\)-family, define:

\[
V_{\arith}^{+\alpha}
=
V_{\arith}^{\fixed}
+
\mathbf C\,\partial_\alpha\xi.
\]

Then rerun the theorem with \(V_{\arith}^{+\alpha}\).

There are four possible outcomes:

## Still zero

\[
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{+\alpha})=0.
\]

Rank-two remains possible.

## Graph-only

\[
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{+\alpha}
\subseteq
\mathbf C(1,\rho_Y).
\]

Rank-two collapses to rank-one.

## Vertical

\[
(0,1)\in
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{+\alpha}.
\]

Current \(Y12\) route dies locally.

## Full rank

\[
\operatorname{rank}
\Pi_{\fib,Y}\mathcal{II}_{\arith,9}^{+\alpha}=2.
\]

Local live fiber absorbed.

This cleanly isolates the \(\alpha\)-risk.

---

# 8. Interaction with \(C_7\)-row

The \(s_9^Y\)-row proof does not decide rank two alone.

Rank two requires also:

\[
\ell_7(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2)})=0.
\]

The \(s_7\)-row is independent and governed by:

\[
A_7\subseteq\operatorname{ran}D_7,
\qquad
c_7^{\live}\notin\operatorname{ran}D_7.
\]

Thus:

\[
\boxed{
\mathrm{CanonicalImageAvoidsLiveFiber}
=
\mathrm{C7CarrierAvoidance}
+
\mathrm{FixedAlphaY12SlotExclusion}.
}
\]

This factorization is the cleanest local canonical-image theorem.

---

# 9. Remaining proof burden for Agent 2

Agent 2’s remaining finite burden is to prove one of the three strong blockers:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\xi_1\notin P_1V_{\arith}^{\fixed}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\xi_2\notin P_2V_{\arith}^{\fixed}.
}
\]

The most promising appears to be:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed}
}
\]

because same-point Bottleneck-C whitening is more specialized than global chart/Gram variation.

If that fails, try branch/parity:

\[
\boxed{
\Pi_{\Sigma(Y12)}
D^2N_0[\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi,\mathcal M_{\diag}\xi]=0.
}
\]

Only then do the full bilinear Schur computation.

---

# Final status

The \(s_9^Y\)-row is now a layered finite proof:

1. first tangents killed by origin;
2. second curvatures reduced to slot-rank;
3. fixed-\(\alpha\) actual variations form a diagonal/coherent module;
4. diagonal/coherent module should miss at least one \(Y12\) source/whitening slot;
5. if not, use branch/parity or coupled bilinear Schur.

The expected conclusion remains:

\[
\boxed{
\ell_9^Y(\mathcal I_{\can}^{(2),\fixed})=0.
}
\]

That supports \(B_Y=0\) and the rank-two D4 vector-wall branch.

## Agent 2 — continued attack: prioritizing the same-point whitening slot

The \(s_9^Y\)-row proof currently has three possible blockers:

\[
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed},
\qquad
\xi_1\notin P_1V_{\arith}^{\fixed},
\qquad
\xi_2\notin P_2V_{\arith}^{\fixed}.
\]

The most promising is the first:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed}.
}
\]

Reason: the source slots \(\xi_1,\xi_2\) may be hard to separate from diagonal EF variations, but the same-point whitening input \(a_Y\) is much more structured. It is tied to the Bottleneck-C same-point whitening slot, not to ordinary \(m,\lambda,\) phase, chart, background, or gamma variation. The current rank-two branch depends on proving this kind of typed exactness rather than broad Taylor cancellation. 

---

# 1. Define the whitening variation space

Let

\[
W_{\same}^{Y}
\]

be the same-point whitening input space for the primitive \(Y12\) carrier.

The \(Y12\) carrier contains

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2].
\]

For fixed-target actual parameters, define

\[
V_W^{\fixed}
=
P_WV_{\arith}^{\fixed}
=
\operatorname{span}
\{P_W\partial_\theta\xi:\theta\in\Theta_{\fixed}\}.
\]

The theorem we want is:

\[
\boxed{
a_Y\notin V_W^{\fixed}.
}
\]

If this holds, then no fixed-\(\alpha\) actual second curvature can generate the primitive \(Y12\) carrier, regardless of whether the source slots \(\xi_1,\xi_2\) are accessible.

---

# 2. What fixed-target parameters can do to whitening

The fixed-target parameters are:

\[
m,\lambda,\mathrm{phase},\mathrm{chart},\mathrm{normalization},\gamma/\mathrm{background}.
\]

Their whitening variations are generated by:

\[
DG[\partial_\theta \xi],
\]

or by chart/gauge derivatives of the Gram normalization.

These are global or representative-level changes of the whitening operator. Their origins are:

\[
\mathrm{Gram/normalization},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},
\quad
\gamma/\mathrm{background}.
\]

The \(Y12\) whitening slot is instead:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y],
\]

where \(a_Y\) is coupled to the same-point Bottleneck-C quadratic source-Hessian carrier.

So the desired separation is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{ordinary Gram/chart whitening variation}
\neq
\text{same-point Bottleneck-C whitening input}.
}
\]

This is a typed statement. It should not depend on raw coefficient size.

---

# 3. Same-point support test

A strong sufficient condition is support separation.

Let

\[
\operatorname{Supp}_{\mathrm{pt}}(a_Y)=\mathrm{same}
\]

mean that \(a_Y\) is supported at the same collision point as the two \(Y12\) source-loss inputs.

Ordinary fixed-target whitening variations have support labels:

\[
\mathrm{transported},
\quad
\mathrm{global},
\quad
\mathrm{chart},
\quad
\mathrm{background},
\]

not

\[
\mathrm{same\ Bottleneck\ point}.
\]

Then:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\mathrm{same}}V_W^{\fixed}=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
a_Y\notin V_W^{\fixed}.
}
\]

So the first finite audit should be:

\[
\boxed{
P_{\mathrm{same}}P_W\partial_\theta\xi=0
\quad
\forall \theta\in\Theta_{\fixed}.
}
\]

If true, the \(s_9^Y\)-row is killed at the whitening slot.

---

# 4. Origin-pair test

Even if ordinary whitening variations have same-point support, they may still have the wrong origin.

The primitive \(Y12\) origin pair is:

\[
(o_N,o_W)=(D^2N_0,DW_{\same}).
\]

Fixed-target whitening variations have origin pairs like:

\[
(\mathrm{EF\text{-}linear},DW),
\]

\[
(DN_0,DW),
\]

\[
(N_0,D^2W),
\]

\[
(\gamma/\mathrm{background},DW).
\]

Thus a second sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
P_{(D^2N_0,DW_{\same})}V_W^{\fixed}=0.
}
\]

That is, fixed-target actual-source whitening never appears attached to primitive \(D^2N_0\)-origin with the same-point slot.

This is probably the cleanest formulation.

---

# 5. Whitening-rank matrix

To make the test finite, choose a basis of \(W_{\same}^{Y}\). Let \(w_Y\) be the coordinate vector of \(a_Y\).

For each fixed-target parameter \(\theta\), compute

\[
w_\theta=P_W\partial_\theta\xi.
\]

Build

\[
M_W=[w_\theta]_{\theta\in\Theta_{\fixed}}.
\]

Then the finite condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{rank}[M_W\ w_Y]>\operatorname{rank}M_W.
}
\]

Quantitatively:

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{dist}(w_Y,\operatorname{ran}M_W)
\ge Q^{-A}\|w_Y\|.
}
\]

If this holds, fixed-target actual second curvature cannot enter \(s_9^Y\).

This test is smaller than the full bilinear \(D^2N_0\)-image test.

---

# 6. If \(a_Y\in V_W^{\fixed}\)

If the whitening slot is not separated, we do **not** immediately lose.

Then we must test the source slots.

Construct:

\[
M_1=[P_1\partial_\theta\xi]_\theta,
\qquad
M_2=[P_2\partial_\theta\xi]_\theta.
\]

Check:

\[
\xi_1\in\operatorname{ran}M_1,
\qquad
\xi_2\in\operatorname{ran}M_2.
\]

If either fails, the \(Y12\) row still vanishes.

Only if all three individual memberships hold do we go to the coupled bilinear image:

\[
DW_{\same}[a_Y]D^2N_0[\xi_1,\xi_2]
\in
DW_{\same}[V_W]D^2N_0[V_1,V_2].
\]

---

# 7. Coupled bilinear image is smaller than the tensor product

Even if

\[
a_Y\in V_W,\qquad \xi_1\in V_1,\qquad \xi_2\in V_2,
\]

actual second curvature produces only parameter-paired combinations:

\[
D^2N_0[v_i,v_j].
\]

So the true image is:

\[
\mathcal B_{\II}
=
\operatorname{span}_{i,j}
DW_{\same}[a_{ij}]D^2N_0[P_1v_i,P_2v_j],
\]

not the full tensor product.

\[

}
